<a id="t-6f2f80c244"></a>
## Thread: COMTI data "conversion" problems! — 3 this month / 9 total

_Thread started [1998-12](1998-12.md#t-6f2f80c244)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COMTI data "conversion" problems!

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1999-01-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990101171819.01153.00005053@ng-cb1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-IOOL6pHbXO8S@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
On  31 Dec 98 20:40:16 GMT

posted a valuable opinion in the context of loss of precision in numeric format
transitions, as follows ...

<<
This is why you should use a COBOL tool for you GUI and not VB! 
>>

I think we all agree that tools have to be selected and used in a manner that
retains the precision required by the application.  Yet I do not fully agree
with the poster that the problem decribed at the origin of this thread nor the
technical issues engaged when trying to hold fractions in various formats leads
necessarily to the conclusion that VB is not good at the front end (in GUI
programs).

I have used BASIC for many dollar-and-cents, and engineering factor fields
holding non integral values. BASIC works fine for this. You just have to be
aware of the internal technology. 
Such data can easily be stored in integer fields by multiplying and dividing by
appropriate powers of 10.

Engineering calcs are very sensitive to initial values, and it is both very
important to preserve the exact original input and to exploit floating point
technology to enable large scale interation during which ranges can vary
dramatically (that is, beyond the integer field facility).  In short you
sometimes have to move data back and forth from integer to floating point
fields.

This is not unlike what the COBOL compiler does for us automatically when it
converts a display numeric filed to integer or packed for efficient calc, and
then automatically converts it back to display for us.  In BASIC you need to do
some of the house keeping yourself. Which doesn't of itself argue conclusively
against BASIC.

BASIC has many advantages. In Windows contexts BASIC, like Perl in UNIX, allows
for rapid deployment, ease of transfer to the next programmer, and direct
simple hooks to the presentation managers.

The importance of this is that you can get a show to the user much quicker and
keep projects on track with earlier reviews, compared to COBOL. Speaking in
economic terms, you can also use a different class of worker.

As relative processing requirements grow, the stronger discipline of  COBOL,
though slower in deployment, gains comparative advantage in project control. So
it is not surprising to see BASIC up front, and COBOL in the core.

It is no more difficult to understand precision loss in a movement from single
precision floating point to integer, than it is to understand the loss of
precision in a movement from a PIC 9(n).99(2) to a PIC 9(n).9(1). We just have
to pay attention to the tools.

Also, I might add that the mainframes are finally coming around to the PC IEEE
floating point format in hardware. That will coerce COBOL into a more standard
floating point computational type.  At which point the roosters come home to
COBOL as a GUI deployment toolset.

When you move data from a field of one type to a field of another type, you
must be conscious of the potential for loss of information (precision, sign,
justification, editing).  It does not matter what tools you are using, it is
the technology implicit in the field type specifications that counts. 

What does happen to some users of BASIC in their early experience, is that they
assume that a non integer must be stored in either a single or double precision
number format.  Not really.  You can get these things into integer format
without much trouble, and it is simply required when you are sensitive to
specific limitations of floating point format.

(On some occassion you can also preserve precision by carrying data as
character).

In quite a few situations storing the number in single or double precision
format preseves enough information. It depends on the application specifics. 
But if you have a challenge with a few fields, that is not enough reason to
abandon BASIC as the tool of choice. In many situations it has economic
advantages.

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COMTI data "conversion" problems!

- **From:** dssmith@NA2.US.ML.COM
- **Date:** 1999-01-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78agj2$9ds$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<75tlpj$sgl2@dm1cns.on.bell.ca> <19981224143453.01167.00001204@ng-cc1.aol.com>`

```
Simply use the DECIMAL automation type.  This is a supported
variant type and implements a fixed point data representation.
(I believe it is a 64 bit representation with 32 bits left and
right of the implied decimal point)

No rounding errors to worry about.  The only problem is with large
numbers, > 4gig.  See the MSDN docs for info on VT_DECIMAL datatype.
(This is the underlying data structure for the CURRENCY automation type.)

Dean Smith

In article <19981224143453.01167.00001204@ng-cc1.aol.com>,
  rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk) wrote:
> "Jean-Philippe Valois" <jean-philippe.valois@bellsygma.com>

item

> 1/5 which in binary is a continuous fraction.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> the comp-3 (BCD) field.  I am fairly confident that CICS is not doing this,
but
> confess ignorance about your COMTI. Most likely candidate is VB, as the
> truncator.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Not being familiar with the objectives of the specific application, one can
not
> criticize your data definitions.  But it is worth mentioning, that if you are
> sensitive to the difference between 4.1 and 4.2, you MAY need to preserve
> atleast another digit of precision.  A COBOL definition of 9(x).9 instructs
the
> computer to ignore hundredths. Yet you are telling us you care about the
effect
> in data format conversion of hundredths.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> presentation by dividing by ten, (perhaps in COBOL with exact control per
field
> with ROUNDING phrases).
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> The value 1/5 can not be represented exactly in binary. Same effect in 4.2,
8.2
> and 12.2, ad infinitum.
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
On Mon, 28 Dec 1998 16:31:07, vasanthraghavan@hotmail.com wrote:

> Hi,
>     Could somebody tell me if there are any telecommuting jobs for a cobol /
> sas, mainframe programmer , so I can work from home ? even if I can get some
> information or links to this topic, I would greatly appreciate it.

For many of the job ads I see posted here, I e-mail the originator 
asking if they have "part time" telecommuting for these - on a 
contract basis, figuring that if they bite I can make some extra 
money.  So far nearly all have said "What an interesting idea" but 
none have taken it any further.  Good luck.
```

_Thread started [1998-12](1998-12.md#t-411096245a)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: JCL...evil entity???

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Warren G. Simmons" <warrens@inficad.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<368D22AD.8E44359C@inficad.com>`
- **References:** `<3686597E.13678B48@at.cbl> <3687F765.233C9B7@at.cbl> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-jJ3OHOfCtIVt@dsm4merlin.iix.com> <368BAAF0.83CA1E08@CapGemini.Co.UK> <76gbsl$9d5$1@clarknet.clark.net>`

```
I know of one case where a set of programs were written to be a shell
around the needs for creating JCL. This shell allowed application
porgrammers to describe their chain of runs, thus the combination of the
key words, and file names were called a Chain Of Runs Executive.
CHORE. Still some staff personal had to make jcl decks for some
problems, and to maintain the software from the vendor. I am of the
opinion that some computers have such higher level languages now. I am
happy that I don't have to use batch files in Windows. <G>

Warren Simmons
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: JCL...evil entity???

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-01-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990101105415.26246.00004584@ng125.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<368BAAF0.83CA1E08@CapGemini.Co.UK>`

```
Simon Elliott  wrote...

[snip]

>Incidentally. Is JCL a langauge? I reckon that strictly speaking it
>isn't, but I'm not sure why. Can anyone confirm my prejudice?

Depends. It has a grammar, a syntax, and a vocabulary. It is not a compiled
language (although it gets "converted"), however, nor an interpreted language.
I teach it as a specification language [which term I leave undefined].

Cheers,


Steve Comstock
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: JCL...evil entity???

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-01-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<368d2e10.4314193@netnews>`
- **References:** `<368BAAF0.83CA1E08@CapGemini.Co.UK> <19990101105415.26246.00004584@ng125.aol.com>`

```
'Twas 1 Jan 1999 15:54:15 GMT, when scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>Simon Elliott  wrote...

>>Incidentally. Is JCL a langauge? I reckon that strictly speaking it
>>isn't, but I'm not sure why. Can anyone confirm my prejudice?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>I teach it as a specification language [which term I leave undefined].

By my reckoning, JCL *is* compiled, though it is not compiled to object
code.  The conversion of JCL is not just tokenizing -- it resolves name
references, and that is compiling.

And back to the subject of this thread:  Yes, JCL is an evil entity.  When
I see all the things I can do in Unisys WFL, and in the various Unix
shells, and what I could do in MS-DOS using HDM or COMMO or even with just
BAT files, I wonder how MVS guys have settled for such a wimpy language as
JCL for so long.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: JCL...evil entity???

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** JeffreyFarkas@nospamearthlink.net (Jeff Farkas)
- **Date:** 1999-01-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.10f7103a3e0fe02b9896f0@news.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<368BAAF0.83CA1E08@CapGemini.Co.UK> <19990101105415.26246.00004584@ng125.aol.com> <368d2e10.4314193@netnews>`

```
In article <368d2e10.4314193@netnews>, Barticus@att.spam.net says...
=> By my reckoning, JCL *is* compiled, though it is not compiled to object
=> code.  The conversion of JCL is not just tokenizing -- it resolves name
=> references, and that is compiling.
=> 
=> And back to the subject of this thread:  Yes, JCL is an evil entity.  When
=> I see all the things I can do in Unisys WFL, and in the various Unix
=> shells, and what I could do in MS-DOS using HDM or COMMO or even with just
=> BAT files, I wonder how MVS guys have settled for such a wimpy language as
=> JCL for so long.

Just a thought or two on this topic.

First, if you use JCl on a daily basis you discover that it is neither 
wimpy nor is it hard to learn. It's a very powerful language that allows 
you to execute you programs and various utilities that support your 
programs. 

Second, it has improved over time and if the current trend continues JCL 
will be very powerful and very easy to use. 

Please note that these comments are in reference to IBM JCL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ IBM JCL v Unisys WFL

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-01-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<368db4de.38635947@netnews>`
- **References:** `<368BAAF0.83CA1E08@CapGemini.Co.UK> <19990101105415.26246.00004584@ng125.aol.com> <368d2e10.4314193@netnews> <MPG.10f7103a3e0fe02b9896f0@news.earthlink.net>`

```
'Twas Fri, 1 Jan 1999 17:03:28 -0500, when
JeffreyFarkas@nospamearthlink.net (Jeff Farkas) illuminated
comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>First, if you use JCl on a daily basis you discover that it is neither 
>wimpy nor is it hard to learn. 

I never said JCL was hard to learn.  If it's no longer wimpy, I am
pleased.

>It's a very powerful language that allows 
>you to execute you programs and various utilities that support your 
>programs. 

It would be a pretty sad excuse for a scripting language that *didn't* let
you execute programs and utilities.  The question is how much control you
have over those programs.  If JCL isn't wimp, educate me by showing how
you would write the job below.

Specifications:

1)  This job has one parameter:  The name of a company.
2)  There are up to three input files.  The first node of each file is
INA, INB, or INC, the second node of the file name is the company name,
and the third node is today's date.
3)  If INA/company/date is present run PROGA which produces
OUTA/company/date.  Likewise INB->PROGB->OUTB and INC->PROGC->OUTC.
4)  Run the three programs concurrently.
5)  If more than one output file is produced, run PROGM to
create MERGED/company/date.  This program will correctly detect if only
two or all three files are present, so it is run only one way.
6)  If exactly one output file was produced, run PROGX with that file for
input.  If more than one output file is produced, run PROGX agaisnt the
MERGED file.
7)  Feel free to use "." instead of "/" in filenames.

Unisys WFL:

BEGIN JOB UP/TO/3/FILES (STRING COMPANY);
STRING COMPDATE, MFILE;
INTEGER I;

BEGIN SUBROUTINE PASS1;
    IF  FILE INA/#COMPDATE IS RESIDENT THEN
        PROCESS RUN PROGA;
            FILE IN  = INA/#COMPDATE;
            FILE OUT = OUTA/#COMPDATE;
    IF  FILE INB/#COMPDATE IS RESIDENT THEN
        PROCESS RUN PROGB;
            FILE IN  = INB/#COMPDATE;
            FILE OUT = OUTB/#COMPDATE;
    IF  FILE INC/#COMPDATE IS RESIDENT THEN
        PROCESS RUN PROGC;
            FILE IN  = INC/#COMPDATE;
            FILE OUT = OUTC/#COMPDATE;
END PASS1;

COMPDATE := COMPANY/TODAYSDATE(YYYYMMDD);
PASS1;
I:=0
IF  FILE OUTA/#COMPDATE IS RESIDENT THEN 
    BEGIN
    I:=I+1;
    MFILE:= OUTA/#COMPDATE;
    END;
IF  FILE OUTB/#COMPDATE IS RESIDENT THEN 
    BEGIN
    I:=I+1;
    MFILE:= OUTB/#COMPDATE;
    END;
IF  FILE OUTC/#COMPDATE IS RESIDENT THEN 
    BEGIN
    I:=I+1;
    MFILE:= OUTC/#COMPDATE;
    END;
IF I > 1 THEN 
    BEGIN
    MFILE:= MERGED/#COMPDATE;
    RUN PROGM;
        FILE INA  = OUTA/#COMPDATE;
        FILE INB  = OUTB/#COMPDATE;
        FILE INC  = OUTC/#COMPDATE;
        FILE OUT  = #MFILE;

    END;
IF I > 0 THEN
    RUN PROGX
        FILE IN = #MFILE;
END JOB;        

I just wrote that off the top of my head.  There are probably a couple
syntax errors, but the functionality is all available.  RUN causes WFL to
wait for it the program to complete.  PROCESS RUN causes the program to
run simultaneous with WFL.  I'm using the subroutine PASS1, because a
subroutine never exits until all the programs it started have completed.
If you want a more complex multi tasking job, you can assign task
variables to all the tasks, and test their status.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM JCL v Unisys WFL

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1999-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369C3AF2.B7FF3556@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<368BAAF0.83CA1E08@CapGemini.Co.UK> <19990101105415.26246.00004584@ng125.aol.com> <368d2e10.4314193@netnews> <MPG.10f7103a3e0fe02b9896f0@news.earthlink.net> <368db4de.38635947@netnews> <3697669F.31B6@netbox.com> <369af204.90899446@netnews>`

```
Randall Bart wrote:
...
> Can my example be coded in REXX?  If REXX is so much better than JCL, why
> is everyone still using JCL?  What does JCL do that REXX doesn't?
...

I have read of some MVS installations that actually do use batch REXX
for controlling file allocation and program sequencing logic in their
batch environment.  However, there are some potential drawbacks of
replacing JCL logic with REXX logic:
(1) Allocation of files in REXX (which uses SVC99 for dynamic allocation
under the covers) fails if the file is enqueued by some other process
rather than waiting until the file is available, as happens with a JCL
reference.  This may cause a REXX implementation to fail requiring
complex restart scenariors while a JCL implementation might be only be
delayed for a time before completing correctly.
(2) SVC99 does not support UNIT=AFF allocations, so there is no easy way
to allocate a dataset which is a concatenation of tape datasets without
requiring a physical tape drive for each of the datasets, even though
there is no logical reason they could not share a tape drive.  Also
distinct tape files required during a single program exeuction cannot
share a physical tape drive, even if at most one will be OPEN at the
same time.
(3) Batch job streams will occasionally fail for all sorts of reasons,
some of which may be difficult to predict in advance.  In JCL each
execution of a main program constitutes a jobstep, which is a "natural"
restart point for a jobs that fails somewhere in midstream.  JCL
structure is sufficiently "simple" that a number of products are around
to support typical step restart situations (CA-11, ZEBB, etc.).  The
additional flexibility of REXX also means that if you want to support
restart in a complex sequence of executions implemented as a REXX
routine, you must anticipate and custom design that restart support into
the REXX exec itself.

It should certainly be technically feasible to enhance SVC99 support to
counter deficiencies (1) and (2), provided some consensus is reached as
to a reasonable convention, but these limitations have been around for a
long time.  I don't see any "easy" way to address (3), and for the kinds
of nontrivial batch processes that abound in large main frame shops, you
really need ability to restart as close to the point of failure as
possible, both to avoid wasting resources and to keep job execution
within what may be a narrow batch time window.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM JCL v Unisys WFL

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77loa0$v28@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<368BAAF0.83CA1E08@CapGemini.Co.UK> <19990101105415.26246.00004584@ng125.aol.com> <368d2e10.4314193@netnews> <MPG.10f7103a3e0fe02b9896f0@news.earthlink.net> <368db4de.38635947@netnews> <3697669F.31B6@netbox.com> <369af204.90899446@netnews>`

```


JCL is BATCH based; REXX is interactive based. (Well, they do cross
around each other a bit, but that is a good ballpark explanation.)

Also REXX replaces CLIST, which is sort of like MVS batch, and it *is*
the JCL for VM systems. But then again, VM Systems are not very batch based.
-Paul
```

```
In article <3690E296.70A428B9@home.com>,
  Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com> wrote:
> > Incidentally. Is JCL a langauge? I reckon that strictly speaking it
> > isn't, but I'm not sure why. Can anyone confirm my prejudice?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I'll add that in the UNIX world you have scripting languages which would be
the closest equivalent to JCL. Doing shell scripts is referred to as
programming and they look pretty much like a program in other language. Also,
Perl (a full blown programming language) is based on shell scripting
languages. I suppose JCL could be "souped up" and be made into a general
purpose language. I would not expect that to happen as I have heard that IBM
is moving toward a UNIX-like shell language on the mainframe. Has that ever
come about? I've been away from the mainframe for a while, but in my mind I
would have liked that sort of thing for those machines. JCL is fine if you
want to do a batch job, and the ISPF menus were fine for some things.
However, there are times when I just wanted to be at a command prompt where I
could interactively do exactly what I needed to do.
```

```
> I'll add that in the UNIX world you have scripting languages which would be
> the closest equivalent to JCL. Doing shell scripts is referred to as
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> could interactively do exactly what I needed to do.

And you can pick your flavor of shell script language.  IBM has tried to
get away from JCL a few times.  You can run jobs from TSO (although I
have never done so), and you can use Rexx on all IBM platforms. 
(possibly Unix also)  They say they will come out with Virtual Java
Machines on all levels also, which will be nice for creating the world's
largest Web server!!

But there is a lot of inertia to overcome to wean us from JCL.  They
have to be careful, most customers who are likely to replace JCL are
just as likely to replace IBM.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: JCL...evil entity???

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Thomas Rowe" <trowe@greenville.infi.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be39f7$ed00a3a0$5f0785d0@default>`
- **References:** `<3686597E.13678B48@at.cbl> <36869fe5.93245829@news1.ibm.net> <36877FF2.60560303@at.cbl> <76852a$5po$1@news.igs.net> <3687F765.233C9B7@at.cbl> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-jJ3OHOfCtIVt@dsm4merlin.iix.com> <368BAAF0.83CA1E08@CapGemini.Co.UK> <3690E296.70A428B9@home.com> <7709n1$tid$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I personally don't feel that JCL qualifies as a language.  Since, all it
really consist of is control cards to submit a series of programs to a
batch queue.  JCL is definately not a shell scripting language, like you
would find with UNIX, or like DCL is for VMS.  You can even do more with
DOS batch files than you can with JCL.  My understanding is that REXX is a
scripting language however I haven't really used it.

By the way all decent operating system, don't even require a lot of the
crap you have to code in JCL.  If a program wants to create a new file, the
operating system should be smart enough to go out and create it itself, if
you want to be able to use a file the operating system should be able to
determine that it exist and open the file by itself.
etc.

What ever happened to good old Run <ProgramName>.  And it's worse if you
want to Compile.  You need three pages of information, file names, library
concatinations etc.  I personally prefere COBOL <ProgramName>, Link
<ProgramName>.  Sure you can throw some parameters on the end of that if
you want to, but the point is you can compile 95% of you program without
having to.

Tom Rowe
```

- **Message-ID:** `<369b6d6a.0@news1.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<3686597E.13678B48@at.cbl> <36869fe5.93245829@news1.ibm.net> <36877FF2.60560303@at.cbl> <76852a$5po$1@news.igs.net> <3687F765.233C9B7@at.cbl> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-jJ3OHOfCtIVt@dsm4merlin.iix.com> <368BAAF0.83CA1E08@CapGemini.Co.UK> <3690E296.70A428B9@home.com> <7709n1$tid$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <01be39f7$ed00a3a0$5f0785d0@default>`

```

Thomas Rowe wrote in message <01be39f7$ed00a3a0$5f0785d0@default>...
>I personally don't feel that JCL qualifies as a language.  Since, all it
>really consist of is control cards to submit a series of programs to a
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>etc.

The operating system should be smart enough?  Mainframes are capable of
dynamic allocation (SVC99), it's just rarely used, and you would have to do
it from a program written in assembler (or use COBOL options or commands
I've never heard of).  The only utility on our mainframe that uses SVC99
regularly is plain vanilla FTP.  Running that utility in the same job with
other programs that use MVS's normal file allocation is a major pain in the
butt (it has to do with generation data sets; running an FTP step against a
+1 generation, you have to ask for the +0 generation; restarts are worse).

The main difference between dynamic and requested allocation is that in
dynamic allocation, if the system doesn't have enough space, it will write
and write data until it does run out of space, and then blow up.

As an application programmer, it's possible to have a pretty good idea of
the size of the data being working with.  In requested allocation, you enter
this amount, and then provide a second amount to allow for future expansion
(that's simplifying matters).
It is true that under this system you can blow up at both ends (not having
enough space to answer an allocation request; or not having an allocation
that is big enough), at least when you don't have enough space to answer the
allocation, it blows up right away; instead of very likely running for hours
and then blowing up when it runs out of disk space.

The requested allocation system is not intuitive, and actually can be a pain
in and of itself.  But our Unix system servers experience space problems
just like our mainframe.

The real answer is a good storage administrator with good tools and enough
authority to dictate what happens on the harddrives (not always the case).


>What ever happened to good old Run <ProgramName>.  And it's worse if you
>want to Compile.  You need three pages of information, file names, library
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't do any of that on our mainframe.  Someone wrote a quick ISPF panel
system over a decade ago, now I just go through and set T (for Test), B (for
Batch: COBOL without CICS), and the program name, hit enter twice and I'm
sent directly to SDSF where I can watch my program wait for an intiator, run
(including all it's runtime stats displayed in realtime (try that in Unix)),
and finish and look at it's output.
There are many third-party change management software packages that provide
similar services.

>Tom Rowe
>
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
```

- **Message-ID:** `<77g3o2$3ef@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3686597E.13678B48@at.cbl> <36869fe5.93245829@news1.ibm.net> <36877FF2.60560303@at.cbl> <76852a$5po$1@news.igs.net> <3687F765.233C9B7@at.cbl> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-jJ3OHOfCtIVt@dsm4merlin.iix.com> <368BAAF0.83CA1E08@CapGemini.Co.UK> <3690E296.70A428B9@home.com> <7709n1$tid$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <01be39f7$ed00a3a0$5f0785d0@default> <369b6d6a.0@news1.uswest.net>`

```
Chris Osborne wrote in message <369b6d6a.0@news1.uswest.net>...
>
>Thomas Rowe wrote in message <01be39f7$ed00a3a0$5f0785d0@default>...
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>>crap you have to code in JCL.  If a program wants to create a new file,
the
>>operating system should be smart enough to go out and create it itself, if
>>you want to be able to use a file the operating system should be able to
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
   <much snippage>

ACTUALLY,
  if you are using the current strategic COBOLs on MVS (i.e. COBOL for
this-and-that) with LE *and* you take the WRONG LE run-time option - which
happens to be the default - and meets the ANSI/ISO Standard)
    CBLQDA
then you get SVC99's all over the place.

On VM, the ANSI Standard requirement that any OPEN OUTPUT "create" a file
works well - as it does on PC's, UNIX, and most every where else.  It *COULD
HAVE* been designed to work well on MVS too - if only IBM had used a
compiler option and/or the jobname to figure out a highlevel note - and
created cataloged output files.

HOWEVER, what happens on IBM right now (again as the IBM provided default)
is whenever you make a typo in your JCL or in your Select/Assign statement,
is that a TEMPORARY (totally unfindable) file is created (via our friend
SVC99) is created when your COBOL program runs.
```

```
Now, mind you, I am no Cobol expert.

The Cobol statement would be something like --

  CALL 'xxx' USING arg1, ...

and it is equivalent to this Assembler --

         CALL xxx,(arg1,...),VL
```

```
There really shouldn't be any problems or issues.  The one thing to think
about is the fact that the opposite (HLASM calling COBOL) *does* have some
issues about when the COBOL environment is set up.  So be a little careful
about "who's on top" of your application)
```

#### ↳ Re: Call HLASM program from COBOL MVS program

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-02T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<368E5783.A5DC5158@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<76dfli$e6q2@news.asiaonline.net>`

```
A note about the parameters on the assembler side.  If register 1 = 0, no
parameters were passed.  If all parameters were passed by reference, register
1 will point to a parameter list.  After doing the using, register save, and
new save area, do a  LM 4,7,0(1) and registers 4-7 will point to the
parameters passed them.

Kin wrote:

> I decided to write a COBOL MVS program which would pass several parameters
> to an high level assembler program. Would anyone have some idea on that?
>
> The programs are expected to run on OS/390 platform.
```

#### ↳ Re: Call HLASM program from COBOL MVS program

- **From:** "Chris Craddock" <crashclc@netin.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.asm370
- **Message-ID:** `<76oa46$llg$1@news.onramp.net>`
- **References:** `<76dfli$e6q2@news.asiaonline.net>`

```

Kin wrote in message <76dfli$e6q2@news.asiaonline.net>...
>I decided to write a COBOL MVS program which would pass several parameters
>to an high level assembler program. Would anyone have some idea on that?


It is pretty simple. If the asm program is called from Cobol like this...

    CALL asmcode USING arg1 arg2 etc

Then the asm program will receive control (via standard linkage) with R1
pointing to a list of addresses. The first one is the address of arg1, the
second is the address of arg2 etc.

>The programs are expected to run on OS/390 platform.

In that case you can probably save yourself a little bit of programming
effort by having your assembler code run under the control of LE. Go look at
the LE book on interlanguage calls. The CEEENTRY macro can be used to
perform entry linkage and obtain working storage (if required) and the
CEEEXIT macro can be used to return control to your calling program.

Enjoy!
```

```
Aside from all the good answers given already, I have a couple of questions
for you to ponder.

1) Does the sub-program already exist ? If so then the only concern you should
have is providing the parameters it requires.

2) If you intend to write the assembly and need help on basic parameter list
stuff, do you know enough assembler to complete the task ?

3) Is assembly language the best language for the sub-program? Is there a
requirement that cannot be satisfied with COBOL ?
```

```
>
>Chris Franklin wrote in message <369127C5.FB8019DA@co.slo.ca.us>...

[snipped first part, but it is excellent - after all, he had a good teacher
  at a good school  :-)]

>>3) Is assembly language the best language for the sub-program? Is there a
>>requirement that cannot be satisfied with COBOL ?
>>
>The subprogram need to output message from MVS console. I wonder if COBOL
>program can do that.

DISPLAY whatever UPON CONSOLE is how to do it in COBOL. If you need
a response from the operator, ACCEPT whatever FROM CONSOLE.

ObASM370:  If you need to call a WTO program, it probably has two parameters,
the length of the text and the text itself.

Later,
Ray
```

```
Kin,

Do you need to issue the console messages in CICS or Batch? For CICS, use the
WRITE OPERATOR command (CICS/ESA only). For Batch, use DISPLAY UPON CONSOLE. I
believe CICS/VSE 2.2 and greater supports the WRITE OPERATOR command as well.

HTH....

WOB,
Atlanta
```

#### ↳ Re: Call HLASM program from COBOL MVS program

- **From:** John A Parke <jparke@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.asm370
- **Message-ID:** `<3697BDE6.54BE8427@mindspring.com>`
- **References:** `<76dfli$e6q2@news.asiaonline.net>`

```
I don't know if I understand your question correctly.  If you  mean that your
Cobol program is going to call an Assembler program then the answer is pretty
straight forward.

The MVS convention is for the calling program, in this case COBOL to pass the
address in register 1 of a list of addresses of the passed parameters.  COBOL
will do this for you automatically.  The Assembler program  gets the address
of the passed parameters by again using register 1.  Just remember that R1
points to a  list of addresses of the passed parameters and not the actual
parameters themselves. Each address is 4 bytes long, so that the first address
is pointed to by R1, the 2nd address is at the address in R1 + 4, etc.

The last parameter address will have it's high order bit set to x'80' ,
however this isn't a concern if you're always passing a fixed number of
arguments.

I hope I understood your question and provided some help.

John

Kin wrote:

> I decided to write a COBOL MVS program which would pass several parameters
> to an high level assembler program. Would anyone have some idea on that?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks.
```

```
+<huge snip regarding banks, reserves, economic colapse, bank collapse, etc.>
+
+>But this is getting rather far off-topic, I think.
+
+
+Come on, Doug, we all know that after 2000 AD
+we are going to have to convert all dollar amounts
+to floating point due to the inflation rate ...
+
+
Naaah. We can still use fixed-point, we just need to standardize on a 
different unit of currency. I propose the "Douglas" (symbol D), the value of 
which will be 78 kg of 0.9999 fine gold. Under this standard, the current cost 
of a new Buick would be about D3.75 -- you see, no need for floating point. 
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3690DB9C.C2FBD366@home.com>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net> <3680241d.28188023@news.prodigy.net> <PNMf2.2822$au3.2607502@news4.mia> <36809e09.2807256@news3.ibm.net> <Ojch2.1895$vu.1926719@news2.mia> <36870b70.1457936@news3.ibm.net> <OYMh2.2815$vu.2819480@news2.mia> <768kof$cm2$2@server.cntfl.com> <76ap3b$9rb$2@server.cntfl.com> <76fmas$ghl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <76fr4u$2u0_002@news.netdirect.net> <76fu11$t0c$1@news.igs.net> <76hei8$2k0_004@news.netdirect.net>`

```
> Naaah. We can still use fixed-point, we just need to standardize on a
> different unit of currency. I propose the "Douglas" (symbol D), the value of
> which will be 78 kg of 0.9999 fine gold. Under this standard, the current cost
> of a new Buick would be about D3.75 -- you see, no need for floating point.

According to the Constitution, a mill is legal currency.  I once had a
bill which had me calculate an interest charge and I wrote my check to
the exact mill.  They returned it to me!  I can see problems with the
Euro where Italians are used to a much more finely divided currency. 
Lots of money in the rounding.

The feds allow you to write your personal taxes by truncating to the
nearest dollar.  I wonder whether we will in the future round higher -
getting rid of pennies for instance, or whether e-commerce will allow us
to keep whatever precision we want without work - mills for instance!

I still value having learned to use a slide rule.  It taught me the most
useful parts of my mathematical education far better than kids are
learning nowadays:
1.  I had to learn to estimate my final result to know where to place
the decimal.
2.  I had to learn how many digits are significant by knowing how
precise my input data were.
3.  I had to understand proportion (which is mainly what I use now).

Those thinking skills listed above apply to far more than math.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76imal$jub$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net> <3680241d.28188023@news.prodigy.net> <PNMf2.2822$au3.2607502@news4.mia> <36809e09.2807256@news3.ibm.net> <Ojch2.1895$vu.1926719@news2.mia> <36870b70.1457936@news3.ibm.net> <OYMh2.2815$vu.2819480@news2.mia> <768kof$cm2$2@server.cntfl.com> <76ap3b$9rb$2@server.cntfl.com> <76hg6d$ajg$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <m63j2.1526$Zk.555221@news3.mia>`

```

Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>Jerry Peacock wrote:
>>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>--
Excellent analogy!

The well-publicized loss of a, supposedly, unsinkable ship
and loss of life among the passengers did not cause the
collapse of the trans-oceanic cruise ship business. This
tragedy, along with others, helped to bring about changes
in the cruise ship business to insure that there would,
for example, be enough lifeboats for all passengers on
board.

The trans-oceanic cruise ship business continued for
many more years, eventually losing to competetion
from the trans-oceanic airline business.

Likewise, the well-publicized closings of some S&Ls did
not cause the collapse of the savings and loan industry.
It led to some systemic changes to reduce future problems;
but, ultimately, the savings and loan industry lost to
competetion, and its "passengers" now "cruise" with banks.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<l55k2.2843$0t.1782955@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net> <3680241d.28188023@news.prodigy.net> <PNMf2.2822$au3.2607502@news4.mia> <36809e09.2807256@news3.ibm.net> <Ojch2.1895$vu.1926719@news2.mia> <36870b70.1457936@news3.ibm.net> <OYMh2.2815$vu.2819480@news2.mia> <768kof$cm2$2@server.cntfl.com> <76ap3b$9rb$2@server.cntfl.com> <76hg6d$ajg$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3690D962.39B924D6@home.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
>Jerry Peacock wrote:
>> Rick Smith wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>got paid anyway.

Yeah, by *us*, the taxpayers!  Those S&Ls and their depositors got by
for years not paying for FDIC insurance, while all the rest of us were
paying.  Then when the bottom drops out, we bail them out anyway.  It
is getting so people don't think there should be any penalty for stupid
and irresponsible actions.  Year after year we the taxpayers bail out
people who build on river banks and sea shores and get wiped out by
flood or hurricane, just to build right back in the same place.  If I
get burned because of my own stupidity, why should others have to pay
for it?  If there are no consequences for irresponsible and stupid
behavior, why should anybody bother to be responsible?  Why take away
hard earned money from those who live responsibly, and give it to those
who lost out because they were irresponsible?  Penalize the industrious
and reward the irresponsible.  Wow!  What a recipe for success!
```

```
In article <l55k2.2843$0t.1782955@news4.mia>,
   "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:
+Howard Brazee wrote:
+>Jerry Peacock wrote:
+>> Rick Smith wrote:
+>> >There are now, IMO, adequate safeguards to protect against
+>> >the conditions that had previously caused bank failures. There
+>> >are also safeguards against other conditions that might
+>> >potentially cause bank failures. Therefore, IMO, the possibility of
+>> >a banking collapse is, effectively, zero.
+>>
+>> Isn't that about what they said regarding the Savings & Loan
+>> industry just a few years ago?
+>
+>So do you know anybody who lost his S&L deposits?  The S&L crises had
+>the Feds get real worried and use taxpayer money to buy high and sell
+>low.  Those who bought the debt from the Feds got real rich as that debt
+>got paid anyway.
+
+Yeah, by *us*, the taxpayers!  Those S&Ls and their depositors got by
+for years not paying for FDIC insurance, while all the rest of us were
+paying.  Then when the bottom drops out, we bail them out anyway.  It
+is getting so people don't think there should be any penalty for stupid
+and irresponsible actions.  Year after year we the taxpayers bail out
+people who build on river banks and sea shores and get wiped out by
+flood or hurricane, just to build right back in the same place.  If I
+get burned because of my own stupidity, why should others have to pay
+for it?  If there are no consequences for irresponsible and stupid
+behavior, why should anybody bother to be responsible?  Why take away
+hard earned money from those who live responsibly, and give it to those
+who lost out because they were irresponsible?  Penalize the industrious
+and reward the irresponsible.  Wow!  What a recipe for success!

Hear, hear!! Perhaps you remember a well-publicized event of about 1986, in 
which numerous expensive homes on bluffs (sand dunes, actually) overlooking 
Lake Michigan's eastern shore were damaged or destroyed by erosion of said 
bluffs caused by unusually high water levels. Homeowners were demanding that 
the government DO SOMETHING.

As I saw the news footage of million-dollar homes sliding into the lake, I 
remembered a story I once heard about a fool who built his house upon sand. 
And the rains came, and the waters rose... I'm sure this story is familiar to 
you, Judson :-) It's not a new concept, folks. 
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76r852$bhd$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net> <3680241d.28188023@news.prodigy.net> <PNMf2.2822$au3.2607502@news4.mia> <36809e09.2807256@news3.ibm.net> <Ojch2.1895$vu.1926719@news2.mia> <36870b70.1457936@news3.ibm.net> <OYMh2.2815$vu.2819480@news2.mia> <768kof$cm2$2@server.cntfl.com> <76ap3b$9rb$2@server.cntfl.com> <76hg6d$ajg$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3690D962.39B924D6@home.com> <l55k2.2843$0t.1782955@news4.mia>`

```

Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
[...]
>
>Yeah, by *us*, the taxpayers!  Those S&Ls and their depositors got by
>for years not paying for FDIC insurance, while all the rest of us were
>paying.

Plese reconsider your statement.

Banks that were part of the Federal Reserve System paid insurance
premiums to FDIC.

Savings and Loans that were part of the Federal Home Loan Bank
Board paid insurance premiums to FSLIC (Federal Savings and
Loan Insurance Corporation).

Your comment seems to imply either that no premiums were being
paid, which is not true, or that Savings and Loans should have been
required to pay two insurance premiums, which seems rather unfair.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<U_9k2.38$EC3.84902@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net> <3680241d.28188023@news.prodigy.net> <PNMf2.2822$au3.2607502@news4.mia> <36809e09.2807256@news3.ibm.net> <Ojch2.1895$vu.1926719@news2.mia> <36870b70.1457936@news3.ibm.net> <OYMh2.2815$vu.2819480@news2.mia> <768kof$cm2$2@server.cntfl.com> <76ap3b$9rb$2@server.cntfl.com> <76hg6d$ajg$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3690D962.39B924D6@home.com> <l55k2.2843$0t.1782955@news4.mia> <76r852$bhd$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
>
>Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>required to pay two insurance premiums, which seems rather unfair.

Sorry I wasn't clear.  The S&L's which were bailed out were in states
which did not require any federally insured deposits in S&L's, so the
S&L's did not elect to take Federal deposit insurance.  However, we
taxpayers in other states who were paying our Federal deposit
insurance premiums all along still had to bail out those S&Ls.  If
the S&Ls had Federal insurance, it would not have taken an act of
Congress to bail them out, no?  At the very least, the depositors
should have lost the Federal insurance premiums which they avoided
paying all those years, or the state governments which had permitted
the fiasco should have done it.  At least the people more directly
responsible would have paid, in one way or another.  I want to say
that I don't have a problem with disaster relief when there is no
irresponsibility involved.  I just don't see why we should reward
the irresponsible at the expense of the responsible.  I also believe
in freedom.  If you want to be irresponsible, fine, as long as you
don't endanger others.  But you should have to bear any penalty or
cost of that irresponsibility yourself.  If others want to help,
fine, but it certainly should not be forcefully placed on the backs
of others.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76rmof$ln7$2@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net> <3680241d.28188023@news.prodigy.net> <PNMf2.2822$au3.2607502@news4.mia> <36809e09.2807256@news3.ibm.net> <Ojch2.1895$vu.1926719@news2.mia> <36870b70.1457936@news3.ibm.net> <OYMh2.2815$vu.2819480@news2.mia> <768kof$cm2$2@server.cntfl.com> <76ap3b$9rb$2@server.cntfl.com> <76hg6d$ajg$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3690D962.39B924D6@home.com> <l55k2.2843$0t.1782955@news4.mia> <76r852$bhd$1@server.cntfl.com> <U_9k2.38$EC3.84902@news3.mia>`

```

Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
[...]
>Sorry I wasn't clear.  The S&L's which were bailed out were in states
>which did not require [any federally insured deposits in S&L's],

With apologies for editing, I will understand that to read
"S&L deposits to be federally insured."

> so the
>S&L's did not elect to take Federal deposit insurance.  However, we
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>responsible would have paid, in one way or another.
[...]
>--
1. I find it remarkable that the states would not have required some
deposit insurance. This would mean that the state legislators were
irresponsible or that the provided insurance proved inadequate. In
either case, I would take that any assistance given by the federal
government would have been to "cover the state's ass" and not
solely to protect the depositors.

2. While the fiasco was occurring, I read the news articles with
"some" interest. I do not remember any reference to state-
chartered instituitons. Since these would have more importance
as local news items, it is reasonable that, were state-chartered
institutions involved, I would not have seen the articles.

3. The "act(s) of Congress" came in at least three parts, if I
remember correctly (Yahoo search, keyword: fslic). The first was
a 10 billion dollar "loan" to FSLIC. When that proved to be
insufficient, Congress formed the Resolution Trust Corporation
(RTC) with a 30 billion dollar "loan." After things cooled-off, the
Congress authorized the Office of Thrift Supervision (OTS) to
replace the RTC. Since Congress was involved, it would seem
that only (depositiors at) federally-chartered, specifically
(depositors at) FSLIC-insured, institutions should have been
covered. I do understand that "responsible politcian" is an
oxymoron; hence, it does not seem unreasonable that a politician
may have been able to include some state-chartered non-FSLIC
institutions.

4. Since the Federal insurance premiums would have applied to
those depositors who closed their accounts prior to the closing
of the thrift, how do you fairly assess the premiums that should
have been collected during the years leading to the closing?
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How will Y2K affect ME?????

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Vrmk2.632$EC3.486954@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<36749915.86480533@news.prodigy.net> <3680241d.28188023@news.prodigy.net> <PNMf2.2822$au3.2607502@news4.mia> <36809e09.2807256@news3.ibm.net> <Ojch2.1895$vu.1926719@news2.mia> <36870b70.1457936@news3.ibm.net> <OYMh2.2815$vu.2819480@news2.mia> <768kof$cm2$2@server.cntfl.com> <76ap3b$9rb$2@server.cntfl.com> <76hg6d$ajg$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3690D962.39B924D6@home.com> <l55k2.2843$0t.1782955@news4.mia> <76r852$bhd$1@server.cntfl.com> <U_9k2.38$EC3.84902@news3.mia> <76rmof$ln7$2@server.cntfl.com>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
>
>1. I find it remarkable that the states would not have required some
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>solely to protect the depositors.

I too find it remarkable.  I don't remember all the specific states,
but if you research carefully, you will see that only S&Ls in certain
states were involved.  Those were the states and S&Ls which did not
carry federal deposit insurance.  Didn't matter though, we bailed
them out anyway, entirely at our expense.


>2. While the fiasco was occurring, I read the news articles with
>"some" interest. I do not remember any reference to state-
>chartered instituitons. Since these would have more importance
>as local news items, it is reasonable that, were state-chartered
>institutions involved, I would not have seen the articles.

I read the articles with great interest and dismay, because it was
my money (and yours) they were spending.  I and my whole family
were outraged at the time.

>3. The "act(s) of Congress" came in at least three parts, if I
>remember correctly (Yahoo search, keyword: fslic). The first was
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>institutions.

The first issue was whether to bail them out at all.  After it was
decided to do that, it was necessary to substantially subsidize
the FSLIC, in part because they had been receiving absolutely no
money from those S&Ls.

>4. Since the Federal insurance premiums would have applied to
>those depositors who closed their accounts prior to the closing
>of the thrift, how do you fairly assess the premiums that should
>have been collected during the years leading to the closing?


You can't do it with absolute fairness.  But isn't it better to at
least approach fairness than abandon it altogether?  Justice in
this world is at best only an approximation.  Should we then totally
abandon any attempt at justice?  Apparently so, in many minds.
```

```
Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>Rick Smith wrote:
>>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>them out anyway, entirely at our expense.

It is also important to point out that one of the primary reasons for
their failure was that not only were they not federally insured, the
state cash reserve and lending restrictions were much looser.  Had they
been federally insured, they would have been audited to comply with the
stricter federal requirements, and most of them would probably not have
folded, just as S&Ls in other states requiring federal insurance did
not fold.
```

```
I remember one story, in particular, wherein a chuch lost over
$300,000.

It seems that the church had deposited all of its building fund, as I
recall about $450,000, into a single S&L. When that S&L failed,
FSLIC tried to get another instution to take over the failed S&L,
when none would, FSLIC was left with no choice but to pay off the
depositors, up to the insurance limit of $100,000.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Basic as a business language

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-04T00:00:00

- **References:** `<76g10o$1e5$1@news.igs.net> <76g80h$kqe$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com> <76gbuc$7ef$1@news.igs.net>`

```
I believe REXX can be set up to handle any size integers!!!  If your
integers were larger than the computer would handle natively, it slowed
down considerably.

I suppose a string handling language such as lisp may do the same
thing.  I wonder which languages are effective in calculating pi to
5,000 places.

Who here has had to fudge numbers larger than what their COBOL
implementation can handle natively?  How did you do it?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Basic as a business language

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yV5k2.2857$0t.1812763@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<76g10o$1e5$1@news.igs.net> <76g80h$kqe$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com> <76gbuc$7ef$1@news.igs.net> <3690CFC0.1900D24@home.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
>
>Who here has had to fudge numbers larger than what their COBOL
>implementation can handle natively?  How did you do it?

I have written long precision routines in COBOL, but it has been quite
a while, and I no longer have them.  If you want to see high precision
(up to 1075 digits) handled in C, you can download BIGCALC.ZIP from my
web page below.  BIGCALC is a RPN calculator which can do logs, trig,
powers, etc. as easily as a normal calculator.  Add and subtract are
simple, and multiply is not difficult.  Divide is much more complex,
especially if it is efficient.  Everything else is built on the basic
four functions.

The simplest conceptual way is probably to use a table for each number,
where one element is a digit.  Then write algorithms to handle them as
you learned to do in grammar school.  Keep the signs separate, and deal
with the numbers as if they were positive, then set the signs.  To add
numbers with opposite signs, subtract the smaller absolute value from
the larger absolute value and set the sign of the result to the sign of
the larger absolute value.  For multiply and divide the resultant sign
is positive if the two input signs are the same, negative if they are
different.  To subtract a negative number, reverse the sign of the
subtrahend and add.  And so on.

For example, to multiply you can do just what you do on paper:

       1 2 3 4 -
      x  5 6 7 +  (signs different, result negative)
   -------------
       8 6 3 8
     7 4 0 4
   6 1 7 0
   -------------
   6 9 9 6 7 8 -

However, you don't have to keep the intermediate rows.  Add all results
immediately into the product field.

Divide is usually implemented as a series of subtracts.  Left align the
subtrahend with the minuend, and start subtracting.  Each time you
successfully subtract the subtrahend from the minuend, add 1 to the
quotient, shifted as many digits as you shifted the subtrahend to match
the left of the minuend.  When you get an underflow, then restore the
minuend by adding back any part of the subtrahend which you have already
subtracted from the minuend on that cycle, then shift the subtrahend one
position to the right, as well as the pointer into the product where you
are adding each subtract cycle.  If you structure your number tables so
it is easy to do, you can compare the subtrahend against the part of the
minuend it is shifted to, so you don't have to subtract when the minuend
is smaller.  If the compare is efficient, it is faster than the partial
subtract.  Continue this process until the minuend is zero or until the
desired accuracy is reached.  In BIGCALC I divide the first few digits
of the subtrahend into the first few digits of the subtrahend, and use
that as a multiple to do the subtracts in one cycle.  This is probably
efficient only if you have a large number of digits.

You can speed up the basic processes by keeping more than one digit in
each table entry.  In COBOL you can keep up to 18 digits, but you should
keep only 9 digits to avoid overflow on multiply.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Basic as a business language

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76qv30$phv$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<76g10o$1e5$1@news.igs.net> <76g80h$kqe$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com> <76gbuc$7ef$1@news.igs.net> <3690CFC0.1900D24@home.com> <yV5k2.2857$0t.1812763@news4.mia>`

```
While the digit by digit method works quite well, the way
that I have done it in the past is a small variation.  You take
the large number, and redefine it as an array of large integers
that can be handled.  One then does exactly the same thing
(without the tables) as one would do with single digits,
adding the carry from the low order to the top.  I have no
idea how the two would compare in speed.  The time that I
did it I only had a couple of numbers to deal with, and time
was not critical enough to experiment.

Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>Howard Brazee wrote:
>>
…[65 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Basic as a business language

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76ivmm$q3m$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<76g10o$1e5$1@news.igs.net> <368bddea.46528011@netnews> <76grrn$j59$1@news.igs.net> <76h6g4$ngc$1@server.cntfl.com> <76hd5e$sjm$1@news.igs.net> <76hngb$5oi$1@server.cntfl.com> <XB3j2.1546$Zk.562050@news3.mia>`

```

Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>Rick Smith wrote:
>>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>--
I was not aware of its existence!

This allows for any representation in the form:

        PIC S9(m)V9(n) BINARY; where m + n < 29.

It still has all the problems of floating point; since one would
necessarily need to force rounding or truncation to ensure that
values extending past 'n' always contain zeros.

Usable, but still clumsy when compared with COBOL!

I get to learn something new every day.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Basic as a business language

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Uz3j2.1545$Zk.561532@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<76g10o$1e5$1@news.igs.net> <368bddea.46528011@netnews> <76grrn$j59$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
>The real problem with basic is that it converts to floating
>point for all decimals.  As soon as you get away from integers
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>times as verbose as Cobol.

The same holds equally true for C and most other languages which
do not support scaled decimal math.  In my opinion, anybody who
chooses to write money handling programs in C/C++, Java, etc.,
in situations when COBOL could be used, has a screw loose.  Yes,
you could write classes in C++ and Java to handle it after a
fashion, but why should you?  Note that VB and PDS 7.1 have a
CURRENCY data type which is equivalent to PIC S9(14)V9(4).  VB
has 'Decimal' a 96 bit decimal floating point variant data type.

>This one always astonishes beginners:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>40 print x


Even simpler, try this

10 FOR I=0 TO 1 STEP .1
20   PRINT I,
30 NEXT

It makes one shudder to think that people actually use this stuff
to handle money.  Ugh!
```

#### ↳ Re: Basic as a business language

- **From:** "Kevin G. Rhoads" <T_Rhoads@NoSpam.CLASSIC.MSN.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-01-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be34f9$d5b89f20$LocalHost@stupidwin95>`
- **References:** `<76g10o$1e5$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Curious, I tried it both as written, and with an auxilliry variable
15 J = (1/I)*(I*I)
with QuickBasic 4.5 -- when compiled to an EXE either
from the IDE or from the command line it runs fine.
(i.e., nothing printed.)  This was true for both 1 .. 100
and 1 .. 10000 ranges.  The (uncompressed) EXE is under
30k if anyone wants it by e-mail.

When executed interactively, it runs fine with the auxilliary
variable (again, nothing printed) .

When executed interactively W/O the auxilliary variable
results varied -- sometimes printing EVERY line with
two identical numbers but more often printing nothing.

But it never printed just four numbers.   

I thought QBasic was just a stripped down version of
QuickBasic, but it looks like the differences may be
more fundamental.  

If I get a chance I'll see if I can translate this into VB4
for windows (16 and 32 bits) and see how it does.
```

<a id="t-fb7fabdaeb"></a>
## Thread: No accented characters in COBOL? (Newbie Q) — 3 this month / 8 total

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

_Thread started [1998-12](1998-12.md#t-fb7fabdaeb)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Yes, they are there, but (Re: No accented characters in COBOL?)

- **From:** cpcohen@inforamp.net (Charles P. Cohen)
- **Date:** 1999-01-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<368e457a.4550052@news.istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<76d5vj$irf$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <76gcki$1cg$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <76hbo1$r1g$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:

>If you do not mind scrambling the index key, you can cause the
>records to be arranged in any order you choose.

What you suggest is a neat trick.  

But it doesn't work  well for the situation in which two different
characters (say, "a" and "accented a", or Finnish "v" and "w") have
the _same collating order_, or where a _pair_ of characters collates
the same as a single character (I believe this occurs with "i" and
"ij" in Dutch).

The "general case" needs a record key, and a sort key -- related but
different fields.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Yes, they are there, but (Re: No accented characters in COBOL?)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76on7q$t2p@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<76d5vj$irf$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <76gcki$1cg$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <76hbo1$r1g$1@server.cntfl.com> <368e457a.4550052@news.istar.ca>`

```

Charles P. Cohen wrote in message <368e457a.4550052@news.istar.ca>...
>"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>different fields.

Please do check with your specific vendor.  I know that Micro Focus has had
NLS (National Language Support) for years (not to be confused  - in this
case - with National Character Support - i.e. DBCS/MOCS).  It does include
(as I recall) support for all the "extended" Western European Character
sets.  (Again, vague memory is that it supported both indexed keys and
collating sequences - or a combination of the two.  However, this is only a
vague memory.)

Obviously, full support will come with the next Standard and any ISO-10646
supporting system.
```

```
COBOL's screen handling began as a way to talk to the console printer to
communicate with the operator and tape jockey.

I have no doubt that some of you younger folks will see the time when
business applications can be produced with user interfaces that are
customized to use pictures, music, and voice in several different
languages. Programs that do basic interviews for a variety of purposes,
and capture the spoken replies seem not that far away, if not already in
place somewhere. I don't think we have to get smarter to do this, but
learn how to use the resources we have.

Warren Simmons
```

```
popsinit@popular.com.sg
```

```
to be swapped out for the standard file handling DLL.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol I/O error ,34,01 on file

- **From:** "Robert Heady" <rheady1@flash.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ghzj2.786$fR1.945@news.flash.net>`
- **References:** `<76hs3p$eu4$1@mawar.singnet.com.sg>`

```
I suspect that maybe you are running into the ulimit.  The ulimit is defined
in the UNIX kernel and sets the maximun
file size for a user.  Type ulimit at a UNIX prompt, the number returned is
a maximum number of 512? blocks allowed.  See
your system documentation for extending the ulimit.

For indexed files the maximum file size depends on the block size used for
the file.
 (MAX_LOCK_BYTE / (BlockSize + 256) ) * BlockSize

The larger the block size, the closer the FILE_LIMIT will approach the
MAX_LOCK_BYTE.
Examples:

 BlockSize = 512
                       (2147478525 / ( 512 + 256)) * 512    =
1,431,652,350  bytes   (1.33 GB)

 BlockSize =  4096
                       (2147478525 / ( 4096 + 256)) * 4096 =   2,021,156,258
bytes  (1.88 GB)

stcheong wrote in message <76hs3p$eu4$1@mawar.singnet.com.sg>...
>I need some answer for the problems which I encountered.
>I am running a called program under unix ATT V5.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: Abend handling using Language Environment — 1 this month / 4 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

_Thread started [1998-12](1998-12.md#t-b5df8b658f)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Abend handling using Language Environment

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-01-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,comp.programming,comp.protocols.ibm
- **Message-ID:** `<368c38d1.249537471@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<76gnvd$ahp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <76gu3r$c79@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
On Thu, 31 Dec 1998 16:34:34 -0600, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>
>georgelnj@my-dejanews.com wrote in message
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>place to start.)


It's interesting.  I have programmed on mainframes, minis and PC's.
On the PC we used the lastest and greatest from Realia and MicroFocus.


But our mainframes were antique.  We used COBOL/VS.  So I got used to
how abends worked under that - under VSE.   When I changed jobs I went
straight to LE.  I am sure Dwight remembers my asking, when a job blew
up, "Where's my dump?".  Anyway, I've come around to the LE way of
doing things. 

When it is truly an application problem the abend is within the
address range of your verb list.  I have seen abends outside that
address range however - and in every case it has been a "setup"
problem.  A problem with the data definitions matching etc.  The
Reason codes are very accurate and explicit in LE.
```

```
On Fri, 1 Jan 1999 15:38:44 -0800, "Vic Spainhower"
<vicspain@sprynet.com> wrote:

>Hello,
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>Stop.


Vic, go to the compiler options and choose to make it a MAIN program.

An easy alternative is to insert a line of source at the very
beginning of the program - ALL CAPS, starting in column 8:

@OPTIONS MAIN

That will fix you up!  Fujitsu defaults each compile to sub program
status unless you set the option for the compile.
```

## Thread: Where are all the jobs? — 26 this month / 36 total

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs)_

_Thread started [1998-12](1998-12.md#t-3fecb0793a)._

#### ↳ Re: Where are all the jobs?

- **From:** "LSDemon" <no.one@fuckyouspammer.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be3658$1cb1f840$8788c998@main1>`
- **References:** `<19981229131356.24254.00003332@ng-fc2.aol.com>`

```
> I've been listening for months on end about this so called shortage of
> qualified personnel for the ever increasing number of IT jobs out there.
So how
> come after going the tech school route and busting my butt for eight
months
> learning COBOL, CICS, DB2, VBasic, etc; all I get is "we don't hire entry
> level"...Surely there must be mundane jobs in the multitude of IT shops
out
> there that don't require 2 year's experience.

The "IT shortage" is a bullshit perspective that has propagated for years.
See this link for more info: http://heather.cs.ucdavis.edu/itaa.real.html

Even after you get your first job, you still will have a mountain to climb
on your way to the title of "experienced programer". You can't just have
one language skill. most employers today want programmers with several
skills, i.e., COBOL, CICS, VB, SQL, PowerBuilder, etc. 

"And just how am I supposed to get all these skills?" you ask. That's the
IT industry for you. The easy job and easy-to-get-job exists in the minds
of news reporters.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Where are all the jobs?

- **From:** "Henrik Paludan-Moerk" <hpmpcmag@inet.uni2.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be4276$47f27a00$cad08e81@hpm.kreativ-it.dk>`
- **References:** `<19981229131356.24254.00003332@ng-fc2.aol.com> <01be3658$1cb1f840$8788c998@main1>`

```
The IT shortage is very real, but perhaps not as you perceive it.
People *with experience* can generally get high rates at perm. positions,
or even better, 
work as consultants. at least over here in Europe.

But the bar is high for entry here. It is SKILLED GUYS that there is a lack
of. Many companies do not want to pay tuition and courses to educate new
people, only to have them stolen by competitors, the instant they are
learned to just some degree. (which will very often actually happen!)
So the market gets almost no fresh meat, and the lack of trained people
remains.
Here in denmark, my native country, they have made an estimate, that there
will be a need for more than 40.000 extra experienced people in 2002. And
this is a 5 million people country.

So you may have to go to a large company in order to get an entry position,
or even accept y2k work. But after, say, three years, and no major
blemishes, you are part of the family!

My opinion, 
Henrik PM
:)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Where are all the jobs?

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77vgjs$oim$1@clarknet.clark.net>`
- **References:** `<19981229131356.24254.00003332@ng-fc2.aol.com> <01be3658$1cb1f840$8788c998@main1> <01be4276$47f27a00$cad08e81@hpm.kreativ-it.dk>`

```
In article <01be4276$47f27a00$cad08e81@hpm.kreativ-it.dk>,
Henrik Paludan-Moerk <hpmpcmag@inet.uni2.dk> wrote:

[die kleine snippigkeit]

>But the bar is high for entry here. It is SKILLED GUYS that there is a lack
>of. Many companies do not want to pay tuition and courses to educate new
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>remains.

Hmmmmm... I am getting a beginning of an idea which I may give more flesh
to as time goes by.  I am familiar with the Argument Against Training, of
course:

To train a person both costs money and increases that person's value in
the jobmarket; once another company recognises this increase in value and
offers a larger salary the cost of the training is lost.  As a result of
this no training can be done.

Oddly enough this argument ignores a fact with which every businessperson
(and most property-owners) are familiar:  when the value of an asset is
increased there are usually associated increases in the costs of
maintenance and insurance.  What appears to be desired in the case of
training is an increase in the value of an asset (a better-trained
employee) *without* an increase in maintenance-costs (higher salary) or
'insurance against theft' (higher salary).

DD
```

```
One could think of several scenarios to abuse this idea.  Someone fired for "just"
cause like your example would still owe his employer for the training.  OTOH, the
employee should be protected if the company says, "We don't need our new (whatever)
guru with our new structure, let's try to get our money back for the training while
we let him go."

Does anyone have an example of a workable (min number of court appearances) contract
of this type?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Where are all the jobs?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-01-18T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<7803n2$o3o$1@clarknet.clark.net>`

```
In article <36A382B9.9FB5B412@ASPMnetdoor.com>,
Warren Porter  <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com> wrote:
>One could think of several scenarios to abuse this idea.  Someone fired for "just"
>cause like your example would still owe his employer for the training.

'Just cause'?  Pfoo, I say; respectful disagreement does not constitute
this and Exercise of Religious Freedom most *definitely* does not
constitute this.

Of course, my examples *were* a bit... 'rough-hewn' and might stand a bit
of refining.

> OTOH, the
>employee should be protected if the company says, "We don't need our new (whatever)
>guru with our new structure, let's try to get our money back for the training while
>we let him go."

Ahhhh, there's the rub... few organisations would attempt so bald-faced a
manuever, I believe, and thus the fabric of 'just cause' must be woven
*very* precisely.

Apropos of this I recall the case of a back-office worker at BankAmerica a
few years back... the lad received 'Outstandings' on his job-evals but was
suddenly fired for 'poor performance' when his superiors found out that he
was picking up a few bucks on the side by 'exotic dancing' in a gay men's
bar.

He sued, BankAmerica countered with 'poor performance', he presented his
copie of the evals... and then the Bank switched tactics, claiming that
this lad's practises, even on his own time, reflected poorly on
BankAmerica's Corporate Image, honed, as it were, by years of forclosing
on the mortgages of orphanges.

The judge said 'Hmmmm... you've got, what, 32,000 employees and you
contend that the legal, off-work-hour behaviors of a single back-office
clerk damages your Corporate Image?  Got any proof of that?'

The lad is rather wealthy now... the moral, in so 'immoral' a situation,
is that 'firing for cause' has become a bit of a minefield these days.

>
>Does anyone have an example of a workable (min number of court appearances) contract
>of this type?

What, and sully all our fine speculations with tawdry *facts*?

DD

>
>docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
In article <36A3AFEB.A7D91C72@ASPMnetdoor.com>,
Warren Porter  <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com> wrote:
>docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
>
�>> In article <36A382B9.9FB5B412@ASPMnetdoor.com>,
>> Warren Porter  <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com> wrote:
>> >One could think of several scenarios to abuse this idea.  Someone fired for "just"
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>I interpreted your example as someone who was TRYING to get himself fired as quickly as
��>possible to accept a new job elsewhere, but, like you say, the example
is "rough hewn".

How utterly coarse, low-born and devious of you... have you thought of a
career in politics?

In case I wax *too* obscure (a rare and unusual occurrence, rest assured!)
that is *precisely* what my examples were intended to demonstrate... I am
not in favor of *either* side of the Employment Equation holding all the
cards.

From my own experience... I don't employ, I consult, and there have been
times when I've found that the contract to be... slightly *other* than was
presented.  ('Well, we know that we asked you here to do batch and we're
paying you a batch rate... but there's this CICS/DB2 system that needs
some looking-at so why don't you...')  I've told pimps that things were
not the way I'd been told they would be and that I'd be seeking a contract
elsewhere; at times the pimps, fearful of losing a few weeks' commission
R���ߛ�on my labors, have pointed out in the contract how it is detailed
that if
I leave before anyone *but* me wants me to leave that I am liable for a
sum similar to, say, Indonesia's National Debt.

When all attempts to settle the matter in a reasonable, businesslike
manner have failed I will then suggest, *ever*-so-sweetly, that a Mutually
Agreeable Solution just *might* be found somewhere... and if it cannot
then on the next day, at approximately (n) o'clock, my pimp will get a
call from the client *furiously* demanding that I be removed, forthwith,
and that this pimp and his agency will, in all likelihood, *never* place
another consultant at this client again.

'Whadday ya gonna do, make 'em *fire* ya?'

'No, Mr Jones, I am not going to make them fire me; I have *never* been
Dismissed for Cause.  I am going to make them *want* to fire me... being
Dismissed By Discretion is *very* different than being Dismissed for
Cause.  I'd say we both believe that such an activity is within my
capabilities.'

>A
>happy medium (or unhappy medium, havn't met her yet <g>) might be where the new guru is
>dissattisfied working in a new section or doing the grunt work needed to apply his new
>skills.
>A new area to explore?!?

I would say it is an old, well-explored area... unless it is truly a Life
Or Death situation then *everyone* should be willing to give a little and
take a little.  Sure, training is good... but not only is a trained person
worth more money but a person who can be trained is more valuable than one
who cannot, as well.  A possible solution would be that after the
successful completion of the course there's an immediate, but small,
increase in salary with a *written* guarantee that after (m) months with
the company salary will rise to (low end of advertised market for said
skills).

DD
```

```
Makes sense.  An attorney I had an offhand conversation with last night (we are singing a

I think he implied a person who left to take a job outside of the area would be off the
hook, as would someone who left the profession.  My guess is these have been written enough
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Where are all the jobs?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<782fbs$e2q$1@callisto.clark.net>`

```
In article <36A4BC3E.75C29A5A@ASPMnetdoor.com>,
Warren Porter  <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com> wrote:
>docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Makes sense.

Well, if it makes sense to the coders then Management, in my experience,
will *never* stand for it... it was a Pretty Thought, though.

>An attorney I had an offhand conversation with last night (we are singing a
>Wagner concert this weekend FWIW) suggested a "non compete" (?!?) clause in a contract.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>on a leaving employee are too broad have been shot down by courts.

Well, there's non-compete and there's non-employ... 'Sorry, you cannot use
your brain in any way, shape or form in order to earn a living if you
leave this Fine Company before we get a chance to boot you'; I think this
falls under the 'too broad' rubric you mention.

I remember hearing of a 'change in atmosphere' in a very paternalistic
organisation that I once had a contract with; the company went from a
'womb to tomb, once you're with X Co you're here 'till you die' mentality
to a 'we have no responsibility towards you, you're responsible for your
advancement and maintaining your employability' ethos.  This resulted in a
lot of 'deadwood' just sorta hanging around ('Whaddaya mean, learn
something new?  I been doin' it this way for orty-scorg years!') while the
younger, more dynamic employees educated themselves and Got Out...

... the latter, of course, were derided by Management for their lack of
loyalty.

What does this Mean?  I dunno... but I'm glad I went the Consulting Route
long ago; work an hour, pay an hour, that's all I ask.

DD
```
