<a id="t-ac6ce0be7b"></a>
## Thread: Fun with CA-Realia COBOL: Recursive PERFORMs — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fun with CA-Realia COBOL: Recursive PERFORMs

- **From:** Jeffrey Friedman <jfriedm@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369031D0.718CA028@ibm.net>`

```
CA-Realia has a compile directive which instructs the compiler to
generate CALL and RET instructions for PERFORM and perform EXIT (the
CALL directive).  While this could lead to non-standard (and quite
non-portable) code if abused, it is a perfectly acceptable
implementation for compliant code which also happens to obey the
constraint that a perform exit point is always an exit point (PERFORM A
THRU B and PERFORM A THRU C would not work with this directive in place,
as there would be a RET instruction generated at the end of paragraph
B). What I demonstrate here is a side benefit (or detriment depending on
your way of thought) in that programs "get away" with recursive PERFORMs
(as they nicely stack their exit points). Here is an example I had
written a while back to demonstrate this, and solve the Knight's Tour
problem.  Note that this program uses the SMALLCOMP directive (which
allocates a single byte to a binary number if the precision is small
enough), and it uses the ANSI escape sequences to interact with the
screen (ANSI.SYS should be loaded in CONFIG.SYS), and is obviously not
an GUI program so run it at a DOS prompt. It should also be cut and
pasted to a file with a .CBL extension as columns 1-6 are not present
(or set COBEXT to that extension with the /r option).  Have Fun!

Jeff Friedman, Developer
CA-Realia COBOL

*$CALL,SMALLCOMP
 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID. KTOUR.
*
*    This program does an entire knight's tour on the screen
*    (if you have the time to watch).
*
 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
 SOURCE-COMPUTER. IBM-PC.
 OBJECT-COMPUTER. IBM-PC.
 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
 FILE-CONTROL.
 DATA DIVISION.
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01  MISC-STACK-AREAS.
     05 DEPTH                 VALUE 1          PIC S9(2) COMP-5.
     05 DEPTH-FORMATTED-X.
         10 DEPTH-FORMATTED                    PIC 99.
     05 NEW-POSITION                           PIC S9(2) COMP-5.
     05 CURRENT-POSITION      VALUE 22         PIC S9(2) COMP-5.
     05 CURRENT-POSITION-STACK OCCURS 65       PIC S9(2) COMP-5.
*
*    Board is 12 wide by 10 high for convienience
*
 01 BOARD-AREA.
     05  BOARD OCCURS 120 TIMES INDEXED BY BX PIC S9(2) COMP-5.
 01 FILLER REDEFINES BOARD-AREA.
     05  FILLER PIC X(21).
     05  BOARD-LINES-GRP OCCURS 8 TIMES.
         10  BOARD-LINE PIC X(8).
         10  FILLER     PIC XX.
*
 01 MISC-AREAS.
     05  MOVE-NO  OCCURS 65 TIMES          PIC S9(2) COMP-5.
 01  OPEN-SQ-AREAS.
     05  OPEN-SQ-PTR                       PIC S9(4) COMP-5.
     05  FILLER REDEFINES OPEN-SQ-PTR.
         10  OPEN-SQ-PTR-LSB               PIC S9(2) COMP-5.
         10  OPEN-SQ-PTR-MSB               PIC S9(2) COMP-5.
     05  SAVE-SQUARE                       PIC S9(2) COMP-5.
     05  OPEN-SQUARE      OCCURS 64 TIMES  INDEXED BY OIX
                                           PIC S9(2) COMP-5.
*
 01 MOVE-TYPE-DEFINITIONS.
     05  FILLER VALUE +08                  PIC S9(2) COMP-5.
     05  FILLER VALUE -21                  PIC S9(2) COMP-5.
     05  FILLER VALUE +21                  PIC S9(2) COMP-5.
     05  FILLER VALUE +12                  PIC S9(2) COMP-5.
     05  FILLER VALUE -19                  PIC S9(2) COMP-5.
     05  FILLER VALUE +19                  PIC S9(2) COMP-5.
     05  FILLER VALUE -12                  PIC S9(2) COMP-5.
     05  FILLER VALUE -08                  PIC S9(2) COMP-5.
 01  FILLER REDEFINES MOVE-TYPE-DEFINITIONS.
     05  MOVE-OFFSET OCCURS 8 TIMES INDEXED BY MOVE-TYPE
                                           PIC S9(2) COMP-5.
*
 01  POSSIBLE-FLAG          VALUE 'y'      PIC X.
     88  WIN-POSSIBLE   VALUE 'y'.
     88  WIN-IMPOSSIBLE VALUE 't'.
*
 01  WS-ROW                                PIC S9(2) COMP-5.
 01  WS-COLUMN                             PIC S9(2) COMP-5.
 01  LAST-RECURSE-DEPTH VALUE 65           PIC S9(2) COMP-5.
 01  COUNT-REACHABLE-FROM-1                PIC S9(2) COMP-5.
 01  REACHABLE-FROM-COUNT                  PIC S9(2) COMP-5.
 01  WIN-COUNT                             PIC S9(9) COMP-5.
 01  CTRL-BRK-FLAG VALUE LOW-VALUES        PIC X.
 01  WS-START-TIME.
     05  ST-HH                             PIC 99.
     05  ST-MM                             PIC 99.
     05  ST-SS                             PIC 99V99.
 01  WS-CURRENT-TIME.
     05  CT-HH                             PIC 99.
     05  CT-MM                             PIC 99.
     05  CT-SS                             PIC 99V99.
 01  START-DELAY-TIME.
     05  SD-HH                             PIC 99.
     05  SD-MM                             PIC 99.
     05  SD-SS                             PIC 99V99.
 01  END-DELAY-TIME.
     05  ED-HH                             PIC 99.
     05  ED-MM                             PIC 99.
     05  ED-SS                             PIC 99V99.
 01  TIME-PER-SOLUTION                     PIC 999.9(5).
 01  DELAY-TIME                            PIC S9(4)V99 COMP-5.
*
 01  WS-POSITION-CURSOR.
     05  FILLER                            PIC X VALUE X'1B'.
     05  FILLER                            PIC X VALUE '['.
     05  WS-POS-ROW                        PIC 99.
     05  FILLER                            PIC X VALUE ';'.
     05  WS-POS-COL                        PIC 99.
     05  FILLER                            PIC X VALUE 'f'.
*
 01  WS-BOARD.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE X'1B'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(3) VALUE '[2J'.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE X'1B'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(5) VALUE '[3;10'.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE 'f'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(41) VALUE ALL '-'.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE X'1B'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(5) VALUE '[4;10'.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE 'f'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(41) VALUE ALL 'I    '.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE X'1B'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(5) VALUE '[5;10'.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE 'f'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(41) VALUE ALL '-'.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE X'1B'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(5) VALUE '[6;10'.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE 'f'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(41) VALUE ALL 'I    '.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE X'1B'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(5) VALUE '[7;10'.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE 'f'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(41) VALUE ALL '-'.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE X'1B'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(5) VALUE '[8;10'.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE 'f'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(41) VALUE ALL 'I    '.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE X'1B'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(5) VALUE '[9;10'.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE 'f'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(41) VALUE ALL '-'.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE X'1B'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(6) VALUE '[10;10'.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE 'f'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(41) VALUE ALL 'I    '.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE X'1B'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(6) VALUE '[11;10'.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE 'f'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(41) VALUE ALL '-'.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE X'1B'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(6) VALUE '[12;10'.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE 'f'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(41) VALUE ALL 'I    '.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE X'1B'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(6) VALUE '[13;10'.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE 'f'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(41) VALUE ALL '-'.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE X'1B'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(6) VALUE '[14;10'.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE 'f'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(41) VALUE ALL 'I    '.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE X'1B'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(6) VALUE '[15;10'.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE 'f'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(41) VALUE ALL '-'.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE X'1B'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(6) VALUE '[16;10'.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE 'f'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(41) VALUE ALL 'I    '.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE X'1B'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(6) VALUE '[17;10'.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE 'f'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(41) VALUE ALL '-'.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE X'1B'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(6) VALUE '[18;10'.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE 'f'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(41) VALUE ALL 'I    '.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE X'1B'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(6) VALUE '[19;10'.
     05  FILLER PIC X VALUE 'f'.
     05  FILLER PIC X(41) VALUE ALL '-'.
*
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
 B000-INITIALIZATION.
*
*    The basic loop is display a move, try another, backup
*    when completed or stuck.
*    First display the board outline and initialize areas.
*    Note that the board is 8X8 but inside of a 12X10 grid.
*    This way no need to test if a move takes knight off the
*    board: just mark these positions as already used. First
*    mark top and bottom as in-use (non-zero), then mark sides.
*
     PERFORM VARYING BX FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL BX IS GREATER THAN 21
         MOVE 65 TO BOARD (BX)
     END-PERFORM.
     PERFORM VARYING BX FROM 120 BY -1 UNTIL BX IS LESS THAN 100
         MOVE 65 TO BOARD (BX)
     END-PERFORM.
     MOVE 65 TO BOARD (20) BOARD (21)
                BOARD (30) BOARD (31)
                BOARD (40) BOARD (41)
                BOARD (50) BOARD (51)
                BOARD (60) BOARD (61)
                BOARD (70) BOARD (71)
                BOARD (80) BOARD (81)
                BOARD (90) BOARD (91).
*
*    Maintain a table of open squares - points into BOARD
*
     MOVE 22 TO OPEN-SQ-PTR.
     SET OIX TO 1.
     PERFORM 8 TIMES
         PERFORM 8 TIMES
             MOVE OPEN-SQ-PTR-LSB TO OPEN-SQUARE (OIX)
             ADD 1 TO OPEN-SQ-PTR
             SET OIX UP BY 1
         END-PERFORM
         ADD 2 TO OPEN-SQ-PTR
     END-PERFORM.
     MOVE 2 TO OPEN-SQ-PTR.
*
*    Display the empty board
*
     DISPLAY WS-BOARD.
*
*    Make first move to upper left hand corner, display the
*    move, and start recursing.
*
     MOVE CURRENT-POSITION TO CURRENT-POSITION-STACK(1)
                              NEW-POSITION.
     MOVE 1 TO BOARD(CURRENT-POSITION) DEPTH-FORMATTED.
     PERFORM B200-DISPLAY-MOVE.
*
 B100-MAKE-MOVE.
*
*    See if the board configuration is already unwinnable.
*    If so, just back up and try other moves, else try all
*    possible moves from this arrangement.
*
     PERFORM B300-CHECK-FOR-IMPOSSIBLE THRU B300-EXIT.
     IF WIN-POSSIBLE
       PERFORM VARYING MOVE-NO(DEPTH) FROM 1 BY 1
               UNTIL MOVE-NO(DEPTH) IS GREATER THAN 8
         SET MOVE-TYPE TO MOVE-NO(DEPTH)
         ADD CURRENT-POSITION
             MOVE-OFFSET (MOVE-TYPE)
             GIVING NEW-POSITION
         IF BOARD (NEW-POSITION) IS EQUAL TO ZERO
             ADD 1 TO DEPTH
             MOVE DEPTH TO BOARD (NEW-POSITION)
                           DEPTH-FORMATTED
             PERFORM B200-DISPLAY-MOVE
*
*            If depth is 64, board filled, backup and keep
*            searching for other solutions
*
             IF DEPTH IS EQUAL TO 64
                 PERFORM B400-RECORD-WIN
                 MOVE ZERO TO BOARD(NEW-POSITION)
                 MOVE SPACE TO DEPTH-FORMATTED-X
                 PERFORM B200-DISPLAY-MOVE
                 SUBTRACT 1 FROM DEPTH
             ELSE
*
*                Need to try subsequent moves from here
*                Mark square no longer open, and recurse
*                Note: recursive PERFORM requires CALL
*                directive to work properly.
*
                 PERFORM B300-OPEN-SQ-MAINT
                 MOVE NEW-POSITION TO CURRENT-POSITION
                                     CURRENT-POSITION-STACK(DEPTH)
                 PERFORM B100-MAKE-MOVE
             END-IF
         END-IF
       END-PERFORM.
*
*    Have tried all possible moves or current configuration
*    is unwinnable. Back up a move and try other moves.
*
     MOVE CURRENT-POSITION-STACK (DEPTH) TO NEW-POSITION.
     MOVE ZERO TO BOARD(NEW-POSITION).
     MOVE SPACE TO DEPTH-FORMATTED-X.
     PERFORM B200-DISPLAY-MOVE
     SUBTRACT 1 FROM DEPTH
                     OPEN-SQ-PTR.
     MOVE CURRENT-POSITION-STACK (DEPTH) TO CURRENT-POSITION.
*
 B100-END.  EXIT.
*
*    It will take quite a while, but when entire tree is traversed
*    - fall through and stop.
*
     STOP RUN.
*
*    Come here to display current move (or clean last move)
*
 B200-DISPLAY-MOVE.
*
     DIVIDE NEW-POSITION BY 10
         GIVING WS-ROW REMAINDER WS-COLUMN.
     MULTIPLY WS-ROW BY 2 GIVING WS-POS-ROW.
     MULTIPLY WS-COLUMN BY 5 GIVING WS-POS-COL
     ADD 2 TO WS-POS-COL.
     DISPLAY WS-POSITION-CURSOR DEPTH-FORMATTED-X
         WITH NO ADVANCING.
*
*    If any open square cannot be reached, impossible to win
*    Also, if more than 1 square which can only be reached
*    from one other square, then cannot win. A square cannot
*    be reached if all square around it are used (and none
*    are the current position); same for other optimization.
*
 B300-CHECK-FOR-IMPOSSIBLE.
*
     SET WIN-POSSIBLE TO TRUE.
*
*    If almost done, just try combinations - faster than
*    checking if impossible win. 57 is a guess at probable
*    break-even point.
*
     IF DEPTH IS GREATER THAN 57
         GO TO B300-EXIT.
*
*    Temporarily zero the current square so looks reachable
*    and zero count of squares reachable from only one other
*    square.
*
     MOVE ZERO TO BOARD (CURRENT-POSITION)
                  COUNT-REACHABLE-FROM-1.
*
*    Only test squares listed as open in the OPEN-SQUARE table
*
     PERFORM VARYING OIX FROM OPEN-SQ-PTR BY 1
             UNTIL OIX IS GREATER THAN 64
         SET BX TO OPEN-SQUARE (OIX)
         MOVE ZERO TO REACHABLE-FROM-COUNT
*
*        Count number of square this square is
*        is reachable from.
*
         IF BOARD (BX - 12) EQUAL ZERO
             ADD 1 TO REACHABLE-FROM-COUNT
         END-IF
         IF BOARD (BX - 08) EQUAL ZERO
             ADD 1 TO REACHABLE-FROM-COUNT
         END-IF
         IF BOARD (BX + 08) EQUAL ZERO
             ADD 1 TO REACHABLE-FROM-COUNT
         END-IF
         IF BOARD (BX + 12) EQUAL ZERO
             ADD 1 TO REACHABLE-FROM-COUNT
         END-IF
         IF BOARD (BX - 19) EQUAL ZERO
             ADD 1 TO REACHABLE-FROM-COUNT
         END-IF
         IF BOARD (BX - 21) EQUAL ZERO
             ADD 1 TO REACHABLE-FROM-COUNT
         END-IF
         IF BOARD (BX + 21) EQUAL ZERO
             ADD 1 TO REACHABLE-FROM-COUNT
         END-IF
         IF BOARD (BX + 19) EQUAL ZERO
             ADD 1 TO REACHABLE-FROM-COUNT
         END-IF
*
         IF REACHABLE-FROM-COUNT IS EQUAL TO ZERO
             SET WIN-IMPOSSIBLE TO TRUE
             GO TO B300-EXIT-WITH-RESTORE
         END-IF
*
*        If this square is reachable from only 1 square
*        and already found another square only reachable
*        from one square, win is impossible.
*        Cannot do test when depth is greater than 62 as
*        there WILL only be if winning position.
*
         IF REACHABLE-FROM-COUNT IS EQUAL TO 1
             IF COUNT-REACHABLE-FROM-1 IS EQUAL TO 1
                 SET WIN-IMPOSSIBLE TO TRUE
                 GO TO B300-EXIT-WITH-RESTORE
             ELSE
                 MOVE 1 TO COUNT-REACHABLE-FROM-1
             END-IF
         END-IF
     END-PERFORM.
*
*    Must restore depth to current position before leaving.
*
 B300-EXIT-WITH-RESTORE.
*
     MOVE DEPTH TO BOARD (CURRENT-POSITION).
*
 B300-EXIT.  EXIT.
*
*    Come here to maintain list of open squares.
*
 B300-OPEN-SQ-MAINT.
*
     SET OIX TO OPEN-SQ-PTR.
     MOVE OPEN-SQUARE (OIX) TO SAVE-SQUARE.
     SEARCH OPEN-SQUARE VARYING OIX
         AT END
             DISPLAY 'Impossible NF during open sq maint'
             STOP RUN
         WHEN OPEN-SQUARE (OIX) IS EQUAL TO NEW-POSITION
             MOVE NEW-POSITION TO OPEN-SQUARE (OPEN-SQ-PTR)
             MOVE SAVE-SQUARE TO OPEN-SQUARE (OIX)
             ADD 1 TO OPEN-SQ-PTR
     END-SEARCH.
*
*    Come here to display the number of solutions found
*    so far.
*
 B400-RECORD-WIN.
*
     ADD 1 TO WIN-COUNT.
     DISPLAY X'1B' '[24;5f' 'Solutions:' WIN-COUNT
         WITH NO ADVANCING.
```

<a id="t-aae1b129a6"></a>
## Thread: indicator field — 4 msgs

### indicator field

- **From:** carlwenrich@mindspring.com (Carl Wenrich)
- **Date:** 1999-01-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<368ed8d7.1066507@news.mindspring.com>`

```
Can anyone tell me what the legal entries for the indicator field are,
and what they mean?
```

#### ↳ Re: indicator field

- **From:** bbello5778@aol.com (BBello5778)
- **Date:** 1999-01-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990102214825.21429.00006715@ng-fp1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<368ed8d7.1066507@news.mindspring.com>`

```
If you are refering to the indicator field used when accesing DB2. It is used
with fields that may have null values. It is used to tell DB2 if a column being
inserted/updated  is null or not. If you do a select DB2 will update the
indicator field appropriately. I believe a -1 means it's null and 1 means it's
not null.

Someone in the NG can probably explain better.


Good luck

Bosun

BBello5778@aol.com 
http://members.aol.com/bbello5778/bosun.htm
Programmer/Analyst. Bloomington, IL
```

#### ↳ Re: indicator field

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-01-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<368f0308.45794817@netnews>`
- **References:** `<368ed8d7.1066507@news.mindspring.com>`

```
'Twas Sun, 03 Jan 1999 02:42:15 GMT, when carlwenrich@mindspring.com (Carl
Wenrich) illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>Can anyone tell me what the legal entries for the indicator field are,
>and what they mean?

Gladly:

-    Continuation.  The last token on the previous line continues onto
     this line
D    Debugging line.  This line is a comment unless USE FOR DEBUGGING
     was specified.
*    Comment line.
/    Comment line, print this line at the top of a new page.

These are the only ones defined in the standard.  Unisys uses $ in the
indicator position for compiler directives.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: indicator field

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-03T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<76nvf5$ske$1@news.igs.net>`

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 DATE PROBLEM AT LEVEL II COBOL

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3693125D.15D1A196@att.net>`
- **References:** `<76qq45$b5v$1@aristotelis.cytanet.com.cy> <76r9qe$4s5@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com> <76toc4$dbh$1@aristotelis.cytanet.com.cy>`

```
Kyriacos Christou wrote:
> 
> 01 WDATE.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> It gives me  0?/01/05

If the date being returned is edited, you need the following:

 01 WDATE.
         02 YY   PIC 99.
	 02 FILLER  PIC  X.
         02 MM PIC 99.
	 02 FILLER  PIC  X.
         02 DD PIC 99.

	(have to allow for the "/"s).

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 DATE PROBLEM AT LEVEL II COBOL

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>

```
If the date is being returned edited, the implementation would
not be ANSI 74 compliant. Since the implementation is
compliant, it is not being returned as edited! Instead, Chris
is inserting the separators to clarify his comments.
```

```
>Kyriacos Christou wrote:
>> 
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I think the poster added the /'s. Accept from date returns a NUMERIC.


I think this would help the poster out:

01  Date-Fields.
     03  WDATE-N Pic 9(6).
     03  WDATE Redefines WDATE-N.
           05  YY Pic 99.
           05  MM Pic 99.
           05  DD Pic 99.

Accept WDATE-N from Date.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 DATE PROBLEM AT LEVEL II COBOL

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36A735AF.9D42A6E9@home.com>`
- **References:** `<76qq45$b5v$1@aristotelis.cytanet.com.cy> <76r9qe$4s5@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com> <76toc4$dbh$1@aristotelis.cytanet.com.cy> <3693125D.15D1A196@att.net> <36a69d3d.169189455@news1.ibm.net>`

```
> I think this would help the poster out:
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Accept WDATE-N from Date.

Except I would never, never, never accept a date with a two digit year. 
Some Cobols (VAX) have always accepted a 4 digit year.  Others (IBM)
have only recently added DATEX and functions to get 4 digit years.  Only
if by "level II COBOL you mean IBM COBOL II, you are stuck with
dangerous code.  

ASAP get in the habit of having all dates, with the possible exception
of output reports and screens be in the format of CCYYMMDD.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 DATE PROBLEM AT LEVEL II COBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-01-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36a7a798.237385945@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<76qq45$b5v$1@aristotelis.cytanet.com.cy> <76r9qe$4s5@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com> <76toc4$dbh$1@aristotelis.cytanet.com.cy> <3693125D.15D1A196@att.net> <36a69d3d.169189455@news1.ibm.net> <36A735AF.9D42A6E9@home.com>`

```
On Thu, 21 Jan 1999 07:11:59 -0700, Howard Brazee
<NOSPAMbrazee@home.com> wrote:

>> I think this would help the poster out:
>> 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>of output reports and screens be in the format of CCYYMMDD.

But that wasn't the question.  FYI VAX/VMS COBOL has a call to get the
full year, but ACCEPT FROM DATE still returns the 2 digit year.

Also - since it became available I use FUNCTION CURRENT DATE for
processing - but I have used ACCEPT FROM DAY quite recently to get a
Julian day for a timestamp (year not required).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 DATE PROBLEM AT LEVEL II COBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1999-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36add6ed.10081997@news.enterprise.net>`
- **References:** `<76qq45$b5v$1@aristotelis.cytanet.com.cy> <76r9qe$4s5@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com> <76toc4$dbh$1@aristotelis.cytanet.com.cy> <3693125D.15D1A196@att.net> <36a69d3d.169189455@news1.ibm.net> <36A735AF.9D42A6E9@home.com>`

```
On Thu, 21 Jan 1999 07:11:59 -0700, Howard Brazee
<NOSPAMbrazee@home.com> wrote:

>  Only
>if by "level II COBOL you mean IBM COBOL II, you are stuck with
>dangerous code.  

Nope. He meant 'Level II COBOL' which was a product from Micro Focus
up until about 1987. The problem seems to occur only in v2.1 so far
and not in v2.5 which is probably more common.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 DATE PROBLEM AT LEVEL II COBOL

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76viuj$8e9$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<76qq45$b5v$1@aristotelis.cytanet.com.cy> <76r9qe$4s5@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com> <76toc4$dbh$1@aristotelis.cytanet.com.cy>`

```
[Posted and e-mailed]

If you are using LEVEL II COBOL V2.6 for PCDOS or LEVEL II
COBOL V2.5 for MSDOS, it should be possible to CALL the OS
to get the date. Where you use ACCEPT FROM DATE, CALL
the following program. (This program was tested using
Professional Cobol V1.1, which is a LEVEL II implementation).

For example, CALL "OS-DATE" USING WDATE.

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. OS-DATE.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  DOS-INT  PIC X VALUE X"21".
       01  DOS-GET-DATE PIC 99 COMP VALUE 42.
       01  DOS-FLAGS PIC X VALUE X"FF".
       01  DOS-REGS.
           05  AX PIC S9(4) COMP.
           05  FILLER REDEFINES AX.
               10  AH PIC 99 COMP.
               10  AL PIC 99 COMP.
           05  BX PIC S9(4) COMP.
           05  FILLER REDEFINES BX.
               10  BH PIC 99 COMP.
               10  BL PIC 99 COMP.
           05  CX PIC S9(4) COMP.
           05  FILLER REDEFINES CX.
               10  CH PIC 99 COMP.
               10  CL PIC 99 COMP.
           05  DX PIC S9(4) COMP.
           05  FILLER REDEFINES DX.
               10  DH PIC 99 COMP.
               10  DL PIC 99 COMP.
       01 DATE-WORK PIC S9(4) COMP.
       LINKAGE SECTION.
       01  CURRENT-DATE.
           05 YY PIC 99.
           05 MM PIC 99.
           05 DD PIC 99.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION USING CURRENT-DATE.
           MOVE DOS-GET-DATE TO AH.
           CALL X"84" USING DOS-INT, DOS-FLAGS, AX, BX, CX, DX.
           MOVE DH TO MM.
           MOVE DL TO DD.
           DIVIDE CX BY 100 GIVING DATE-WORK REMAINDER YY.
           EXIT PROGRAM.
           STOP RUN.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >

Kyriacos Christou wrote in message
<76toc4$dbh$1@aristotelis.cytanet.com.cy>...
>01 WDATE.
>        02 YY   PIC 99.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 DATE PROBLEM AT LEVEL II COBOL

- **From:** "Sven Brueggemann" <Sven.Brueggemann@brem.ftn.de>
- **Date:** 1999-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<000c8d24@ftn.de>`
- **References:** `<76toc4$dbh$1@aristotelis.cytanet.com.cy>`

```

KC> 01 WDATE.
KC>         02 YY   PIC 99.
KC>         02 MM PIC 99.
KC>         02 DD PIC 99.
KC> ...
KC> ACCEPT  WDATE FORM DATE.
KC> It gives me  0?/01/05

WDATE is not numeric. Try

01 WDATE                PIC 9(06).
01 WDATE-R REDEFINES WDATE.
   02 YY PIC 99.
   02 MM PIC 99.
   02 DD PIC 99.

Best regards

To receive my PGP public key, send an eMail with subject "SEND PGPKEY"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 DATE PROBLEM AT LEVEL II COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>

```
>
>KC> 01 WDATE.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>To receive my PGP public key, send an eMail with subject "SEND PGPKEY"

Although the DATE "theoretical field" is numeric.  There is not now (or as
long as I remember) ANY problem "sending" a valid integer field to an
alphanumeric field.  If it works with a numeric item as receiving from
ACCEPT FROM DATE, it *should* work for an alphanumeric (or group) item.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 DATE PROBLEM AT LEVEL II COBOL v2.1

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpdi.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<915815044.24617.0.nnrp-04.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<76toc4$dbh$1@aristotelis.cytanet.com.cy> <000c8d24@ftn.de> <7744s3$1tk@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <774tor$hu9$1@aristotelis.cytanet.com.cy>`

```
I can't remember who started this thread but I think you originally referred
to changing an old program/system.  Do the old programs get the correct
date?  If you recompile the live source does it process correctly?

If the first is correct then there is nothing wrong with your system.  If
the second is correct then there is nothing wrong with your
compiler/compiler options.  Then you have to look at your code.

If the recompiled programs don't work but the original ones do then
something has changed in your compiler environment I guess which has nothing
to do with the date.

<774tor$hu9$1@aristotelis.cytanet.com.cy>...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: 1999 DATE PROBLEM AT LEVEL II COBOL

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1999-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36934ed3.45113539@news.freeserve.net>`

```
On Mon, 4 Jan 1999 14:55:33 -0600, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>Micro Focus had already dropped that product when I started working for them
>over 10 years ago. 

I'm not so sure about that Bill. I know when I started 12 or so years
ago there were still a few UNIX versions of Level II COBOL. 1.1.5 ET
springs to mind. The DOS product certainly was dead then though.
V2.5.4 being the last I think. There were a few later versions in the
OEM market particularly in Japan but they were v2.5.4 with a few bug
fixes. Version numbers between DOS and UNIX were completely different
back then.

I remember this as the first product I worked on was VS COBOL
Workbench 1.3.2 on a Wang PC. I can remember that well as it was twice
as fast as my PC XT. Then I got my first PC3270 - Colour!

I have a copy of Professional COBOL v1.2 from 1985 here. I guess I
should check that out. I have a customer using software written with
that though they are way outside of any support contract now. It never
went wrong so didn't want support.
```

```
lived on in the Unix and OEM sides of Micro Focus for quite a while after
COBOL/2, Workbench, and all those "new-fangled" (a decade ago) mainframe
off-loading solutions were introduced.

I do remember that there was a pretty poster with Level II on it - in one of
the hallways at West Street in Newbury when I visited - even in the '90s.
```

```
Murray) wrote:


>I have a copy of Professional COBOL v1.2 from 1985 here. I guess I
>should check that out. I have a customer using software written with
>that though they are way outside of any support contract now. It never
>went wrong so didn't want support.

To follow myself up. I just tried this. ACCEPT ws-date FROM DATE works
fine on Professional COBOL v1.2 which has a release date of May 1st
1985.

Incidently, it doesn't run under Windows NT well, even with using
FORCEDOS. The editor pads garbage onto the end of the source out to
512 byte boundaries. I had to find a proper DOS machine.
```

#### ↳ Re: 1999 DATE PROBLEM AT LEVEL II COBOL

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1999-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36912c1a.39133300@news.enterprise.net>`
- **References:** `<76qq45$b5v$1@aristotelis.cytanet.com.cy>`

```
On Mon, 4 Jan 1999 18:23:40 +0200, "Kyriacos Christou"
<kyrchr@cytanet.com.cy> wrote:

>Our system who is wrote in LEVEL II COBOL in the ACCEPT  FROM Command in the
>year 1999, it gives 0? .

Hmm. Bit late now to test your code.

>
>There anybody who know how can i solve this.

Yes. upgrade to a new compiler. ;-)

Or, if you get back a 0, take it to mean 1999. You will have problems
next year of course but then you would anyway using 2 digit years. 

If the system is compliant and you get a decent result from the
operating system, maybe an OS command would help. Eg. on DOS you can
do DATE /T which will output the date so DATE /T > date.txt will
produce a text file with the OS date in it. Read that into your cobol
program as a check.

Out of interest, which version of Level II COBOL is this? It's useful
for the rest of us just in case we come across old systems. I've still
got a customer using Workbench 1.3.5 which I know is not 2000
compliant but at least is 1999 compliant. (so far and in 9/9/99)
```

#### ↳ Re: 1999 DATE PROBLEM AT LEVEL II COBOL

- **From:** cpcohen@inforamp.net (Charles P. Cohen)
- **Date:** 1999-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36901714.5565292@news.istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<76qq45$b5v$1@aristotelis.cytanet.com.cy>`

```
"Kyriacos Christou" <kyrchr@cytanet.com.cy> wrote:

>Our system who is wrote in LEVEL II COBOL in the ACCEPT  FROM Command in the
>year 1999, it gives 0? .

Try using:

	MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE TO CURRENT-DATE-AND-TIME.

The first 8 characters are:
	YYYYMMDD  (that is, 4-digit year, 2-digit month, 2-digit 
                             day within month).

If the operating system has trouble, that's a whole different problem!
```

<a id="t-5156fe24e1"></a>
## Thread: COBOL I vs. COBOL II — 17 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### COBOL I vs. COBOL II

- **From:** smiley@izap.com (smiley)
- **Date:** 1999-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36904444.19076935@news.izap.com>`

```
What's the difference between COBOL I and COBOL II?

Thanks if you respond.

Aloha Smiley
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL I vs. COBOL II

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76pt61$n5f@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36904444.19076935@news.izap.com>`

```

smiley wrote in message <36904444.19076935@news.izap.com>...
>What's the difference between COBOL I and COBOL II?
>
>Thanks if you respond.
>
>Aloha Smiley

You don't say so, but my guess is that you are talking about IBM MVS COBOL
products.  (Sorry Mark, if he really is VSE, you can modify this for your
side of the world)

COBOL I (and it is *NEVER* called that) is probably the OS/VS COBOL compiler
(supporting the ANS'68 and ANS'74 Standards - as well as many extensions).
It is "long obsolete" (but still in all too common use)

COBOL II (and it is called that, although its correct name is VS COBOL II)
from Release 3 on supported the ANS'85 Standard (and with very minor
exceptions ANS'74).  It has been removed from marketing by IBM already and
will lose support very soon after Y2K time (for which it has minimal
support).

VS COBOL II was replaced by the now dead COBOL/370 which has been replaced
by IBM COBOL for MVS & VM which was replaced by IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM
(with or with out the Millennium Language Extensions).  All of these require
LE (Language Environment) to be available as their run-time.

The farther along this chain you go, the more "modern" features you get and
the more of the old (particularly the undocumented ones) extensions you
lose.

I hope this VERY brief note answers your question.  As I wrote a book on it,
needless to say, there is MUCH more that could be said.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL I vs. COBOL II

- **From:** Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3690d737$6$tfs$mr2ice@news>`
- **References:** `<76pt61$n5f@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com> <19990104083712.07345.00003054@ng124.aol.com>`

```
In <19990104083712.07345.00003054@ng124.aol.com>, on 04 Jan 1999 at 13:37,
scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock) said:

>>COBOL I (and it is *NEVER* called that)

>Never say never, Bill. I call it that informally in lecture and it helps
>people relate the various releases at a simple level.

Same here; we commonly use the terms COBOL 1 for OS/VS and DOS/VS COBOL,
COBOL 2 for VS COBOL II, and COBOL 3 for COBOL/370 and its successors
(COBOL for MVS, OS/390, VM, VSE).  These are convenient, albeit unofficial
terms.

Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL I vs. COBOL II

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36913708$8$tfs$mr2ice@news>`
- **References:** `<76pt61$n5f@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com> <19990104083712.07345.00003054@ng124.aol.com> <3690d737$6$tfs$mr2ice@news> <76r9ve$4uv@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In <76r9ve$4uv@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>, on 04 Jan 1999 at 14:58, "William
M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> said:

>I guess that is why (in my book), I *invented* the terms OldBOL and
>NewBOL (and said they were inventions).   Usually, in more formal
>situations, I hear the term COBOL/VS used to cover both OS/VS COBOL and
>DOS/VS COBOL - after all, that is what their common LRM said.

IIRC, there are two separate Language Reference Manuals, one for "VS COBOL
for OS/VS", the other for "VS COBOL for DOS/VS"; and their titles contain
"VS COBOL for xxx/VS", not "COBOL/VS".  COBOL/VS and COBOL/LE are terms
many people use, but are not official IBM terms.

Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL I vs. COBOL II

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3690D5EC.1A1CD6F2@home.com>`
- **References:** `<36904444.19076935@news.izap.com>`

```
smiley wrote:
> 
> What's the difference between COBOL I and COBOL II?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Aloha Smiley

What is COBOL I?

COBOL had had extensions and changes over the years.  ANSI creates
standandards which are accepted by the various compiler companies - or
not, and the compiler companies often have extensions.

I have used ANSI 68 & ANSI 72 which have minor differences.  Then I
worked on a VAX which had a COBOL compiler with useful extensions.  When
I later worked on an IBM again, I found out that COBOL II was IBM's name
for the version of COBOL which I had been working on the VAX (but
interviewers still wanted to know if you had COBOL II, rather than
whatever ANSI version that was).  But COBOL II still wasn't Y2K
compliant and has been replaced by a COBOL which is (and had better
called libraries).

Here's an incomplete list of differences.
CURRENT-DATE is gone
EXAMINE is still gone
EXHIBIT is gone  (was only IBM, but I miss it)
OTHERWISE is gone
REMARKS is gone
THEN is gone
TRANSFORM is gone
WRITE AFTER POSITIONING is gone
REPORT WRITER is gone
UNSTRING DELIMITED BY ALL is gone
READY TRACE is gone  (I miss this)


Commands have scope terminators, most of which are pretty much unneeded,
but the following can be very useful:
   END-IF
   END-EVALUATIE
   END-SEARCH
   END-PERFORM
ALPHABETIC compares include lower-case
We now have in-line PERFORMS
CONTINUE is a nop within if clauses which does not go to NEXT SENTENCE.
PERFORM has an option for "WITH TEST AFTER" instead of the default
testing before
All? test options now understand NOT
EVALUATE is a new command which is very useful and widely used 
INITIALIZE initializes data to zeros or spaces (it can initialize to
values too).  It is often mis-used.
We have pointer variables.
We can find LENGTH OF FIELD-A
the SET command is greatly expanded.  New COBOL II programmers will use
it primarily to SET an 88 level to TRUE.
You can have nested COPY statements.

Usage has new reserved words, PACKED-DECIMAL, BINARY, and POINTER
COBOL II will let you move a field with a PIC 9999.99 to one PIC 9999V99
You can have nested programs
You can have double byte character strings (just when everybody else is
going to unicode!)
Source can be in mixed case.
```

```
Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL I vs. COBOL II

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<784d9f$5d1@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36904444.19076935@news.izap.com> <3690D5EC.1A1CD6F2@home.com> <36A572E6.D9F01F7A@vr-net.com>`

```
  01  MyGroup.
    05 Alpha    Pic X.
    05 Num    Pic 99.
    05  Num-Ed    Pic 9.9.
         ...

     Initialize MyGroup
             Replacing Alphanumeric by "X"
                               Numeric by 1
                               NumericEdited by 2.3

(I haven't tried this code snip, but it is the general way to initizlize to
a specific value.  The ability to initialize based on the VALUE clause in
Working-Storage will only come with the NEXT Standard.)
```

```
If there is anyone out in here who is currently using Micro Focus File Share
for Windows NT Server 4.0, and you are having problems with it please email
me this is very important.
Problems like:
1.  File share running very slow
2.  CPU resources being hogged by file share
3.  Corrupted date
4.  ANY THING ELSE PERTAINING TO FILE SHARE

THIS IS VERY IMPORTANT

twright@larimore.net
```

#### ↳ Re: Micro Focus File Share Component

- **From:** <kenmullins@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76tbgf$mln$1@camel0.mindspring.com>`
- **References:** `<Uj7k2.965$x5.17896239@news1.i1.net>`

```
Haven't had problems with corrupted dates, but I can tell you that fileshare
is VERY CLIENT HEAVY...i.e. most of the processing seems to be done on the
client machine rather than the server machine...I have used it in several
applications now, and find it is better to have faster client machines than
a faster server...I posted complaints and documentation etc to MF, but as
usual, no response from them as to why nor any info on possibly tuning the
environments...

Ken Mullins
Atlanta, GA

Share

>me this is very important.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: Micro Focus File Share Component

- **From:** "J���rgen Ibelgaufts" <ibelgaufts@gfc-net.de>
- **Date:** 1999-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369DE328.3EA2E9A0@gfc-net.de>`
- **References:** `<Uj7k2.965$x5.17896239@news1.i1.net>`

```
HI,

I tried fileshare a couple of years ago and I took my fingers off it because:

- it was very slow (although faster than opening fines through a network without
fileshare)

- you have to install the complete software on all client machines

- you have to UPDATE all client machines when your application changes

- you have to carefully think about security because ALL data will be transferred
through the network

I changed our software into client/server software where instead of
DISPLAYing/ACCEPTing I make calls to a communication program that sends and
receives the screens to/from the client machines. The client machines contain only
the client software while the server holds the data files and runs the user
programs. All files are opened locally by the server. The network will only see
the screen data and not the complete data. Files do not break when a user kills
his client program, and the thing is quite fast. When you sit in front of the
server, you can see what the users do because every user has a console program
running on the server printing out the currently used screen name. 

Juergen Ibelgaufts

----------------------------------------------------------------------

Tom Wright schrieb:
> 
> If there is anyone out in here who is currently using Micro Focus File Share
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> twright@larimore.net
```

## Thread: Fujitsu COBOL Ver. 3.0 — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu COBOL Ver. 3.0

- **From:** "Ian Stoddart" <Ian@stoddart60.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be381d$953d9280$b311883e@stodfamily.u-net.com>`

```
Hi,

anybody with experience of Fujitsu Ver. 3.0 ?  I have a problem with the
first program I have used this compiler with.  I defined a data field as
PIC X(10).  In the procedure division when using the Accept statement I
cannot use the enter key until I have entered 10 characters !!  I have used
a VALUE clause and also used PIC A.  Initially I forgot to use a VALUE
clause and when added I thought it had worked but it now doesn't though I
think I might have used another compiler at that stage.
Is anybody aware of this with Fujitsu, is there some way round it ?  

Regards,

Ian.
```

- **References:** `<01be381d$953d9280$b311883e@stodfamily.u-net.com>`


##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL Ver. 3.0

- **From:** "Dana Brasie" <dbrasie@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<776ebk$o7m$1@camel21.mindspring.com>`
- **References:** `<01be381d$953d9280$b311883e@stodfamily.u-net.com> <775f6e$idb$2@hermes.btu.de>`

```
You can use 'accept <variable> from console' to enter fewer characters than
defined in the PIC clause.  The example below will demonstrate this.


       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. CONSOLE-TEST.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

       01  TEST-VAR            PIC X(10) VALUE SPACES.
       01  CHOICE              PIC X(01) VALUE SPACE.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
             DISPLAY 'TEST USING "FROM CONSOLE" (Y/N):  '
               WITH NO ADVANCING.
             ACCEPT CHOICE FROM CONSOLE.

             DISPLAY 'ENTER CONSOLE TEST VARIABLE:  ' WITH NO ADVANCING.
             IF FUNCTION UPPER-CASE(CHOICE) = 'Y'
                ACCEPT TEST-VAR  FROM CONSOLE
             ELSE
                MOVE SPACES TO TEST-VAR
                ACCEPT TEST-VAR
             END-IF.

             DISPLAY 'YOUR INPUT = <' TEST-VAR '>'.

             STOP RUN.


Dana

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
```

## Thread: INDEXED FILES AND SORT — 10 this month / 11 total

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

_Thread started [1998-12](1998-12.md#t-6ccef8fc37)._

#### ↳ Re: INDEXED FILES AND SORT

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<flYj2.1996$hE2.12747831@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<76fq80$i21$1@hermes.iol.it>`

```
>If I want order a sequential file with the sort task.
>When I Run the program, it says me "run-time error".
>
>If I want create a indexed file, it says me File status error 95.
>I before run ISAM.exe.


Not sure what OS you are working under, but on MYV the only way I have ever
put data into an indexed file is by using the REPRO program. Sorting the
data is not required, because the REPRO utility inserts the records into the
INDEXED file according to how the key is defined.

Also on MYS the only command I have used to create an indexed file was by
using a DEFINE statement from within IDCAMS. When defining in this manner
there is always a DELETE before the define to make sure nothing exists
beforehand. Your error sounds like you may have the definition parameters
wrong. Verify them with what is in a mnaual, or try modifying a currently
working definition to fit what you want.
```


###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INDEXED FILES AND SORT

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Y1xk2.2174$hE2.13959416@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<76fq80$i21$1@hermes.iol.it> <flYj2.1996$hE2.12747831@storm.twcol.com> <369086D0.265976F0@IMN.nl>`

```
Yes I meant MVS.

>Try this with thousands or more records. The speed gained by using sorted
input
>is enormous.


I regularly use this method on thousands and sometimes millions of records.
But the data is always from some type of backup which would already be
sorted. Sometimes I use SyncSort or DFSort to sort and load a file, but
never VSAM, but supposedly it works. Using Syncsort or DFSort would
definitely be recommended since they do funny things to optimize thier IO
beyond what would normally be accomplished from a normal, say COBOL,
program.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INDEXED FILES AND SORT

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36929e54.174649099@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<76fq80$i21$1@hermes.iol.it> <flYj2.1996$hE2.12747831@storm.twcol.com> <369086D0.265976F0@IMN.nl> <Y1xk2.2174$hE2.13959416@storm.twcol.com>`

```
On Tue, 5 Jan 1999 18:14:51 -0500, "Jeff" <a@a.com> wrote:

>Yes I meant MVS.
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>program.

This is a common misconception.  For COBOL program sorts DF or
SYNCSORT is often used ANYWAY.  (fastsrt compiler option).  Removing
the sort from the program OFTEN (not always) does NOTHING to improve
performance.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INDEXED FILES AND SORT

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36937610.C8DD2AF9@home.com>`
- **References:** `<76fq80$i21$1@hermes.iol.it> <flYj2.1996$hE2.12747831@storm.twcol.com> <369086D0.265976F0@IMN.nl> <Y1xk2.2174$hE2.13959416@storm.twcol.com>`

```
Jeff wrote:
> 
> Yes I meant MVS.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> program.

The main reason to use a second step to create a flat file then REPRO it
to a KSDS file is to allow REPRO to make it more efficient.  I love
SyncSort's speed and power.  When I have it available I often merge one
file with conditions to create an extract.

I knew of a shop which had no sort utilities.  They sorted by copying to
an ISAM file and back again!

Also - I once converted a Univac 9030 shop to IBM.  (This computer was
the successor to the RCA - but after Univac laid off the design
engineers IBM hired them to build the 360, so these are similar
machines).  To keep our functionality, I made virtually every file
VSAM.  We had relative, flat, and indexed files (mostly relative). 
Anymore, just about everybody uses VSAM == IBM's ISAM, apparently ESDS &
RRDS files aren't used hardly at all anymore.  I guess most permanent
data are stored in databases.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INDEXED FILES AND SORT

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)

- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-YP4S4vLHvxsr@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```

wrote:

 
> Also - I once converted a Univac 9030 shop to IBM.  (This computer was
> the successor to the RCA - but after Univac laid off the design
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> data are stored in databases.

We use some ESDS but where I might have "used" to have chosen to use 
ESDS I just use a QSAM file now.  (But I changed OS's too).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INDEXED FILES AND SORT

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1999-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990106195631.25439.00002800@ngol06.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36937610.C8DD2AF9@home.com>`

```

All our VSAM reorgs are done with SORT (was CA-SORT; now it is IBM's DFSORT)
with the MERGE FIELDS=COPY to copy out to tape (or flat file), and SORT again
with MERGE FIELDS=COPY after the DEL/DEF to repopulate the cluster.

For VSAM KSDS clusters being rebuilt from scratch (e.g., we have a couple "poor
man's alternate index" files), we use SORT (OUTFIL KSDS,TOL,REUSE) and the
SORTOUT is the freshly-defined cluster.

Mark A. Young
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: INDEXED FILES AND SORT

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36917aa6.99967737@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<76fq80$i21$1@hermes.iol.it> <flYj2.1996$hE2.12747831@storm.twcol.com>`

```
On Mon, 4 Jan 1999 00:29:33 -0500, "Jeff" <a@a.com> wrote:

>>If I want order a sequential file with the sort task.
>>When I Run the program, it says me "run-time error".
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>INDEXED file according to how the key is defined.

Just FYI - repro works much better if the data *is* sorted - it's much
faster.

Second - you don't HAVE to use REPRO to populate a VSAM file.  Once
defined, you can populate it with any program.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INDEXED FILES AND SORT

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<y4xk2.2176$hE2.13961486@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<76fq80$i21$1@hermes.iol.it> <flYj2.1996$hE2.12747831@storm.twcol.com> <36917aa6.99967737@news1.ibm.net>`

```
>Second - you don't HAVE to use REPRO to populate a VSAM file.  Once
I always do have sorted input, geuss I'm just lucky that way.

>defined, you can populate it with any program.


I try not to write programs which are already designed and ready to use for
the same purpose.
Such as REPRO, IEBGENER, DFSort, or better yet SyncSort.
```

## Thread: cobol-85 upgrade book — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### cobol-85 upgrade book

- **From:** "don ferrario" <don@ferrario.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76rli5$k4p$1@news1.epix.net>`

```
I've been using cobol for 20+ years.  Although
all the compilers I use now are cobol-85, I never
did really learn any of the "new" features when
'85 came out.  I am probably using 99% just
syntax that was available in cobol-74 (which was
current when I learned it !!).

Since its been out for about 14 years or so now,
I probably should find out what the "improvements" are.

I don't want to read through my compiler reference
manuals looking for differences.  I also don't want to
read through a basic tutorial, which will try to re-teach
basic cobol.

Can anyone recommend a text which specifically
points out the changes from '74 to '85 cobol?

don ferrario
http://www.epix.net/~ferrario
```

```

to start.  It is included (I am pretty certain) in the COBOL FAQ.  and/or
you could contact Don - who posts fairly regularly in this NG (check
DejaNews for his id).
```

```
Trouble is, now is not the time to learn COBOL II, as it is no longer
supported.  Skip to whatever they're calling the next version.
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol-85 upgrade book

- **From:** bgwillia@vcu.edu (Boyce G. Williams, Jr.)
- **Date:** 1999-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36921e4c.3638226@usenet.acw.vcu.edu>`
- **References:** `<76rli5$k4p$1@news1.epix.net>`

```
"don ferrario" <don@ferrario.com> wrote:

>I've been using cobol for 20+ years.  Although
>all the compilers I use now are cobol-85, I never
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>points out the changes from '74 to '85 cobol?

I always keep two non-IBM COBOL books at hand.  One is "VS COBOL II:
highlights and Techniques" by James G. Janossy (ISBN 0-471-57205-5)
and "Advanced ANSI COBOL with Structured Programming: For VS COBOL II
and Microsoft/Micro focus COBOL" by Gary DeWard Brown (ISBN
0-471-54786-7) which is, of course, colored blue (inside joke for
those who tell the FNG's in one's shop to "get the Brown book").  I
keep two IBM COBOL books handy:  the Language Reference and
Programming Guide.  I refer to the IBM books first, but if I need a
more non-jargoneze description on the purpose of the syntax then I
refer to the others.

The Janossy is good for transistion to COBOL-85 from COBOL-74 and even
tells you what verbs are new or dropped.  Both Janossy and Brown has
good examples and code you can use to get started in the brave new
COBOL.

Enjoy,

Boyce G. Williams, Jr.

 .--------------------------------------------------------------------.
 | "People should have two virtues:  purpose- the courage to envisage |
 | and pursue valued goals uninhibited by the defeat of infantile     |
 | fantasies, by guilt and the failing fear punishment;  and wisdom- a|
 | detached concern with life itself, in the face of death itself."   |
 |                                                     Norman F. Dixon|
 '--------------------------------------------------------------------'
```

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
>I've been using cobol for 20+ years.  Although
>all the compilers I use now are cobol-85, I never
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>points out the changes from '74 to '85 cobol?

Why stop there? There were amendments in '89, and many vendors offer
enhancements that are in the proposed new standard (whenever that may come
out).

If you don't want to read, how about a course? We have a two day course
("Beyond COBOL II" is the official title, but some people offer it as "COBOL
For the New Millenium") that covers all this and includes some hands on
exercises. The course is specifically designed for IBM mainframe compilers.

Check out the website in my signature.

Cheers,
```

<a id="t-b564c4d4fb"></a>
## Thread: RFD: comp.sys.acucobol — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### RFD: comp.sys.acucobol

- **From:** "Scott Woodward" <scottw@southpacfinance.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** news.announce.newgroups,news.groups,alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<915475698.556@isc.org>`

```
                     REQUEST FOR DISCUSSION (RFD)
                 unmoderated group comp.sys.acucobol

This is a formal Request For Discussion (RFD) for the creation of a
world-wide unmoderated Usenet newsgroup comp.lang.cobol.acucobol.
This is not a Call for Votes (CFV); you cannot vote at this time.
Procedural details are below.

Newsgroup line:
comp.sys.acucobol	All aspects of the use of Acucobol.

RATIONALE: comp.sys.acucobol

Acucobol is a company based in San Diego CA. Currently all users and
developers use email to communicate ideas and problems to Acucobol
(now known as Acucorp). These ideas and problems however are not
released to the general populous of Acucobol users. This presents a
problem for both Acucobol and the Users.

The purpose of creating this comp.sys.acucobol newsgroup is to have a
central point of contact for all users. Discussions involving
Acucobol-specific information would be greatly enhanced by this group
due to the world wide use of Acucobol.

Due to the specific nature of some of the Acucobol products, there is
a need for a seperate group to discuss these topics as opposed to just
the cobol language.

Here are some reasons in favor of a comp.sys.acucobol:

Currently, most developers cannot easily come in contact with other
developers other than by attending Acucorp's yearly developer's
conference.

Acucobol contains significant extensions to the COBOL standard.
Discussion of these extensions would be of little interest to
comp.lang.cobol readers who are not Acucobol developers.

There are a number of Acucorp products which are used in conjunction
with Acucobol that do not directly involve COBOL. Because these
products tend to be used in larger, more complex systems they also
tend to cause more discussion than the base language. Such discussion
would be only marginally on topic in comp.lang.cobol.

CHARTER: comp.sys.acucobol

This newsgroup is for discussion of all aspects of the use of
Acucobol.  Some examples of relevant topics are:

  Coding techniques
  Learning and teaching materials
  Discussions on coding
  User Support
  Technical Support
  User Discussions
  General Information

The primary purpose of this group is to pass information between the
users of Acucobol. Explicitly discouraged are the posting of
commercial advertisements, any kind of flaming and personal arguments,
as well as the posting of binaries of any kind.

END CHARTER.

PROCEDURE:

This is a request for discussion, not a call for votes.  In this phase
of the process, any potential problems with the proposed newsgroups
should be raised and resolved.  The discussion period will continue
for a minimum of 21 days (starting from when the first RFD for this
proposal is posted to news.announce.newgroups), after which a Call For
Votes (CFV) may be posted by a neutral vote taker if the discussion
warrants it.  Please do not attempt to vote until this happens.

All discussion of this proposal should be posted to news.groups.

This RFD attempts to comply fully with the Usenet newsgroup creation
guidelines outlined in "How to Create a New Usenet Newsgroup" and "How
to Format and Submit a New Group Proposal".  Please refer to these
documents (available in news.announce.newgroups) if you have any
questions about the process.

DISTRIBUTION:

This RFD has been posted to the following newsgroups:

  news.announce.newgroups,
  news.groups,
  alt.cobol,
  comp.lang.cobol

Proponent: Scott Woodward <scottw@southpacfinance.com.au>
```

<a id="t-440774dcad"></a>
## Thread: Loading the key before a random read — 3 msgs

### Loading the key before a random read

- **From:** robert2504@aol.com (Robert2504)
- **Date:** 1999-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990104215358.05605.00001371@ng-ch1.aol.com>`

```
Hi everyone,
 I'm working on a project and I'm kind of new to the real world programming.
I'm developing a program to print labels that will get the information from the
master.dat file. First the clerk enters an account number and the program
checks to see if the account number is on the master.dat if it finds it it will
move some of the fields to the labels record to print. My problem is the key. I
have not much experience with keys and I cannot read the record because I don't
know how to load the key before reading the record. Here is the file-control
for that master.dat

select armaster1 assign to "armaster.dat"
                                organization is index
                                access mode is dynamic
                                record key is ar-key
                                alternate record key is ar-name
                                with duplicates
                                alternate record key is ar-phone-4
                                 with duplicates
                                 file status is fl-stat.
could someone give me some examples of how to load the key before a read.

Thanks in advance

Robert
```

#### ↳ Re: Loading the key before a random read

- **From:** Will Hopwood <whopwood@mailbag.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36918120.4574C54B@mailbag.com>`
- **References:** `<19990104215358.05605.00001371@ng-ch1.aol.com>`

```
You should just need to move the key value into ar-key before the read.
Assuming the the account number is the key...
  
      MOVE some-account-number-field  TO AR-KEY
      READ armaster1 ...

  Will


Robert2504 wrote:
> 
> Hi everyone,
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> Robert
```

#### ↳ Re: Loading the key before a random read

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76t4mh$797$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<19990104215358.05605.00001371@ng-ch1.aol.com>`

```
Simply do a move to the key field.  Using the select clause
you gave the reads would go as follows:

       move "this is key" to ar-key.
       read armaster1 invalid key
            perform key-not-in-file.
or
       move data-name-containing-key to ar-name.
       read armaster1 key equal to ar-name invalid key
            perform ...

Lots more permutations and combinations, but that
is the basic idea.  For anything but the primary key,
use the key name and a condition.

<19990104215358.05605.00001371@ng-ch1.aol.com>...

key. I

don't

file-control
>for that master.dat
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
the stuff for composing drafts of programs via pencil.

The paper I need has a grid with (150) little square cells across its
width (ie. 10 char./in.), and (6) rows of these cells per verticle inch.
Its designation is sometimes called :

'150-10-6 Print Chart'

I don't know who manufactures it, but its model number appears to be :

Form 18-10-0230

WHERE  CAN  I  BUY  THIS  STUFF ?
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76q2cd$vgd$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
How can access a one record like "read invalid key ..."  on ODBC Data Base.
```

#### ↳ Re: DB Access Control and PowerCobol

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3690C55E.DF97EABE@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<76q2cd$vgd$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```


Euromercante wrote:

> How can access a one record like "read invalid key ..."  on ODBC Data Base.

EXEC SQL
Select col, col, ...
  from table(s)
 where (...)
END-EXEC
Evaluate SQL-CODE
When zero
   process row/record found
When 100
   not found
When other
   SQL problem
End-evaluate

The Frog
```

<a id="t-a62bb40dad"></a>
## Thread: DELAY COMMAND or whatever — 3 this month / 13 total

_Thread started [1998-12](1998-12.md#t-a62bb40dad)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DELAY COMMAND or whatever

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch
- **Date:** 1999-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76qjnc$tcf$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<368052f1.9329575@news.inter.net.il> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-fnCSn9dutg0m@dsm4merlin.iix.com> <76bcc5$ft1@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <36899A6D.121FBDB8@att.net> <76cb85$qb5@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In article <76cb85$qb5@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>,
  "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

<snip>

> >
> >What does the time field passed to ILBOWAT look like?
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Don't try PIC S9(04)! You'll get a returncode of 8 and no delay at all. It
has to be a fullword binary, not a halfword. I tested the following little
programm, compiled with COBOL for MVS & VM V1R2M1 and LE/370 Version 1.8
(OS/390 V2.4):

CBL DATA(24)
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. I49804.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  DELAY-AMT               PIC S9(9) COMP.
       01  ILBOWAT0                PIC X(8) VALUE 'ILBOWAT0'.
       01  CURRENT-TIME            PIC 9(8).
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
           MOVE 100 TO DELAY-AMT
           ACCEPT CURRENT-TIME FROM TIME
           DISPLAY CURRENT-TIME
           CALL ILBOWAT0 USING DELAY-AMT
           ACCEPT CURRENT-TIME FROM TIME
           DISPLAY CURRENT-TIME
           GOBACK.

With the following result:
13590982
14004985

So 100 means 100 seconds. By the way, there is no documentation at all in the
COBOL for MVS & VM manual. And: don't try to call ILBOWAT instead of ILBOWAT0!
The CSECT starts with a "DC H'0'" at displacement 0, so you'll get a ABEND
S0C1. ILBOWAT0 is an ALIAS of ILBOWAT, but with entry point at displacement 2.

Cheers

Daniel

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DELAY COMMAND or whatever

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-04T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<76rgdj$8b4@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`

```

ONLY place that I know that this module is/was ever documented was in the LY
(licencensed) "run-time logic" manual for OS/VS COBOL.

>>
>> (Easy way to test it - but I don't have a compiler and run-time handy) is
to

display

>>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: AcuCobol - Screenpainter

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** mattr@mentis.com.au
- **Date:** 1999-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76pnqb$78d$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<74tnpc$e26$2@hermes.btu.de> <75649v$v1q$1@fountain.mindlink.net> <3677A98B.DBA661EE@IMN.nl> <7592kl$qe0$1@hermes.btu.de> <75mn7g$21f$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <75r8v9$kdv$1@hermes.btu.de>`

```
In article <75r8v9$kdv$1@hermes.btu.de>,
  "Hajo Schepker" <schepker@geocities.com> wrote:
> OK, but when I later want to change some controls or want to add some
> controls, all my changes are overwritten, right?
>

You can avoid overwriting your code changes by going into Tools, then Options
and under the Generate Document tab, turn off Program File (the .cbl file).
Then, only the copy libs will be generated.  Mind you, it won't add any
paragraph headings etc to the .cbl file, so you'll have to keep an eye on that
(it's not usually a problem).

Hope that made it a little clearer.

> mattr@mentis.com.au schrieb in Nachricht
> <75mn7g$21f$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: AcuCobol - Screenpainter

- **From:** mattr@mentis.com.au
- **Date:** 1999-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76po07$7g1$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<74tnpc$e26$2@hermes.btu.de>`

```
In article <74tnpc$e26$2@hermes.btu.de>,
  "Hajo Schepker" <schepker@geocities.com> wrote:
> Hi,
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> But where do I add action-code to the menu-items?

There's a copy library that gets generated called .mnu - try looking in
there. You should be able to follow the variables in the Working Storage
(.wrk) and see the code in the Procedure Division (.prd).

The easiest way would be to compile the screen code with the listing option -
it'll give you a list of all the code from the copy files etc.
>
> Thanks
> H. Schepker
>
>

http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### MicroFocus v4.1 & Oracle (on AIX) - Cannot run the program

- **From:** Aspi Engineer <aspi_engineer@striderite.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36911372.B9EAE9B3@striderite.com>`

```
Hi All,

We are currently running Microfocus COBOL V4.0, revision 005 on AIX
v4.2.1. This works fine. However when we made a migration to Microfocus
COBOL v4.1, revision 005 on the same AIX platform, I am unable to create
an executable that works. The executable has embedded SQL and the DBMS
is ORACLE 8.0.5.

Simple COBOL programs (no SQL) work fine, however Pro*COBOL files just
abort when it encounters the SQL CONNECT call, with no error messages at
all.

If I copy the Oracle provided "rtsora" as "$COBDIR/rts32", even the
compile fails with the error message
Load error : file 'clmf_get_tty'
error code: 173, pc=0, call=4, seg=0
173     Called program file not found in drive/directory

Any suggestions,

Thx,
Aspi
aspi_engineer@striderite.com


PS: Does anyone have any instructions for creating an RTS on AIX which
is MQSeries and Oracle enabled. If yes, then please send it to me.
```

<a id="t-c04b94dd54"></a>
## Thread: Newbie to COBOL — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Newbie to COBOL

- **From:** "Ian Stoddart" <Ian@stoddart60.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be382a$bb608780$b311883e@stodfamily.u-net.com>`

```
Hello you COBOL people,

As a newbie to COBOL I have read with interest your recent postings.  I
joined a company last April in the UK to re-train in IT at age 38.  I
currently have half  a degree from study with the Open University.  I am
one of 150 new starters, half who are now placed with client companies.  I
myself have been Y2K testing with Xerox thougth only user acceptance
testing, but it is a good way to get to know the systems.  Training
consisted of 3 months writing COBOL programs using MicroFocus workbench (10
of us at a time, a new group every fortnight !!).  The programs were not
too taxing though the latter ones did use about 9 input/output files so
plenty to think about for a newcomer.  I was paid a full salary whilst
training comparable to my previous (corporate finance) role and was also
paid at full pay for 5 weeks whilst waiting for a placement.  On completion
of training we were tested using TeckChek.  Whilst hoping to get into a
mainstream role soon I agree with an earlier comment "take what you can". 
I am currently using Fujitsu V3 to convert my old Pascal progs to COBOL and
I am also working through Mastering COBOL Programming (ISBN 0-333-69343-4).
 This is an excellent intro to COBOL with lots of sample programs
(available via FTP) nearly all the guys on my course used it.  I hope my
story is of some interest to you all either experienced or like me a new
guy.

Regards,
Ian

PS I sent an earlier message re: Fujitsu COBOL.  Being new to newsgroups
I'm not sure if I sent it correctly as I can't see it.  Can somebody
hopefully reading this email me a confirmation.
PPS Somebody mentioned FAQ's - were can I see this ?
```

---

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: New Online COBOL II Certification (Free for limited time)

- **From:** "Robert" <rls@ktis.!remove!.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76s573$sdd$1@dumber.ktis.net>`
- **References:** `<3690C228.137995D2@tekmetrics.com>`

```

Mike Russiello <mike.russiello@tekmetrics.com> wrote in message
news:3690C228.137995D2@tekmetrics.com...
>TekMetrics announces its new E-Certification program.  You
>register and take a test online and,  depending on how you do, you can
>be certified at either the standard or master levels.  In the past week,

I will say that I thought this was a scam.  But I went over to the website,
and took the exam (COBOL II) and scored a 3.67 on it.  The exam was pretty
good, I saw a lot of weak points in it though.

But my question is -> Who certifies TekMetrics?  I recently completed my CCP
designation and I know it is worth something because of the organization
that is behind it.

TekMetrics looks like a good resource for those in the training field.  But
I would not rely on it solely as a basis for certification.

Anyway, can't wait until my certificate arrives.  March myself down to HR
and demand a raise! :-)

Rob
```

## Thread: Roman Numerals revisited — 50 this month / 52 total

### Roman Numerals revisited

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,alt.lang.basic,comp.lang.basic.misc,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qlnk2.656$EC3.507828@news3.mia>`

```
Below is an interesting article from my local paper.  It was
entered using OCR, so please excuse any typos.

--------------------------------
1999 has its own numeric problem
Thursday, December 31, 1998

By MICHAEL E. RUANE
The Washington Post

WASHINGTON - With anxious eyes fixed on the approaching millennium,
many members of the public seem to have missed a serious problem
facing them this very night.

Sneaking up for numerous centuries - ever since the ancient Romans
left things so fuzzy - the dilemma is as follows:

Does Friday begin the year MCMXCIX?

Or MDCCCCLXXXXVIIII?

Or MCMXCVIII?

Or MIM?

The sticky issue, how to render 1999 in Roman numerals, has been
raised by the National Institute of Standards and Technology - the
old Bureau of Standards - which, among other things, is the arbiter
of cosmic queries: How long is a second? When does the next century
begin? Etc.

As this dire moment approached, the institute began receiving
questions about what it calls "the Year 1999 Problem," and its
researchers began scratching their heads.

What should be the answer?

And who the heck cares about Roman numerals?

More people, it turns out, than you might think.

Architects. Movie producers. Kings. Popes. Officials of the Olympics,
certain newspapers and the Super Bowl. Families in which males pass
their names to their sons.

The main trouble with the Roman numeral system was that it made for
very long numbers, no doubt bloodying stone carvers of its day. Thus,
shortcuts, or what some experts call contractions, came into use.

The most difficult of these is the so-called subtraction principle,
in which a theoretically long Roman numeral is shortened to manageable
breadth.

The number 9, for example, which technically might be VIIII V for 5,
plus HU for 4 - is shortened to IX - 10, or X, minus I.

And therein lies the source of the Year 1999 Problem.

The Romans, and many other ancients, were not consistent in how they
used the subtraction principle.

Most observers believe the correct 1999 rendering is MCMXCIX: M stands
for 1000; C for 100, subtracted from the second M, equaling 900; X for
10, subtracted from the second C, equaling 90; and I for 1, subtracted
from the second X, equaling 9.

Total purists, though, might opt for MDCCCCLXXXXVIIII, which uses no
subtraction: M equals 1000; D equals 500; CCCC equals 400; L equals 50;
XXXX equals 40; V equals 5; and IIII equals 4. Total: 1999.

Partial purists can go for MCMXCVIIII, a combination of the two.

And for the ultimate subtractors, there's the racy MIM: M for 1000;
the I subtracting I from the second M, for 1999.

The institute says that MCMXCIX is "favored," but the rules are not
written in stone, so to speak. Said spokesman Michael E. Newman: "It's
one of those quirky things."

However it turns out, this trouble will be short-lived.

The year 2000 in Roman numerals is blessedly simple:

MM.
```

```
is that no symbol can be repeated more than three times.
```

```
Early Romans wrote VIIII, but IX was the standard form long before Caesar.
When the number was just four it was written IIII because IV was too much
like the god IVPITER, but that was the only special case.

>> >> Does Friday begin the year MCMXCIX?
>> >>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >because it looks more like a Roman numeral than #3.  

I'm not sure which one you mean by your #s.  MCMXCIX is the correct form,
and that's what I have been seeing.  See my web page
http://users.aol.com/PanicYr00/MCMXCIX.html

>> >After all, you use
>> >Roman Numerals to get fancy not to be efficient.

Or as I put it on my page, a trendy Roman Numeral is preposterous.
```

```
Phil Brady wrote:
> 
(snip)
> >
> What about all those clocks with fancy Roman numerals - they always have
> IIII instead of IV
> Regards
> Phil Brady

Phil,
The IIII on clocks and watches is an old clockmaker's tradition.  There
are always exceptions, but in general you will see IIII only if the
numbers at the bottom of the dial are upside down.  If they are
right-side up the clockmakers used IV instead.  (I didn't make this up.
LaRich is in the jewelry business--this info is courtesy of our
watchmaker.)
```
