#### ↳ Re: Roman Numerals revisited

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1999-01-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1999Jan8.174834.19474@giant>`
- **References:** `<qlnk2.656$EC3.507828@news3.mia>`

```
In article <qlnk2.656$EC3.507828@news3.mia>,
"Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> OCRed:
>
> MICHAEL E. RUANE [wrote, tongue firmly in cheek:]
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Or MIM?

Although the modern system of notation called "Roman" numerals is not the
one that the Romans used, it is nevertheless well-defined.  It is a
right-recursive system with three symbols per level of recursion.  This
time I hope to make it a little clearer by providing constructive
definitions rather than the usual mathematical formulae.

NOTE: A dot after a numeral indicates an ordinal.  For example,
"the I. cycle" is pronounced "the first cycle".

The I. cycle traditionally uses the symbols "I" (one), "V" (five), and
"X" (ten).

    I. CYCLE (I, V, X): I II III IV V VI VII VIII IX

The II. cycle is derived from the I. cycle by shifting two new symbols in
from the right, "L" (fifty) and "C" (one hundred).

    II. CYCLE (X, L, C): X XX XXX XL L LX LXX LXXX XC

After each entry in the II. cycle, the _entire_ I. cycle is repeated,
recurring on the right.  For example:

    XI XII XIII XIV XV XVI XVII XVIII XIX

The III. cycle is derived from the II. cycle in the same manner.  The two
new symbols are traditionally "D" (five hundred) and "M" (one thousand).

    III. CYCLE (C, D, M): C CC CCC CD D DC DCC DCCC CM

After each entry in the III. cycle, the _entire_ II. cycle is repeated,
recurring on the right.  For example:

    CX CXX CXXX CXL CL CLX CLXX CLXXX CXC

After each entry in the repeated II. cycle, the _entire_ I. cycle is
repeated, always recurring on the right.  For example:

    CXI CXII CXIII CXIV CXV CXVI CXVII CXVIII CXIX

The two new symbols in the IV. cycle are traditionally overbar-V (five
thousand) and overbar-X (ten thousand).  This character set doesn't have
overbars, so I can't exhibit the IV. cycle, but you know that it starts
with "M", "MM", and "MMM".

The two new symbols in the V. cycle are overbar-L (fifty thousand)
and overbar-C (one hundred thousand).

The two new symbols in the VI. cycle are overbar-D (five hundred
thousand) and overbar-M (one thousand thousand).

The Romans used various symbols from time to time for five thousand
thousand and ten thousand thousand, but none of them made it into the
"Roman" numerals.

"MIM" is not a "Roman" numerals because an "I" (from the I. cycle) is to
the _left_ of an "M" (from the III. cycle).  By definition, earlier
cycles appear to the _right_ of later cycles.

Notice that each different cycle is one-based and contains nine entries. 
"Arabic" numerals have only one cycle, which is zero-based and contains
ten entries.


Here is a grammar of the printable "Roman" numerals in Backus-Naur form:

    RN = { 4 | 4A | A }
    A  = { 3 | 3B | B }
    B  = { 2 | 21 | 1 }

    1  = { I | II | III | IV | V | VI | VII | VIII | IX }
    2  = { X | XX | XXX | XL | L | LX | LXX | LXXX | XC }
    3  = { C | CC | CCC | CD | D | DC | DCC | DCCC | CM }
    4  = { M | MM | MMM                                 }


Last but least, here is a syntrax diagram (pun intended) of the printable
"Roman" numerals:

>>-------------------------------------------------------------------><
      |-- M ----|    |-- C -----|    |-- X -----|    |-- I -----|
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
                     |-- CM ----|    |-- XC ----|    |-- IX ----|
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Roman Numerals revisited

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-01-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3696ab00.27168211@netnews>`
- **References:** `<qlnk2.656$EC3.507828@news3.mia> <1999Jan8.174834.19474@giant>`

```
'Twas 8 Jan 99 17:48:34 EST, when cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris
Westbury) illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>Although the modern system of notation called "Roman" numerals is not the
>one that the Romans used, it is nevertheless well-defined.  

You're not the only one to claim this.  I realize that early Roman
numerals didn't have subtraction, but AFAIK Roman bookkeepers were using
"Roman Numerals" before Caesar's time.  Do you have a reference saying
otherwise?  If so, I'll correct my page
(http://users.aol.com/PanicYr00/MCMXCIX.html).  

When I was a small child I saw a movie where the hero retrieves an eagle
with an IX on it.  Are you telling me Hollywood could be wrong? 

>The two new symbols in the IV. cycle are traditionally overbar-V (five
>thousand) and overbar-X (ten thousand).  This character set doesn't have
>overbars, so I can't exhibit the IV. cycle, but you know that it starts
>with "M", "MM", and "MMM".

AIUI, the overbar was a single stroke over all the digits, and therefore
M-overbar-V is an unlikely combination.   

Of course, we are used to a numeric system where there is no redundancy.
In Roman numerals there are combinations of digits which are invalid (eg,
MIM) and multiple combinations which are the same value (eg, IV = IIII).
Just as we have many people who misspell words, how many Romans misspelled
their numbers?  Does anyone have a 2000 year old math text?  Come to think
of it, most Roman mathematicians used Greek numerals.  Does anyone have a
2000 year old bookkeeping text?
```

```
Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>Randall Bart wrote:
>>
>>[snip]  Does anyone have a 2000 year old math text?
>
>As I recall, that's about how old my college math books are. ;-)


Unfortunately, my 2000 year old texts exist only on 2000
year old machine readable media, and the 2000 year old
computer needs repair.

Last summer, I visited a museum for one of Canada's better
known authors.  On display were notes on many of his major
works, with notes and so on in the margins of the hand written
drafts.  A treasure drove for scholars.  I could not help thinking
that tomorrows scholars and museum curators are going to
have a rough time.  One hundred 5 inch floppies on a wall is
not very visually rewarding, though it could be made into a
one hundred verse song fairly easily.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Roman Numerals revisited

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1999-01-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990111044153.10398.00010260@ng18.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3696ab00.27168211@netnews>`

```
Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
On 9 Jan 1999 01:29:51 GMT
said in part

lots of snippets

<< ...Come to think
of it, most Roman mathematicians used Greek numerals.
...>>

Now wait a minute! This phrase 'Greek numerals', what do you mean by that?

I jump in here with trepidation. Many of the posters at this point in this
on-again off-again thread are being successfully humorous which is hard on the
net, and I don't want to spoil that.

But let me take up this notion briefly, because it is very interesting.  

I am guessing that Barticus is not refering to the Arabic Numerals, which we in
the West sometimes blindly assume came from the Arabs when in fact, as anyone
who cares can tell you, that the Arabs got them from the Indian Scholars, and
that the shapes of the numerals are actually graphically abrevieate indications
of the first two characters of the spelling of the numeral names in Hindi (just
as the & sign is a glob for 'et', a beshortened rendering of etcetera - meaning
'and other(s)' in Latin), ... and yes this is all one sentence.

And, as Barticus would know the transmission of the Arabic Numerals was part of
the Renaisance, a post-Roman phenomenon.

But surely we are not saying that the Romans used the actual Greek numerals?! 
Do you know there are such things?

The Greeks had two systems of numeration.  The first was alphabetic. The
letters were used as ordinals. They used 27 symbols, matching the system of
Semitic alphabets. The meaning was 1-9, 10-90, 100-900. This ordinal
signification of Greek letters in accient texts is one of the most important
corroborations of the transmission of the Greek Lettering system from
non-Indo-European sources: the general phonetic correspondence being the other.

This system, like Roman numerals, had no zero.

The greeks had a second set of number symbols.
The used the first letter of the Greek word for the numbers to represent the
value.
The pi being the first letter of the word five, stood for that quantity, but
also stood as a multiplier of ten and hundred, which values were represented by
the letter that started the Greek word for the number (Delta and 'Eta).

This second Greek system had truely magnificent ligature glyphs for the
five-times ciphers. The seven bit ASCII characterset common to most net access
software does not have all the Greek characters, and even the eight bit
extented ASCII does not have the very specialized ligatures for the Greek
numbers.

So, with all that having been said, let me ask this.
What Romans used either of these Greek number systems? Or do you have some
symbol system in mind distinct from these two, and distinct from the Roman
numerals, which you are calling Greek?

Best Wishes

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Roman Numerals revisited

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1999-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1999Jan12.113207.19478@giant>`
- **References:** `<qlnk2.656$EC3.507828@news3.mia> <1999Jan8.174834.19474@giant> <3696ab00.27168211@netnews>`

```
In article <3696ab00.27168211@netnews>,
Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart) wrote:
>
> In article <1999Jan8.174834.19474@giant>,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> using "Roman Numerals" before Caesar's time.

With respect specifically to subtraction, "IX" coexisted happily with
"VIIII" over vast spans of time and space because "IX" is the "literary"
form and "VIIII" is the "bookkeeping" form.  "IX" minimizes the energy
expended in cutting an inscription into stone because an inscription is
not processed further.  "VIIII" costs a bookkeeper a bit more to write
initially, but the savings in the addition phase more than make up for
it.

Actually, I was coming at this from a slightly different angle, but
failed to express myself clearly.  The concept of a complete and
nonredundant mathematical system is quite modern, and even today there
are few who care whether a given system is complete and nonredundant or
not.  The system of "Roman" numerals from 1850 that I posted is a modern
system in this sense, but none of the Roman numerals used by the
historical Romans is.


[...]
> When I was a small child I saw a movie where the hero retrieves an
> eagle with an IX on it.  Are you telling me Hollywood could be wrong?

Hey, I remember that one!  It said IX BC, didn't it?  :-)


> [...]
> > The two new symbols in the IV. cycle are traditionally overbar-V
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> therefore  M overbar-V  is an unlikely combination.

This is really tough to discuss in the ASCII character set.  Remember
that when we use the ASCII character set, Roman numerals come out
automatically in the literary form, e.g., III.  The bookkeeping form does
not have serifs, e.g. |||.

I translated the word "viniculum" as "overbar".  The viniculum is part of
"Roman" numerals; it does not occur in Roman numerals.  The single stroke
over all the digits that you mention does occur in Roman numerals, but it
does not change the value.  It's kind of a grouping thing, like the comma
between groups of three decimal digits.

One interesting thing is that there is no particular reason for the
strokes to be upright, e.g. | or ||.  They could just as well be
horizontal, e.g. - or =.  The numbers 2 and 3 that we use today are
derived from the ancient = and <three horizontal lines>.

The original Roman numerals are not letters.  A vertical stroke was used
for "one", which later became the letter "I".  A "V" shape (in various
orientations) was widely used in Asia minor for "hand", and it was
adopted for "five", which later became the letter "V".  Two "V" shapes
for ten later became the letter "X".  The Greek <upper-case chi>, which
has an "X" shape, was used for "fifty", which later became the letter
"L"!  The Greek <upper-case theta> was used for "one hundred", which
later became the letter "C" under the influence of the word "centum". 
The Greek <upper-case phi> was used for "one thousand", which later
became the letter "M".

The Romans wrote (|) for 1000.  If you squint at it, you can almost see
how it evolved into "M".  Another interesting thing is that the Roman (|)
also evolved into the "lazy 8" that we now use to symbolize infinity. 
Not quite "one, two, three ... infinity" but the same priniciple.

Now, what is five hundred?  Well, half of (|) is |), which looks like "D"
if you squint.  But the other half of (|) is (|, which looks like a
backwards "D"!  In fact, the Romans wrote five hundred both ways, because
Roman numerals may look like letters, but they aren't.  "Roman" numerals,
of course, are all letters.  I have a snapshot of an inscription in San
Marco, Venice with the year 1656 expressed as MDCLVI - except that the D
is backward!

As I mentioned, Romans did not use the viniculum at all.  They wrote
((|)) for ten thousand, (((|))) for one hundred thousand, and so on.  On
the columna rostrata erected in the Roman forum to commemorate the
victory over the Carthaginians in 260 BC, the symbol (((|))) is repeated
twenty-three times to represent 2,300,000!

Of course, there are millions of bookkeeping entries for every
inscription, which is how Roman numerals underwent such a "genetic
drift".  I mention that many Roman accounts were kept in the coefficient
form, in order to make their summation easier:

    |.(|)    - 1000
    ||.(|)   - 2000
    |||.(|)  - 3000
    ||||.(|) - 4000
    \/.(|)   - 5000
    [etc]


> Of course, we are used to a numeric system where there is no
> redundancy.  In Roman numerals there are combinations of digits which
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Greek numerals.  Does anyone have a 2000 year old bookkeeping text?

Whew!  If you are really interested in this, you might enjoy "Die
Zahlzeichen und das elementare Rechnen der Griechen und R"omer" by G.
Friedlein (Erlangen, 1869).

Briefly, the Greek literary system uses
 a vertical stroke for one
 <upper-case pi> for five (<pi>ente, as in English "pentagon")
 <upper-case delta> for ten (deka, as in English "decade" or
  "decaliter"), often elided into a circle
 <rough breathing> for one hundred (<rough breathing>ekaton, as in
  English "hectoliter", "hectare", or "hectograph" [the purple printer])
 X for one thousand (xilioi, in case you've ever wondered how the English
  word "chiliastic" ever came to refer to the Millennium)
 M for ten thousand (mupioi, English "myriad")

The Greek bookkeeping system starts with three cycles:

             1     2     3      4       5       6   7       8     9
    units    ALPHA BETA  GAMMA  DELTA   EPSILON VAU ZETA    ETA   THETA
    tens     IOTA  KAPPA LAMBDA MU      NU      XI  OMICRON PI    QOPH
    hundreds RHO   SIGMA TAU    UPSILON PHI     CHI PSI     OMEGA SAMPI

Thousands are expressed by a bar to the left, as |K = 20,000.

                                            K
Myriads are expressed by a symbol above, as M = 200,000.


You can see that the great advantage of Roman numerals is that you need
learn only V, X, L, and C.  The great disadvantage of Roman numerals is
the same: with so few symbols, arithmetic is hard.  The great advantage
of Greek numerals is that with so many symbols, arithmetic is easy (not
only easier, but more compact that in our decimal system!).  The great
disadvantage is the same: there are so many symbols to be learned before
you can begin.  We have the same dichotomy today between GUIs that are
easy to learn but difficult to use, and command line interfaces that are
difficult to learn but easy to use.  It's no wonder that so many Romans
kept their books in Greek numerals.
```

```
In elementary school (50ish), I was taught the "I" represented one finger,
the "V" was a whole hand (open hand, look at thumb and little finger), and
the "X" was two hands (one upside down) for the numbers 1, 5, & 10.  Can't
recall the higher numbers except "C" and "M" probably came from "century"
or "centurian" and "millenium".

Howard Brazee wrote:

> Do you know if there's any truth to the story that each Arabic numeral
> was designed by the number of angles in the digit?  It's fun to draw
> them with the appropriate number of angles to demonstrate this story -
> but that doesn't make it true.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Roman Numerals revisited

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1999-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1999Jan19.131258.19484@giant>`
- **References:** `<qlnk2.656$EC3.507828@news3.mia> <1999Jan8.174834.19474@giant> <369B8FE5.6EE47889@home.com>`

```
In article <369B8FE5.6EE47889@home.com>,
Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com> wrote:
>
> Do you know if there's any truth to the story that each Arabic numeral
> was designed by the number of angles in the digit?  It's fun to draw
> them with the appropriate number of angles to demonstrate this story -
> but that doesn't make it true.

That's interesting.  I hadn't heard that one.  It fits with words like
"pentagon" (which means "five angles", not "five sides" as is
occasionally supposed).  Unfortunately, it is no more true than my own
childhood theory about the number of line segments in the digit, for the
simple reason that the Hindu-Arabic numerals we use today were not
"designed" in the modern sense.  I would call these "restrospective"
theories -- an attempt to find or impose a mnemonic order on the data.

If you are interested in this subject, you might enjoy "The Development
of Numerals in Europe" by G. F. Hill (Oxford, 1915).  For example,
between the X. Century, when Hindu-Arabic numerals became widespread in
Europe, and the XVI. Century, when the mutation of Hindu-Arabic numerals
was slowed by the invention of printing, the numeral "4" was most often
written as a "B" shape in various orientations!
```

- **References:** `<qlnk2.656$EC3.507828@news3.mia> <1999Jan8.174834.19474@giant> <3696ab00.27168211@netnews> <1999Jan12.113207.19478@giant>`

```
'Twas 12 Jan 99 11:32:07 EST, when cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris
Westbury) illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>In article <3696ab00.27168211@netnews>,
>Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart) wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>> using "Roman Numerals" before Caesar's time.

Further off topic from an off-topic thread:  Why did you change "claim" to
"[assert]"?  After reviewing the definitions in the two dictionary's on my
desk, I conclude the better choice is "assert", but "claim" is certainly
correct.  In Britannica-Webster the second sense is "to state as fact :
maintain".  In American Heritage the second sense is "to state to be true;
assert or maintain".  I was correctly using "claim" in this sense.  Are
you claiming "claim" has some pejorative, demeaning or disrespectful
color, or are you only asserting "assert" is better?  (This last sentence
is an example where superior word choice is dictated by humorous
juxtaposition, rather than color of meaning.)

I frequently criticize people's English, with the intent of improving
communication.  Now that you have brought this to my attention, I will be
more aware of these words, but if I were to criticize word choices of this
magnitude, I would have no time to do anything else.  I infer that you
find my communicative skills so advanced that it could best be improved by
better discrimination of the words "claim" and "assert".  I take that as a
compliment.

>With respect specifically to subtraction, "IX" coexisted happily with
>"VIIII" over vast spans of time and space because "IX" is the "literary"
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>it.

The more I think about it, the more likely that you are right.  It is
quite likely that bookkeepers used strictly bi-quinary notation, because
of it's simplicity.  It is therefore likely that I have this backwards on
my page at http://users.aol.com/PanicYr00/MCMXCIX.html.  But when I
correct my page I want to get his right.  Do you have a reference, such as
a web site with genuine pictures of monuments and ledger books and letters
home to Mater?  

>The concept of a complete and
>nonredundant mathematical system is quite modern, and even today there
>are few who care whether a given system is complete and nonredundant or
>not.

As I reflect on this, our numbering system has a few traces of redundancy
left, but only involving decimal points, commas, and leading zeros.  When
is it correct to use a comma?  I had a teacher say it should be used in
every number one thousand or greater, yet the comma is very rarely used
for the years 9999 BC to 9999 AD.  

And we do have font variations:  Is the 4 open or closed, is th 9 straight
or curly.  In many countries we prefer one form when written by hand, and
another form written by machine.

>  The system of "Roman" numerals from 1850 that I posted is a modern
>system in this sense, but none of the Roman numerals used by the
>historical Romans is.

What is the reference from 1850?

>This is really tough to discuss in the ASCII character set.  Remember
>that when we use the ASCII character set, Roman numerals come out
>automatically in the literary form, e.g., III.  The bookkeeping form does
>not have serifs, e.g. |||.

Correct.

>I translated the word "viniculum" as "overbar".  The viniculum is part of
>"Roman" numerals; it does not occur in Roman numerals.  The single stroke
>over all the digits that you mention does occur in Roman numerals, but it
>does not change the value.  It's kind of a grouping thing, like the comma
>between groups of three decimal digits.

No, I'm not refering to running the serifs together to group the numbers.
AIUI, the vinculum was written as a single stroke over all the digits.  If
your reference is from 1850, perhaps printing the vinculum over each digit
is a typesetting convention.  

>The original Roman numerals are not letters.

Correct.

>As I mentioned, Romans did not use the viniculum at all.  

When was it first used?

>Whew!  If you are really interested in this, you might enjoy "Die
>Zahlzeichen und das elementare Rechnen der Griechen und R"omer" by G.
>Friedlein (Erlangen, 1869).

Haben Sie eine Buch auf English?  

>Briefly, the Greek literary system uses
> a vertical stroke for one
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> M for ten thousand (mupioi, English "myriad")

Didn't know that one.

>The Greek bookkeeping system starts with three cycles:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>    hundreds RHO   SIGMA TAU    UPSILON PHI     CHI PSI     OMEGA SAMPI

That one I had heard of.  The classic Greek alphabet has only twenty-four
letters, this has three more:  VAU, QOPH, and SAMPI.  QOPH must be Q, and
one of the others (most likely VAU) must be di-gamma (F), but what does
the third character look like?

>Thousands are expressed by a bar to the left, as |K = 20,000.
>
>                                            K
>Myriads are expressed by a symbol above, as M = 200,000.

This is mixing K = 20 from one system with M = 10,000 from the other,
right?

>It's no wonder that so many Romans
>kept their books in Greek numerals.

The Greek bookkeeping system seems the easiest to me.  You need to learn
the alphabet anyway.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Roman Numerals revisited

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** ttate@my-dejanews.com

```
'Twas Thu, 14 Jan 1999 19:33:42 GMT, when ttate@my-dejanews.com
illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>In article <369ced51.91062738@netnews>,
>  Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart) wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Maybe you should criticize their apostrophe usage as well.

So true.  I've always had a homonym problem.  It got worse when I started
using St John's Wort about a year ago, and it's still bugging me.  I keep
typing "they're" for "there" and "hear" for "here" and in this case
"dictionary's" for "dictionaries" and "it's" for "its".  That isn't quite
as stupid looking as "of" for "have", but the context always makes an
incorrect use of "dictionary" quite noticeable.

"People's" is correct, nicht wahr?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Roman Numerals revisited

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369F6226.53E5616C@home.com>`
- **References:** `<qlnk2.656$EC3.507828@news3.mia> <1999Jan8.174834.19474@giant> <3696ab00.27168211@netnews> <1999Jan12.113207.19478@giant> <369ced51.91062738@netnews> <77lgql$gpt$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <369eab67.25940198@netnews>`

```
> So true.  I've always had a homonym problem.  It got worse when I started
> using St John's Wort about a year ago, and it's still bugging me.  I keep
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> incorrect use of "dictionary" quite noticeable.

That's the type of typos which bother me the most, because I read it as
written and then have to go back and re-read when it doesn't make sense.

I always assumed that programmers would be less likely to make these
errors - at least when the homonym uses apostrophes, because apostrophes
use rules.  e.g. "If your word is a contraction, it needs an
apostrophe."

On the other hand, at home, I will post on a MOVIES NG, and people
express amazement when I misspell a person's name - after all, the
actor's names are written in 10' high letters!!!   But as a programmer,
I don't see any logic is the difference in how H.G. Wells and Orson
Welles spell their name.  I need the logic.

But there is a break in the logic for possessive.  "John's", "the
Broncos'", follow one pattern, but "its" and "his" don't.

I also use programming logic to misplace periods inside of quotes. 
Actually, I would like this next line to be correct:  Joe said "I am
ready now.".
At least English doesn't allow double negatives!!

I always mispronounced "prerogative", so when my spell checker couldn't
find it, I tried finding it in the dictionary.  I never thought that the
second letter was an "R", and couldn't find it.  So I tried a half dozen
programs until one recognized my spelling and gave me an option.  Now I
think that "pre" makes perfect sense.  Because it is logical, I will
remember it, but if I wasn't able to find a rule, I would not remember
it.
```

```
change things that are correct.  I always use it on posting,
my typing is atrocious, however I find that I override it
at least 50% of the time.

The mistakes that always strike me as silly are the
common sayings that people misuse. For example,
a response like "here here".  I always wonder if they
understand the language at all.
```

```
Donald Tees wrote:

> The mistakes that always strike me as silly are the
> common sayings that people misuse. For example,
> a response like "here here".  I always wonder if they
> understand the language at all.

Now that's an example which I have read contradicting meanings from
"experts".  Most Americans only know of that expression when we see a
movie with a scene in the British Parliament.   Since the experts
disagree, I simply try to avoid writing it.

Too bad we can't set up spell checkers to multiple word phrases. 
Convert "Here here" to "Hear here" (or whatever we decide is correct). 
To look at the first name before deciding whether Wells or Welles is
correct would be nice.   And then when it questions our choice, it can
ask me "Do you mean the guy who wrote the WAR OF THE WORDS book, or the
one who wrote WAR OF THE WORLDS radio adaptation?"

Foreign phrases should be included also.  Bartlets should be available
```

```
As our teacher once told us when I was trying to learn English

Is it, or isn't it??   It's is not, it isn't ain't, and it's it's, not its, if you mean it
is.  If you don't, it's its.  Then too, it's hers.  It isn't her's.  It isn't our's
either.  It's ours, and likewise yours and theirs!


I'll never forget that one
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Roman Numerals revisited

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369F5F13.FAD9849D@home.com>`
- **References:** `<qlnk2.656$EC3.507828@news3.mia> <1999Jan8.174834.19474@giant> <3696ab00.27168211@netnews> <1999Jan12.113207.19478@giant> <369ced51.91062738@netnews>`

```
> And we do have font variations:  Is the 4 open or closed, is th 9 straight
> or curly.  In many countries we prefer one form when written by hand, and
> another form written by machine.

I always assumed the crossed 7 was for clarity sake - until I read that
each Arabic character has the number of angles (< 90%) corresponding to
its value.  I love this story, but I have no idea whether it is true or
not.  I wish I knew whether or not it was the origen or something
created after the fact.

0: has zero angles
1: is drawn sort of like a sloppy 7, with an angle at the top
2: is drawn like a Z
3: is drawn like a pair of vertical "]"
4: is closed but not crossed.  The triangle has 3 angles, and one below
5: is drawn with all straight lines
6: is just like the 5, but the bottom square is closed
7: has a cross bar which looks like a "not" sign.  Also the top cross
bar has a descender
8: Looks like two squares
9: Looks like the 8, except the end curls around to get the extra angle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Alphabetical Order

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36b0fee2.11993423@netnews>`
- **References:** `<qlnk2.656$EC3.507828@news3.mia> <1999Jan8.174834.19474@giant> <3696ab00.27168211@netnews> <1999Jan12.113207.19478@giant> <369ced51.91062738@netnews>`

```
'Twas 13 Jan 1999 23:24:44 GMT, when Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>'Twas 12 Jan 99 11:32:07 EST, when cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris
>Westbury) illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>>The Greek bookkeeping system starts with three cycles:
>>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>    hundreds RHO   SIGMA TAU    UPSILON PHI     CHI PSI     OMEGA SAMPI

I'm still wondering on this one.  The classic Greek Alphabet has 24
letters.  By virtue of name and position in the Latin Alphabet I'm
concluding that vau is F and qoph is Q, but what is SAMPI?  There's no
Latin letter unaccounted for.  Is it a glottal stop?  What does the symbol
look like?

I had two more questions, but on reflection they've answered each other:
Since the Latin letters C and G are descended from the same character, why
aren't they adjacent in alphabetical order, like I-J, and U-V-W?   
AND
Which came first, the use of letters as numbers, or alphabetical order?

I think I've answered both questions.  The Latin Alphabet is 26 letters.
If we add a 27th between N and O (the xi between nu and omicron) there is
an amazing correspondence with the chart above, all the way thru the
letter upsilon.  The Roman Alphabet cam from Etruscan which came from
Carthaginian which came from Phoenician.  The Greek Alphabet came from
Phoenician by a different route.  I'm simplifying and assuming a lot here,
but for the sake of argument let's call the chart above "original"
Phoenician.  On the way to becoming the Latin Alphabet, some old letters
were dropped, and old letters were dissimilated into multiple forms.  Zeta
wasn't used in Latin, so it was tossed aside to make room for G.  Theta
was dropped and iota split into I and J.  Upsilon split into U, V, and W,
pushing out some little used characters.  Then Greek literature was
translated into Latin, and X, Y and Z were added at the end.

So clearly the use of letters for numbers pre-dates alphabetical order.
If alphabetical order had come first, C anf G would be adjacent.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Roman Numerals revisited

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369A0BA3.C2A5EA5F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<qlnk2.656$EC3.507828@news3.mia> <1999Jan8.174834.19474@giant>`

```
Is there any evidence that people who put dates on buildings and movies
are starting to come to their senses and use Arabic numerals?

I know programmers and lawyers who like to obfuscate to make their work
look fancy - but this is bad business practice.  If your desire is to
communicate a date - do so so that the reader doesn't have to stop and
translate.  If your desire is to be pretty, put a flower on that stone,
not a date.
```

```
>The year 2000 in Roman numerals is blessedly simple:

More on this diversion -- as a horologist specialising in antique
watches, I will comment that it is generally regarded as a matter of
symmetry that the Roman (4) is written IIII on clock and watch dials,
to counterbalance the bulky nature of the (8) VIII.
Best wishes  for MM
```

```
If you are using Windows95, take a look at MarshalSoft.  I do
not have the URL, handy, but a search should give you the web
page.  I am using their routines with Fujitsu V4, and am quite
happy with them.

Mike Kelly wrote in message <369254AE.DFB9E41A@guardco.com>...
>Can anyone tell me if Cobol supports serial communications? If so, how?
>
>Mike
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Serial Communications

- **From:** tom@taltech.com (Thomas Lutz)
- **Date:** 1999-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36a1289d.33193832@news.p3.net>`
- **References:** `<369254AE.DFB9E41A@guardco.com>`

```
From what I have been seeing in the newsgroups, Cobol does not support
Serial I/O directly however you may be aboe to do things indirectly.

The company that I work for sells a serial communications program
called the Software Wedge that you may find to be a good tool for
adding serial communications capabilities to your application. The
Software Wedge is an executable program that can pass serial data back
and forth to other programs using either DDE (Dynamic Data Exchange)
or by converting incoming serial data to keystrokes (i.e. it stuffs
the keyboard buffer with the incoming serial data).
The program is extremely easy to use and is designed to have you up
and running sending and receiving serial data directly from within
your application in just a few minutes.
Please visit http://www.taltech.com for more information.


On Tue, 05 Jan 1999 18:03:47 GMT, Mike Kelly <mike737@guardco.com>
wrote:

>Can anyone tell me if Cobol supports serial communications? If so, how?
>
>Mike
```

### Help I'm New

- **Message-ID:** `<76t4ku$1qa$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Hi, I'm about to start Cobol from scratch. I've started with "Cobol for
Dummies" but want to know what software I need or what you recommend. I know I
need a compiler but what else?

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Help I'm New

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pFsk2.413$A76.643281@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<76t4ku$1qa$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
richard@clowery.freeserve.co.uk wrote:
>Hi, I'm about to start Cobol from scratch. I've started with "Cobol for
>Dummies" but want to know what software I need or what you recommend.
>I know I need a compiler but what else?

If you bought COBOL for Dummies you already have several COBOL compilers
and interpreters on the CD which comes with the book.  Beyond that, you
need knowledge and understanding.  Study to get the knowledge, practice
to get the understanding.  If you have specific questions, ask them here.

If you want a good COBOL tutorial, get "Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours"
by Thane Hubbell.  $20 US from www.amazon.com.  It includes Fujitsu COBOL
3 on the CD, and all the example code compiles and runs using it.  If you
don't like it, you can complain to the author, who often frequents this
newsgroup. :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: Help I'm New

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1999-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369263f5.118968387@news.enterprise.net>`
- **References:** `<76t4ku$1qa$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

wrote:

>Hi, I'm about to start Cobol from scratch. I've started with "Cobol for
>Dummies" but want to know what software I need or what you recommend. I know I
>need a compiler but what else?

Have to say I wouldn't reccomend that book. It's terrible and teaches
bad habits IMHO. Very old coding style. Burn it now ;-)

Try Thanes's book or COBOL Unleashed instead. Both of which you should
be able to get from amazon.co.uk. Don Nelson (who frequents this group
too) may still have some copies of his COBOL-85 book which although
isn't really a beginners book is a good one. It's what I learn't ANSI
85 out of anyway.
```

```
server programs) from C or Java using MicroFocus COBOL for Solaris Sparc?  I
```

#### ↳ Re: java/C to COBOL question

- **From:** Stephen Gennard <spg@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3692181C.B4D1902B@mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<76sm9d$ofa$1@hercules.spirit.net.au>`

```
Hi Robert,

Unfortunately the current Micro Focus Unix products are not thread safe,
which is a prerequisite for Java/COBOL integration.  This combined with
the lack of support for callable shared objects in the current Object COBOL
developers suite means you will not able to-do this using JNI. 

However you could use sockets or a similar transport mechanism, if COBOL
and Java were in separate processes.

I would recommend contacting your Micro Focus sales person and ask them
about the next generation of Unix products that will be available as it
may have some features you are interested in.

Robert Masters wrote:
> 
> Hi all, Is it possible to call COBOL shared objects (effectively business
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Java Analyst/Developer
```

```
regarding the Java and C problems. Fujitsu COBOL 4.0 is written in the same

in meeting your needs.

Gary Roush
```

<a id="t-23a068b700"></a>
## Thread: comp/comp3 numbers in Perl or maybe C — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### comp/comp3 numbers in Perl or maybe C

- **From:** aixgod@ix.netcom.com
- **Date:** 1999-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.perl.misc,comp.lang.c
- **Message-ID:** `<76tolm$kh2$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I'm trying to write out a data file from Perl (on unix) that the mainframe
guys are telling me needs to have a "comp3" number in it (I think this is
also called a Cobol signed comp-3 number maybe).  What I'm encoding is
actually a date listed as "julian packed" on my little sheet of info here,
for that's worth.  Clearly, I have little idea what these numbers are or how
to make one....I'm just throwing this post out here in hopes that someone has
some code that will do the conversion.	I'd like to write this number out
from Perl, but I'll use C if necessary.

Additionally, for a separate file that will eventually be loaded into DB2 on
the mainframe, I need to write out a normal "comp" number (not comp3),
whatever that is.  Any help here would be appreciated to.

I'm hoping that if I select binary format on the ftp to the mainframe that
none of this data will get distorted or converted on the way up....

Thanks for any assistance!
Dylan Tynan

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: comp/comp3 numbers in Perl or maybe C

- **From:** JBHenry@excite.com
- **Date:** 1999-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.perl.misc,comp.lang.c
- **Message-ID:** `<77058f$pcm$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<76tolm$kh2$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In article <76tolm$kh2$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>,
  aixgod@ix.netcom.com wrote:
>I'm trying to write out a data file from Perl (on unix) that the mainframe
>guys are telling me needs to have a "comp3" number in it (I think this is
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>from Perl, but I'll use C if necessary.

A "COMP-3" number in Cobol is a binary coded decimal integer. Basically,
each digit of your number is "packed" into 4 bits (nyble) of the COMP-3
number, with the last nyble becoming the sign. For example, converting
the number 1234 would give you 0x01234F (in hexidecimal). Hex 'F' or 'C'
are used for a positive sign, and 'D' is used for negative. Note the
extra leading 0 fill it out to a full byte. Your mainframer's will
probably expect a field of a certain size, so you may have to do further
pading. This should be pretty easy to do using perl's pack and unpack
functions with the 'H' format. Sorry I don't have any example code
handy.
By "julian packed" they probably mean something in the format YYDDD or
YYYYDDD. This will pack nicely into a 3 or 4 byte COMP-3.

>Additionally, for a separate file that will eventually be loaded into DB2 on
>the mainframe, I need to write out a normal "comp" number (not comp3),
>whatever that is.  Any help here would be appreciated to.

A "COMP" in Cobol is just a binary integer, like an int or long in C.
Again, pack and unpack will be your friends.

>I'm hoping that if I select binary format on the ftp to the mainframe that
>none of this data will get distorted or converted on the way up....

Shouldn't be a problem, but remember that if you use binary mode, all of
your text data will _not_ get translated from ASCII to EBCDIC. The m/f
probably expects EBCDIC for everything. Looks like you might be stuck
doing your own translation.

>Thanks for any assistance!
>Dylan Tynan
>
I hope this helps, and good luck!

Jeff Henry.

P.S. Please don't tell anybody that I acted like I know COBOL! :-)

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: comp/comp3 numbers in Perl or maybe C

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.perl.misc,comp.lang.c

- **References:** `<76tolm$kh2$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <77058f$pcm$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
For those who are about to scream about the answer that was given by Jeff

    BUT ALL THE COMP values are HARDWARE/SOFTWARE/COMPILER dependent,

Please notice the original reference to "DB2" which makes the assumption
that the "mainframe" is an "IBM mainframe" fairly safe.

Note to original requester:  If you are *NOT* talking about an IBM
mainframe, then the answers given below may (and probably ARE) *wrong*

FYI,
  The response given for "COMP" (not COMP-3) is a little incomplete.  There
are methods in IBM mainframe COBOL of specifying COMP fields that create
half-word, full-word, or double-word binary fields (and with many PC
products single-byte binary fields - but if you get into PC's, then you need
to worry about big-endian/little-endian issues as well).
```

```
Wow, I knew someone was going to be able to help me with this - you guys
rule.  Ok, I'm guilty of not giving enough information up front.

I'm starting on AIX version 4 on an RS/6000 (which is PowerPC 604 I think).
I'll make my data file from there using Perl (the pack function) and then
I'll ftp it up to the mainframe, which is an Amdahl.  I believe it's running
OS/390. I think PowerPC chips support either little or big endian operation
(maybe) so I don't really know what AIX does....I think it's big endian but
I'm not sure.

Now, obviously I can get ftp to translate to EBCDIC for me but will that mess
up my packed numbers?  The other option is to use a Perl module to translate
into EBCDIC in advance and then send up in binary mode...pretty sure I can do
that.  Opinions?

From Pack in Perl it looks like I give some type of format field specifier
(sequence of characters).  I see that 'h' represents a hex string, low nybble
first while 'H' is high nybble first.  From one of the earlier posts it sounds
like I use 'H' for generating the comp3 number?  What do I use for the regular
comp number?  FYI, the comp numbers are actually just variable field length
specifiers in a data file (so it might 12 chars on one line representing a
name, then 15 char on the next, etc.).  I guess DB2's load facility wants it
like that.  I'm gonna try and give this a shot even though I'm in over my head
on this...

Thanks again,
Dylan
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.perl.misc,comp.lang.c
- **Message-ID:** `<7757ob$gt8$1@callisto.clark.net>`
- **References:** `<76tolm$kh2$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <770851$ct6@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <7729lv$kqp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <773h41$o4c$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In article <773h41$o4c$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>,  <aixgod@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
>Wow, I knew someone was going to be able to help me with this - you guys
>rule.  Ok, I'm guilty of not giving enough information up front.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>I'm not sure.

Hold it right there, please.  Translating packed/binary numbers across
platforms is an... intricate exercise, to say the least; some of the
comments you've received here might have indicated this.

Translating display numerics (X'F0' - 'X'F9' in EBCDIC, X'30' - X'39') is
child's play and it is likewise an elementary exercise to write a program
to 'comp down' fields; this leads to the sugesstion of:

AIX side:

Extract data.
Convert data to display numeric format with sign indicator, leading or
trailing.

FTP file, AIX ==> Amdahl

Amdahl side:

Convert data from display numeric w/ sign indicator to desired packing
method.
Program/utility to load data appropriately.

If, however, there is resistance - and there are both strong reasons and
traditions why there might be - on the mainframe side to structuring the
job in this manner then you need someone in a corner office to say 'Do
This'.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comp/comp3 numbers in Perl or maybe C

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1999-01-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990108173555.01162.00009106@ng-cc1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7757ob$gt8$1@callisto.clark.net>`

```
DD has it.

When transfering cross platform, transfer in text mode. The sign should be
separate also. Don't send anything BINARY (comp) or PACKED (comp-3).  It is
very surprising how many times folks will leave off the separate sign.

Generally transmit in ASCII, but that can be a hard decision if you are bring
it into major online software like IMS or CICS as a transaction.  

If you are transfering a file, there are actually ways to get the mainframe to
read an ASCII file and present it to your program as EBCDIC.  (You have to
convince a batch program it is reading an ASCII tape!). But it is no major
problem in COBOL to convert the thing to ASCII yourself.

In UNIX environments you do have to be concerned about the notion of
white-space. Generally, you do not want the mainframe to be dealing with tabs
or form feeds or vertical tabs or newlines.  So hidden in this task is the
_possible_ need to generate a fixed length file on the UNIX side.  If you make
this a line sequential file, somewhere you will have to expand the UNIX newline
to a CR/LF sequence for the mainframe.

But really life will be easier on the Amdahl if you similate a fixed length
file (which to run the risk of confusing terms, will necessitate a binary file
on the PC as opposed to a text file).

On your text data be very careful of long quoted strings with lots of spaces. 
They can look like spaces as you view your edit session for the Perl script,
but the editor can generate tabs as the script file is saved.  ((It is a good
idea to have a look at the generated files in hex, at either end of the
transmission the first few times through)).

The auto tabbing problem can also apply to numerics that might be expressed as
quoted data, as in
     "987654"
     "     321" 
     "       13"
Your editor can turn those spaces into tabs.
If you are expresing numerics without quotes, then you have to have some kind
of format expression to align the data to a fixed format.

If you are up to speed on tabs, and know how to control your editor you won't
need this suggestion: but sometimes you need to generate data like "###321" and
"ANY#WORD###", and at some later point CONVERT the special character to spaces.

It is not a bad idea ot put a little extra bagage on your records when
transfering across platforms. Put maybe "BEG" in bytes 1-2-3, and "END" in the
last three bytes.  And a header record and trailer record are very nice ideas. 
The trailer might could have a record count. Check every record for integrity. 
But I bet you have thought of that stuff.  A lot can happen when you do this
transfer and these little shepard can save you a lot of time because they make
problem obvious. You can eventually weed them out if transmission time or file
size is an issue.


For your information the PowerPC 604 and the mainframe (IBM or Plug Compatible)
are both Big Endian. (The PPC can go otherwise but not so in AIX).  But don't
transmit in binary.

Oh by the way, and again I bet you all have work this out already,  the PC is
an environment where shifting case is easy.  Generally, the mainframe does
better with upper case only.  Your translator may need a hook to clean that up
(which actually argues for source code  translation, rather than JCL
parameter). EBCDIC 
does have lower case, but the traditions are different in the mainframe world. 
(A tiny thing that can burn you for example, is that some of the 3270 hardware
and emulators will display lower case as upper case. So you will think you have
it all upper case as you try to diagnose it with a browser on the mainframe,
whereas the data may be mixed or all lower case).

So anyhow, transmit text and CONVERT on the mainframe. Transmit signs as
SEPARATE character.  Probably transmit a fixed record length file.  Beware of
tabulation endemic to PC and infectious to mainframe.  And determine if you
need all upper case characters.

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

## Thread: WinExec API won't run on WinNT — 3 msgs

### WinExec API won't run on WinNT

- **From:** "Tom Wright" <twright@larimore.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<i1uk2.984$x5.18654456@news1.i1.net>`

```
I've got a problem.  I wrote a cobol program using Micro Focus Cobol WB
4.XXX.  It allows us to run any application that's executable.  example:
notepad.exe or test.bat.  This works fine under Windoes 95 yet, I can't seem
to get it to work under WinNT4.0 workstation.  Do I need to use a different
API call?

Someone suggested that I try "CreateProcess" but I'm having trouble getting
that to work.
Any helpful hints would be appreciated.

Tom Wright
twright@larimore.net
```

#### ↳ Re: WinExec API won't run on WinNT

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gw@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76vanu$ha4@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<i1uk2.984$x5.18654456@news1.i1.net>`

```

Tom Wright wrote in message ...
>I've got a problem.  I wrote a cobol program using Micro Focus Cobol WB
>4.XXX.  It allows us to run any application that's executable.  example:
>notepad.exe or test.bat.  This works fine under Windoes 95 yet, I can't
seem
>to get it to work under WinNT4.0 workstation.  Do I need to use a different
>API call?

Tom, you should be able to use the same API on both NT and Win95.  I tried a
simple demo, which I have attached below, and it worked fine so it may be
worth checking it against your own code to see if there is a difference.

I hope it helps.


>
>Someone suggested that I try "CreateProcess" but I'm having trouble getting
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

 special-names.
     call-convention 74 is win32.
 data division.
 01 cmd pic x(80) value z"notepad.exe".
 procedure division.
     call "cob32api"
     call win32 "WinExec" using cmd
                                                     by value 1
     stop run.
```

```
Tom,

Note for example that directory names could be different on the two systems.

What error do you get?

Robert Rayhawk
```

```
Could you use program id + date + time?

as in

	PGMNAMYYMMDDHHMMSS

Unix allows large file names.  Another approach would be to have a
file that holds a NEXT-RPT-NUMBER field.  Make the number 9(9) and you
will probably not run out for a while.  Then make your file name
	PGMNAM + NXT-RPT-NUMBER
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Getting Process id in MFCOBOL under UNIX

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <Kevin.Digweed@dial.pipex.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3693722E.B9334FFE@dial.pipex.com>`
- **References:** `<76rnr5$tlh$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <3692962b.1374021@news.enteract.com> <76ti42$edu$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
shanmugam@my-dejanews.com wrote:
> 
> I have to create print spool files having file name as program-id +
> process-id. When you execute a cobol program, it should not overwrite old
> file. Also the file manager is not MFCOBOL one, it is a proprietory one.

You can call any C function directly from COBOL, so there is no
reason you can't use the getpid() call. Obviously, you need to make
sure your parameter and return types are correct when porting (as you
cannot just include a system header file as you could if you were
coding in C - perhaps writing a small C wrapper function would give
you a more portable solution).

However, given your stated intent (building an output filename which
"should not overwrite old file") you must be very careful. The
allocation of process ID's is dependant on the OS you are running on.
Some operating systems will re-use low process id's as they become
available - it will not wait for the PID to "wrap". [This was certainly
the case for some versions of AIX, AFAIR]. This will greatly
increase the possibility of your file being overwritten.

A better solution would be to either use date/time information, as
suggested by someone else, or to use the system tmpnam() or
tempnam() functions which provide exactly this functionality. Again,
a small C wrapper around this function might give you the best/most
portable solution for use from COBOL.

Cheers, Kev.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Getting Process id in MFCOBOL under UNIX

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** g1dlc@Radix.Net (David L. Craig)
- **Date:** 1999-01-06T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<770288$3bm$1@saltmine.radix.net>`

```
In article <3693722E.B9334FFE@dial.pipex.com>,
Kevin Digweed  <Kevin.Digweed@dial.pipex.com> wrote:
>
>A better solution would be to either use date/time information, as
>suggested by someone else, or to use the system tmpnam() or
>tempnam() functions which provide exactly this functionality.

Also look at mktemp(3).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Getting Process id in MFCOBOL under UNIX

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <Kevin.Digweed@dial.pipex.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3693A85A.AF5438AF@dial.pipex.com>`
- **References:** `<76rnr5$tlh$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <3692962b.1374021@news.enteract.com> <76ti42$edu$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <3693722E.B9334FFE@dial.pipex.com> <770288$3bm$1@saltmine.radix.net>`

```
David L. Craig wrote:
> 
> In article <3693722E.B9334FFE@dial.pipex.com>,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Also look at mktemp(3).

I'm not as keen to recommend mktemp() as it's (a) not ANSI and (b) is
more likely to "use up" the available namespace - at least from reading
the docs I have, the variable part of the name returned is composed of
a single letter and a PID. If running on an OS where the PID reuse
problem is an issue, it depends on what constitutes "a letter" as to
how many unique filenames can be created, assuming the COBOL process
is repeatedly allocated the same PID.

I agree that it's better than just the PGMID/PID combination, though.

Cheers, Kev.
```

## Thread: fujitsu cobol v.3 — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### fujitsu cobol v.3

- **From:** "Mark Reid" <markmreid@tinet.ie>
- **Date:** 1999-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76u54j$ski$1@spock.tinet.ie>`

```
I'm using fujitsu cobol v.3, and when trying to open a new program, the
format for writing it had changed from the normal cobol screen to a notepad
screen.  Any ideas as to why this has happened, and how can I change it
back? CK
```

#### ↳ Re: fujitsu cobol v.3

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-n7hWWJQVKo5v@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<76u54j$ski$1@spock.tinet.ie>`

```
On Tue, 5 Jan 1999 22:52:12, "Mark Reid" <markmreid@tinet.ie> wrote:

> I'm using fujitsu cobol v.3, and when trying to open a new program, the
> format for writing it had changed from the normal cobol screen to a notepad
> screen.  Any ideas as to why this has happened, and how can I change it
> back? CK

check to see if you changed the default editor.  It's under:  File, 
Customize Editor, 

It should have: (obviously C:\FSC\ should be your install path)

C:\FSC\PCOBOL32\F3BXE000.EXE /pstaff
```

<a id="t-1936143dca"></a>
## Thread: 04E Error — 1 this month / 6 total

_Thread started [1998-12](1998-12.md#t-1936143dca)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: 04E Error

- **From:** "gh029@ghit.co.uk" <gh029@ghit.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.databases.ibm-db2,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3692ED1A.6E64@ghit.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<01be2f3a$1645e2c0$60173d03@FLW0302396> <75ucpm$ad0$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
mark_arnold@mailexcite.com wrote:
> 
> In article <01be2f3a$1645e2c0$60173d03@FLW0302396>,
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> >


s04E is a generic "DB2 had a problem" abend code, which could be caused
by any one of a thousand reasons.  

DB2 normally puts out more helpful messages pointing at the exact cause,
either in the job itself and/or in one of the DB2 started tasks
(xxxxIRLM, xxxxMSTR, xxxxDBM1 jobnames) and/or in the syslog.  Armed
with the appropriate Messages and Codes manual and a suitably warped
brain you should be able to work out what went wrong.

Some problems are transitory, so you may already have found that
rerunning the job cures the problem.  As Mark Arnold says, it's not
normally caused by application code, so try palming off responsibility
to someone else!

Happy hunting

Charles Gaskell
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: On 1

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76uek8$mpa@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36926F02.A100C5EB@home.com> <369354F1.D74@idis.com.au> <76u1j5$eea@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <36936E88.6064@idis.com.au>`

```

Chris Carroll wrote in message <36936E88.6064@idis.com.au>...
>Interesting. I once tried at a site some four years ago on a AT&T UNIX
>box using MF thinking I would save some typing for a quick and dirty
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Make certain that you have the OSVS directive turned on (as well as any
others that you want).  By the way, this also allows you to have (as
extensions) in ANS'85 code:

   TRANSFORM
    EXHIBIT
    READY/RESET TRACE

and several other "oldies but semi-goodies"

As I recall, the only thing that you will "lose" is some combinations or
pemuations of combined abreviated conditions with parenthesis and reference
modification (but this is all from quite vague memory - check your LRM
"bubbles" for correct support).

>
>William M. Klein wrote:
>>
>> Don't let anyone at Micro Focus here me saying this, but it is fully
>> supported with all Micro Focus products (even when running in ANS'85
modes).
>> To anyone who like "truly structured" code, this thing is a nightmare!
>>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-2e777fc8fa"></a>
## Thread: LE, COBOL, CICS, Y2K and related topics — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### LE, COBOL, CICS, Y2K and related topics

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000.tech
- **Message-ID:** `<76uhv6$2ga@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
FYI, (aimed particularly at IBM mainframers)
  I just put out the following in the IBM-MAIN newsgroup(list-server)

    *****

At the end of last year (1998) there was a thread that started out
concerning LE 40xx ABEND codes.  It got a lot of "recommendations" - some of
which I agree with and some I don't.  I just thought that I would put out MY
(not IBM's) recommendations - and see how much attacking I get. (Most of
this is common sense, already well known, or familiar to many readers of
IBM-MAIN - but given some of the comments that I read last month, I thought
I would put out this summary anyway.)

1) If you have lots of OS/VS COBOL programs running in production and
haven't moved to LE (or OS/390) yet (probably don't even have a Y2K plan in
effect yet), please do NOT try to "jump" to IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM (with
or without MLE) and LE quickly.  Especially, do NOT recompile (or even
re-link) existing COBOL programs that are running just fine in production
now. (unless you know they need specific Y2K remediation). DO plan on
upgrading your run-time environment long (or as long as you have now) before
you upgrade your compilers.

2) If you have existing OS/VS COBOL or VS COBOL II *nores* applications
running in production now and they (and their subprograms) do not have
specific Y2K problems, don't change, recompile, or re-link them either.

3) If you are using the VS COBOL II *MIXRES* option, plan on a lot of
research to move to a reliable environment (whether or not your programs are
Y2K compliant).  If you are running with RES, then with a lot of prayer and
as much testing as possible, your applications SHOULD move to LE with
minimal difficulty (and need not be recompiled - unless you have specific
Y2K changes required).

4) If you can (i.e. you have already done some LEARNING, planning, and
testing) do try and get to an LE run-time environment.  This means LE (and
*NO* other libraries - no concatenation) in your Linklist.  Clearly this is
what OS/390 (all releases) thinks is "optimal" and this will be the "nicest"
(cleanest?) environment for everything else.

5) You can run with LE in the Linklist and Steplib to VS COBOL II or run
with VS COBOL II in the Linklist and (for most - but not all applications)
run with LE steplibed.  This works OK, you only get in trouble when try
concatenating the two libraries either in a steplib or a Linklist.  (P.S.
Do understand the order for CICS run-time libraries as they related to the
"common" run-time libraries.  This is true for both LE and VS COBOL II
run-times)

6) If you have one release of LE and are looking at (particularly for
OS/390 - especially TCP/IP -reasons) needing another release of LE
available, do look at RTLS (documented in the current LE manuals).  It isn't
perfect but it is better than nothing (which - by the way is what you have
under CICS - where RTLS isn't working).

7) Whether you are looking at in-house or contracted services, don't let
ANYONE tell you that you must recompile ALL *cobol* programs (no matter how
down-level they are).

8) Do look at the Edge Portfolio product if you are just now beginning (or
even in the middle) of working on Y2K remediation.  You may well be
surprised at what you have (and don't have) running in production.
(Normally, I would say "Edge or any other comparable product" - but I don't
know of any that are comparable.)

9) If you haven't started moving all your Y2K applications into production
yet, please, PLEASE, at least check out the lists of Y2K compliant "vendor"
products.  You have got to be on the right levels of CICS, DB2, IMS,
DFSort/SyncSort, Abend-Aid, etc, ETC - or it won't matter how much
"application remediation" you have done.  By the way, did I mention a Y2K
compliant OS running on hardware that works for Y2K?

10) Get as much training as you need and/or get contracted support in house
(AND training to take over when they leave).  LE is NOT easy.  Newer
releases of COBOL, CICS, and DB2 are NOT easy.  Just because the
installation IVP works, doesn't mean that your production will run (possibly
not even walk).

  *****

Bottom-Line: (IMHO)

If you still have OS/VS COBOL production programs and are not well along on
your Y2K remediation, there are two equally reliable solutions available to
you:

A) Pray a WHOLE lot

B) Concentrate on inventing a time-machine so you can go back 5 to 10
years - so your company will start working on these when they should have!
```

```
> Thane is indeed an impressive guy, but he doesn't prefer Cessnas, he

> fuselage as opposed to OVER the fuselage...although if someone forced
> me to get in, I guess I could tolerate a Citabria.

> Somehow it just seems less likely that the wings are going to fold UP
> than the ones which are built over the head....but then again, I'm
> persnickety about things like that.

Mooney's what?   I loved the only Mooney I ever flew, but a higher wing
is LESS likely to fold up because it is less likely to hit the ground! 
I can't see it making any difference otherwise.  Hmmm, if the design is
between one single wing vs two separate wings, it could, but it seems
that could favor the high wing.

Passengers like high wings for the shade and view.  But I prefer a low
wing because when I want to look to the left, I dip my wing.  With a low
wing, it gets out of my way, with a high wing, it gets in my way!
```

```
Heheheh.... 

I love the Mooney.  One interesting thing is that the spar is SINGLE -
not two pieces like most high wings and low wings - and it's milled
from a single piece of aluminium(sp).  I like the picture of all the
people standing ON the wing from root to tip.  Thats one strong wing.
It's a wierd bird to fly for many though.  I specialized in transition
training to the Mooney.  I loved the reaction on flap extension - what
do you mean the nose pitches DOWN?  It's a slick, fast, strong
airplane.  I used to rent a 252TSE - and I instructed for a time in a
180C - so I've seen the whole gamet - sat in a Mustang once and a TLS
- but never flew either.   My dream Mooney would be the 252 TSE - now
called the Encore - with TKS deicing (my flying popsicle story is for
another time).

If I can't have a mooney - I'll take a Wing Derringer.
```

<a id="t-66144496a8"></a>
## Thread: Encryption Algorithims for COBOL — 24 msgs

### Encryption Algorithims for COBOL

- **From:** Leigh Moncher <dvor@voicenet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36942CEB.ED7CCFB8@voicenet.com>`

```
Anyone have a *fairly* robust encryption alogrithm written in COBOL?

Let me define "fairly robust".   Better then ROT13 but faster(and or less
robust) than PGP?

I'm trying to overcome some problems in a piece of software written in COBOL
(cross platform reasons).  Of course all the existing examples I'm finding are
written in C or C++ and I'm starting to dig through those but all the byte
manipulation is looking hairy to do in a platform independant manner.

Even if it's got platform/compiler dependant code I'd appreciate a look. (unless
of course you're os shelling out to pgp!)
```

#### ↳ Re: Encryption Algorithims for COBOL

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3694C3B9.C1168F70@home.com>`
- **References:** `<36942CEB.ED7CCFB8@voicenet.com>`

```
Leigh Moncher wrote:
> 
> Anyone have a *fairly* robust encryption alogrithm written in COBOL?
> 
> Let me define "fairly robust".   Better then ROT13 but faster(and or less
> robust) than PGP?

Interesting criteria.  Why do you want encryption?  Who don't you wish
to view your data?  Why do you think your compromise will be worth
while?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Encryption Algorithims for COBOL

- **From:** Leigh Moncher <dvor@voicenet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36957E3F.C4EC95D9@voicenet.com>`
- **References:** `<36942CEB.ED7CCFB8@voicenet.com> <3694C3B9.C1168F70@home.com>`

```
The encyption is a second layer defense.  The first layer is (obviously)
os-level security on read access to the file.  A crypt(unix) level encryption
would be enough (as it's enough for the /etc/passwd file on most *nix boxes)
which is somewhere around DES16 I think.

I have to store a master userid and password combination into a file (this being
the only cross platform compatible way to store the information).  Thus the need
for the encryption.

Besides, I thought it would be a challenge. :)

Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Leigh Moncher wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> while?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Encryption Algorithims for COBOL

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36961645.B306EF0E@home.com>`
- **References:** `<36942CEB.ED7CCFB8@voicenet.com> <3694C3B9.C1168F70@home.com> <36957E3F.C4EC95D9@voicenet.com>`

```
Then you might not need a regular encryption algorithm.  You might just
use a combination of the following ideas I am pulling off the top of my
head:

1.  Have the password file 80 columns, filled with random characters,
with the password characters mixed up in with those characters.
2.  Rot-13 one character of the password, Rot-14 another, etc.  (don't
let anybody look for his own password to figure out your algorithm)
3.  Mix up where the characters of the password are - have a key show
where to start.
4.  If your RANDOM routine will always give the same value with a given
seed, incorporate this "feature".
5.  Each time a password is entered or changed, change the random filler
characters.

Sure these are breakable, but they are cheap, fast, and adequate for
your needs.

Leigh Moncher wrote:
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Encryption Algorithims for COBOL

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36961A2E.12839412@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<36942CEB.ED7CCFB8@voicenet.com> <3694C3B9.C1168F70@home.com> <36957E3F.C4EC95D9@voicenet.com>`

```


Leigh Moncher wrote:

> I have to store a master userid and password combination into a file (this being
> the only cross platform compatible way to store the information).  Thus the need
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > robust) than PGP?

A few years ago I had to reverse compile a small assembler routine which (as it
turned out) for each set of four bytes did a circular shift of a few bits (opposite
directions for encrypt / decrypt) and reversed the value of all bits.  In assembler
it was simple, but when told to rewrite this in COBOL ... ?!?  You are right, it WAS
a challenge.  The solution involved short binary fields redefining a long binary
field and with that I'll leave the challenge with you.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Encryption Algorithims for COBOL

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-08T00:00:00

```
The PWRDPK routine in ETKPAK at http://www.etk.com then downloads,
then etkpak, may be just what you need. It is written specifically to
provide
cross-platform password authorization in Cobol.

Leigh Moncher wrote in message <36957E3F.C4EC95D9@voicenet.com>...
>The encyption is a second layer defense.  The first layer is (obviously)
>os-level security on read access to the file.  A crypt(unix) level
encryption
>would be enough (as it's enough for the /etc/passwd file on most *nix
boxes)
>which is somewhere around DES16 I think.
>
>I have to store a master userid and password combination into a file (this
being
>the only cross platform compatible way to store the information).  Thus the
need
>for the encryption.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>> > Let me define "fairly robust".   Better then ROT13 but faster(and or
less
>> > robust) than PGP?
>>
>> Interesting criteria.  Why do you want encryption?  Who don't you wish
>> to view your data?  Why do you think your compromise will be worth
>> while?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Encryption Algorithims for COBOL

- **From:** "Sven Brueggemann" <Sven.Brueggemann@brem.ftn.de>
- **Date:** 1999-01-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<000ce16e@ftn.de>`
- **References:** `<36957E3F.C4EC95D9@voicenet.com>`

```

LM> I have to store a master userid and password combination into a file
LM> (this being the only cross platform compatible way to store the
LM> information).  Thus the need for the encryption.

Use an enyryption routine that can't be reversed (so that there is no   
possible algorithm that can generate the password from the encrypted  
password). I think these algorithms are called trap-door algorithms.

Encrypt the password entered and compare it to the encrypted password  
stored in the file.

Trap-door algorithms are both fast and (for your purpose) reliable. You  
can find examples in the well-known algotithm books.

Best regards

Sven

To receive my PGP public key, send an eMail with subject "SEND PGPKEY"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Encryption Algorithims for COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** cadams@acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
- **Date:** 1999-01-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369a5a4a.3821645@news.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<36957E3F.C4EC95D9@voicenet.com> <000ce16e@ftn.de>`

```
On Sat, 09 Jan 1999 08:31:00 +0100, "Sven Brueggemann"
<Sven.Brueggemann@brem.ftn.de> wrote:

>
>LM> I have to store a master userid and password combination into a file
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>password). I think these algorithms are called trap-door algorithms.

You'll also see "One-way hash", "cryptographic hash" and "message digest". This
is how Unix passwords are handled. A secure hash of the password is saved. When
the user attempts to login, their attempted password is hashed; if the hashes
match, it's successful.

In any case, the definitive work is probably Bruce Schneier's Applied
Cryptography.
(http://www.amazon.com/exec/obidos/ASIN/0471117099/002-2887882-0420252)



# Chris Adams <cadams@acucorp.com>
```

#### ↳ Re: Encryption Algorithims for COBOL

- **From:** Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com
- **Date:** 1999-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7735cs$egi$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<36942CEB.ED7CCFB8@voicenet.com>`

```
A couple of years ago I had to write a routine in cobol to encrypt/decrypt
database passwords that needed to be kept in a text file.  Without giving
away the farm, I can tell you it involved converting the source string to its
hex equivilant ('A' = '41') with some twists to scramble both the original
string and the resulting hex string.  I don't know how strong it is. 
Probably full of holes, but it serves our purpose.

In article <36942CEB.ED7CCFB8@voicenet.com>,
  Leigh Moncher <dvor@voicenet.com> wrote:
> Anyone have a *fairly* robust encryption alogrithm written in COBOL?
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Even if it's got platform/compiler dependant code I'd appreciate a look.
(unless
> of course you're os shelling out to pgp!)
>
```

```
>How very interesting!  Obviously you are working on an ASCII platform...
>just for giggles you might want to include a translate-to-EBCDIC ('A' =
>'C1') to scramble things a bit further.


Now there's an idea.  To encrypt, painlessly, just define
your own character set in a subroutine.  Since Cobol
data really only encompasses 7 bits, and most platforms
are eight, you should be able to do something that will
confuse the average VBasic programmer all to ratshit.
```

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<775ovh$ccn$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36942CEB.ED7CCFB8@voicenet.com> <7735cs$egi$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7756ql$jgv$1@clarknet.clark.net> <7759uk$5av$1@news.igs.net> <369644B9.8D671D28@home.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <369644B9.8D671D28@home.com>...
>Either way, don't let a user set up a password of AAAAAA, then read the
>password file to derive what AAAAA got converted to.


I was thinking of this later on, and if one wanted a very
basic encryption to discourage casual snooping, the

                INSPECT ENCRYPT-DATA CONVERTING

statement would do just fine.  It is not going to prevent the
CIA from breaking in, but if you just want to disguise things
a bit for amateurs, it is real easy to do and to undo ... just
switch the two quotes.  You could code it in two minutes
for all printable characters.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Encryption Algorithims for COBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
So "AAAAA" gets converted to "EEEEE"?  Maybe a bit too easy to break. 
Might be better to have different INSPECT command for each character.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Encryption Algorithims for COBOL

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<775vio$g1k$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36942CEB.ED7CCFB8@voicenet.com> <7735cs$egi$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7756ql$jgv$1@clarknet.clark.net> <7759uk$5av$1@news.igs.net> <369644B9.8D671D28@home.com> <775ovh$ccn$1@news.igs.net> <36966B95.4675646D@home.com>`

```
The converting option does do an inspect for each character.
The full statement looks like:

                 INSPECT ENCRYPT-DATA CONVERTING
                      " !"#$%&'-./01234567890 <all ASCII characters> ..."
                      BY "zaQbYELmblkjhgfdsa8765432190 <all ASCII
characters> ...".

Each character in the first line is replaced by the
corresponding character in the second line. Each printable
character has to be in each quote once and only once. To
write the decrypt program, you just use cut and paste to switch
the two strings.

Howard Brazee wrote in message <36966B95.4675646D@home.com>...
>So "AAAAA" gets converted to "EEEEE"?  Maybe a bit too easy to break.
>Might be better to have different INSPECT command for each character.
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>> for all printable characters.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Encryption Algorithims for COBOL

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-08T00:00:00

```
I understand that.  But in your example "!!!!!!" would be replaced by
"zzzzz".  By inspecting each position separately with different
converting strings, it would be harder to locate where in the file the
password is hidden.

Donald Tees wrote:
>

…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
```
