###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Encryption Algorithims for COBOL

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Albert Ratzlaff <albert@conexion.com.py>
- **Date:** 1999-01-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36972918.CA67D324@conexion.com.py>`
- **References:** `<36942CEB.ED7CCFB8@voicenet.com> <7735cs$egi$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7756ql$jgv$1@clarknet.clark.net> <7759uk$5av$1@news.igs.net> <369644B9.8D671D28@home.com> <775ovh$ccn$1@news.igs.net> <36966B95.4675646D@home.com> <775vio$g1k$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:

> The converting option does do an inspect for each character.
> The full statement looks like:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Define two working storage variables, one goes like this:

01  INPUT-VALUE.
    02  A PIC X(1).
    02  B PIC X(1).
    ...
    02  Z PIC X(1).

The other has the same fields but in your random order:

01  OUPUT-VALUE.
    02  X PIC X(1).
    02  F PIC X(1).
    ...
    02  S PIC X(1).

Then: MOVE CORRESPONDING INPUT-VALUE TO OUTPUT-VALUE.

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff

(At last a use for MOVE CORRESPONDING ;)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Encryption Algorithims for COBOL

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** jcj0347@aol.com (JCJ0347)
- **Date:** 1999-01-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990108185538.19488.00009052@ng106.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36966B95.4675646D@home.com>`

```
>So "AAAAA" gets converted to "EEEEE"? 
>Maybe a bit too easy to break. 
>Might be better to have different INSPECT command for
>each character.

Reminds me of a hospital OR scheduling system
I wrote for the Wang VS back in the 80's.

We couldn't use real patient names or doctor names for
demo purposes, so I collected up about a week's worth
of data from one of the hospitals using the system.

Then I did an inspect replacing on the name fields,
merely replacing all the vowels with hard consanants.
What a huge laugh we got when we started looking at
the results.  If you have a file with names in it, try it
once for a grin.

JC Jones.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Encryption Algorithims for COBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Sven Brueggemann" <Sven.Brueggemann@brem.ftn.de>

```
DT> I was thinking of this later on, and if one wanted a very
DT> basic encryption to discourage casual snooping, the
DT>
DT>                 INSPECT ENCRYPT-DATA CONVERTING
DT>
DT> statement would do just fine.

That's about the weakest "encryption" one can think of. You only need one  
plain-text password and the encrypted version and you have the code.  
Secondly, you can easily find the conversion tables in the executable.

Best regards

Sven

To receive my PGP public key, send an eMail with subject "SEND PGPKEY"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Encryption Algorithims for COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com
- **Date:** 1999-01-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77d31o$kth$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<36942CEB.ED7CCFB8@voicenet.com> <7735cs$egi$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7756ql$jgv$1@clarknet.clark.net> <7759uk$5av$1@news.igs.net>`

```
I really like the idea of getting EBCDIC involved on an ASCII platform.
Definately confuse the VB and "power user" crowd.  Another twist I used in my
routine was to use the last digit of system time to control the shifting and
scrambling, then hiding that value in the encrypted string so that the
decryption would know how to handle it -- so even a given string gets
encrypted differently every time. In our app, it is used for storing a
password that applications use to log on "under the covers" rather than
confirming a users entered value, so there is no problem with someone
entering a pswd of "AAAA" and analyzing the results.

In article <7759uk$5av$1@news.igs.net>,
  "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote:
>
> docdwarf@clark.net wrote in message <7756ql$jgv$1@clarknet.clark.net>...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Ed Stevens
Nissan Motor Mfg. Corp., USA

http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

<a id="t-407e602513"></a>
## Thread: MF COBOL to Fujitsu COBOL — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF COBOL to Fujitsu COBOL

- **From:** Richard Yates <r.j.yates@open.ac.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36932E7D.4BAD37D8@open.ac.uk>`

```
Is anyone who has had experience of converting from MF COBOL to
Fujitsu COBOL prepared to comment on the ease of switch and (in
a nutshell) any advantages/disadvantages of the product?

It seems that MF 4.1 has become much more expensive for us than
it was.

Richard.
```

#### ↳ Re: MF COBOL to Fujitsu COBOL

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369439C0.6D407D63@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<36932E7D.4BAD37D8@open.ac.uk>`

```
Richard Yates wrote:
> 
> Is anyone who has had experience of converting from MF COBOL to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> it was.

We sell product called MF2FSC which does what 
you think -- converts MicroFocus Cobol to Fujitsu.

If you have a large code then automating conversion
is important if you want to cut down migration time
from months to days.

There is a free download available from 
http://www.siber.com/sct/tools/

In there you can find file SPEC.txt that describes
all known differences between MF and FSC Cobol dialects.

Best regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

#### ↳ Re: MF COBOL to Fujitsu COBOL

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76vouh$pkm$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36932E7D.4BAD37D8@open.ac.uk>`

```
The switch is fairly painless.  There are a few things that
are not, mainly in the screen section.  The cursor control
works completely differently, and very poorly in comparison
to MF.  Clearing the screen has to be changed as the syntax
is different.  Copies have to be put in parenthesis, as does
program id.  I had to pull most of the code into the Fujitsu
editor to get rid of leading tabs and replace with leading
spaces.(just a load save).  First system took about four
weeks for 50 or so programs.  Second about two.

If you have assembler subroutines, that may be a problem.
I rewrote most of mine in Cobol.

Split keys have a different syntax.

The problems are there, but they are mainly simple syntactical
differences.  The code all tested once it compiled.

All in all, a fair amount of work, but straightforward.

Richard Yates wrote in message <36932E7D.4BAD37D8@open.ac.uk>...
>Is anyone who has had experience of converting from MF COBOL to
>Fujitsu COBOL prepared to comment on the ease of switch and (in
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>may be incorrect and is used at reader's own risk.
```

#### ↳ Re: MF COBOL to Fujitsu COBOL

- **From:** "Gary Roush" <support@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77ho79$4rd$1@remarQ.com>`
- **References:** `<36932E7D.4BAD37D8@open.ac.uk>`

```
Richard,

You're right, they have become much more expensive. I choked the last time I
saw their prices.  Anyway, there is a new converter from MF to Fujitsu COBOL
that does a much better job. If you have a program that you want Fujistu to
convert for you and send back to you, send an attached file to
support@adtools.com  You will then be able to get the idea of how much hand
converting you'd need to do after the results. Nothing is every 100%
convertible, but Fujitsu comes close. You may also want to see the home page
on the latest Fujitsu COBOL tools at: http://www.adtools.com/  Hope this
helps.

Regards,
Gary Roush
Fujitsu COBOL support

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
```

<a id="t-17522c6f64"></a>
## Thread: [REQ] s999v999 how many bytes occupy in memory? — 1 msgs

### Re: [REQ] s999v999 how many bytes occupy in memory?

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3693C376.4A8A66D3@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<36ad9b9a.25407073@news.comm2000.it>`

```
Assuming there is nothing extra in the line such as 'SIGN
LEADING/TRAILING SEPARATE' or a USAGE clause like COMP-3,  PIC S999V999
will occupy 6 bytes.  In EBCDIC, the sign will cause the zone part of
the last byte (first 4 bits) to change:  a positive number ending in 2
would be a 'B' while a negative 2 would be 'K'.  This dates from punched
card usage.

The 'V' tells the compiler this field contains three digits after the
decimal point.  Code generated when this field is used will account for
this.  HTH

Lucci wrote:

> 8 bytes?  or 6 bytes? and why occupy 8/6 bytes?
>
> I'm an italian students anybody can help me, please?
> Please e-mail me the response! italian newsserver are very poor.--

Warren Porter
---
Rainy Days OR Mondays Always Get Me Down.
```

<a id="t-ca293c1683"></a>
## Thread: microfocus cobol (bart need your help) — 1 this month / 3 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

_Thread started [1998-12](1998-12.md#t-ca293c1683)._

#### ↳ Re: microfocus cobol (bart need your help)

- **From:** bill_gaphardt@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<770pmg$crp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<01be288d$627cd300$64880318@cc372369-a.narltn1.nj.home.com>`

```
In article <01be288d$627cd300$64880318@cc372369-a.narltn1.nj.home.com>,
  "Cascio" <ilenang@home.com> wrote:
> hi bart,
> i just purchased microfocus cobol and would like to know how to link my
> data files with my program
> thanks
>

  I'm assuming you don't mean link as in compiling and linking your
program into executable code, but rather as in having your program
know the name (and location) of your files.  If this is the case, I
know two ways:

1) You can set your file with a DOS .bat file...
SET TFPGMLOG=C:\MFORGW\TFPGMLOG.TXT
   or, if you're animating, in Mfextmap.dat...
TFPGMLOG C:\MFORGW\TFPGMLOG.TXT
   and then in your program use the Compiler Directive
   ASSIGN"EXTERNAL" and a standard Select Statement:
            SELECT PROGRAM-LOG
                   ASSIGN TO TFPGMLOG.

2) You can have a Working Storage item...
        01  WS-PGM-LOG     PIC X(22) VALUE 'C:\MFORGW\TFPGMLOG.TXT'.
   with a Select Statement:
            SELECT PROGRAM-LOG
                   ASSIGN TO DYNAMIC WS-PGM-LOG.



  Bill Gaphardt

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

```
Randall Bart wrote in message <368ad93c.108284088@netnews>...

>The Unisys A Series COBOL85 compiler produces multi-threadable code (after
>I had them fix a couple bugs).


I should kill you. And then hurt you. Badly. Multi-threading Cobol. Sheez!
:-))

Oh the joys of Cobol. Hands up who did -real- Cobol stuff. Like reading and
merging tapes in those good ol' days of batch processing of transaction
files and stuff. Or writing re-entrant programs to support TP monitors. Hey,
I wonder how much Cobol code there's left? Quite likely more than C or any
other programming language...

Remember when Natural 1 and the other 4GLs came out. They started tolling
the death bell for Cobol. And Cobol is still around. Like those Rambo and
Rocky movies. Sequel after sequel after sequel. And like those movies, it
does -not- improve with age!

;-)
Billy
```

<a id="t-0fc21822ee"></a>
## Thread: Network Traffic — 1 msgs

### Network Traffic

- **From:** "Rob Annandale" <rob_a@unipharm.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.unix.admin,comp.unix.questions
- **Message-ID:** `<770sbr$ft1$2@fountain.mindlink.net>`

```
I have a question regarding a COBOL program running on a SCO 5.04.
The COBOL version is AcuCOBOL 4.0.

I have a few screens where the time is updated every second and flashed back
to the screen.
Usually I have twenty of these screens open at any given time across the
intranet.
I also have a few users who run these programs across the internet

1031            BEFORE TIME 100 NO BEEP TAB ON EXCEPTION

5810           COLOR WC POP-UP AREA IS TIME-WINDOW.
```

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### bar/pie charts on mainframe using COBOL?

- **From:** "Gary R Hamaker" <gary.r.hamaker@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<773mmb$6e1$1@news-1.news.gte.net>`

```
I help maintain a billing system written in COBOL. The output from our
process is an AFP file that is sent to a printer. Presently our graphics
capability is pretty limited. Does anyone have knowledge of how to output
bar and pie charts in an AFP file on a mainframe? I've read a little about
an IBM product called GDDM; could this be one way to achieve my goal?
Your advice is appreciated.
```

```
You may want to look up Fujitsu COBOl as having the product to answer your
needs. Take a look at the website at:
http://www.adtools.com/

In version 4, when it is purchases includes the Crystal Reports program and
they now use version 6.0.  Makes for a very powerful report generator using
the data that could be transferred from the mainframe to PC. Something to
think about.

Regards,
Gary Roush
Fujitsu COBOL support

>process is an AFP file that is sent to a printer. Presently our graphics
```

- **From:** "Gary Roush" <support@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77ipu2$5lr$1@remarQ.com>`
- **References:** `<773mmb$6e1$1@news-1.news.gte.net> <77hlt1$7uc$1@remarQ.com>`

```
Gary,

I neglected to say in the last message about the GDDM product from IBM to
use on the mainframe. I used that product a long time ago. It works, but can
be a tedious project in setting up the program settings to get the pie-chart
you want. They may have upgraded the program since then so it's well worth
the look and seeing if it's worth the money to purchase it and use it. It
does use up a lot of CRU's or computer resources, but works.

When I was talking about Fujitsu COBOL and Crystal Reports in my previous
post, I was suggesting that the pie charts could be created on the PC by
downloading the data from the mainframe and running it through a COBOL
program that creates an ODBC database and then Crystal Reports could be used
to read that data and produce the pie charts or any type of report wanted.
Hope this is clearer and not an misunderstanding to the last post I wrote.

Gary
```

<a id="t-0329f7c786"></a>
## Thread: 16 bit MF 3.4 Compiler allocation of mem segs in PC environment? — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### 16 bit MF 3.4 Compiler allocation of mem segs in PC environment?

- **From:** "Pat Smith" <seawiseoffice@compuserve.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#IUlCuiO#GA.74@nih2naaa.prod2.compuserve.com>`

```
How does the 16 bit MF 3.4 Compiler allocate memory segments for a PC
executable?
I work on a system that currently exists as a large executable MS Windows
application.  This is generated by statically linking 40 or so .OBJ's
generated by the 16 Bit MF 3.4 Compiler in LARGE memory model.   Recently I
tried linking a new .OBJ into this .EXE but the LINK failed due to the
number of segments in the .EXE now exceeding 255.
So I backtracked recompiled/linked sucessfully and examined the executable:

.EXE size (bytes)  237110
CODE Segments 39
DATA Segments 213

Total Segments  252

The program makes extensive use of Working Storage, Linkage Section, Local
Storage and EXTERNAL data items.
Pertinent Compiler Directives used:
target(286)
64kSECT
NOSEG

Given that a segment max. size is 64k I'm interested to know: How/Why are so
many segments are used?

Thank in advance for any insights,

Pat.
```

#### ↳ Re: 16 bit MF 3.4 Compiler allocation of mem segs in PC environment?

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7783j1$fni$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<#IUlCuiO#GA.74@nih2naaa.prod2.compuserve.com>`

```

Pat Smith wrote in message <#IUlCuiO#GA.74@nih2naaa.prod2.compuserve.com>...
>How does the 16 bit MF 3.4 Compiler allocate memory segments for a PC
>executable?

Who knows what evil lurks in the hearts of compiler writers?

>[...]   Recently I
>tried linking a new .OBJ into this .EXE but the LINK failed due to the
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Storage and EXTERNAL data items.
[...]
>
>Given that a segment max. size is 64k I'm interested to know: How/Why are
so
>many segments are used?
>
Though you didn't ask, you can increase the maximum number of
segments by using the '/SEG:n' linker option; where 'n' may
be up to 16384.

Studying the MAP output of the linker may help you to understand
some of the "why" for the extra data segments. Use the '/MAP'
option to create the file.

Think of each data segment as a "named data space", that is,
each set of data definitions has its own separatey named data
space. The WORKING-STORAGE for each program is a data
space, each EXTERNAL is a data space, etc. Except that
LINKAGE SECTIONs and LOCAL-STORAGE SECTIONs do
not have their own separate data space. Furthermore, many of
the data segments are used by the COBOL run time.

HTH
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: 16 bit MF 3.4 Compiler allocation of mem segs in PC environment?

- **From:** "Pat Smith" <seawiseoffice@compuserve.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ONJe93gP#GA.405@nih2naaa.prod2.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<#IUlCuiO#GA.74@nih2naaa.prod2.compuserve.com> <7783j1$fni$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```

>Though you didn't ask, you can increase the maximum number of
>segments by using the '/SEG:n' linker option; where 'n' may
>be up to 16384.


Thanks, I tried this but the 255 segment limitation appears to be a feature
of Windows ,unless I am mistaken.

>Studying the MAP output of the linker may help you to understand
>some of the "why" for the extra data segments. Use the '/MAP'
>option to create the file.


many thanks,

Pat
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 16 bit MF 3.4 Compiler allocation of mem segs in PC environment?

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77l41e$420$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<#IUlCuiO#GA.74@nih2naaa.prod2.compuserve.com> <7783j1$fni$1@server.cntfl.com> <ONJe93gP#GA.405@nih2naaa.prod2.compuserve.com>`

```

Pat Smith wrote in message ...
>
>>Though you didn't ask, you can increase the maximum number of
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I copied the following from the Microsoft MSDN Knowledge Base
-------------
Maximum Number of Segments LINK Supports
Article ID: Q31990
Creation Date: 22-JUN-1988
Revision Date: 24-JAN-1995
The information in this article applies to:

Microsoft LINK for MS-DOS, versions 5.01.20, 5.01.21, 5.03, 5.05, 5.1,
5.13, 5.15, 5.3, 5.31.009, 5.5, and 5.6

The /SEGMENTS:number linker option specifies the maximum number of
program segments. The default limit in Microsoft LINK is 128 if the linker
command line does not specify the /SEGMENTS option or if no number is
specified. The maximum number of segments supported by LINK versions
5.10 and greater is 16,375. Segment definitions are constrained by the
memory available at link time.
--------------
There are no other references to limitations on the maximum number of
segments for either DOS or Windows.

I don't know why; but I can see no reason why it won't link more than 255
segments.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 16 bit MF 3.4 Compiler allocation of mem segs in PC environment?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Pat Smith" <seawiseoffice@compuserve.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uz8Aq74Q#GA.205@nih2naab.prod2.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<#IUlCuiO#GA.74@nih2naaa.prod2.compuserve.com> <7783j1$fni$1@server.cntfl.com> <ONJe93gP#GA.405@nih2naaa.prod2.compuserve.com> <77l41e$420$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
Following your lead I took a look at Microsoft MSDN Knowledge Base too.
Found this which is just a little more enlightening than the original linker
error description:

 ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
-----
PRB: Link Error L1073: "File-segment Limit Exceeded"
Last reviewed: July 17, 1997
Article ID: Q34142
3.x 5.01.20 5.01.21 5.03 5.05 5.10 5.13 5.15 5.30 5.31.009 5.50 5.60 MS-DOS
kbtool kberrmsg kbprb
The information in this article applies to:
Microsoft LINK for MS-DOS, versions 3.x, 5.01.20, 5.01.21, 5.03, 5.05, 5.1,
5.13, 5.15, 5.3, 5.31.009, 5.50, and 5.60

SYMPTOMS
An attempt to link an application with Microsoft LINK fails and the linker
displays the following message on the screen:

L1073: File-segment Limit Exceeded

CAUSE
The application contains more than the 255 segment limit imposed by the
Microsoft Windows and OS/2 protected mode operating systems for each
application and dynamic-link library (DLL).

RESOLUTION
Reduce the number of segments in the application. With Microsoft language
compilers, modify the compiler command line to specify the /PACKCODE option,
the /PACKDATA option, or both to place more information into each segment.
MORE INFORMATION
The linker creates a file segment for each group definition, non-packed
logical segment, and set of packed segments.
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
```

```
Pat Smith wrote in message ...
[...]
>My application's design is going to have to change!
>It seems that the overhead in segments use following the setup I described
>in my original posting is just too high.
>
IMO, the only good reason to change the design of an application
is if the design is defective -- a limit in the OS is not a good reason.

If the PACKCODE and PACKDATA linker options are not
sufficient. You could possibly use the RTE. Basically, you compile
all the programs to .GNT. Place all the .GNT files into library (.LBR)
files and either include the libraries in the configuration (.CFG) file
or CALL the libraries from your main program. If you do not, or can
not, use XM, you may need to add some CANCEL statements.

This is the technique I use. Although I do not run the system as
a Windows application, for demonstration purposes, I did put it
together as a non-GUI Windows application. Even with 60
programs and EXTERNAL data, there were no problem with
segment limits.

If you must use static linking with object (.OBJ) files. It is possible
to reduce the number of object files (and, therefore, segments)
without changing the design.

If the following suggestions make you uncomfortable, do not
use them. They all involve single-level nested programs.

1. If prog-D calls prog-E and prog-F and neither prog-E nor
prog-F have any COPY statements and are not CALLed by
other programs; you could copy prog-E and prog-F into
prog-D as nested programs. This will save at least two
segments per contained program. Source code changes
to prog-D would consist of two COPY statements and three
END PROGRAM statements. Compile prog-D with the
ANS85 and NESTCALL directives.

2. If prog-D calls prog-E and prog-E calls prog-F and neither
prog-E nor prog-F have any COPY statements and are not
CALLed by other programs; you could copy both into prog-D,
as above, except the prog-F will need the IS COMMON clause
after the program name.

3. If prog-D, prog-E, and prog-F share access to an EXTERNAL
data item, that EXTERNAL data item could be placed into a
program's WORKING-STORAGE as a GLOBAL data item. The
PROCEDURE DIVISION would consist of ENTRY statements
with the same names and parameters as prog-D, etc. Each
ENTRY would contain a CALL to the actual program which
would be a nested program as above. After each CALL
would be an EXIT PROGRAM. For example,

    ENTRY "prog-D" USING D.
    CALL "prog-D" USING D.
    EXIT PROGRAM.

Finally, COPY the programs as before. The same restriction
against nested COPY remains. This works because the
precedence for resolving program names is to look first at the
names for contained programs. Since "prog-D" is a contained
program, it will be called instead of creating a recursive call.
The savings is at least two segments per program, after the
first, plus the segment for the EXTERNAL data item.

Demo code for item 3; tested with MF WB 3.2.50:
-------------------
      $set ans85 nestcall
       program-id. prog-A.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01 A pic x value "A".
       01 B pic x value "B".
       procedure division.
           call "prog-DEF"   *> required for dynamic linking
           call "prog-D" using A
           call "prog-E" using B
           call "prog-F"
           stop run.
       end program prog-A.

       program-id. prog-DEF.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01 was-EXTERNAL GLOBAL.
         05 we-A pic x.
         05 we-B pic x.
       linkage section.
       01 D pic x.
       01 E pic x.
       procedure division.
           exit program.

       entry-for-D.
           entry "prog-D" using D
           call "prog-D" using D
           exit program.

       entry-for-E.
           entry "prog-E" using E
           call "prog-E" using E
           exit program.

       entry-for-F.
           entry "prog-F"
           call "prog-F"
           exit program.

       program-id. prog-D. *> simulated copy
       data division.
       linkage section.
       01 D pic x.
       procedure division using D.
           move D to we-A
           exit program.
       end program prog-D.

       program-id. prog-E. *> simulated copy
       data division.
       linkage section.
       01 E pic x.
       procedure division using E.
           move E to we-B
           exit program.
       end program prog-E.

       program-id. prog-F. *> simulated copy
       data division.
       procedure division.
           display we-A
           display we-B
           exit program.
       end program prog-F.

       end program prog-DEF.
-------------------
In summary, use the RTE if it is available. Use the others if you
are comfortable with them. None of these suggestions requires
any significant change in the design of the application.

I hope these suggestions are not too weird!
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

```
For the group -
  I don't care how many times we get asked this, I do *NOT* plan on putting
the answer into the FAQ.  I might some day put in a medium long item on the
fact that

   A) Grace Hopper did NOT invent the language by herself  standing on some
hill top contemplating whether God did or did not understand 1's and 0's
   B) Yes, she is dead
   C) Read DejaNews for all the exciting stories that various people in the
NG have about when THEY met or saw her and exactly how long that piece of
wire was!

P.S. And these facts I always have to look up myself:
  When she retired
    When she came back
       and what rank she was when she did die

P.P.S.  And exactly who did find what genus of "bug" in what hardware!
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hLml2.4108$EC3.2464990@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<773uot$kps$1@ins8.netins.net>`

```
Randy Graff wrote:
>Who invented COBOL???

Nobody knows for sure, the information was lost in WWI.  But
we are pretty sure it was one of three guys: Charles, Bob or
Larry.  So the original name of the language was the acronym
for Charles-Bob-Larry, or CBL.  However, this was difficult
to pronounce, and because of the uncertainty in origin, they
changed it to an acronym of Charles-Or-Bob-Or-Larry: COBOL.
The only problem is that people sometimes confuse COBOL with
a substance invented much earlier by God.  You can keep it
straight by remembering that COBOL is not radioactive, and is
therefore rarely used in cancer treatment. :-J
```

```
geologists.  It is also the preferred language of IBM, ergo "Big
Blue."
```

<a id="t-89340652c3"></a>
## Thread: USAGE, COMP, Storage, etc (for the FAQ - a first draft) — 17 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### USAGE, COMP, Storage, etc (for the FAQ - a first draft)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<773k65$so8@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com>`

```
I would like comments on adding the following to the long overdue update of
the COBOL FAQ.  (There are a few "???" in this draft and I think a few
errors.  Please give me comments AND corrections)

What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?

A) For "alphanumeric" data (or alphanumeric edited data), you should be OK,
if you simply take the default of USAGE DISPLAY for your data.  In other
words, the definition of COBOL insures (more or less) that such data is
pretty portable and that each symbol in the PICTURE clause takes 1 byte of
storage.  HOWEVER, the only way to really insure that such data is
completely portable is to:

  - Use the Standard-1 (or Standard-2) Collating Sequence
  - Use the Standard-1 (or Standard-2) CODE-SET for input/output files
  - Not rely on the order of keys of indexed files

However, in most environments, you should be fairly safe in using the
"native" coding system for alphanumeric items - as long as you have a method
of converting such data from environment to environment (for example - an
upload or download system that converts from ASCII to EBCDIC) if this is now
or may ever be an issue for your application.  The problems you might
encounter with such data include:

   -Comparisons not working (are alphanumeric numbers "smaller" or "larger"
than "letters"?)
  - What order are indexed files stored in?
  - Remember that "true" ASCII (Standard-1) only includes 7-bit characters.
(All the "extended" characters or the "top-half" of most common ASCII
environments are NOT standard and are not necessarily portable)

B) When it comes to numeric fields the *ONLY* way to insure "portable"
numeric fields is to define them as:
   USAGE DISPLAY
   SIGN IS SEPARATE

Such fields may need to go thru the same type of ASCII/EBCDIC conversions as
alphanumeric data, but they will retain their complete numeric values.
HOWEVER, this type of numeric field is highly INefficient in some operating
systems and with some (most?) COBOL compilers.  If a field is just used for
input/output from external devices (files or screens), then this definition
may serve you well - but if you are doing a lot of arithmetic - or
interacting with other operating system "programs",  it is possible
(probable?) that you will not be able to use this format in
resource/time-sensitive applications.

C) "Standard" more or less numeric data types include USAGE BINARY and USAGE
PACKED-DECIMAL.  These were introduced in the '85 Standard and provide for
portable source code - but NOT NECESSARILY PORTABLE STORAGE.  Taking the
most simple example,

   05  How-Many-Bytes   Pic 9(04) Packed-Decimal.

may be stored as either 2 or 3 "bytes" (a concept that isn't in the current
Standard - but will be in the next one) of storage - depending on whether
your compiler "requires" a sign-nibble for even unsigned packed-decimal
items.  The general rule is that all "meaningful" digits of a packed-decimal
item take 1 nibble (half of a byte) and the sign takes another.  But when
the data definition does NOT include a sign (S), then some systems still
require the storage and some don't; some store an unsigned numeric field
with the same sign-nibble as a positive number and some don't.  (Most common
examples - Most IBM-compatible PC's treat positive and unsigned numeric
fields with a X'3' sign-nibble - while most IBM compatible mainframes treat
positive numeric fields with a X'C' while unsigned have a X'F' sign-nibble).

The standard USAGE BINARY has even more variations.  (The Standard says that
its exact storage is "hardware dependent".)  Therefore, your system may
   - allow 1 byte binary fields (or may require half-word as the smallest
storage)
   - may use 1 or 2's complements for negative numbers
   - may be "big-endian" or "little-endian"

All of these ARE hardware/operating system concepts, so you need to check
your specific environment to determine their relevance (especially if you
don't know what the concept means).

D) USAGE COMP (or COMPUTATIONAL) *is* a Standard definition (unlike COMP-1,
COMP-2, ... COMP-n or COMP-x).  HOWEVER, what it means is totally
implementor defined.  There is a common MISCONCEPTION that the Standard
requires USAGE COMP to be the "most efficient" USAGE for numeric fields (I
have even seen this quoted in some manuals).  However, this requirement
certainly does NOT exist in the current ANSI/ISO Standard.  Nevertheless,
often (but definitely NOT always) this USAGE is the most efficient for
numeric fields used in frequent arithmetic operations.

  Some common meanings of USAGE COMP include:
     - USAGE BINARY (on IBM mainframes)
     - USAGE PACKED-DECIMAL (on IBM COBOL/400)
     - USAGE DECIMAL (on ???)
     - USAGE COMP-5 (or COMP-X) (on some Unix or PC compilers)

Without checking your compiler's documentation, you simply canNOT know which
it is.  Furthermore, your compiler MAY have a compiler option or directive
that will switch it from meaning one thing to another.  (For those that mean
BINARY, all the variations listed above apply here as well.)

E) Once you get to the non-Standard USAGE COMP-n, "all bets are off".
Because, IBM mainframes USED TO dominate the COBOL world, the following
meanings are quite common (but are certainly far from universal):

   COMP-1 - Short Floating Point
   COMP-2 - Long Floating Point
   COMP-3 - Packed-Decimal
   COMP-4 - Binary

Other common USAGEs are:
  COMP-5  - "native" binary
  COMP-X - "native" binary (allocated by bytes, not digits)
  COMP-6 - Packed-Decimal not requiring sign-nibbles.

      ******

OK, so now you STILL want to know how many bytes some data field takes (or
what the values within a numeric field "mean").  Clearly the best way to get
the answer to this (for anything other than USAGE DISPLAY SIGN IS SEPARATE)
is to

     CHECK YOUR compiler's LANGUAGE REFERENCE or USER MANUALS!!!

However, if you don't have access to the proper documentation, the answers
for various implementations have been given (often) in the comp.lang.cobol
newsgroup - and a check of DejaNews (see elsewhere in the FAQ for how to get
there) should provide you a good answer.

If all else fails, please do ask the newsgroup for help, but  make certain
you EXPLICITLY say:
   What compiler you are using
    What Operating System you are running on
         and
       Why you couldn't find the answer already in either DejaNews or your
documentation

Given the answers to these 3 questions, you will probably get a quick and
accurate answer - without them you will usually get a "rather pointed"
reply.

P.S. Never, NEVER, use a "REDEFINES" where you change the USAGE of a field -
and expect the application to be portable from operating system to operating
system - much less from compiler to compiler!
```

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>

```
William M. Klein wrote in message <775ca2$847@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>...
>
>William M. Klein wrote in message <773k65$so8@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com>...
>>I would like comments on adding the following to the long overdue update
of
>>the COBOL FAQ.  (There are a few "???" in this draft and I think a few
>>errors.  Please give me comments AND corrections)
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: USAGE, COMP, Storage, etc (for the FAQ - a first draft)

##### ↳ ↳ Re: USAGE, COMP, Storage, etc (for the FAQ - a first draft)

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1999-01-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1999Jan11.162309.19475@giant>`
- **References:** `<773k65$so8@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com> <775ca2$847@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In article <775ca2$847@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>,
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
>
> In article <773k65$so8@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com>,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> The "deathly silence for a day in this thread has me scared:

This is Usenet, not the internet.  You have to allow about five days
before you can be sure an article has reached most Usenet sites around
the world, and it will still be trickling in here and there ten days from
now.  Worse, any given article randomly misses about 10% of Usenet sites
entirely.

An interesting sidelight is that on the average smaller articles
propagate faster than larger articles.  Thus, I am able to follow up on
your plaint, but your original article has not yet reached my cesspool,
er, newsspool.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: USAGE, COMP, Storage, etc (for the FAQ - a first draft)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<779oln$a6r@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<773k65$so8@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com> <77816a$eir$1@server.cntfl.com> <778mdr$2s7$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```

>>[...]
>>>  Some common meanings of USAGE COMP include:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No, these are actual "meanings".  With COBOL/400 for example,
     05    This              Pic S9(03) COMP.
     05    That             Pic S9(03) Comp-3.
     05    Whatever     Pic S9(03) Packed-Decimal.

all "mean" the same thing while with COBOL on an IBM mainframe.

     05    This              Pic S9(03) COMP.
     05    That             Pic S9(03) Comp-4.
     05    Whatever     Pic S9(03) Binary.

all mean the same.

Do you still think my wording needs work - or were you just unaware of some
of the other "meanings" for USAGE COMPUTATIONAL?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: USAGE, COMP, Storage, etc (for the FAQ - a first draft)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77cjj2$t76$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<773k65$so8@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com> <77816a$eir$1@server.cntfl.com> <778mdr$2s7$1@server.cntfl.com> <779oln$a6r@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <779oln$a6r@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>...
>
[...]
>Do you still think my wording needs work - or were you just unaware of some
>of the other "meanings" for USAGE COMPUTATIONAL?
>
In a much longer response to e-mail, I suggested replacing "meanings"
with "equivalents."

Hence,
  Some common meanings of USAGE COMP include:
     - USAGE BINARY (on IBM mainframes)
     - USAGE PACKED-DECIMAL (on IBM COBOL/400)
     - USAGE DECIMAL (on ???)
     - USAGE COMP-5 (or COMP-X) (on some Unix or PC compilers)
becomes,
  Some common equivalents for USAGE COMP include:
     - USAGE BINARY (on IBM mainframes)
     - USAGE PACKED-DECIMAL (on IBM COBOL/400)
     - USAGE DECIMAL (on ???)
     - USAGE COMP-5 (or COMP-X) (on some Unix or PC compilers)
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

#### ↳ Re: USAGE, COMP, Storage, etc (for the FAQ - a first draft)

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-01-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36989DFB.1D645DD4@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<773k65$so8@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Sorry my modem died and I have just borrowed another so that is why my
answer was not instant.

1.  I would include the stronger of the words where you put a ? mark on
them.  But then that's me just tell 'em like it is...

2.  In section D you brush efficiency of numeric types.  This within the
MVs compiler for one depends upon the operation and perhaps this idea
can be brushed upon.

When creating an subscript you are better using Binary or COMP when you
are doing extensive maths calculations then use PACKed or COMP-3 to
split the maths processing from the program processing (The packed maths
is done by a separate CPU).  In MS-DOS (I assume) you would never do
maths with Packed as it is not natively supported.

Ken
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: USAGE, COMP, Storage, etc (for the FAQ - a first draft)

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77am56$obk$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<773k65$so8@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com> <36989DFB.1D645DD4@zip.com.au>`

```

Ken Foskey wrote in message <36989DFB.1D645DD4@zip.com.au>...
[...]
>  In MS-DOS (I assume) you would never do
>maths with Packed as it is not natively supported.
>
Those who learned on IBM mainframes sometimes have rules, such
as, "Always use COMP-3 for accumulators." These rules are
ingrained and spill over to programming on other systems.

I did some tests with arithmetic on S9(7)V99 with COMP, COMP-5,
and COMP-3 using MF 3.2 on MS-DOS as the implementation.
These tests included add, subtract, multiply, and divide.

When interpreted, the performance was nearly identical. I attribute
this to the overhead of the interpeter.

When compiled, the performance of COMP and COMP-5 was nearly
identical and both were, IIRC, 30 times faster than COMP-3. Also there
was no significant difference in performance between aligned and
unalligned data. This is because the MF compiler moves the data to
a work area where it becomes aligned for native arithmetic.

As a result of these tests, I converted all COMP-3 data to COMP.
Furthermore, I no longer use COMP-5 for any except subscpripts
(or where required by an API) since its use is problematic in fields
for index keys and sorts.

It seems ironic, that with all the available numeric USAGEs (and with
more to come in the proposed standard), DISPLAY and COMP seem
to get the job done with few problems.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: USAGE, COMP, Storage, etc (for the FAQ - a first draft)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77apee$6vs@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com>`

```
 -  Expand on efficiency (probably to be done in another section)
 -  Stress/explain "portability" is between COBOLs, not between languages

Anything else that I have missed - that I should add to the next draft?
```

```
> As a result of these tests, I converted all COMP-3 data to COMP.
> Furthermore, I no longer use COMP-5 for any except subscpripts
> (or where required by an API) since its use is problematic in fields
> for index keys and sorts.

I've never used COMP-5 in an IBM environment.  One reason I have liked
COMP-3 is that it is much easier to read in unformatted dumps than COMP
is.  I suppose I really should learn to like on-line debuggers now, but
I don't.  Formatted dumps are great though!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: USAGE, COMP, Storage, etc (for the FAQ - a first draft)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77fvde$784$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<773k65$so8@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com> <36989DFB.1D645DD4@zip.com.au> <77am56$obk$1@server.cntfl.com> <369A0D11.7DAF20AC@home.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <369A0D11.7DAF20AC@home.com>...
>> As a result of these tests, I converted all COMP-3 data to COMP.
>> Furthermore, I no longer use COMP-5 for any except subscpripts
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>I've never used COMP-5 in an IBM environment.
[...]
>
There really is no need for COMP-5 except for use on those
processors that do not store data properly <g> such as Intel.

The following note appears in the IBM Language Reference
for COBOL for OS/390 & VM, VisualAge for COBOL, and
COBOL Set for AIX, SC26-9046-00.

"COMP-5 and COMPUTATIONAL-5 are only supported on
AIX, OS/2, and Windows."
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ On-line or Interactive Debugging (was: Re: USAGE, COMP, Storage, etc (for the FAQ - a first draft))

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77g43u$9gk$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<773k65$so8@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com> <36989DFB.1D645DD4@zip.com.au> <77am56$obk$1@server.cntfl.com> <369A0D11.7DAF20AC@home.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <369A0D11.7DAF20AC@home.com>...
>
>[...]  One reason I have liked
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Though I can appreciate how COMP-3 may simplify the
reading of unformatted dumps, I haven't seen a "core"
dump in over 20 years!

Maybe others can offer comments about on-line
debugging in mainframe environments; but in the world
of PCs, it would be very difficult to solve problems with
dumps, even if the OS would make them. This is
because the COBOL language does not map very well
to the instruction set and data storage used on PCs.

However, the nature of source-level debugging, using tools
such as Animator for Micro Focus COBOL, makes it
generally unneccesary to know the instruction set of the
computer on which the program is run. The interactive
nature of such tools makes it much easier to trace and
analyze the execution of the program up to the point of
failure. The big difference is time -- my time!

To paraphase American Express,
"Interactive debugging -- don't debug without it!"
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **Date:** 1999-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369b9af0.8291726@news>`
- **References:** `<773k65$so8@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com> <36989DFB.1D645DD4@zip.com.au> <77am56$obk$1@server.cntfl.com> <369A0D11.7DAF20AC@home.com> <77g43u$9gk$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
On Tue, 12 Jan 1999 13:24:55 -0500 ""Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>" posted
to "comp.lang.cobol" about: "On-line or Interactive Debugging (was: Re: USAGE, COMP,
Storage, etc  (for the FAQ - a first draft))" :

-->
-->Howard Brazee wrote in message <369A0D11.7DAF20AC@home.com>...
-->>
-->>[...]  One reason I have liked
-->>COMP-3 is that it is much easier to read in unformatted dumps than COMP
-->>is.  I suppose I really should learn to like on-line debuggers now, but
-->>I don't.  Formatted dumps are great though!
-->>
-->Though I can appreciate how COMP-3 may simplify the
-->reading of unformatted dumps, I haven't seen a "core"
-->dump in over 20 years!
-->
-->Maybe others can offer comments about on-line
-->debugging in mainframe environments; but in the world
-->of PCs, it would be very difficult to solve problems with
-->dumps, even if the OS would make them. This is
-->because the COBOL language does not map very well
-->to the instruction set and data storage used on PCs.
-->
-->However, the nature of source-level debugging, using tools
-->such as Animator for Micro Focus COBOL, makes it
-->generally unneccesary to know the instruction set of the
-->computer on which the program is run. The interactive
-->nature of such tools makes it much easier to trace and
-->analyze the execution of the program up to the point of
-->failure. The big difference is time -- my time!
-->
-->To paraphase American Express,
-->"Interactive debugging -- don't debug without it!"
-->-------------------------------
-->Rick Smith

Using the Animator, I set break points just before suspected problem areas of the
code, then executed the program.  It would run to the break and then stop, awaiting
command.  Then I had it step thru the code line by line till it crashed or whatnot.
This allowed me to see exactly what it choked on, with options to display the
contents of variable fields 'real time' as the program runs, etc.  Mucho helpful.  TS
```

```
>>
>>Tron Clark wrote in message ...
>>>I would like an indepth understanding of the y2k issue. I hear it dates
to
>>>the puncard days, but that's all I can find on the net. In programming
>>>language, just what, where, why and how is it that this is an ISSUE? ThkS
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>--
I have never been in that newsgroup, is it really that bad?
[I assume from your comment that it is!]

Actually, it was a semi-serious response. That newsgroup is,
strictly speaking, the proper newsgroup for the discussion of
Y2K issues. Even if it has become a lair for crackpots, lunatics,
and idiots; that does not diminish the fact that it is still the
proper newsgroup for an "indepth understanding" of Y2K
issues (even if those issues are imagined).

The first alternative, for a programming-related discussion,
of Y2K, should probably be < news:comp.programming >.

Finally, issues that relate specifically to a language and Y2K
should be duscussed in the appropriate < news:comp.lang.* >
newsgroup.

Hence, my recommendation for the poster to ask in another
newsgroup was given with the best of intentions.

[One of those intentions was to avert, yet another, Y2K
history lesson in this newsgroup.]  :-)
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

#### ↳ Re: lifeeverlasting@att.net

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vinl2.4116$EC3.2478091@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<zThl2.14869$n4.113452613@WReNphoon2>`

```
Tron Clark wrote:
>I would like an indepth understanding of the y2k issue. I hear it dates to
>the puncard days, but that's all I can find on the net. In programming
>language, just what, where, why and how is it that this is an ISSUE? ThkS

The basic problem is that to conserve space dates were commonly stored
with only two digits for the year, omitting the century.  For example,
today's date would be stored on file as 990109.  This causes problems
in generally two ways.

First, programs do a lot of comparing of data fields, including dates.
If a program compares 981201 (Dec. 1, 1998) with 990101 (Jan. 1, 1999),
the latter is clearly greater, or later.  As long as both dates are in
the same century, everything is okay.  But if a program compares 991201
(Dec. 1, 1999) with 000101 (Jan. 1, 2000) the latter is smaller, and
appears to be earlier, not later.  If those dates represented payment
due dates, or some such, it would cause the comparison to give a wrong
result, and the logic of the program would not work correctly.  The
result would depend on what the program is doing and how it is written.
It is easy to fix these problems by changing the year to four digits,
then the compare will be on 19991201 and 20000101, the latter now is
clearly greater and later.  A no-brainer fix.

The second way this can cause problems is in date calculations.  Every
program which calculates interest, or rental fees, or anything based on
elapsed time over days, will normally calculate elapsed time using some
sort of date calculation.  However the calculations are done, it should
be easy to see that when you use a two digit year in such calculations,
and the year changes from 99 to 00, it is going to look like you went
99 years *backwards*.  There is no doubt in my mind that someone,
somewhere, sometime in early 2000, is going to get a bill for negative
99 years and 11 months interest!  Correcting these type of calculations
is more subtle, because you cannot just plug a four digit year into a
calculation designed for a two digit year and expect it to work.  The
calculation must be examined and corrected or replaced with code which
does what the program needs to do at that point.  This is a thinking
fix, and the programmer must be very careful and thorough.  Another
common date calculation is to calculate the day of the week from the
date.  Again, replacing a two digit year with a four digit year does
not automatically make these calculations work; the algorithms are a
bit different.  The pattern of weekdays is different between the
20th century and the 21st century (Jan. 1, 1900 was a Monday and Jan.
1, 2000 will be a Saturday), and 1900 was not a leap year, but 2000
will be a leap year.  The rule is that years divisible by 4 are leap
years, unless they are divisible by 100, in which case they are not,
unless they are divisible by 400, in which case they are.  1900 is
divisible by 100 but not 400, so is not a leap year, but 2000 is also
divisible by 400, so it is a leap year.

Virtually all Y2K problem areas are variations on these two issues.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: lifeeverlasting@att.net

- **From:** "David W. Furin" <dfurin@larich.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<82F03FBBD7D26CCB.303489187E9E8FE0.8349B6E8A8709AEF@library-proxy.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<zThl2.14869$n4.113452613@WReNphoon2> <vinl2.4116$EC3.2478091@news3.mia>`

```
Tron,

Judson left one thing out of his excellent reply:  The third basic
problem is date storage.  The programming fixes he mentions address the
*logical* problems with two-digit years.  There is another layer of y2k
work needed to fix the data *files* containing two-digit years in record
keys.  Many data files use the date as part of the file key so the
natural order of a file is chronological.  (For example, the transaction
detail file on a credit card system will likely have the date/time as
part of the unique key to the record.)  Unless these data files are
re-created with expanded years, new records will be stored 'before' the
old ones, which may cause other types of program misbehaviors.

This is a variation of the problems Judson mentioned, but you can see
how the problem keeps getting bigger.
```


## Thread: Problem with Cobol for MVS — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Problem with Cobol for MVS

- **From:** hebertgi@connectmmic.net
- **Date:** 1999-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3694D04B.35CC@connectmmic.net>`

```
Good Morning,

At work we are experiencing a problem with Cobol for MVS (1.2.1) under
L.E. v1 r6.0.  
The problem we are having is as follows:

Within the working storage section the variable WRP-ENT-L3-LITTERAL is
defined as below.

01   WRP-ENTETE-LIGNE3-626.
        05 PIC X(01)   VALUE SPACES.
        05 PIC X(07)   VALUE 'NO'.
        05 PIC X(21)   VALUE 'NOM'.
          .
          .
        05   WRP-ENT-L3-LITTERAL             PIC X(23)   VALUE SPACES.

Then within our procedure DIVISION we have an IF statment which moves a
word (ex.  problem) into the WRP-ENT-L3-LITTERAL variable which is then
asked to be printed.

Example:
                   IF   WRP-CIE = '01'
                          MOVE 'PROBLEM'  TO WRP-ENT-L3-LITTERAL
                   END-IF

                   WRITE DETAIL-LINE FROM WRP-ENTETE-LIGNE3-626.


Our problem is that the word (WRP-ENT-L3-LITTERAL) does not get printed
and a line of dots (ex...............) is printed instead. Trying to
figure out our problem we
have realized that the variable without using DISPLAYs was a packed
numeric
field even though it was defined as an alphanumeric field in our working
storage.

We have found that if we DISPLAY the variable anywhere in the program
the
word will be printed properly.  We have also realized that if we execute
the same program with version Cobol II but without any DISPLAYs, our
variable is printed properly without an difficulties.

Would you have an answer for our problem?  Why would the variable be
printed with the DISPLAY command with Cobol for MVS and for Cobol II we
do
not need to place any DISPLAYs within the program?

Thank You for your time and cooperation.
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem with Cobol for MVS

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<772p1n$js6@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3694D04B.35CC@connectmmic.net>`

```
I am not positive from your description exactly WHICH field is the field
which contains packed-decimal data - but is defined as alphanumeric.
Possibly, if you could clear this, up I could answer more accurately.
HOWEVER,  I do have one other possible "solution".  Is it possible that your
VS COBOL II compile was done with the NOOPT compiler option (no
optimization) and that your  COBOL for MVS compile was done with OPT?
"Unpacking" could be something that was optimized out of your newer compile.

In general, I think you should compare your compiler options for the two
compiles and see what is different.  This (rather than the DISPLAY
statement) *may* explain the difference.


hebertgi@connectmmic.net wrote in message <3694D04B.35CC@connectmmic.net>...
>Good Morning,
>
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thank You for your time and cooperation.
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem with Cobol for MVS

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-01-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3698928A.CDB1DDB0@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<3694D04B.35CC@connectmmic.net>`

```
This sounds like a memory over write a table is being addressed past
it's boundaries.   Compile with SSRANGE in the compile options to check
whether you are and if you compile TEST(PATH,SYM) it will even give you
theline number.

Good Luck
Ken

hebertgi@connectmmic.net wrote:
> 
> Good Morning,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
..snip..
> Example:
>                    IF   WRP-CIE = '01'
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

..snip..
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem with Cobol for MVS

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Yj5m2.2783$hE2.17329949@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<3694D04B.35CC@connectmmic.net>`

```
What it looks like is that you may be looking at the wrong offset to
determine where the value stored in WRP-ENT-L3-LITTERAL actually is in the
written record. To verify this use Fileaid to look at the written record to
see what is actually being written. If you do not have fileaid, look at the
cross reference in the compile listing off to the left of the source code.
This may be in hexadecimal format, I haven't looked at those in a while.

From what you have written it sounds like not all of the fields are being
filled in thus everywhere in the record where there is not a value filled
in, hex'00' or low-values, is being filled in by default. Thus if you look
at the wrong place in the output record you will see '..........', but if
you DISPLAY the individual field WRP-ENT-L3-LITTERAL you will see the
correct value.

>Within the working storage section the variable WRP-ENT-L3-LITTERAL is
>defined as below.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>




>Then within our procedure DIVISION we have an IF statment which moves a
>word (ex.  problem) into the WRP-ENT-L3-LITTERAL variable which is then
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>storage.

Once again verify proper offset from COBOL listing

>
>We have found that if we DISPLAY the variable anywhere in the program
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>variable is printed properly without an difficulties.

may be caused by compiler options, verify different compiles have same
compiler options. If you cant change them in the compile, use the PROCESS or
CBL statements in the source to override.

>
>Would you have an answer for our problem?  Why would the variable be
>printed with the DISPLAY command with Cobol for MVS and for Cobol II we
>do
>not need to place any DISPLAYs within the program?

Confused about this one. Are you saying when you have a DISPLAY in the
program for both compiles the record is written to the output file
correctly, or is it simply being DISPLAYED to DD SYSOUT properly?
```

## Thread: Dialog 3.0 menu question — 2 msgs

### Dialog 3.0 menu question

- **From:** "Bill Wood" <beavis27@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OsBC3$pO#GA.224@upnetnews05>`

```
I am currently using Dialog 3.0 along with NetExpress 2.0.  I am adding a
menu to trigger events.  I would like to capture the events of the primary
menu items (i.e. File, Edit, etc.) being selected but it doesn't seem to
work.  Is there anyway to capture this event?  Also I have a submenu of the
last 5 documents I edited and I wanted to trap the event of this menu item
being selected before it blows up the 5 documents as a submenu.  I have the
same problem where I can't trap the event.  Any help would be appreciated.

Bill
```

## Thread: Screen Save in MFCOBOL under UNIX — 5 msgs

### Screen Save in MFCOBOL under UNIX

- **From:** shanmugam@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<770th5$ghk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Dear friends,

Can anybody tell me how to save snap shot of a screen and restore it back. I
am having a user interface COBOL program written using MFCOBOL DISPLAY/ACCEPT
statements (without any screen section). I want to do..  1) Save the screen
contents  2) Display another screen (say LookUp/HELP Screen)  3) Display back
the saved Screen. Basically I have want to provide field level help screens
using DISPLAY/ACCEPT statements.


-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Screen Save in MFCOBOL under UNIX

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <Kevin.Digweed@dial.pipex.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3694A020.8AF06099@dial.pipex.com>`
- **References:** `<770th5$ghk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
shanmugam@my-dejanews.com wrote:
> Dear friends,
> 
> Can anybody tell me how to save snap shot of a screen and restore it back. I
> am having a user interface COBOL program written using MFCOBOL DISPLAY/ACCEPT
> statements (without any screen section).

Take a look at the "Call-by-name Routines" chapter of your
documentation. The relevant routines are in the CBL_READ_SCR_???? and
CBL_WRITE_SCR_???? family.

Cheers, Kev.
```

#### ↳ Re: Screen Save in MFCOBOL under UNIX

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>

```
shanmugam@my-dejanews.com wrote
>
>Can anybody tell me how to save snap shot of a screen and restore it back. I
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>using DISPLAY/ACCEPT statements.

Put the code which displays the screen, and the code which displays
the help in separate paragraphs.  Perform the associated paragraph
whenever you want to display that screen.
```

```
In article <770th5$ghk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>,
 <shanmugam@my-dejanews.com> wrote:
>
> Can anybody tell me how to save snap shot of a screen and
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> using DISPLAY/ACCEPT statements.

I would study the "Screen Section" documentation that
begins on page 4-155 of the "Micro Focus Object COBOL
and COBOL Language Reference" manual, Issue 15, May 1995.
This syntax is XOPEN compliant and facilitates everything
you mention.
```

#### ↳ Re: Screen Save in MFCOBOL under UNIX

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-n27nTRxC8tC4@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<770th5$ghk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
On Thu, 7 Jan 1999 00:01:43, shanmugam@my-dejanews.com wrote:

> Dear friends,
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Look up the Library Routines: CBL_READ_SCRCHRATTRS (from memory) and 
CBL_WRITE_SCRCHRATTRS
```

#### ↳ Re: Where is MS-COBOL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<772lqb$9tn@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3p3l2.3449$Zp4.5209@news.bora.net>`

```

�� �� �� wrote in message <3p3l2.3449$Zp4.5209@news.bora.net>...
>I need ms-cobol
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

What you are mentioning is a VERY "dead" product.  There was a Microsoft
produced compiler (some time in the 80's - I think - but I am not sure),
then Microsoft started selling the Micro Focus compiler with a Microsoft
logo on it (and some VERY minor changes), then they stooped doing even that
(about `1990 - although my dates may be off).

Are you asking for someone to help you find a 5 or 10 year old compiler
(that you can buy) or are you asking about any "current" Microsoft COBOL
compiler - which doesn't exist (although many compilers do work with MS
operating systems - of course).
```

<a id="t-d3aaeebf57"></a>
## Thread: Federal Screw — 2 msgs

### Federal Screw

- **From:** mixxerdw@eye_eye_echs.com
- **Date:** 1999-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-RbezOfNc5OdB@dsm4merlin.iix.com>`

```
I apologize if this is a duplicate post, but i think my poster thread 
failed; I never saw this get up on the server.
======================================================================
========
 
Here's an oldy mouldy for you:
 
Anyone ever use a Votrax Type-'n'-Talk voice synthesizer?  It was 
built c. 1980 by the Votrax Division of Federal Screw Works, Troy, 
Michigan, and produced a pretty decent Robbie-the-Robot voice from 
ASCII text.  With a little skill and some clever spelling, you could 
get quite good with it.
 
It also has a full phonetic language built in that would allow darn 
near any language to be synthesized if you knew your phonemes.
 
Anyway, I have one of them, still in fine working order, but I have 
mislaid the manual with the details of th hardware setup and the 
phoneme code.  If anyone has such a manual, I would be glad to pay for
copying and mailing.
 t
 T
```

#### ↳ Re: Federal Screw

- **From:** jlw@psulias.psu.edu (j.lance wilkinson, (814) 865-1818)
- **Date:** 1999-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77308j$27e2@r02n01.cac.psu.edu>`
- **References:** `<1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-RbezOfNc5OdB@dsm4merlin.iix.com>`

```
In article <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-RbezOfNc5OdB@dsm4merlin.iix.com>, mixxerdw@eye_eye_echs.com writes:

>Anyone ever use a Votrax Type-'n'-Talk voice synthesizer?  It was 
>built c. 1980 by the Votrax Division of Federal Screw Works, Troy, 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>copying and mailing.

	Can't help on the manuals, but the folks who developed Bull CP-6
	(a fine OS, never widely adopted, unfortunately) used a feature
	in that OS to define a console profile for one.  It was a "Device"
	console, and they configured it to use a specific "language" file
	to issue its messages.  Device consoles on CP-6 were used to report
	and administer devices like printers and tape drives -- so when
	a tape needed to be mounted or a form on a printer needed to be
	changed, their system would issue audible reports to the operations
	staff.
	
	One of the neat things about CP-6 was that error/console messages
	could be ESCALATED when the OS cycled around and repeated them
	due to inaction.  Thus the "requests" for operator action eminating
	from the Votrax console got more and more caustic and abusive as
	the operators ignored them ;-)  

	Gosh, how I miss that OS.  Now I'm stuck with boring OpenVMS ;-{

+-"Never Underestimate the bandwidth of a station wagon full of mag tapes"--+
| J.Lance Wilkinson ("Lance")            InterNet:  Lance.Wilkinson@psu.edu | 
| Systems Design Specialist - Lead       AT&T:      (814) 865-1818          |
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
| VMS GopherMeister             Is busy, hung or dead.                      |
+------"He's dead, Jim. I'll get his tricorder. You take his wallet."-------+
<A Href="http://perdita.lcs.psu.edu">home page</a> 
<a Href="http://perdita.lcs.psu.edu/junkdec.htm">junk mail declaration</a>
```

## Thread: Security key Micro Focus cobol — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Security key Micro Focus cobol

- **From:** "Super Stevo" <s_stevo@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7731pf$75p$1@news1.skynet.be>`

```
At work I use Micro Focus cobol on an SCO UNIX
and on my pc.  The compiler (editor, animator) on my
pc needs a key on the parallel port.  Taking this key
home is a drag so I was wondering if anyone has a way around this (crack of
some sort)

Thanks in advance,

Steven
```

#### ↳ Re: Security key Micro Focus cobol

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<773d5c$kaj@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7731pf$75p$1@news1.skynet.be>`

```
If the same key ("dongle") works in both places, then it sounds like you
"copied" the software from one computer to another.  As I recall the
European contracts ("dongles" were never as common-place in the US), this
was a 'contractual no-no".  Therefore, you may have some trouble getting
help with this.  You may want to check out your licensing agreement before
pursuing this farther (or contact MF - if the license says that you can do
this - i.e. multiple machines but not simultaneous use).

Super Stevo wrote in message <7731pf$75p$1@news1.skynet.be>...
>At work I use Micro Focus cobol on an SCO UNIX
>and on my pc.  The compiler (editor, animator) on my
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **Date:** 1999-01-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<775fm7$k2f$1@dildog.lgc.com>`

```
Can anyone point me to information on how to do this. We are interested in
creating DCOM objects using NetExpress.
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress and DCOM

- **From:** "Gary Roush" <support@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77hlik$7l0$1@remarQ.com>`
- **References:** `<775fm7$k2f$1@dildog.lgc.com>`

```
Scott,

You may want to look into Fujitsu COBOL 3.0 or better yet, the new 4.0
version.  There is full support using DCOM objects. You may go to the
website at: http://www.adtools.com/ and look around at the products.  Hope
this helps.  You can contact support@adtools.com for further questions.

Regards,
Gary Roush
Fujitsu COBOL Support
mailto:support@adtools.com,gkr@adtools.com


Scott Biggerstaff wrote in message <775fm7$k2f$1@dildog.lgc.com>...
>Can anyone point me to information on how to do this. We are interested in
>creating DCOM objects using NetExpress.
>
>
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress and DCOM

- **From:** smb@mfltdz.co.ukz (Steve Biggs)
- **Date:** 1999-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77n3e6$40g@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<775fm7$k2f$1@dildog.lgc.com> <77hlik$7l0$1@remarQ.com>`

```
Scott Biggerstaff wrote in message <775fm7$k2f$1@dildog.lgc.com>...
>Can anyone point me to information on how to do this. We are interested in
>creating DCOM objects using NetExpress.

Hi Scott,
The examples to look for are in the \netexpress\base\demo\oledemos\server 
and (in version 3.0) the \netexpress\base\demo\oledemos\dcom directories.
The first provides examples of creating both in-process and out-of-process 
COM servers, and the second shows a simple DCOM client and server. No code 
changes are required to move from local COM to DCOM.

There are also full docs available (in the Distributed Computing on-line 
book; search for OLE in the index and follow links).

In addition, the NetExpress Class Wizard (File/New/Class) will allow you 
to create skeleton DCOM (OLE Automation) programs with mimimum effort. 
Full context help is provided for every dialog.

Finally, our on-line AnswerLab service (on our support web site) has 
already had many questions about this area and serves as a useful 
knowledge base for technical queries that have come up in the past.

Hope this helps,
Steve.
Micro Focus Development.
```

```
rebuild to work on a machine other than mine (I get RTS 173 on
CALLRB).  On my machine, it works fine.  I also have the MF software
on my machine, so I'm obviously missing something in the linking or
distribution.  I'm looking in the Programmer's Guide to File Handling,
page 7-20 at what is required to create your own rebuild.  I've linked
in:
callrb.obj
rbldmain.obj
rbldsub.obj
extfh.obj
mffh.obj

I'm not using compression or anything else listed at the top of page
7-21 - I'm just using the /k keyword to rebuild the indexes in a
parameter file (@filename syntax).  The example on 7-21 shows the
minimum for a Windows environment (NT/95) and the only thing I can't
figure out is what is EXTERNL.  I can't locate a .obj, .lib or .lbr
file by that name.  I guess this is the problem.  What am I missing?

Thanks,
Doug
```

## Thread: Using the "RegSetValueEx" API... — 3 msgs

### Using the "RegSetValueEx" API...

- **From:** tommy nilsen <tommy.nilsen@merkantildata.no>
- **Date:** 1999-01-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369623AE.6AD8666D@merkantildata.no>`

```

Hi.

I need to set a hex value in the registry using the "RegSetValueExA"
win32-api..
Couild someone please give a hint of how to do this...

I have no problem using the same api to set a string or int value...

Any help would be greatly appreciated.

Tommy
tommy.nilsen@merkantildata.no
```

#### ↳ Re: Using the "RegSetValueEx" API...

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gw@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<775fin$oar@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<369623AE.6AD8666D@merkantildata.no>`

```
Tommy,

The data items used in the API calls are straightforward numerics, so you
should be able to pass in decimal values. However, various compilers will
allow you to define hex values so either of the following would do exactly
the same

move 16 to val
move h"10" to val

I hope this helps.


tommy nilsen wrote in message <369623AE.6AD8666D@merkantildata.no>...
>
>Hi.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
Hi.
Thanks for answering.

In the program below you can see what I'm trying to do.
The value that is actually set in the registry is incorrect...

If you decide to try this program, be shure to take a backup of the
specified registry-key..


Tommy

----------------------------------------------------------------------------
---------------------------------------------------------------
      $set ans85 comp
       identification division.
       program-id. login.

       environment division.
       configuration section.
       special-names.
       call-convention 74 is WINAPI.
       input-output section.

      *-------------------------
       working-storage section.
      *-------------------------
       01 link-api32.
          03 link-api32-TextBuffer          pic x(200).
          03 link-api32-TextBufferLn        pic 9(9)  comp-5.
          03 link-api32-ApiStatus           pic 9(9)  comp-5.
          03 link-api32-TextBuffer2         pic x(200).
          03 link-api32-TextBuffer2Ln       pic 9(9)  comp-5.
          03 link-api32-NumBuffer           pic 9(9)  comp-5.
       78 HKEY-LOCAL-MACHINE               value 2147483650.
       78 KEY-ALL-ACCESS                   value 983103.
       01 register-status                  pic 9(1) value 0.
       01 win32-ApiStatus                  pic s9(9)  comp-5.
       01 win32-TextBuffer                 pic x(200).
       01 win32-NumBuffer                  pic 9(9)  comp-5.
       01 wskeyHandle                      pic s9(9)  comp-5.

       procedure division.

       call "cob32api.dll"
           initialize wsKeyHandle
           move HKEY-LOCAL-MACHINE to win32-NumBuffer
           move z"Software\Microsoft\Internet Explorer" to
                                                        win32-TextBuffer
           call WINAPI "RegOpenKeyExA" using
                   by value     win32-NumBuffer
                   by reference win32-TextBuffer
                   by value 0 *> Ikke i bruk
                   by value     KEY-ALL-ACCESS
                   by reference wsKeyHandle
               returning        win32-ApiStatus
           end-call
           move z"IntegratedBrowser" to link-API32-TextBuffer
           move 0 to link-API32-NumBuffer *> Type of KEY,not shure this is
the right value
           move h"01"      to link-API32-TextBuffer2*>The value I want  to
set in the registry
           move 2          to link-API32-TextBuffer2Ln
           call WINAPI "RegSetValueExA" using
                   by value wsKeyHandle
                   by reference link-API32-TextBuffer
                   by value 0
                   by value link-API32-NumBuffer
                   by reference link-API32-TextBuffer2
                   by value link-API32-TextBuffer2Ln
               returning link-API32-ApiStatus
           end-call

           stop run
           .
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
---------------------------------------------------------------
```
