<a id="t-f3c5907d60"></a>
## Thread: Book: Horrible Things You Can Do In COBOL — 25 this month / 29 total

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Book: Horrible Things You Can Do In COBOL

- **From:** jimkeo@multi-platforms.com (Jim Keohane)
- **Date:** 1999-01-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jimkeo-0901991338080001@ip15.islip8.ny.pub-ip.psi.net>`

```
   Recently I've found myself dealing with COBOL again. I've had
too much fun with C/C++, assembler, PL/X and even a little Java!
   Still I fondly recount some good old COBOL war stories when I
was obliged to use COBOL for purposes definitely un-COBOL-like!

Examples:

   A COBOL language/syntax parser/tokenizer written in COBOL itself.

   COBOL low resolution graphics.

   COBOL disassembler which compared zapped modules to their unzapped
   versions and generated the missing AMASPZAP statements.

   Prime number and Fibonacci series generator and more. {dumb}

Well, there must be much better war stories out there and I'm seriously
planning to collect some for a book. So all you COBOL'ers writing CGI
on mainframes or using Fujitsu's NETCOBOL to write Java applets or
using COBOL POINTERS to traverse OS control blocks or ...

   Writing COBOL for GUI's is getting quite commonplace. Nice. Still,
a min-blowing example for the "big iron" COBOL'ers who still disdain
the pc world could be fun!

   You get the picture. Email me if you have any such inglorious tasks
in your past. Let me know if you have source and, even better, if you
can provide source and output for the book.

   I will cite all contributors unless they request otherwise.

Thanks for an anticipated overwhelming response! {fat chance}  - Jim
```

#### ↳ Re: Book: Horrible Things You Can Do In COBOL

- **From:** "Stephen Thomas" <talkingcats@gobble_clara.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EL1m2.4594$hs6.3364@nnrp2.clara.net>`
- **References:** `<jimkeo-0901991338080001@ip15.islip8.ny.pub-ip.psi.net>`

```

Jim Keohane wrote in message ...
>   Recently I've found myself dealing with COBOL again. I've had
>too much fun with C/C++, assembler, PL/X and even a little Java!
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>   A COBOL language/syntax parser/tokenizer written in COBOL itself.


The Micro Focus syntax checker (CBL -> INT) is itself written in COBOL.  I
know because I used to work for Micro Focus :)

snip, snip ...

>Well, there must be much better war stories out there and I'm seriously
>planning to collect some for a book. So all you COBOL'ers writing CGI
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>--

A few years ago I was team leader and lead developer on a project that
developed a nearly-real time customer information system for British
Railways.  That system ran on RS/6000s and controlled customer information
for stations oin the Welsh Valleys line.  The core functions of that system
were more like C in COBOL syntax !.  It took its main information feed from
a satellite tracking system ... each train had a little satellite dish which
updated the tracking system as to its whereabouts.

Stephen Thomas.
```

```
Hi all,

I'd like to know what the biggest single data file anyone has used , either in a
mainframe environment or a PC/Lan environment.

I'm just curious, because a system I completed in 1992 now has a main data file
running at 230Mb, the index file is 60Mb... Is this small potatoes or should I
get those archiving procedures in place? <g> 
It's written in MF cobol running on NT - started life on an IBM System/36...
```

```
our archive is all that big compared to some others (anyone have any

Atlantic, etc.?)

Bill Lynch
```

- **From:** WOB@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78r2q7$b9i$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<jimkeo-0901991338080001@ip15.islip8.ny.pub-ip.psi.net> <EL1m2.4594$hs6.3364@nnrp2.clara.net> <369a0e2b.327867688@news.enterprise.net> <369FCF87.C4EDF587@net.ntl.com> <77tafq$ta6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36A49200.8C69D71C@home.com> <36AA16B8.C70FE5B4@net.ntl.com> <%Cqq2.1148$oi.1096861@news3.mia> <78ouff$g3r$1@news.fsu.edu>`

```
Calculate a square root (not using a function):

03 WS-VALUE PIC S9(01)       PACKED-DECIMAL.
03 WS-SQRT  PIC S9(02)V9(09) PACKED-DECIMAL.

MOVE +2                 TO WS-VALUE.
COMPUTE WS-SQRT ROUNDED = (WS-VALUE ** .5).

ANSWER ===> 01.414213562

================================================================================

Cheers,

WOB

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Book: Horrible Things You Can Do In COBOL

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6L9s2.319$Z9.317619@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<jimkeo-0901991338080001@ip15.islip8.ny.pub-ip.psi.net> <EL1m2.4594$hs6.3364@nnrp2.clara.net> <369a0e2b.327867688@news.enterprise.net> <369FCF87.C4EDF587@net.ntl.com> <77tafq$ta6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36A49200.8C69D71C@home.com> <36AA16B8.C70FE5B4@net.ntl.com> <%Cqq2.1148$oi.1096861@news3.mia> <78ouff$g3r$1@news.fsu.edu> <78r2q7$b9i$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
WOB@my-dejanews.com wrote:
>
>Calculate a square root (not using a function):
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>COMPUTE WS-SQRT ROUNDED = (WS-VALUE ** .5).
                                     ^
                                     |
What do you call that, if not a 'function'? ;-)  It's called the
'power function', and is probably several times as much code, and
much, much slower than a straight square root function.  But you
are correct that it will calculate square roots, if FUNCTION SQRT
is not available.  This method can also calculate other roots, even
non integer roots such as the 4.35th root.  If N=number and R=root
(e.g. R=2=square, R=3=cube, etc.):

   COMPUTE WS-ROOT = N ** (1 / R).
```

```
> >COMPUTE WS-SQRT ROUNDED = (WS-VALUE ** .5).
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>    COMPUTE WS-ROOT = N ** (1 / R).

As an exercise only, one could use Newton's method of successive
approximations.  I'm working off the top of my head, but let's say you
want to find the square root of 100.

1.  Guess 50  test (by squaring it), that is too large
2.  Guess 2   test, that is too small
3.  Guess 25 and 4.
4.  Guess 12 and 8
7.  Guess 10.

The difficulty in creating this program is in picking the correct
precision for the task and testing where your answer is not perfect.

This difficulty applies when using irrational numbers anyway.  People
expect that if we divide $10 by 3 and then multiply it by 3, we will
have $10.  Occasionally we will need to make sure that happens in a
COBOL program.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Book: Horrible Things You Can Do In COBOL

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<w4ns2.88$Fs.218553@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<jimkeo-0901991338080001@ip15.islip8.ny.pub-ip.psi.net> <EL1m2.4594$hs6.3364@nnrp2.clara.net> <369a0e2b.327867688@news.enterprise.net> <369FCF87.C4EDF587@net.ntl.com> <77tafq$ta6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36A49200.8C69D71C@home.com> <36AA16B8.C70FE5B4@net.ntl.com> <%Cqq2.1148$oi.1096861@news3.mia> <78ouff$g3r$1@news.fsu.edu> <78r2q7$b9i$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <6L9s2.319$Z9.317619@news4.mia> <36B1CE23.AE3782FF@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
>
>As an exercise only, one could use Newton's method of successive
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>COBOL program.

Except for cases important enough to use a polynomial approximation
with pre-computed coefficients, or a table lookup, Newton's Method is
still about the fastest way to calculate a square root.  The key to
making Newton's Method sing is in choosing a good first approximation.
There are a zillion ways to do it, but here is an interesting one of
my own devising.  Take a look at this chart I prepared years ago when
writing a high precision floating point math library.  'Exp' is the
exponent which would be used if the number was expressed as a
normalized decimal floating point number.

                                Square Roots

             Number              Exp         Square Root   Exp
    ---------------------------- ---       --------------- ---
               0.000000000005555 -11            0.00000236  -5
               0.00000000005555  -10            0.00000745  -5
               0.0000000005555    -9            0.0000236   -4
               0.000000005555     -8            0.0000745   -4
               0.00000005555      -7            0.000236    -3
               0.0000005555       -6            0.000745    -3
               0.000005555        -5            0.00236     -2
               0.00005555         -4            0.00745     -2
               0.0005555          -3            0.0236      -1
               0.005555           -2            0.0745      -1
               0.05555            -1            0.236        0
               0.5555              0            0.745        0
               5.555               1            2.36         1
              55.55                2            7.45         1
             555.5                 3           23.6          2
            5555.                  4           74.5          2
           55550.                  5          236.           3
          555500.                  6          745.           3
         5555000.                  7         2360.           4
        55550000.                  8         7450.           4
       555500000.                  9        23600.           5
      5555000000.                 10        74500.           5
     55550000000.                 11       236000.           6
    555500000000.                 12       745000.           6

Note that the exponent of the square root will tend to be about half
the exponent of the number.  For floating point numbers, that means
you can have a reasonably good approximation using any mantissa with
an exponent of half the number.  Or expressed another way, a square
root will tend to have about half as many digits between the decimal
point and the high order digit as the number.  This last observation
provides a way to make a good first approximation in COBOL.  For real
numbers, you can only obtain the square root of a non-negative number,
so create a number field which is DISPLAY and has no sign.  Example:

  01  ROOT-WORK.
      03  RW-DIGIT      PIC S9(02)       COMP     VALUE 0.
      03  RW-NUMBER     PIC  9(09)V9(09) DISPLAY.
      03  RW-SQRT       PIC  9(09)V9(09) DISPLAY  VALUE 0.
      03  RW-APPROX     REDEFINES SQRT
                           PIC  X(18).

Now calculate how many digits from the decimal point is the high order
digit of the number (assuming a non-zero RW-NUMBER.  (Note: there are
significant efficiency issues here which I will address below.)

  INSPECT RW-NUMBER TALLYING RW-DIGIT FOR LEADING ZEROS.
  COMPUTE RW-DIGIT = 9 - RW-DIGIT.

To calculate the approximate number of digits from the decimal point
which the square root will have:

  DIVIDE 2 INTO RW-DIGIT.

To calculate the high order digit position for a first approximation
in RW-SQRT above:

  COMPUTE RW-DIGIT = 10 - RW-DIGIT.

In summary, to create a 'good' first approximation using the above:

  INSPECT RW-NUMBER TALLYING RW-DIGIT FOR LEADING ZEROS.
  COMPUTE RW-DIGIT = 10 - ((9 - RW-DIGIT) / 2).
  MOVE "5" TO RW-APPROX(RW-DIGIT:1).

Then loop through Newton's Approximation a half dozen times or so.

  PERFORM 6 TIMES
      COMPUTE RW-SQRT = (RW-NUMBER / RW-SQRT + RW-SQRT) * .5
  END-PERFORM.

For a process like this, it is easy to eat up more machine time in
calculating the approximation than you save by having a better
approximation.  Some machines have efficient scanning instructions
which the COBOL compiler uses to implement constructs like the
INSPECT above.  The Unisys Medium Systems, for example, would take
only two inline instructions to do that INSPECT.

I'm not recommending this method as a general approach.  Just more
food for thought. :-)  The following program will run using MF COBOL
3.2, but you may have to change the ACCEPT for other compilers which
do not automatically align the decimal point.

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.    SQRT.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       77  WS-NUMBER           PIC  ZZZ,ZZZ,ZZ9.999999999.
       77  WS-SQRT             PIC  ZZZ,ZZZ,ZZ9.999999999.
       01  ROOT-WORK.
           03  RW-DIGIT        PIC S9(02)       COMP     VALUE 0.
           03  RW-NUMBER       PIC  9(09)V9(09) DISPLAY.
           03  RW-SQRT         PIC  9(09)V9(09) DISPLAY  VALUE 0.
           03  RW-APPROX       REDEFINES RW-SQRT
                                   PIC  X(18).
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       COMPUTE-ROOT.
           DISPLAY "Enter number for Square Root".
           ACCEPT RW-NUMBER.
           MOVE RW-NUMBER TO WS-NUMBER.

           INSPECT RW-NUMBER TALLYING RW-DIGIT FOR LEADING ZEROS.
           COMPUTE RW-DIGIT = 10 - ((9 - RW-DIGIT) / 2).
           MOVE "5" TO RW-APPROX(RW-DIGIT:1).
           PERFORM 6 TIMES
               COMPUTE RW-SQRT = (RW-NUMBER / RW-SQRT + RW-SQRT) * .5
           END-PERFORM.

           MOVE RW-SQRT TO WS-SQRT.
           DISPLAY WS-NUMBER.
           DISPLAY WS-SQRT.
           STOP RUN.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Book: Horrible Things You Can Do In COBOL

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36B20280.7F18D319@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<jimkeo-0901991338080001@ip15.islip8.ny.pub-ip.psi.net> <EL1m2.4594$hs6.3364@nnrp2.clara.net> <369a0e2b.327867688@news.enterprise.net> <369FCF87.C4EDF587@net.ntl.com> <77tafq$ta6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36A49200.8C69D71C@home.com> <36AA16B8.C70FE5B4@net.ntl.com> <%Cqq2.1148$oi.1096861@news3.mia> <78ouff$g3r$1@news.fsu.edu> <78r2q7$b9i$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <6L9s2.319$Z9.317619@news4.mia> <36B1CE23.AE3782FF@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

>
> >
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> precision for the task and testing where your answer is not perfect.

If that is the method I think it is, Newton's method tries to find the
point where the parabola
           y = x**2 -K
crosses the x-axis where you are trying to find the square root of K.  On
any guess, it calculates y and then where a line tangent to the parabola
at that point would cross the x-axis for the next try.  After computing
_y_ compute the next _x_ =  x - y / 2.0 / x . Do that in a loop until _y_
gets close enough to zero.  Starting with 50 as a first guess of the
sqrt(100), the next tries are 26, 14.92, 10.81, 10.03, 10.000047, and
10.000001 .
```

#### ↳ Re: Book: Horrible Things You Can Do In COBOL

- **From:** Albert Ratzlaff <albert@conexion.com.py>
- **Date:** 1999-01-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3699CAFD.52CF3B8A@conexion.com.py>`
- **References:** `<jimkeo-0901991338080001@ip15.islip8.ny.pub-ip.psi.net>`

```
Jim Keohane wrote:

>
>    You get the picture. Email me if you have any such inglorious tasks
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks for an anticipated overwhelming response! {fat chance}  - Jim

It was'nt such a big deal, but for those who remember the NCR cash registers, there was a 2126 model
that had a very special file format. Fixed size blocks, but variable sized records in every block.
They had a 2-byte VLI to indicate the record length. Each record consisted of variable length items,
each of them also with a VLI. The items had fields (in a later model - the 2127 - the type and length
of the fields depended on a bitmap field). So the challenge was to convert this file format to a fixed
length record and field format, to be able to use the data from COBOL programs. It worked, but I had
to convert later to a C preprocesor for speed.

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Book: Horrible Things You Can Do In COBOL

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1999-01-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369a0eaa.327994360@news.enterprise.net>`
- **References:** `<jimkeo-0901991338080001@ip15.islip8.ny.pub-ip.psi.net> <3699CAFD.52CF3B8A@conexion.com.py>`

```
On Mon, 11 Jan 1999 06:57:17 -0300, Albert Ratzlaff
<albert@conexion.com.py> wrote:


>It was'nt such a big deal, but for those who remember the NCR cash registers, there was a 2126 model
>that had a very special file format. Fixed size blocks, but variable sized records in every block.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>to convert later to a C preprocesor for speed.

The IBM 4680 (80286) cash register's software is a preemptive
multitasking (18 tasks) system with protect mode ram. It distributes
it's database across a network of up to 16 tills and performs
transparent disaster recovery across them should one fail. 

All written in COBOL. No C in there at all. ;-)
```

```
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I was using RM/COBOL (still do). It's pretty eficient with file handling, but because it is interpreted,
the string handling isn't the fastest around.

I also have a couple of tools like a menu generator (nifty, with pulldown bars and stuff), a screen
painter, a program to read PBX data off a serial port (sends window popup messages when someone talks too
long), and now i'm working on a Unix server to accept conection requests from Windows (Delphi), something
like database stored procedures.

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Book: Horrible Things You Can Do In COBOL

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369A4C14.4E721AA9@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<jimkeo-0901991338080001@ip15.islip8.ny.pub-ip.psi.net> <3699CAFD.52CF3B8A@conexion.com.py>`

```


Albert Ratzlaff wrote:

> Jim Keohane wrote:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> fields depended on a bitmap field).

Sounds like the file created by some telephone switches.  Some are your basic vanilla IBM Variable
Blocked, Variable Record length files but often the blocks have a fixed length with padding.  Crammed with
COMP-3 fields, the records on these files will find their way to your phone bill.  The block header
includes a sequence number and sometimes other fields in addition to the size (in binary of course) of the
block.
```

#### ↳ Re: Book: Horrible Things You Can Do In COBOL

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369b1488.99734847@netnews>`
- **References:** `<jimkeo-0901991338080001@ip15.islip8.ny.pub-ip.psi.net>`

```
'Twas Sat, 09 Jan 1999 13:38:08 -0400, when jimkeo@multi-platforms.com
(Jim Keohane) illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>   A COBOL language/syntax parser/tokenizer written in COBOL itself.

The Unisys Cobol translator was written in Cobol-68, so it didn't even use
STRING and UNSTRING.  Everything was done with loops to move one character
at a time.  But the most un-Cobolish thing it did was the way it stored
the tokenized program in a heap which was rolled out to disk if it
exceeded some size.

>   COBOL low resolution graphics.

I did that in integer Basic on the Apple ][.  Cobol could hardly be worse.

>   COBOL disassembler which compared zapped modules to their unzapped
>   versions and generated the missing AMASPZAP statements.

Actually I once wrote a Cobol program which read through object programs
and modified filenames.  

>   Prime number and Fibonacci series generator and more. {dumb}

Those are incredibly simple programs, too simple to care what language
they're written in.  I write those in whatever language is handy.  

>Well, there must be much better war stories out there and I'm seriously
>planning to collect some for a book.

On Burroughs (now Unisys) the Cobol compilers were full featured.  We
wrote *everything* in Cobol.  The Message Control System was written in
Cobol.  Our multi-threaded editor was written in Cobol.  Our print-spooler
was written in Cobol.  I wrote a command interface for the operators in
Cobol.

The only truly horrible thing you can do in Cobol is to use a Cobol
compiler from a vendor who says there are things you *shouldn't* do in
Cobol.  
```

```
I've got three search-programs, with a sort-staement in them.  They work
when you run them seperately, but not when they are linked in
one program.

When running the program ==> error 223 (Microsoft Cobol 4.5 compiler)

How can i fix this?
```

#### ↳ Re: Sort problem

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<778gof$2pb$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<777r0k$k3q@news3.euro.net>`

```
The guide says: Sort/Merge error: see status key(fatal).
You probably have a file that is not being closed, but
is being closed by default when a standalone program
is run.  Check the status key of the file that fails to
find out.

Joeri De Backer wrote in message <777r0k$k3q@news3.euro.net>...
>I've got three search-programs, with a sort-staement in them.  They work
>when you run them seperately, but not when they are linked in
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Sort problem

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-01-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3699E9C4.B9D6301D@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<777r0k$k3q@news3.euro.net>`

```
Joeri De Backer wrote:

> I've got three search-programs, with a sort-staement in them.  They work
> when you run them seperately, but not when they are linked in
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> How can i fix this?

My Microfocus (NetExpress, but those guy's re-use their software) says for
RTS error 233:
[start MF quote]
223 SORT/MERGE error: see status keys Fatal
You have tried a SORT/MERGE operation which has been unsuccessful for some
reason. You might have had too many files open when you tried a SORT/MERGE
operation, or the file which you are trying to access might be locked.
Alternatively, you have set the TMP environment variable to point to a
directory that does not exist.
Resolution:
The action you should take depends on the situation in which it occurs.
Either set TMP to point to a directory that does exist, or unset TMP.
[end MF quote]
Are you sure that:
1 - The files "touched" in each sort are not open when entering that sort?
2 - No locking from within another program occurs?
3 - The TMP variable is properly set?
4 - No one sort is within the scope (i.e. input or output procedure) of
another one?

Post your code to get more answers with a lesser quessing-level.

The Frog
```

```
See a couple of separate threads on

   "Merant"

(or as I indicated - something between
    ME  rant
       and
    small insects (ant) running under water (mer)

Easy to know how to pronounce -
   It rhymes with
        ERRANT
```

<a id="t-fc853a1f9c"></a>
## Thread: Mainframe vs. supermini — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: Mainframe vs. supermini

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<778g6f$2cf$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<76p5o4$l8u@netaxs.com> <76qvdp$67g$2@teabag.demon.co.uk> <76r553$22s$1@nntp1.uunet.ca>`

```
This is the first time that I have ever tried to cross post
something ... I thought some people in this NG would
enjoy this ..

In article <76r553$22s$1@nntp1.uunet.ca>,
atbowler@thinkage.on.ca (Alan Bowler) writes:
> I think its was because DEC prided itself as the inventor of the
> mini.  I asked the same question once of a computer science prof
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> on what 1 individual could do.).

Actually, I dug up an old post covering this topic by Tom Harrington
a couple of years ago (hope nobody minds me reposting it)

Chris.

From: tph@longhorn.uucp (Tom Harrington)
Newsgroups: alt.folklore.computers
Subject: Re: What's a VAX
Date: 5 Feb 1997 20:11:16 GMT
Organization: Mechanist Industries

Matthew Crosby (crosby@nordsieck.cs.colorado.edu) wrote:

: These days, it seems "mainframe" has come to mean "any computer I do not
: sit at the console of" in popular parlace.

: I've also heard HP 9000/400s and Sun 3s described as mainframes.  Sigh.

Oh, good.  Now I have an excuse to repost my "It might be a mainframe
if..." list (once again with absolutely no apologies to Jeff Forworthy).


It might be a mainframe if...
-----------------------------

If you could kill someone by tipping it over on them, it might be a
mainframe.

If the only "mouse" it has is the one living inside it, it might be a
mainframe.

If you need earth-moving equipment to relocate it, it might be a
mainframe. (thanks to Tor Sjowall)

If you've ever lost an oscilloscope inside of it, it might be a
mainframe. (thanks to Tim Shoppa)

If it's big enough to be used as an apartment, it might be a
mainframe.

If it has ever had a card-punch designed for it, it might be a
mainframe.

If it weighs more than an RV, it might be a mainframe.

If lights in the neighborhood dim when it's powered up, it might be a
mainframe.

If it arrived in its own moving van, it might be a mainframe.

If its disk platters are big enough to cook pizzas on, it might be a
mainframe.

If Michael Jordan would need his entire annual salary to buy one, it
might be a mainframe.

If keeping all of the manuals together creates a fire hazard, it might
be a mainframe.

If it's so large that a dropped pen will slowly orbit it, it might be a
mainframe.

If it's ever been mistaken for a refrigerator, (or if the disk drive
has ever been mistaken for a washing machine), it might be a
mainframe.

If anyone has ever frozen to death in the room where it's kept, it
might be a mainframe.

If it has a power supply that's bigger than your car, it might be a
mainframe.

If it has its own postal code, it might be a mainframe.

If the operators considered the addition of COBOL to be an upgrade, it
might be a mainframe.

If it was designed before you were born, it might be a mainframe.

If its main power cable is thicker than your neck, it might be a
mainframe.

If the designers have since died from old age, it might be a
mainframe.


: In fact, it seems modern usage goes something like:
<snip>
: "WWW" - Their ISPs computer

And of course "WWW", "web", and "internet" are synonyms, right?
```

#### ↳ Re: Mainframe vs. supermini

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-01-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36980f82.531356623@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<76p5o4$l8u@netaxs.com> <76qvdp$67g$2@teabag.demon.co.uk> <76r553$22s$1@nntp1.uunet.ca> <778g6f$2cf$1@news.igs.net>`

```
On Sat, 9 Jan 1999 16:07:59 -0500, "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
wrote:

>This is the first time that I have ever tried to cross post
>something ... I thought some people in this NG would
>enjoy this ..

Well thanks.  You just confirmed my worst fears.  The new box we just
switched too --  isn't a mainframe.

It's about the size of a dishwasher - has 100 Gig of Disk (and that's
with 1/3 of the RAIDset removed) and a ton of Ram (I can't remember
how much) runs OS/390 - with 3 or 4 CICS regions, TCP/IP, ISPF, TSO
heck all that stuff.  But I can put it in my cube and still jog laps.
It's NOT a mainframe!  ARRRGGHH!!!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframe vs. supermini

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369a26e9.0@news1.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<76p5o4$l8u@netaxs.com> <76qvdp$67g$2@teabag.demon.co.uk> <76r553$22s$1@nntp1.uunet.ca> <778g6f$2cf$1@news.igs.net> <36980f82.531356623@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Hey, our mainframe is jet black with a big red stripe up the middle, and is
smaller than my refrigerator at home; but it was made in 1996.  The DASD is
from the mid 1980s, though, and they're the size (including storage
controllers) of 20 refrigerators for a mere 300 gigs (you should see the
mess of bus and tag cables under the floor).
```

## Thread: Apologies for multiple copies of posts — 1 msgs

### Apologies for multiple copies of posts

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<778itg$rp6$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
I had a problem communicating with the news server
at the ISP.

I tried sending the posts four times over several hours.
In each and every case, Outlook Express provided no
indication the posts had been sent. In fact, in every
case, I had to press the Stop button due to timeouts.

I finally moved the posts to the Draft folder and did not
attempt to re-send the posts.

If you are surprised to see multiple posts, then you
can imagine my surprise at seeing two posts that I
never "actually" sent showing up in the newsgroup!
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

<a id="t-4a3e1d65e7"></a>
## Thread: Intriguing (use your own judgement if it is Off-Topic) — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### Intriguing (use your own judgement if it is Off-Topic)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<776rl1$ano@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`

```
I don't usually pass on "advertisements" but the following item was just
distributed in the IBM-MAIN ListServer/NG.  I canNOT vouch for what it
says/promises, but I thought it might be of some interest to SOME of the
people who read this NG - but not IBM-MAIN.

    ****

For those of you that would like to take your mainframe with you when you
travel, I recommend that you look at FLEX/ESL from Fundamental Software
(http://www.funsoft.com/).

I have a Laptop (PII 266MHZ w/160MB and 6G Disk).  It is setup to dual boot
both Windows 98 and Linux.  On the Linux side I startup FLEX/ESL which is a
software emulator for the ESA/370 instruction set.  I then bring up
VSE+CICS+TCPIP + our product which is IpServer (Could also run OS/390).
It's pretty nice to have a 8 pound mainframe.  We plan to have it at the
Share Conference on Feb 22-24 in San Francisco at the Data 21 booth if you
would like to see it in action.

As far as performance, it is a little faster then a P390-500 when doing
compiles.  When doing CICS type functions or Web Browser access to our Web
Server (IpServer) it is noticeably faster.  When using an FTP client to
access LIBR under VSE I noticed that it will display a directory about 30%
faster.  Remember this is running on a single processor on a laptop where
the disk access is not very fast.  I believe that the performance gain can
be attributed to the hard disk cache that is being used (additional
performance can be had by running OPTI-CACHE from Barnard Software).

If you would like additional information regarding performance when running
on a DELL dual processor PC, check out Jeff Barnards (of Barnard Software)
review at http://www.bsiopti.com/.

*---------------------------------------*
Mike Carver - Lead Developer for IpServer
mike@data21.com (310)214-4101
IpServer:
Native CICS Web Server
CGI Programming in Command Level CICS
HTTP://www.data21.com
*---------------------------------------*
```

#### ↳ Re: Intriguing (use your own judgement if it is Off-Topic)

- **From:** pdw@funsoft.com
- **Date:** 1999-01-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77b958$5hr$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<776rl1$ano@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`

```


Jeff Barnard corrected one of Mike's statement about our mainframe on a laptop
in the message below.  Also, I note the laptop model will be called FLEX-ESP,
which will be and ESL rated S/390 mainframe officially offered quite shortly.
Please contact me at the email address below if you would like to receive a
quote or other information about our new mainframes.

Peter D. Ward
Fundamental Software
510-491-0151 (direct)
pdw@database.com (preferred)

----------------------------------------------------------------
Jeff Barnard said:

Mike,

Cool. I expect to have our VSE Notebook at the VSE & VM Tech Conference
in May. So anyone interested can stop by and check out VSE running on a
notebook. We will have all our products installed on it too.

One note from your post. The FLEX-ES emulator (or micro-code) is ESA/370
not just S/370. This means that VSE runs in ESA mode with 31-bit
storage, access registers and everything else. VSE/ESA does not use or
require ESA/390 features only ESA/370. VSE/ESA 2.4 CICS TS will use
SubSystem Storage Protection and is VSE's first use of ESA/390 features.

Oh, yes. If you want to see speed in an FTP Server you can download the
BSI FTP server on Monday afternoon (TCP/IP-TOOLS 1.33).  Looking at the
directories of POWER queues, VSAM catalogs or VSE libraries (etc) is so
fast you will not believe it. I am working on the doc now. Anyone else
that wants to 'play with' or test our new FTP server is welcome to
download it also. Have fun!

Best regards,
Jeff
```

```
ENTS

II, COBOL for MVS & VM, or COBOL/VSE - those are available at other
locations as well)
```

## Thread: D - my project — 5 msgs

### D - my project

- **From:** dupavoy@aol.combatSPAM (Dupavoy)
- **Date:** 1999-01-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990110130704.01576.00007798@ng36.aol.com>`

```
I am trying to create a program that I want
to market to video stores.
It should list members' names, address,
phone#, video rented, etc...
I want it to be done in either Basic/pascal/C/Cobol.
And I want to charge $100 for it.
I figure that with 10000 video stores in the
city I could make atleast $250,000.
Does it sound like a good idea?
Any suggestions would be appreciated.
```

#### ↳ Re: D - my project

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-01-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369924ee.602387416@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<19990110130704.01576.00007798@ng36.aol.com>`

```
On 10 Jan 1999 18:07:04 GMT, dupavoy@aol.combatSPAM (Dupavoy) wrote:

>I am trying to create a program that I want
>to market to video stores.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Any suggestions would be appreciated.

It's an ambitous project.  Most video stores have their own systems
however.  What will yours provide that will get them to switch?  

Also, $100.00 is pretty cheap.  Are you going to be providing
technical support?    

I think the market is saturated - but you never know - maybe you have
that one innovative idea that will make your system so much better
that everyone with switch to it!
```

#### ↳ Re: D - my project

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77b9ih$mp3$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<19990110130704.01576.00007798@ng36.aol.com>`

```
Something you should be aware of before starting
is that it costs more to write a low cost program than
to write a high cost one.  If you are serious about
making a profit at selling your code, you would be
better off attempting to write a $10,000 program. You
are far more likely to make money.

I have, BTW, written five systems now that have had
better than 1/2 a million in sales.

Dupavoy wrote in message <19990110130704.01576.00007798@ng36.aol.com>...
>I am trying to create a program that I want
>to market to video stores.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: D - my project

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369b731f.0@news1.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<19990110130704.01576.00007798@ng36.aol.com>`

```
I want to know which city has ten thousand video stores.

What's more, I want to know which city has ten thousand video stores all
without any sort of computer automation already in place.

Our local store chain has it's own full video system now, BTW.

It's going away though, in the merger process the new company will implement
one of a number of possible existing third-party packages to replace our
home-grown one.  So such systems do exist.

I've got to admit, writing an entire system sounds a bit ambitious, but it
could be done.  Our video systems handles 200 stores (each about 1/4 the
size of a Blockbuster), and is one of the smallest systems we have.

However, it does incorporate a batch system on the mainframe; an online
system on the mainframe; plus data transfer jobs to push data back and forth
to the Unix servers (where the data is collected from the stores), plus the
online system on the server, plus the system in the store's In Store
Processor.  This involves, JCL, COBOL, COBOL-CICS, COBOL-DB2, FTP, MVS,
Unix, Informix SQL, Informix 4GL, plus knowledge of proprietary third party
Point of Sale software system.

Although I can guess that you mean a single PC system, self-contained, I
think that the majority of outlets are really a part of chains.

You've got you're work cut out for you.


Dupavoy wrote in message <19990110130704.01576.00007798@ng36.aol.com>...
>I am trying to create a program that I want
>to market to video stores.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
Welcome to 1999, we are getting even more international posts for Job offers
as well as resumes, the year is starting off GREAT.

Please remember that you can also post your job offer or resume in HTML if you
choose to do so.

The Automatic EMAIL notification list is growing, this option will send you an
EMAIL the instant a message is posted so you will be aware of ANY new messages
posted on the board, without the need to check !

area that can locate IBM consultants, consulting, resumes, jobs, and news

please send an EMAIL for more info.

after all it is for you.

Resumes Board: http://www.programming-services.com/ibm/index.shtml

out.
```

```
alphanumeric literals) is on the way out.

My favorite "obscure" test is something like this:

    05  Num    Pic  S9(03)v99.
            ...
     Move     -
-      123.
-      45  to Num.

(you should see all the compilers that get sick on that perfectly legal
code.)
```

## Thread: Fujitsu - Version 4 error codes — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu - Version 4 error codes

- **From:** "Vic Spainhower" <vicspain@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77eo2m$75q$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`

```
If this gets posted multiple times then I apologize but I've been have
trouble posting messages and I'm not sure if this went out!
-----

Hello,

I have a program written in Power Cobol using a document created with Power
Form. The problem is when I do the Write to the "HEAD" partition using
"WRITE AFTER ADVANCING PAGE " it cancels with the following message :

JMP0320I-U INPUT/OUTPUT ERROR, STM=WRITE. FILE=PRTFILE, ERRCODE=9022

I don't have a clue what this means and I can't seem to locate these error
codes in the documentation that I just received with the enterprise edition.

Does anyone have these error codes documented?
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu - Version 4 error codes

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77fcui$s61@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<77eo2m$75q$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`

```
I don't know that message (or even that compiler very well) but just a
though.  Do you have a LINAGE clause in your file's FD?  If not, I think the
AFTER ADVANCING PAGE will have a problem.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu - Version 4 error codes

- **From:** "Gary Roush" <support@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77hfvv$i5$1@remarQ.com>`
- **References:** `<77eo2m$75q$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`

```
Vic,

The 9022 error code is telling you that it is unable to open or close the
file. In this case, the file for printing.  The 90 is an error about File
opening and closing and the 22 is the type of error related to 90. I suspect
it is the error of FORM RTS, which is related to Power COBOL. You can read
this in the Fujitsu COBOL Users Guide 4.0 on Appendex B which starts on page
927, which is either in the manual or on the CD.  Hope this helps.

Gary Roush
Fujitsu COBOL Support
gkr@adtools.com


Vic Spainhower wrote in message <77eo2m$75q$1@juliana.sprynet.com>...

edition.
```

## Thread: COBOL Employment? — 4 msgs

### COBOL Employment?

- **From:** ke4yqz-nospam@mindspring.com (Roger D. Cravens, Jr.)
- **Date:** 1999-01-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369a7b9e.91738543@news.mindspring.com>`

```
I have a question for those programmers that are NOT located
in Georgia.  

It's been my experience here in the metro-Atlanta area that 
companies go through programmers like water through
a seive.  The company hires a programmer then within
90-120 days fires them.  That was not my experience while
I was in Kansas City, Missouri.

I myself have been fired more than just several times since
moving to Atlanta..  I recommened to one employer that during 
the time we re-wrote the code, why not take care of the Y2K 
problem that everyone's going to face in a few years anyway.  He
called me in the next morning and I was out looking for a job 10
minutes later.  Georgia is not a "right-to-work" state so getting
a straight and honest answer from an employer hasn't worked
either for me or others.  It appears that being either laid-off
or being fired frequently goes along with the job title!!

It's just not me, I've talked to other COBOL programmers
and my experience has been the same as theirs.  Just 
curious about what is going through these employers
minds when they hire someone?  It would seem to me
that once you have a programmer on staff, then get him/her
trained you would want to keep them.  Not here in Atlanta!?!

I don't honestly know.  I spent well over 20 years working
for the Government, then retired and went out in the
private market.  So far, it's been a real and total bitch
both to find a job, as well as keep one over 120 days.


Roger
```

## Thread: Why does it accept Numerics in Alphabetic Pictures ?? — 13 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Why does it accept Numerics in Alphabetic Pictures ??

- **From:** mafil@globetrotter.net
- **Date:** 1999-01-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77doa8$8uo$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I'm using Microfocus COBOL 4.00.32 and when declaring an alphabetic picture, I
still can use digits and spaces.. Is it normal ?

Martin

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Why does it accept Numerics in Alphabetic Pictures ??

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77e0cs$5rm@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<77doa8$8uo$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
You bet,
  Although some (but not all) "hardware" (terminals/monitors) provide a way
of blocking input to NUMERIC fields (without such characters as "+" or "."),
almost none support blocking Alphabetic (Pic A - much less Pic X) input to
alphabetic characters.

P.S.  Isn't "space" defined as alphabetic (I haven't checked this out
recently)

P.P.S.  Are you talking about "input fields" being blocked or are you
talking about not getting "data exceptions" when you have invalid data in a
field?
```

```
Martin

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why does it accept Numerics in Alphabetic Pictures ??

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77g4hi$42u@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<77doa8$8uo$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <77e0cs$5rm@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <77ff2s$ms1$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
As I said earlier, I don't know of any way (with any current hardware) to
"block" numerics at the input stage.  Therefore, would you are going to have
to do is CHECK (validate) the input that is provided - and send back an
"error message" if incorrectly formatted items are provided.  I don't (never
did) hardware, so I don't know why most systems have a way to "block" to
numerics only but don't have a way to block to alphabets only.  I assume it
has something to do with "book keeping" type data entry - but that is a
guess only.

Bottom-line:
    Do IF Alphabetic and IF Numeric and IF Space checks on each byte of your
input - and send an error message when input is incorrect.  (Or define
user-defined CLASSes via Special-Names - or if you have a system where it is
available, use the VALIDATE verb - now available as an extensions and
included in the draft of the next Standard.)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Why does it accept Numerics in Alphabetic Pictures ??

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369aa607.86530584@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<77doa8$8uo$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <77e0cs$5rm@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
On Mon, 11 Jan 1999 17:09:17 -0600, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>You bet,
>  Although some (but not all) "hardware" (terminals/monitors) provide a way
>of blocking input to NUMERIC fields (without such characters as "+" or "."),
>almost none support blocking Alphabetic (Pic A - much less Pic X) input to
>alphabetic characters.

I completely ignored PIC A in my book.  Mostly because I could not get
any compiler (other than the MVS compiler) to behave the way I thought
it should based on my reading of the standard and many COBOL manuals.
In YOUR understanding, how are PIC A items different from PIC X items?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why does it accept Numerics in Alphabetic Pictures ??

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77ebva$1c0@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<77doa8$8uo$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <77e0cs$5rm@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <369aa607.86530584@news1.ibm.net>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote in message <369aa607.86530584@news1.ibm.net>...
>On Mon, 11 Jan 1999 17:09:17 -0600, "William M. Klein"
><wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
>
>>You bet,
>>  Although some (but not all) "hardware" (terminals/monitors) provide a
way
>>of blocking input to NUMERIC fields (without such characters as "+" or
"."),
>>almost none support blocking Alphabetic (Pic A - much less Pic X) input to
>>alphabetic characters.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>In YOUR understanding, how are PIC A items different from PIC X items?

How does it work?  Well not really very differently than PIC X.  Some
"theoretical" differences are:

   Initializing Replacing should work differently for alphabetic than
alphanumeric in

      Initialize Group
           Replacing Alphabetic by "X"
                             Alphanumeric by "Y"

          ******

    05  Pgm-Name   Pic A(08) Value "ABCDEFGH"
        ....
    Call Pgm-Name

should either get a syntax error or an "extension" flag.

    ****

I think there are a couple of other VERY MINOR differences, but they don't
come to mind immediately.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why does it accept Numerics in Alphabetic Pictures ??

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369B5B9D.44A93A39@home.com>`
- **References:** `<77doa8$8uo$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <77e0cs$5rm@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <369aa607.86530584@news1.ibm.net> <77ebva$1c0@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
>       Initialize Group
>            Replacing Alphabetic by "X"
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> should either get a syntax error or an "extension" flag.

I stopped using PIC A decades ago.  I never saw any advantage in a
construct which is designed to give an uncontrolled error.  I can check
for alphabetic myself and control what happens with PIC X.  I guess it
was designed before programming techniques!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why does it accept Numerics in Alphabetic Pictures ??

- **From:** mafil@globetrotter.net
- **Date:** 1999-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77ff67$msi$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<77doa8$8uo$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <77e0cs$5rm@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <369aa607.86530584@news1.ibm.net>`

```
In article <369aa607.86530584@news1.ibm.net>,
  redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:
> I completely ignored PIC A in my book.  Mostly because I could not get
> any compiler (other than the MVS compiler) to behave the way I thought
> it should based on my reading of the standard and many COBOL manuals.
> In YOUR understanding, how are PIC A items different from PIC X items?

In books, it is supposed to let the user gives only alphabetics and space.
Like if I wanted one to enter a valid postal code like H3E 4CR.

Martin

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why does it accept Numerics in Alphabetic Pictures ??

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369B5DBD.F5C78035@home.com>`
- **References:** `<77doa8$8uo$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <77e0cs$5rm@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <369aa607.86530584@news1.ibm.net> <77ff67$msi$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
> In books, it is supposed to let the user gives only alphabetics and space.
> Like if I wanted one to enter a valid postal code like H3E 4CR.

I've done that with batch entry.  I needed to validate postal codes
which were hand-written by users then typed by data entry operators.  We
had years of non-validated data which may have multiple spaces, and
postal codes from around the world.

I couldn't refuse to accept data which had two spaces.  I also had to
have my program guess at misspelled states and provinces, (or obsolete
abbreviations).  Canadian postal code format is almost unique, but U.S.
format is ubiquitous.  Obviously, I can't use PIC A to check my data
entry which was entered in the past.
```

#### ↳ Re: Why does it accept Numerics in Alphabetic Pictures ??

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77e0gf$2vf@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<77doa8$8uo$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
You bet,
  Although some (but not all) "hardware" (terminals/monitors) provide a way
of blocking input to NUMERIC fields (without such characters as "+" or "."),
almost none support blocking Alphabetic (Pic A - much less Pic X) input to
alphabetic characters.

P.S.  Isn't "space" defined as alphabetic (I haven't checked this out
recently)

P.P.S.  Are you talking about "input fields" being blocked or are you
talking about not getting "data exceptions" when you have invalid data in a
field?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Why does it accept Numerics in Alphabetic Pictures ??

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369AF9DC.A19785D5@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<77doa8$8uo$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <77e0gf$2vf@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> You bet,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> recently)   

"Space" is considered to be an alphabetic character, in ALPHABETIC,
ALPHABETIC-UPPER, and ALPHABETIC-LOWER class tests, in IBM COBOL II and
COBOL/MVS.

I recently had to do some complicated class testing, including a class I
defined under SPECIAL-NAMES and I was concerned about run-time
performance.  So I used the LIST option to get a disassembly and found
that class tests are implemented with a single TRT instruction
(translate and test).
```

#### ↳ Re: Why does it accept Numerics in Alphabetic Pictures ??

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369d9f10.11521186@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<77doa8$8uo$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
On Mon, 11 Jan 1999 20:52:25 GMT, mafil@globetrotter.net wrote:

>I'm using Microfocus COBOL 4.00.32 and when declaring an alphabetic picture, I
>still can use digits and spaces.. Is it normal ?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own    

Sure, that's the way the cookie crumbles....

I assume you try the following

Working-Storage Section.

01 Input-Data PIC AA9AA9.

Procedure Division.

ACCEPT Input-Data FROM CONSOLE
.
.
.

And want the system to allow an input of AB1CD2

but reject, possibly block the terminal with ABC   or  X1

This would require the runtime system to monitor the terminal input and check every single
keystroke.  This is more or less unfeasible, as the CPU usually doesn't handle the
keyboard (in a mainframe environment), and only processes the complete set of data
presented only when the ENTER key is pressed.

COBOL has to maintain standards and portability across platforms, and thus the behaviour
you want cannot be provided by COBOL, as it is impossible to achieve in, let's say, a
mainframe IBM 3270 environment.

Actually, I still have to find a valid reason to define a PIC string containing A...


with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

#### ↳ Re: Why does it accept Numerics in Alphabetic Pictures ??

- **From:** sbricklin@aol.com (SBRICKLIN)
- **Date:** 1999-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990114213742.00908.00000329@ng26.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<77doa8$8uo$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
It is OK until you will try to perform mathem. operations with this field, OC7
waiting for you
================================
let say:
01  fielda pic 9(3)v99  value 100.00..
01 fileldb pic x(6) value spaces.
:
:
move fieldb to fielda.
```

## Thread: MOVE 'Merant plc' TO ws-Micro-Focus-Group-plc? — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MOVE 'Merant plc' TO ws-Micro-Focus-Group-plc?

- **From:** "Peter Dean" <peter.dean@cableol.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77dud6$674$1@nclient3-gui.server.ntli.net>`

```
Hi all,

As a shareholder in the UK company I was slightly taken aback to find an EGM
notice
in my letterbox a few days back and asking me to vote on the proposal to
rename the company to 'Merant'.

Aside from the enclosed
'isnt-everything-in-the-garden-rosy-just-forget-that-Intersolv-fiasco'
blurb, does anybody here have any views on the real reasons behind the move?

I'd be interested in hearing informed?/insider? opinions...

Ta
```

#### ↳ Re: MOVE 'Merant plc' TO ws-Micro-Focus-Group-plc?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77dvsr$5gu@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<77dud6$674$1@nclient3-gui.server.ntli.net>`

```

Peter Dean wrote in message <77dud6$674$1@nclient3-gui.server.ntli.net>...
>Hi all,
>
>As a shareholder in the UK company I was slightly taken aback to find an
EGM
>notice
>in my letterbox a few days back and asking me to vote on the proposal to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>blurb, does anybody here have any views on the real reasons behind the
move?
>
>I'd be interested in hearing informed?/insider? opinions...
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>


NOTE: the following is from another non-employee (former employee though)
who is also a stock holder.  These are OPINIONS only - nothing official!!!

A) The "reason" is that with the acquisition of Intersolve, they wanted to
come up with a "new image" that was not tied to either of the old companies

B)  "Micro Focus" really has NOT reflected their true focus for years.
Furthermore, they now want to get away from their previous "COBOL only"
image (which also hasn't really been true for years).

C)  (see separate note for my HUMOROUS views of the new name)  However, the
best explanation of the new name's origin is:

    Merant (accent on first syllable, like merit)
    n. [from Latin root: 'merere' -- 'to deserve', meritum
                                                  -- 'deserved', source of
English 'merit'.

D) In my personal opinion this is a LOUSY idea.  This is from someone who
followed (a decade ago) the change of Xpediter's vendor's name from "ADS" to
"Centura" (about one year before they were bought by Compuware).  The cost
of such a name change along with the lose of "brand name recognition" seems
awful to me.  Having said that, I suspect there is about ZERO chance of
stopping the change at this time.
```

#### ↳ Merant (humor)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```

Peter Dean wrote in message <77dud6$674$1@nclient3-gui.server.ntli.net>...
>Hi all,
>
>As a shareholder in the UK company I was slightly taken aback to find an
EGM
>notice
>in my letterbox a few days back and asking me to vote on the proposal to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>blurb, does anybody here have any views on the real reasons behind the
move?
>
>I'd be interested in hearing informed?/insider? opinions...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>


See separate note for "serious" reply.  The following is what I sent to
various current and past MF employees.

Any way - as this "name" says very little to me (unlike "Centura" which at
least seemed an improvement over "ADS"), I have been thinking about what
could this mean/be derived from.  My current favorites are:

  Software that is particularly buggy when run under water
      (coming from French word for "sea" and English word for pesky insects
seen at picnics)

  What I used to do when no one would fix my mainframe compatibility PDRs
  (It wasn't him who ranted it was ME RANT)

  What is heard in Boston during Masses where a new problem has been found
by Microsoft
   (Mea culpa, mea culpa,  mera NT  - where the last "mera" is pronounced
with a Boston accept)

  Mainframe
  Express
  Really
  Adds
  Nice (naughty? no? needed?)
  Technology
```

#### ↳ Re: java to cobol converter ?

- **From:** "David Sanders" <dsanders@synkronix.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369abf94$0$16671@nntp1.ba.best.com>`
- **References:** `<77e5mu$kun$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
There is a tool to compile Cobol to Java, it is called PERCobol.
(www.synkronix.com)

PERCobol allows you to continue to work in Cobol, and easily interface with
Java objects.
(Your Cobol will run on the Java Virtual Machine.)

(I don't think that there is a tool to convert Java to Cobol though!)

Regards,
David Sanders
Synkronix, Inc.



ivgirish@hotmail.com wrote in message <77e5mu$kun$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369B586E.CE455A3A@home.com>`
- **References:** `<77e5mu$kun$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
> hi,
> are there any tools to convert Java to cobol ?
> 
> thanks for your help,
> Girish.

Interesting question - There's no way I would want to convert between
Java and COBOL (either direction).  Converted code would be very hard to
maintain and would likely be inefficient as well.

It points out the need to use the right tool to begin with.
```

<a id="t-0e93993817"></a>
## Thread: PACKED CODE — 3 msgs

### PACKED CODE

- **From:** amonaci@protec.it (Andrea Monaci)
- **Date:** 1999-01-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3699ceb3.3152640@news.protec.it>`

```
HI

If a record, belonging to a file DATA, is composed from the following digits 
546876890 and it must be read setting the field of the FD codified PACKED, I
should do:

      fd DATI
           label records standard
           record 9
           data record Rec-dati.
       01  Rec-dati pic 9(9) comp-3.
       fd OUTDATI
           label records standard
           record 9
           data record Rec-outdati.
       01  Rec-outdati pic 9(9) comp-3.

but when do I go to write those digits in a file of output, doesn't the
program create me a record fully grown from the digits 546876890 but a record
fully grown from the characters 5468?. How come? Is there a way for write the
digits 546876890 in Rec-outdati maintaining it does the code Packed of the
Rec-dati field?

THANK YOU

BYE

P.S: You could me write it the answered to this address amonaci@protec.it



Andrea Monaci

My address is:

Via A.Gramsci N. 96
C.A.P.: 35010 Cadoneghe (Padova) (Italy)
Tel/Fax: 0039-049700637
E-Mail: amonaci@protec.it
```

#### ↳ Re: PACKED CODE

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77e0et$bs2$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3699ceb3.3152640@news.protec.it>`

```
Andrea Monaci wrote in message <3699ceb3.3152640@news.protec.it>...

>           record 9
>           data record Rec-outdati.
>       01  Rec-outdati pic 9(9) comp-3.
Change ^ line to
         01 Rec-outdati pic 9(9).
Then recompile and run.
```

#### ↳ Re: PACKED CODE

- **From:** jraben19@mail.idt.net (Jeff Raben)
- **Date:** 1999-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369ba804.1353676@news.idt.net>`
- **References:** `<3699ceb3.3152640@news.protec.it>`

```
amonaci@protec.it (Andrea Monaci) wrote:

>HI
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>but when do I go to write those digits in a file of output, doesn't the
snip
>
Having received the source I have found that LINE-SEQUENTIAL is used
instead of SEQUENTIAL.

Packed / binary data can generate line termination in the data stream.

A real no-no.

JR


and stir with a Runcible spoon...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Boolean operators in COBOL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-12T00:00:00

```
You may want to look at the LE "Bit Manipulation" routines.  See

   http://www2.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/ceea305/4%2e3

Look for the following documented subroutines:

    4.3.1 CEESICLR--Bit Clear
    4.3.2 CEESISET--Bit Set
    4.3.3 CEESISHF--Bit Shift
    4.3.4 CEESITST--Bit Test

Note:  I have NOT tried these myself, so I don't know exactly how easy or
hard they are to use from an LE-enabled COBOL application program.
```

## Thread: Initializing 3-d Table? — 23 msgs

### Initializing 3-d Table?

- **From:** NoLonger@ix.netcom.com (OldUncleMe)
- **Date:** 1999-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369b90af.5665969@news>`

```
Trying to initialize a 3 dimensional table before use.  Will it work to move zeros to
the group item, to the elementary item or to initialize either of these?  Is it
required instead to move zeros to all levels of the table with nested statements of
this nature?

perform varying wi0x-sub from x by x until wi0x-sub is >  x
 move zeros to table-item
end-perform.

I am just looking for the simplest method of initializing the table data.  Still
learning Cobol, the examples I have are conflicting, showing both methods, and I
don't know how to distinguish when to use what method....   Thanks for help here, TS
```

#### ↳ Re: Initializing 3-d Table?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77gl9o$t70$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<369b90af.5665969@news>`

```
If the usage is display throughout the table, you can
do a MOVE ALL "0" TO TABLE-NAME. Any level
of data name that is divided up into further levels
is treated as an x(n) field.

OldUncleMe wrote in message <369b90af.5665969@news>...
>Trying to initialize a 3 dimensional table before use.  Will it work to
move zeros to
>the group item, to the elementary item or to initialize either of these?
Is it
>required instead to move zeros to all levels of the table with nested
statements of
>this nature?
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>I am just looking for the simplest method of initializing the table data.
Still
>learning Cobol, the examples I have are conflicting, showing both methods,
and I
>don't know how to distinguish when to use what method....   Thanks for help
here, TS
```

#### ↳ Re: Initializing 3-d Table?

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369B95D8.A9D9AAD1@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<369b90af.5665969@news>`

```
If the usage of the table is ordinary numeric, i.e., PIC 9(??)V9(??) with no USAGE clause
like COMP-3, you may MOVE ZERO TO group-table-name.  To zero each item one at a time try:

PERFORM VARYING X1 FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL X1 > ??
        AFTER X2 FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL X2 > ??
        AFTER X3 FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL X3 > ??
  MOVE ZERO TO TABLE-ITEM (X1 X2 X3)
END-PERFORM.

HTH.

OldUncleMe wrote:

> Trying to initialize a 3 dimensional table before use.  Will it work to move zeros to
> the group item, to the elementary item or to initialize either of these?  Is it
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
-->        AFTER X2 FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL X2 > ??

Usage is "ordinary numeric, i.e., PIC 9(??)V9(??) with no USAGE clause".  I will try
it as you suggest, and thanks for the response.  It's a bit clearer now where these
different forms are used.  If using comp clauses, would the second method be correct?

To me, this is a rather complex program, in our 3rd quarter of cobol, and I have not
used some of the techniques before.  So I am trying to outline and resolve any
uncertain issues before even the first compile.  I'm relying on modular construction
as much as possible to ease the inevitable debugging.  The table filling and then
reading to print line is largely done in two modules separate from the control logic.
Thanks for your help, TS

 typewriters, and Usenet is NOTHING like Shakespeare." Blair Houghton
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initializing 3-d Table?

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369BC17D.D2BF6EDF@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<369b90af.5665969@news> <369B95D8.A9D9AAD1@ASPMnetdoor.com> <369b9891.7684007@news>`

```
OldUncleMe wrote:

> On Tue, 12 Jan 1999 12:35:05 -0600 "Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>" posted
> to "comp.lang.cobol" about: "Re: Initializing 3-d Table?" :
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> different forms are used.  If using comp clauses, would the second method be correct?

When you reference an elementary item like TABLE-ITEM(X Y Z) above, the compiler will be
aware of it's usage and will insert the appropriate type of literal or provide data
conversion code as appropriate so to answer your question: yes.  If you do something to a
table at the group level (except INITIALIZE), the entire table is treated as alphanumeric
regardless of how individual fields are defined.  If you MOVE ZERO TO
01-table-name-that-has-comp-3-or-packed-in-it you won't discover the error until you run
the program.  If all fields in a table are defined as 99 or 9999v99 with no COMP(-?) in
them doing a MOVE ZERO TO group-name would do exactly what you want.

>
>
> To me, this is a rather complex program, in our 3rd quarter of cobol, and I have not
> used some of the techniques before.

Warren Porter
---
If you choose violin over guitar, don't fret about it.
```

#### ↳ Re: Initializing 3-d Table?

- **From:** <@usaor.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<T5Nm2.304$7x6.24106@news.sgi.net>`
- **References:** `<369b90af.5665969@news>`

```

01  TABLE-NAME
       05 TAB-LEVEL OCCURS 50 TIMES.
             10 TAB-NUM     PIC 9(05) COMP-5.
              10 TAB-ALPH  PIC X(10).

INITIALIZE TABLE-NAME.

Most compiles will set TAB-NUM to 0 (zero) and TAB-ALPH to spaces at all
levels.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Initializing 3-d Table?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77g8e6$nnb@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<369b90af.5665969@news> <T5Nm2.304$7x6.24106@news.sgi.net>`

```

@usaor.net <@usaor.net> wrote in message ...
>
>01  TABLE-NAME
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>


To clarify ALL Standard conforming compilers will set numeric fields to ZERO
and alphanumeric fields to SPACES.  (Of course, in this example, COMP-5 is
not Standard conforming - but no matter WHAT USAGE you use, numeric fields
will be set to zero.)  The INITIALIZE verb is the simplest way to do this
type of thing - but not always the most efficient (varies by compiler,
compiler options, and exact source code).  INITIALIZE would also allow you
to initialize the alphanumeric items to some other value than SPACES (such
as low-values or high-values) or numeric items to some other value (such as
1) - just look at the REPLACING phrase of INITIALIZE in your language
reference.
```

#### ↳ Re: Initializing 3-d Table?

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369D6468.62BBFE12@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<369b90af.5665969@news>`

```
The simplest method is to use no Procedure Divison code at all:

01.
      02     occurs 10.
                03    occurs 20.
                        04   occurs 30.
                               05  zero-field pic s9(5) comp-3   VALUE ZERO.
                               05 disp-field  pic 999                   VALUE 123.
                               05  alpha-field pic x(2)                 VALUE SPACE.


Or method 2: This method had the advantage of easily reinitializing the table multiple
times during program execution, for the cost of a dummy occurrence.
This will also execute considerably faster than INITIALIZE, or PERFORM loops.

01 table-plus-1-occurrence.
      02  pic s9(1) comp-3   VALUE ZERO.
      02  pic 99                   VALUE 45.
      02 pic x(3)                 VALUE '#!&'.
      02     tab    occurs 10.
                03    occurs 20.
                        04   occurs 30.
                               05  zero-field pic s9(1) comp-3..
                               05 disp-field  pic 99.
                              05  alpha-field pic x(3).

MOVE table-plus-1- occurrence TO tab.

Regards,
John



OldUncleMe wrote:

> Trying to initialize a 3 dimensional table before use.  Will it work to move zeros to
> the group item, to the elementary item or to initialize either of these?  Is it
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> don't know how to distinguish when to use what method....   Thanks for help here, TS
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Initializing 3-d Table?

- **From:** cdulledge@hotmail.com (CD)
- **Date:** 1999-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369dcb67.2120677@news.bigwig.net>`
- **References:** `<369b90af.5665969@news> <369D6468.62BBFE12@ix.netcom.com>`

```
On Wed, 13 Jan 1999 22:28:40 -0500, John Trifon
<jtrifon@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>The simplest method is to use no Procedure Divison code at all:
>
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>
i don't normally post to this group, so excuse any bad etiquette....

Method 1 above only works the firsttime the program is called: they
keep drumming it into me at work about 'dirty code' and 'dirty data':
I think they are referring to the fact that when a program is called
on subsequent occasions there is no guarantee that working storage is
in it's "virgin" state.

Method 2 confuses the hell out of me! If I tried this at work
(assuming it is correct), they'd throw it out as not easily
understood, which is the "prime directive".
i threw it into NetExpress and it gave a compile eror as tab is not
subscripted in the MOVE statement. Are you trying to 'cascade' the
data down the table by assuming the data is move a character at a
time, starting at the top of the table and working down? If so this
seems a dangerous assumption. If not, you still need some form of loop
to initialize all the data.

Is processing time really still so valuable that a developer spends
hours on alternatives to INITIALIZE or a simple nested perform? It
certainly isn't for PCs or UNIX boxes (at least that's the parameters
I work to)

ChrisD
Visit Kris' Korner: http:freespace.virgin.net/c.dulledge/home.htm
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initializing 3-d Table?

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369DEB95.FD8819C6@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<369b90af.5665969@news> <369D6468.62BBFE12@ix.netcom.com> <369dcb67.2120677@news.bigwig.net>`

```
Correction.  The code should read:
01 table-plus-1-occurrence.
      02  pic s9(1) comp-3   VALUE ZERO.
      02  pic 99                   VALUE 45.
      02 pic x(3)                 VALUE '#!&'.
      02   tab-GROUP.                    *> new group level.
            03   tab    occurs 10.
                   04    occurs 20.
                          05   occurs 30.
                               06  zero-field pic s9(1) comp-3..
                               06 disp-field  pic 99.
                              06  alpha-field pic x(3).

MOVE table-plus-1- occurrence TO tab-GROUP

You are correct in that the data will cascade. On MVS, the compiler will generate MVC
instructions. This is not a dangerous assumption. From IBM's POP  (SA22-7201-04) manual:
"For MOVE (MVC), each operand is processed left to right. When the operands overlap, the
result is obtained as if the operands were processed one byte
at a time and each result byte were stored immediately after fetching the necessary operand
byte.

As for guaranteeing the WS is in an intial state you can use:
PROGRAM-ID. xxxxxxxx INITIAL.

If a piece of code is not clear or confusing to you, nothing prevents the addition of
sufficient comments to make clear the intent of the code. In fact, ALL code should be
commented heavily. As comments do not become part of the load module, there is no adverse
inpact to adding as many as you think you need.

As far as performance, try executing your loop to initialize the table 100,000 times vs the
MOVE method. These methods are not reinvented each time they are used - at a cost of hours
of programmer effort.  They can be coded as rapidly, or faster than an INITIALIZE loop.

CD wrote:

> On Wed, 13 Jan 1999 22:28:40 -0500, John Trifon
> <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
…[56 more quoted lines elided]…
> Visit Kris' Korner: http:freespace.virgin.net/c.dulledge/home.htm
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initializing 3-d Table?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77ll9v$f50@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<369b90af.5665969@news> <369D6468.62BBFE12@ix.netcom.com> <369dcb67.2120677@news.bigwig.net> <369DEB95.FD8819C6@ix.netcom.com>`

```

John Trifon wrote in message <369DEB95.FD8819C6@ix.netcom.com>...
>Correction.  The code should read:
>01 table-plus-1-occurrence.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>You are correct in that the data will cascade. On MVS, the compiler will
generate MVC
>instructions. This is not a dangerous assumption. From IBM's POP
(SA22-7201-04) manual:
    <much snippage>

This is STILL a dangerous (AWFUL) assumption on IBM mainframes.

There is absolutely NOwhere in IBM's COBOL documentation that says that this
will ALWAYS generate an MVC instruction.  If you compare OS/VS COBOL PMAPs
with COBOL for this-and-that LIST output, you will see that there are lots
and LOTS of places where the same COBOL source code is now compiled into
different "Assembler" instructions.  There are even cases where changes
occur from maintenance level to maintenance level (via a single APAR).

UNLESS IT IS DOCUMENTED as supported,
  PLEASE do not rely on "generated" code for "tricky" performance solutions
(like this).  (Anyone who has had to deal with the differences in what gets
an S0C7 in OS/VS COBOL vs VS COBOL II or COBOL for this-and-that will be
able to tell you that relying on generated assembler code sequences is a
STUPID thing to do - if you think your program will be maintained for more
than a week or so!)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initializing 3-d Table?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369f1194.51352691@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<369b90af.5665969@news> <369D6468.62BBFE12@ix.netcom.com> <369dcb67.2120677@news.bigwig.net> <369DEB95.FD8819C6@ix.netcom.com>`

```
On Thu, 14 Jan 1999 08:05:27 -0500, John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> On MVS, the compiler will generate MVC
>instructions. This is not a dangerous assumption.

John,

are you quite sure about this?  Looking at the generated assembly code of COBOL programs I
have found all kinds of things, MVC, MVCL, L and ST combinations, and others...  Also,
relying special behaviour of one processor does make the COBOL program absolutely
unportable....


with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

```
Yes, with one caveat: As for the portability issue, I can only say it works on IBM mainframes.

One should always test and check results before implementing any code into a production
environment.

Regards,
John
```
