###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initializing 3-d Table?

- **From:** tim.oxler@NOSPAMteo-computer.com (Tim Oxler)
- **Date:** 1999-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369e04fd.38289662@news.teo-computer.com>`
- **References:** `<369b90af.5665969@news> <369D6468.62BBFE12@ix.netcom.com> <369dcb67.2120677@news.bigwig.net>`

```
cdulledge@hotmail.com (CD) wrote:

>On Wed, 13 Jan 1999 22:28:40 -0500, John Trifon
><jtrifon@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
…[49 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Method 2 has in my circles been referred to as an overlay procedure. I
think the above example is incorrect.  The "02  tab" shouldn't have an
occurance.  It should look like this:

01 table-plus-1-occurrence.
      02  pic s9(1) comp-3   VALUE ZERO.
      02  pic 99                   VALUE 45.
      02 pic x(3)                 VALUE '#!&'.
      02     tab.
                03    occurs 20.
                    04    occurs 20.
                        05   occurs 30.
                              06  zero-field pic s9(1) comp-3..
                              06 disp-field  pic 99.
                              06  alpha-field pic x(3).

MOVE table-plus-1- occurrence TO tab.

Before the use of INITIALIZE became available, I used to used this
procedure quite a bit.  It didn't cause any problems or great debates.
Just as the INITIZE verb has done, it many times greatly reduced the
amount of Procedure Division code for a pretty basic function.



Tim Oxler
TEO Computer Technologies Inc.
http://www.teo-computer.com
Take the Y2k Quiz
http://www.teo-computer.com/dev/quiz.html
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initializing 3-d Table?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369E48EF.B7EB0E55@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<369b90af.5665969@news> <369D6468.62BBFE12@ix.netcom.com> <369dcb67.2120677@news.bigwig.net> <369e04fd.38289662@news.teo-computer.com>`

```
Tim Oxler wrote:

> Method 2 has in my circles been referred to as an overlay procedure. I
> think the above example is incorrect.  The "02  tab" shouldn't have an
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> amount of Procedure Division code for a pretty basic function.

Hey, 'sneat!  Back in the punched card days when writing fast compact code was the top
priority, I would initialize an all COMP-3 table with ALL 'dddds' where the 'dddds' was a
multipunched comp-3 zero of the right length (nine digits in this case).  On the printout,
the multipunched columns appear as spaces so a few comments would have been helpful.  Wonder
if I made any? <g>.

The use of overlapping fields like you illustrated was (perhaps still is) a way to clear an
area with assembler.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Initializing 3-d Table?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77l3jo$jgk$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<369b90af.5665969@news> <369D6468.62BBFE12@ix.netcom.com>`

```
That will not work on most Cobol's.  If you want to intitialize
an array using value statements in the working storage, then
the only way that will work with all of them is to define it flat
with value statements, then redefine it as an array.

John Trifon wrote in message <369D6468.62BBFE12@ix.netcom.com>...
>The simplest method is to use no Procedure Divison code at all:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>                               05  zero-field pic s9(5) comp-3   VALUE
ZERO.
>                               05 disp-field  pic 999
VALUE 123.
>                               05  alpha-field pic x(2)
VALUE SPACE.
>
>
>Or method 2: This method had the advantage of easily reinitializing the
table multiple
>times during program execution, for the cost of a dummy occurrence.
>This will also execute considerably faster than INITIALIZE, or PERFORM
loops.
>
>01 table-plus-1-occurrence.
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Trying to initialize a 3 dimensional table before use.  Will it work to
move zeros to
>> the group item, to the elementary item or to initialize either of these?
Is it
>> required instead to move zeros to all levels of the table with nested
statements of
>> this nature?
>>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>> I am just looking for the simplest method of initializing the table data.
Still
>> learning Cobol, the examples I have are conflicting, showing both
methods, and I
>> don't know how to distinguish when to use what method....   Thanks for
help here, TS
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initializing 3-d Table?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77llf2$fab@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`

```



Donald Tees wrote in message <77l3jo$jgk$1@news.igs.net>...
>That will not work on most Cobol's.  If you want to intitialize
>an array using value statements in the working storage, then
>the only way that will work with all of them is to define it flat
>with value statements, then redefine it as an array.
> <much snippage>

Donald,
  Check again.  Using a VALUE clause under and OCCURS is valid for ALL
ANS'85 compilers.  (There are rules that impact ODO's - Occurs Depending
On - but that doesn't seem relevant here.)
```

On Tue, 12 Jan 1999 18:18:35 GMT, NoLonger@ix.netcom.com (OldUncleMe) wrote:

>Trying to initialize a 3 dimensional table before use.  Will it work to move zeros to
>the group item, to the elementary item or to initialize either of these?  Is it
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>don't know how to distinguish when to use what method....   Thanks for help here, TS


Wouldn't

INITIALIZE Level01-Table-Name

be simple enough?

And portable as well?

And independent of the format of the data items?


with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Initializing 3-d Table?

- **From:** "Gary Roush" <support@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77irpb$df6$1@remarQ.com>`
- **References:** `<369b90af.5665969@news> <369c6df8.14705415@news3.ibm.net>`

```
Volker,

The INITIALIZE TABLE-NAME statement would be the best command to use.
If it's not supported on your platform, then the MOVE ZEROS to the 01 level
name would work providing ALL fields are display and NOT comp-3.
If some fields are comp-3, then it is necessary to use the PERFORM ...
END-PERFORM routine.

Gary Roush

<a id="t-191c9e862b"></a>
## Thread: Extremely simple CODE? — 10 msgs

### Extremely simple CODE?

- **From:** "Sally Hall" <Hallsframpton@btinternet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77g3d3$1s1$1@uranium.btinternet.com>`

```
Hello,
My name's Tony Hall, and I'm a computing student. I'm looking for a piece
of straight forward COBOL code, which will do something extremely simple as
display text on screen, or ask the user to input their name or something
similar to this.
Cheers
```

#### ↳ Re: Extremely simple CODE?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77g51p$4d5@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<77g3d3$1s1$1@uranium.btinternet.com>`

```
Probably home work, but the following will work (not Standard) with many
compilers

   Display "Hello World".

(Many compilers allow omitting Identification Division, Procedure Division
Header, and Stop Run.   - I don't recommend it, but it does work.)  Only
slightly more complicated and Standard.

Identification Division.
 Program-Id.  ReallySimple.
Data Division.
Working-Storage Section.
01   GetName    Pic X(80).
Procedure Division.
Mainline.
    Display "Enter Your Name: " upon Console with no Advancing
    Accept GetName from Console
    Stop Run
    .
```

#### ↳ Re: Extremely simple CODE?

- **From:** sbricklin@aol.com (SBRICKLIN)
- **Date:** 1999-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990114220052.00907.00000369@ng25.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<77g3d3$1s1$1@uranium.btinternet.com>`

```
        IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
        PROGRAM-ID. MONEY.
      *****************************************************************
      ** THIS PROGRAM WAS CREATED TO ACCEPT ANY NUMERIC VALUE / UP
TO *
      ** 8 CHARACTERS AND WILL CONVERT THEM TO THE ALPHANUMERIC
STRING*
      *****************************************************************
        ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
        CONFIGURATION SECTION.
        INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
        DATA DIVISION.
        WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
        01  RECORD-10.
            05 DIG1  PIC X(09) VALUE 'ONE '.
            05 DIG2  PIC X(09) VALUE 'TWO '.
            05 DIG3  PIC X(09) VALUE 'THREE '.
            05 DIG4  PIC X(09) VALUE 'FOUR'.
            05 DIG5  PIC X(09) VALUE 'FIVE'.
            05 DIG6  PIC X(09) VALUE 'SIX '.
            05 DIG7  PIC X(09) VALUE 'SEVEN '.
            05 DIG8  PIC X(09) VALUE 'EIGHT '.
            05 DIG9  PIC X(09) VALUE 'NINE'.
            05 DIG10 PIC X(09) VALUE 'TEN '.
            05 DIG11 PIC X(09) VALUE 'ELEVEN '.
            05 DIG12 PIC X(09) VALUE 'TWELVE '.
            05 DIG13 PIC X(09) VALUE 'THIRTEEN '.
            05 DIG14 PIC X(09) VALUE 'FOURTEEN '.
            05 DIG15 PIC X(09) VALUE 'FIFTEEN '.
            05 DIG16 PIC X(09) VALUE 'SIXTEEN'.
            05 DIG17 PIC X(09) VALUE 'SEVENTEEN'.
            05 DIG18 PIC X(09) VALUE 'EIGHTTEEN'.
            05 DIG19 PIC X(09) VALUE 'NINETEEN '.
      * 01  TABEL-RECORD-10  VALUE
      *    'ONE      TWO      THREE     FOUR     FIVE     SIX       SEVE
      *    'N    EIGHT    NINE     TEN      ELEVEN   TWELVE   THIRTEEN F
      *    'OURTEEN FIFTEEN SIXTEEN SEVENTEENEIGHTEEN NINETEEN '.
        01  TABEL-RECORD-10 REDEFINES RECORD-10.
           05 MONEY-10 OCCURS 19 TIMES.
                10 MONEY-WORD-10  PIC X(09).
        01  TABEL-RECORD-20  VALUE
           'TWENTY THIRTY FOURTY FIFTY  SIXTY  SEVENTYEIGHTY NINETY '.
           05 MONEY-20 OCCURS 8  TIMES.
                10 MONEY-WORD-20  PIC X(07).
        01  F-DOLLARS.
            05 F-MONEY      PIC 9(06) VALUE ZEROES.
            05 F-CENT       PIC V99   VALUE ZEROES.
        01  F-SUB           PIC 9(02) VALUE ZEROES.
        01  F-COUNT         PIC 9     VALUE ZEROES.
        01  F-GO-TO         PIC 9     VALUE ZEROES.
        01  MONEY-STRING.
            05 F1           PIC X(09)  VALUE SPACES.
            05 F1A          PIC X(08)  VALUE 'HUNDRED'.
            05 F2           PIC X(07)  VALUE SPACES.
            05 F3           PIC X(09)  VALUE SPACES.
            05 F3A          PIC X(09)  VALUE 'THOUSAND'.
            05 F4           PIC X(07)  VALUE SPACES.
            05 F4A          PIC X(08)  VALUE 'HUNDRED '.
            05 F5           PIC X(07)  VALUE SPACES.
            05 F6           PIC X(09)  VALUE SPACES.
            05 F6A          PIC X(07)  VALUE 'DOLLARS'.
            05 F7           PIC .99    VALUE '.00' .
            05 F7A          PIC X(05)  VALUE 'CENTS'.
        01  MONEY-LINE      PIC X(80) VALUE SPACES.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
            MOVE SPACES TO MONEY-STRING.
            ACCEPT  F-DOLLARS.
            INSPECT F-MONEY TALLYING F-COUNT FOR LEADING ZERO.
            DISPLAY 'F-COUNT:  ' F-COUNT
            ADD 1 TO F-COUNT GIVING F-GO-TO.
            DISPLAY 'F-GO-TO:  ' F-GO-TO.
            GO TO
                  PARA-1
                  PARA-2
                  PARA-3
                  PARA-4
                  PARA-5
                  PARA-6
                  DEPENDING ON F-GO-TO.
              PERFORM PARA-FINAL.
       PARA-6.
           MOVE  F-MONEY(6:1) TO F-SUB
           MOVE  MONEY-WORD-10(F-SUB) TO F6.
           MOVE  'DOLLARS'  TO F6A.
           GO TO PARA-FINAL.
       PARA-5.
           MOVE F-MONEY(5:2) TO F-SUB.
           MOVE  'DOLLARS'  TO F6A
           IF F-SUB = 00
              MOVE SPACES TO F5 F6
           ELSE
             IF F-SUB <= 19
               MOVE  MONEY-WORD-10(F-SUB) TO F6
           ELSE
               IF F-SUB(2:1) NOT = 0
                  PERFORM PARA-6A
                  PERFORM PARA-6
               ELSE
                  PERFORM PARA-6A
               END-IF
             END-IF
            END-IF.
           GO TO PARA-FINAL.
       PARA-4.
           MOVE  F-MONEY(4:1) TO F-SUB
           IF
              F-SUB = 0
              MOVE SPACES TO F4 F4A
           ELSE
              MOVE  MONEY-WORD-10(F-SUB) TO F4
              MOVE  'HUNDRED'  TO F4A.
           PERFORM PARA-5.
       PARA-3.
           MOVE F-MONEY(3:1) TO F-SUB.
           MOVE  MONEY-WORD-10(F-SUB) TO F3.
           MOVE  'THOUSAND' TO F3A.
           PERFORM PARA-4.
       PARA-3A.
            MOVE F-MONEY(2:1) TO F-SUB
            MOVE MONEY-WORD-20(F-SUB - 1) TO F2.
       PARA-2.
           MOVE F-MONEY(2:2) TO F-SUB.
               MOVE  'THOUSAND' TO F3A
           IF F-SUB = 00
               MOVE  SPACES TO F3  F2
               PERFORM PARA-4
           ELSE
           IF F-SUB <= 19
               MOVE  MONEY-WORD-10(F-SUB) TO F3
               PERFORM PARA-4
           ELSE
               IF F-SUB(2:1) NOT = 0
                  PERFORM PARA-3A
                  PERFORM PARA-3
               ELSE
                  PERFORM PARA-3A
                  PERFORM PARA-4
               END-IF
            END-IF.
       PARA-1.
            MOVE F-MONEY(1:1) TO F-SUB
            MOVE MONEY-WORD-10(F-SUB) TO F1.
            MOVE  'HUNDRED'  TO F1A.
            PERFORM PARA-2.
       PARA-6A.
            MOVE F-MONEY(5:1) TO F-SUB
            MOVE MONEY-WORD-20(F-SUB - 1) TO F5.
       PARA-FINAL.
            IF F-CENT  = .00
               MOVE '00' TO F7(2:2)
               MOVE 'CENT' TO F7A
            ELSE
             IF F-CENT = .01
               MOVE 'CENT' TO F7A
               MOVE '01' TO F7(2:2)
             ELSE
               MOVE F-CENT      TO F7
               MOVE 'CENTS' TO F7A
            END-IF
            END-IF.
            MOVE SPACE TO F7(1:1).
            STRING
                 F1  DELIMITED BY SPACE
                 ' ' DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 F1A DELIMITED BY SPACE
                 ' ' DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 F2  DELIMITED BY SPACE
                 ' ' DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 F3  DELIMITED BY SPACE
                 ' ' DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 F3A DELIMITED BY SPACE
                 ' ' DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 F4  DELIMITED BY SPACE
                 ' ' DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 F4A DELIMITED BY SPACE
                 ' ' DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 F5  DELIMITED BY SPACE
                 ' ' DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 F6  DELIMITED BY SPACE
                 ' ' DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 F6A DELIMITED BY SPACE
                 ' ' DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 'AND' DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 ' ' DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 F7  DELIMITED  BY SIZE
                 '/100 ' DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 F7A DELIMITED BY SPACE
                     INTO MONEY-LINE
           END-STRING.
           DISPLAY MONEY-LINE.
           STOP RUN.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Extremely simple CODE?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-15T00:00:00

##### ↳ ↳ Text from Numeric Input -- Was: Re: Extremely simple CODE?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369edf7a.14232936@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<77g3d3$1s1$1@uranium.btinternet.com> <19990114220052.00907.00000369@ng25.aol.com>`

```
On 15 Jan 1999 03:00:52 GMT, sbricklin@aol.com (SBRICKLIN) wrote:


Interesting code snipped..............

The problem solved by the posted program tickled my fancy for some
reason tonight.  I thought about being creative and processing the
input from the tail to the front and creating the output - but further
analysis proved to be interesting.  This was a more challenging
problem than I first thought it was -- but here is my solution.  

Note:  The display in the screen section is too small for some numbers
- that's why I also display it on the console when I am done.  

I have taken advantage of numerous Ansi 85 and 89 features.  

Later, I'll create a GUI version of this with SP2 and post it on my
website. 

Any other solutions out there?  Some of this one is, shall we say,
subtle.
.
000010 @OPTIONS MAIN,TEST
000020 Identification Division.
000030 Program-Id.  Cutenum.
000040* By Thane Hubbell - 01/15/1999 
000041* Compiled and Tested under Fujitsu COBOL
000042 Environment Division.
000050 Configuration Section.
000060 Source-Computer.  IBM-PC.
000070 Object-Computer.  IBM-PC.
000080 Data Division.
000090 Working-Storage Section.
000252 01  The-Number            Pic 9(9) Value Zeros.
000254 01  Text-Field            Pic X(200) Value Spaces.
000255 01  Number-Descriptions.
000256     03  Table-Area-Low.
000257         05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "One".
000258         05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "Two".
000259         05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "Three".
000260         05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "Four".
000261         05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "Five".
000262         05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "Six".
000263         05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "Seven".
000264         05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "Eight".
000265         05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "Nine".
000266         05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "Ten".
000267         05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "Eleven".
000268         05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "Twelve".
000269         05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "Thirteen".
000270     03  Low-Numbers Redefines Table-Area-Low.
000271         05  Low-Number Pic X(8) Occurs 13 Times.
000272     03  Table-Area-Secondary.
000273         05  Filler Pic X(7) Value Spaces.
000274         05  Filler Pic X(7) Value "Twenty".
000275         05  Filler Pic X(7) Value "Thirty".
000276         05  Filler Pic X(7) Value "Fourty". 
000277         05  Filler Pic X(7) Value "Fifty".
000278         05  Filler Pic X(7) Value "Sixty".
000279         05  Filler Pic X(7) Value "Seventy".
000280         05  Filler Pic X(7) Value "Eighty".
000281         05  Filler Pic X(7) Value "Ninety".
000282     03  Secondary-Numbers Redefines Table-Area-Secondary.
000283         05  Secondary-Number Pic X(7) Occurs 9 Times.
000284     03  Table-Area-Ante.
000285         05  Filler Pic X(8) Value Spaces.
000286         05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "Million".
000287         05  Filler Pic X(8) Value Spaces.
000288         05  Filler Pic X(8) Value "Thousand".
000289         05  Filler Pic X(8) Value Spaces.
000290         05  Filler Pic X(8) Value Spaces.
000292     03  Ante-Numbers Redefines Table-Area-Ante.
000293         05  Ante-Number Pic X(8) Occurs 6 Times.
000294 01  Cntr Pic 99 Value Zeros.
000295 01  String-Pointer Pic 99 Value 1.
000296 01  Digits Pic 9 Value Zeros.
000297 01  Offset Pic 9 Value Zeros.
000298 01  Offset-Value Pic 9v9 Value Zeros.
000299 01  Test-Num Pic 99 Value Zeros.
000300 01  Second-Num Pic 99 Value Zeros.
000301 01  Ante-Flag Pic X Value Spaces.
000302     88 Print-Ante Value "Y".
000303     88 No-Print-Ante Value Spaces.
000304 Screen Section.
000305 01  Main-Screen
000306     Blank Screen, Auto, Required,
000307     Foreground-Color Is 7,
000308     Background-Color Is 1.
000309     03  Line 1 Column 1  Value "Enter Amount".
000310     03  Line 3 Column 1  Value "Result ".
000311     03  Line 1 Column 14 Pic 9(9) Using The-Number.
000312     03  Line 4 Column 1  Pic X(80) From Text-Field.
000313 Procedure Division.
000314 Cutenum-Start.
000315     Display Main-Screen
000316     Accept Main-Screen
000324     Perform Varying Cntr From 1 By 1 Until Cntr > 6
000325       If Digits = 2
000326          Subtract 1 From Digits
000327       Else
000328          Add 1 To Digits
000329       End-If
000330       Move Offset-Value to Offset
000331       Add .5 to Offset-Value
000332       Compute Test-Num = 
000334        Function Numval (The-Number (Cntr + Offset:Digits))
000335       Evaluate Digits Also Test-Num
000338          When 1 Also 1 Thru 9
000339            String Low-Number (Test-Num)
000340              Delimited By Space 
000341              " Hundred " Delimited By Size
000342              Into Text-Field
000343              Pointer String-Pointer
000344            Set Print-Ante To True
000345          When 2 Also 1 Thru 9
000346            String Low-Number (Test-Num)
000347              Delimited by Space
000348              Space Delimited by Size
000349              Into Text-Field
000350              Pointer String-Pointer
000351            Set Print-Ante To True
000352          When 2 Also 10 Thru 13
000353            String Low-Number (Test-Num)
000354              Delimited By Space
000355              Space Delimited By Size
000356              Into Text-Field
000357              Pointer String-Pointer
000358            Set Print-Ante To True
000359          When 2 Also 14 Thru 19
000360              Compute Second-Num = 
000361                 Function Rem (Test-Num 10)
000362              String Low-Number (Second-Num)
000363                 "teen" Delimited By Space
000364                 Space Delimited by Size
000365                 Into Text-Field
000366                 Pointer String-Pointer
000367              Set Print-Ante To True
000368          When 2 Also 20 Thru 99     
000369              Move Test-Num To Second-Num
000370              Move "0" To Second-Num (2:1)
000371              Subtract Second-Num From Test-Num
000372              Divide Second-Num By 10 Giving Second-Num
000373              String Secondary-Number (Second-Num)
000374                 Low-Number (Test-Num)
000375                 Delimited By Space
000376                 Space Delimited By Size
000377                 Into Text-Field
000378                 Pointer String-Pointer
000379              Set Print-Ante To True
000380       End-Evaluate
000381       If Digits = 2 And Print-Ante
000382        String Ante-Number (Cntr)
000383         Delimited By Space
000384         Space Delimited By Size
000385         Into Text-Field
000386         Pointer String-Pointer 
000387        Set No-Print-Ante To True
000388       End-if   
000389     End-Perform
000390     Display Main-Screen
000391     Display Text-Field Upon Console
000460     Stop Run.
```

   100     02 LINE 14 COLUMN 75 VALUE "ͼ".
   101 01 INPUT-SCREEN AUTO
   102          BACKGROUND-COLOR  1
   103          FOREGROUND-COLOR  7.
   104     02 LINE 11 COLUMN 15 PICTURE ZZZZZ9.99
   105          FROM NUMBER-IN
   106          TO NUMBER-IN
   107          REVERSE-VIDEO.
   108     02 LINE 13 COLUMN 09 PICTURE X(65)
   109          FROM LITERAL-OUT.
   110 01 OUTPUT-SCREEN AUTO
   111          BACKGROUND-COLOR  1
   112          FOREGROUND-COLOR  7.
   113     02 LINE 11 COLUMN 15 PICTURE ZZZZZ9.99
   114          FROM NUMBER-IN.
   115     02 LINE 11 COLUMN 60 PICTURE ZZZZZ9.99
   116          FROM CHEQUE-CODE-INPUT.
   117     02 LINE 13 COLUMN 09 PICTURE X(65)
   118          FROM CHEQUE-CODE-OUTPUT.
   119  PROCEDURE DIVISION.
   120  MAIN-LINE.
   121          DISPLAY SPACE.
   122          DISPLAY BACKGROUND-SCREEN.
   123          MOVE 1 TO NUMBER-IN.
   124          PERFORM TEST-IT UNTIL NUMBER-IN IS EQUAL TO ZERO.
   125          DISPLAY SPACE.
   126          STOP RUN.
   127
   128
   129  TEST-IT.
   130          MOVE ZERO TO NUMBER-IN.
   131          MOVE SPACE TO LITERAL-OUT.
   132          DISPLAY OUTPUT-SCREEN.
   133          DISPLAY INPUT-SCREEN.
   134          ACCEPT INPUT-SCREEN.
   135          MOVE NUMBER-IN TO CHEQUE-CODE-INPUT.
   136          PERFORM CHEQUE-CODE-CONVERSION.
   137          MOVE CHEQUE-CODE-OUTPUT TO LITERAL-OUT.
   138          IF NUMBER-IN IS NOT EQUAL TO ZERO
   139                  PERFORM SHOW-THE-ANSWER.
   140
   141
   142  SHOW-THE-ANSWER.
   143          DISPLAY OUTPUT-SCREEN.
   144          MOVE ZERO TO CHARIN.
   145          PERFORM GET-A-LETTER UNTIL CHARIN IS NOT
   146                  EQUAL TO ZERO.
   147
   148
   149  GET-A-LETTER.
   150          MOVE ZERO TO CHARIN.
   151          CALL "GETKEYS" USING CHARIN.
   152
   153
*  154          COPY \LIBRARY\COBOL\CHEQUE.COD.
   155  CHEQUE-CODE-CONVERSION.
   156          MOVE SPACE TO CHEQUE-CODE-OUTPUT.
   157          MOVE SPACE TO CHEQUE-CODE-WORK.
   158          MOVE CHEQUE-CODE-1000 TO CHEQUE-CODE-TWO-DIGIT.
   159          IF CHEQUE-CODE-TWO-DIGIT IS EQUAL TO ZERO
   160                  NEXT SENTENCE
   161          ELSE
   162                  PERFORM CHEQUE-CODE-CONVERT-A.
   163          MOVE CHEQUE-CODE-100 TO CHEQUE-CODE-TWO-DIGIT.
   164          IF CHEQUE-CODE-TWO-DIGIT IS EQUAL TO ZERO
   165                  NEXT SENTENCE
   166          ELSE
   167                  PERFORM CHEQUE-CODE-CONVERT-B.
   168          MOVE CHEQUE-CODE-10 TO CHEQUE-CODE-TWO-DIGIT.
   169          IF CHEQUE-CODE-TWO-DIGIT IS EQUAL TO ZERO
   170                  NEXT SENTENCE
   171          ELSE
   172                  PERFORM CHEQUE-CODE-CONVERT-C.
   173          MOVE SPACE TO CHEQUE-CODE-WORK1.
*                         CHEQUE.CBL (\LIBRARY\COBOL\CHEQUE.COD)
   174          IF CHEQUE-CODE-WORK IS EQUAL TO SPACE
   175                  MOVE "Zero*dollars" TO CHEQUE-CODE-WORK.
   176          STRING "*", CHEQUE-CODE-DECIMAL, "/100"
   177                  DELIMITED BY SPACE
   178                  INTO CHEQUE-CODE-WORK1.
   179          STRING "---", CHEQUE-CODE-WORK,
   180                  "---",  CHEQUE-CODE-WORK1
   181                  DELIMITED BY SPACE
   182                  INTO CHEQUE-CODE-OUTPUT.
   183          INSPECT CHEQUE-CODE-OUTPUT REPLACING ALL "*" BY " ".
   184
   185
   186  CHEQUE-CODE-CONVERT-A.
   187          PERFORM CHEQUE-CODE-CONVERT-TWO.
   188          STRING CHEQUE-CODE-WORK1, "*Thousand,*"
   189                  DELIMITED BY SPACE
   190                  INTO CHEQUE-CODE-WORK.
   191
   192
   193  CHEQUE-CODE-CONVERT-B.
   194          PERFORM CHEQUE-CODE-CONVERT-TWO.
   195          STRING CHEQUE-CODE-WORK, CHEQUE-CODE-WORK1,
   196                  "*Hundred,*"
   197                  DELIMITED BY SPACE
   198                  INTO CHEQUE-CODE-WORK.
   199
   200
   201  CHEQUE-CODE-CONVERT-C.
   202          PERFORM CHEQUE-CODE-CONVERT-TWO.
   203          STRING CHEQUE-CODE-WORK, CHEQUE-CODE-WORK1,
   204                  DELIMITED BY SPACE
   205                  INTO CHEQUE-CODE-WORK.
   206
   207
   208  CHEQUE-CODE-CONVERT-TWO.
   209          MOVE SPACE TO CHEQUE-CODE-WORK1.
   210          IF CHEQUE-CODE-TWO-DIGIT IS EQUAL TO ZERO
   211                  NEXT SENTENCE
   212          ELSE
   213          IF CHEQUE-CODE-TWO-DIGIT IS LESS THAN 20
   214                  MOVE CHEQUE-CODE-LIT3 (CHEQUE-CODE-TWO-DIGIT)
   215                          TO CHEQUE-CODE-WORK1
   216          ELSE
   217                SUBTRACT 1 FROM CHEQUE-CODE-TENS
   218                STRING CHEQUE-CODE-LIT6 (CHEQUE-CODE-TENS),
   219                  "-",
   220                  CHEQUE-CODE-LIT3 (CHEQUE-CODE-UNITS)
   221                          DELIMITED BY SPACE
   222                          INTO CHEQUE-CODE-WORK1.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Text from Numeric Input -- Was: Re: Extremely simple CODE?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77nlrp$61c$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<77g3d3$1s1$1@uranium.btinternet.com> <19990114220052.00907.00000369@ng25.aol.com> <369edf7a.14232936@news1.ibm.net>`

```
I realized after I sent it, that the code I sent you has a bug
if compiled under Fujitsu. (or several bugs).  The compiler it was
written for blank fills a receiving field during a string.  The
standard (apparently) leaves whatever was in the field
there, and strings over top, then leaves the old data in
the unused portion (which is rather stupid, in my opinion).

Anyway, the code may need a "move space to receiving
field" in front of the string commands to make it solid.

Thane Hubbell wrote in message <369edf7a.14232936@news1.ibm.net>...
>On 15 Jan 1999 03:00:52 GMT, sbricklin@aol.com (SBRICKLIN) wrote:
>
…[159 more quoted lines elided]…
>000460     Stop Run.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Text from Numeric Input -- Was: Re: Extremely simple CODE?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77n3qu$2he@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<77g3d3$1s1$1@uranium.btinternet.com> <19990114220052.00907.00000369@ng25.aol.com> <369edf7a.14232936@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane,
  Did you know that ANSI/ISO (via FIPS certification tests) ended up
"defining" the result of NumVal and NumVal-C as "approximations"?  This
drove me crazy when it happened - and really only (apparently) impacts some
pretty odd input to the functions.  However, I have heard that many (most?)
compiler vendors have implemented these in FLOATING-POINT (YUCK!!!) so
"programmer beware".  (I doubt it will cause a problem with your program -
but it is an issue to be aware of - you might want to throw a FUNCTION
INTEGER in there - if nothing else)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Text from Numeric Input -- Was: Re: Extremely simple CODE?

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369f2c46.0@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<77g3d3$1s1$1@uranium.btinternet.com> <19990114220052.00907.00000369@ng25.aol.com> <369edf7a.14232936@news1.ibm.net>`

```

Thane Hubbell
>The problem solved by the posted program tickled my fancy for some
>reason tonight.  I thought about being creative and processing the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>subtle.


This is an interesting problem and gets more interesting when done
in several languages. The ETKPAK package at http://www.etk.com
(then download, then etkpak) contains CERTPK which shows how
to do this in English, French, Belgian French, Dutch, Portuguese,
Spanish, German, Danish, Italian, Norwegian, Swedish, and
Icelandic. The code is too long to show here, so go to the
web site.

CERTPK calls the (non-portable) routine CONVPK to convert
to upper case. This call can be replaced by a Function.
```

---

<a id="t-89b4eb50d6"></a>
## Thread: Where are the COBOL Y2K jobs? — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k) · [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs)_

### Where are the COBOL Y2K jobs?

- **From:** kownby@humboldt1.com (Kindrick Ownby)
- **Date:** 1999-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369ca3ac.4313040@news2.humboldt1.com>`

```
I live within 300 miles of San Fransisco CA and, toying with the
notion of perhaps picking up some y2k work, I read the Sunday
Chronicle employment section.

I saw tons of programmer adds, some for COBOL, but none that specified
y2k work.

A couple of ads said "This is not y2k work".

I am just coming up for air after completing y2k remediation on a
COBOL application running on AS/400 Advanced 36, and am just a bit
surprised at the nature of the want ads.

Can you offer any comment or clarification for this aging beatnik?

TIA

Kindrick
```

- **From:** swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1999-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3913935A3301E028.F015DAAC912CC660.C10D0860F3796743@library-proxy.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<369ca3ac.4313040@news2.humboldt1.com>`

```
On Tue, 12 Jan 1999 19:43:53 GMT, kownby@humboldt1.com (Kindrick
Ownby) enlightened us:

>I live within 300 miles of San Fransisco CA and, toying with the
>notion of perhaps picking up some y2k work, I read the Sunday
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>Kindrick

Well it is a matter of timing.  It is already 1999 and if a company is
on schedule with their Y2K remediation, they should have finished
coding in 1998 and be well into Enterprise Testing by now.  Hence they
may not need coders for Y2K at this time.  If a company is still doing
remediation, they are way behind schedule and I would certainly have
to think twice about working for such a company.

As the Y2K remediation work is or should be nearly complete, companies
are now looking at doing everything they put off to do Y2K work.  That
may be what you are seeing.  

Another thought occurred to me as I was typing this.  It could be that
those companies that are not on schedule with Y2K and do still need
coders may not be advertising in the want ads as this could give the
company a very negative image and, if picked up on by the general
public, could drive their stock price down or have other unwanted
effects.

Cheers,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Clothes make the man. Naked people have little or no influence on society.

 Steve
```

<a id="t-7a11f68415"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu and dll shows up in taskbar — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu and dll shows up in taskbar

- **From:** "Herman Zuidema" <hermanz@xs4all.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77fr8c$2ld$1@news2.xs4all.nl>`

```
Hi,

Help wanted
I made a dll in Fujitsu cobol V3. I call it from a VB application.
When I call it it shows in de taskbar. How can I suppress this?

TIA
Herman Zuidema
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu and dll shows up in taskbar

- **From:** "don ferrario" <don@ferrario.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77jmf6$1et$1@news1.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<77fr8c$2ld$1@news2.xs4all.nl>`

```
In Fujitsu V4, you can specify an environmental variable
which will eliminate the task bar entry.  I'm not sure
if it works with V3 (doubt it, actually)

@EnvSetWindow=UNUSE

After entering the item, you must save the environment.
It won't take effect until the *next* time you execute the
program.

The environmental data is saved in a ".cbr" file.  The
file must be in the directory you are running from.  If you
are testing in the debug mode, and are running from
the "debug" subdirectory (this is the default), you need
to make sure the file is saved in that directory.
```

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future) · [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu COBOL still leashed

- **From:** jndmunro@powerup.com.au (Jon Munro)
- **Date:** 1999-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369b2e17.841834@news.powerup.com.au>`

```
I have installed Fujitsu Power COBOL from the CD attached to SAMS
'COBOL Unleashed' by Jon Wessler.  The book is excellent but on
starting up PowerCOBOL, 'F5BA1245 Cannot get registry information'
message appears and persists until it crashes. Has anyone else had
this experience and is a solution possible?  Any clues would be
greatly appreciated.

thanks

Jon Munro
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL still leashed

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-m6q4TxeQj1ty@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<369b2e17.841834@news.powerup.com.au>`

```
On Tue, 12 Jan 1999 12:50:42, jndmunro@powerup.com.au (Jon Munro) 
wrote:

> I have installed Fujitsu Power COBOL from the CD attached to SAMS
> 'COBOL Unleashed' by Jon Wessler.  The book is excellent but on
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> greatly appreciated.

Later editions of the book have this problem fixed.  It is NOT a 
Fujitsu problem - but rather a problem with the way Sams put the CD 
together.  When developing TY COBOL in 24 Hours I was aware of this 
problem and I took great pains to ensure that the CD was a reliable 
copy and install of the Fujitsu compiler.  You CAN order a CD copy for
a price from the Fujitsu web site (http://www.adtools.com) - or if you
want, you can buy my book ($20.00 or so from the online book sellers).
 Or you might be able to call MacMillan (Sams) and have them send you 
a corrected CD.
```

<a id="t-b945e3ee67"></a>
## Thread: OSVS Cobol vs COBOL II under MVS — 22 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### OSVS Cobol vs COBOL II under MVS

- **From:** "Tom Eisenman" <Tom_Eisenman@NOJUNKbigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8O1n2.45$O45.223@testbox>`

```
We have a COBOL program that is CALLed by a program written in another
language.  The COBOL program reads a long file and builds a table in working
storage. It then does a search to find the item that the calling program
needs.

This program has always been compiled with IBM OSVS COBOL.

Recently our amount of data grew to the point that the table size exceeded
the maximum size allowable under OSVS COBOL.  We recompiled the program
under COBOL II.  The program works but we now have a severe performance
problem.

Under OSVS COBOL the program would read the long file and build the table
the first time it was called; but on subsequent calls during the same
execution it would only have to consult the table it had already built.  The
contents of the table would remain available from one call to the next.
This is a difference of reading 10,000 records from a disk once per run and
reading 10,000 records several thousand times!

We know that we can probably set compiler parameters in COBOL II to make the
program behave as it did under OSVS COBOL but nobody here can figure out
just how to set them!

We don't have any of the newer COBOL compilers available.  We are working on
an IBM 3090 with MVS/ESA.
```

#### ↳ Re: OSVS Cobol vs COBOL II under MVS

- **From:** "John Lee" <johndlee@msn.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OAkB513P#GA.145@upnetnews02.moswest.msn.net>`
- **References:** `<8O1n2.45$O45.223@testbox>`

```
There could be lots of possibilities, but I suspect what you need to do is
to make an IGZEOPD with the LIBKEEP option.  We had a similar problem with
Easytrieve calling a Cobol II program.  After running for
18 hours, we cancelled the job the next day. When we ran it with LIBKEEP, it
finished in 20 seconds.  What
was happening, for each call to the cobol program, it was re-establishing
the cobol environment, LIBKEEP
keeps the cobol environment. I later found Easytrieve has a 'cobol
environment' statement, obviating the need for LIBKEEP.  Incidentally,
LIBKEEP was dropped in LE.  I'm working from home, so I can't look in the
manuals for the correct terminologies.

Tom Eisenman wrote in message <8O1n2.45$O45.223@testbox>...
>We have a COBOL program that is CALLed by a program written in another
>language.  The COBOL program reads a long file and builds a table in
working
>storage. It then does a search to find the item that the calling program
>needs.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>execution it would only have to consult the table it had already built.
The
>contents of the table would remain available from one call to the next.
>This is a difference of reading 10,000 records from a disk once per run and
>reading 10,000 records several thousand times!
>
>We know that we can probably set compiler parameters in COBOL II to make
the
>program behave as it did under OSVS COBOL but nobody here can figure out
>just how to set them!
>
>We don't have any of the newer COBOL compilers available.  We are working
on
>an IBM 3090 with MVS/ESA.
>--
>remove the NOJUNK to reply
>
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OSVS Cobol vs COBOL II under MVS

- **From:** raygberg@pond.com (Ray Greenberg)
- **Date:** 1999-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

In our shop, we use something similar to John's solution--an "IGZEOPT"
module with "RETREUS"--this retains the COBOL sub-program W-S state
between calls.  Our applications are ADABAS NATURAL programs calling
COBOL.  We, however, are a VS COBOL II-MVS/XA environment (moving to
OS/390 this April, I promise), so that may not be applicable. 

Ray Greenberg

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OSVS Cobol vs COBOL II under MVS

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77t3r1$q8i@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<8O1n2.45$O45.223@testbox> <OAkB513P#GA.145@upnetnews02.moswest.msn.net> <36a22b1a.3440227@news.pond.com>`

```
Assuming that you will (eventually) be going to OS/390 and LE, the following
is some information that you should know about RTEREUS and LIBKEEP (from the
LE Migration Guide):

LIBKEEP | NOLIBKEEP is not  supported under Language  Environment and is not
applicable  under CICS.

To obtain similar function, use   the Library Routine Retention  (LRR)
feature, which is described in the OS/390 Language Environment Programming
Guide. To  use LRR in an IMS/DC environment, see the OS/390 Language
Environment Customization book.

RTEREUS is not recommended as an  installation default. Use RTEREUS only for
specific applications  and ensure that you understand  the possible side
effects, for  example:

    �   Under Language Environment, RTEREUS(ON) is only supported  in a
single enclave  environment. Applications  that create multiple enclaves
will terminate with error  message IGZ0168S. Multiple  enclaves can be
created by  applications that use SVC LINK or CMSCALL to invoke application
programs. One  example is when an SVC LINK is used to invoke an application
program under  ISPF that is using ISPF  services (such as CALL
'ISPLINK' and ISPF SELECT).

    �   If a Language Environment  reusable environment is  established
(using RTEREUS),  attempts to run a C or PL/I main program under Language
Environment will fail. For example, when running on ISPF with RTEREUS(ON):

         -   The first program invoked by ISPF is a COBOL program; a
Language Environment reusable  environment is established.

        - At another point, ISPF  invokes a PL/I or C  program; the
initialization of the PL/I or C program will fail.

     �   If many COBOL programs are run under the same OS/390  task, "out of
region" abends  may occur. This occurs because all storage acquired by
Language Environment to  run COBOL programs is kept  until the OS/390 task
ends or the Language Environment environment terminates.

    �   Language Environment does not produce storage and run-time options
reports unless STOP  RUN is issued to end the  enclave.
```

```
On Sun, 17 Jan 1999 18:29:59 GMT, raygberg@pond.com (Ray Greenberg)
wrote:

>In our shop, we use something similar to John's solution--an "IGZEOPT"
>module with "RETREUS"--this retains the COBOL sub-program W-S state
>between calls.  Our applications are ADABAS NATURAL programs calling
>COBOL.  We, however, are a VS COBOL II-MVS/XA environment (moving to
>OS/390 this April, I promise), so that may not be applicable. 

Boy, was I prophetic.  We're converting to OS/390 with LE, and testing
to see which of our COBOL programs will run.  Some of our programs use
the COBOL run-time module IGZEOPT, using the option MIXRES (as many of
our programs are a mix of RES and NORES), as I stated in an earlier
message.  Well, someone came to me today with an execution message:

"IGZ0014W IGZEOPT is not longer supported.  Its content was ignored"

Will the Migration Guide (which I don't have in front of me at the
moment) spell out what to do in cases such as this?  Specifically, how
to specify MIXRES, or whether it's even possible (as, from what I
gather, NORES is a thing of the past under LE)?

  



Ray Greenberg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IGZEOPT (was: OSVS Cobol vs COBOL II under MVS)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<786a8e$28q@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<8O1n2.45$O45.223@testbox> <OAkB513P#GA.145@upnetnews02.moswest.msn.net> <36a22b1a.3440227@news.pond.com> <36a6b151.2627062@news.pond.com>`

```
Well, we have some good news and some bad news for you:

Good News: (from "Compiler and Run-Time Migration Guide" GC26-4764-04)
  "In addition, the VS COBOL II application-specific run-time options module
(IGZEOPT) is available to load modules comprised of VS COBOL II programs
compiled with RES. IGZEOPT is ignored under CICS. "
    and
"Applications with COBOL Programs Compiled RES (non-CICS): The VS COBOL II
application-specific run-time options module, IGZEOPT, is available to RES
applications, running under Language Environment. After link-editing an
application with Language Environment, it is possible for both IGZEOPT and
CEEUOPT to exist in the same application. Figure 30 shows the relationship
between IGZEOPT and CEEUOPT for applications that are link-edited with
Language Environment. "

(although you get a warning, current releases of LE do recognize IGZEOPT -
when you aren't under CICS)

Bad news:
  Migrating MIXRES applications is the HARDEST part of many migrations.  The
following is just the tip of the nightmare that you are about to encounter
(again from the Migration Guide)

  "Applications with COBOL Programs Compiled NORES: Any load modules
containing programs compiled with NORES that use the VS COBOL II MIXRES
run-time option must be link-edited with Language Environment. After
link-editing the load module with Language Environment, it will have access
to all Language Environment services (except callable services) in all but
two cases:  ..."

You can start your search into this highly complex area at

http://ppdbooks.pok.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/IGYMG200/3.3.1
```

#### ↳ Re: OSVS Cobol vs COBOL II under MVS

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77iasf$d9l@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<8O1n2.45$O45.223@testbox>`

```
Sounds like your COBOL program isn't really the "top" program in the
application.  When you say "call again in the execution" - what exactly is
the "top" program in the application (i.e. what is it written in)?  What you
need to do is to get the top program to "initialize" the COBOL environment,
and then all will be "as expected" (well, MINOR performance degradations may
still occur).  There are several ways of doing this, depending on what is
really on "top".

  Depending on whether you are talking about IMS transactions, Assembler
drivers, or 3rd party software, different approaches can be the best.

Check out,

http://ppdbooks.pok.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/IGYA1101/3.9.5
..2?ACTION=MATCHES&REQUEST=rtereus&TYPE=FUZZY&searchTopic=TOPIC&searchText=TE
XT&searchIndex=INDEX&rank=RANK#FIRSTHIT

which points to the topic,
  "Invoking Top-Level Programs as Subprograms"
in the VS COBOL II Programmer's Guide for more details.

P.S. You don't say whether you are running with the LE or VS COBOL II
run-time system - but my guess is that it is VS COBOL II.  If you have LE
(and/or OS/390), then there are some slight variations to the answer - even
if you are using the VS COBOL II compiler.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OSVS Cobol vs COBOL II under MVS

- **From:** "Tom Eisenman" <Tom_Eisenman@NOJUNKbigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Lh3n2.50$O45.228@testbox>`
- **References:** `<8O1n2.45$O45.223@testbox> <77iasf$d9l@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`

```
The COBOL program is not the "top" program.  The top program is written in a
reporting language called "Data Analyzer" which interprets statements in its
code and generates Fortran source.  The FORTRAN source is immediately
compiled, linked and run.

We think the crux of the problem is to identify why it worked well with OS
VS Cobol but poorly with COBOL II.  It seems like we should be able to
compile COBOL II to behave the same as OS VS Cobol.

may

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OSVS Cobol vs COBOL II under MVS

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369D691F.E4E31720@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<8O1n2.45$O45.223@testbox> <77iasf$d9l@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <Lh3n2.50$O45.228@testbox>`

```
Tom Eisenman wrote:
> 
> The COBOL program is not the "top" program.  The top program is written in a
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> compile COBOL II to behave the same as OS VS Cobol.

Have you tried compiling the COBOL II program with the NORES option?  I
may be wrong, but I believe a NORES program doesn't need a cobol runtime
environment, and may behave more like your OS/VS COBOL program.

You should be aware that if this solves your problem, NORES is not an
available option in newer IBM COBOL compilers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OSVS Cobol vs COBOL II under MVS

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369D6E36.886023BD@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<8O1n2.45$O45.223@testbox> <77iasf$d9l@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <Lh3n2.50$O45.228@testbox> <369D691F.E4E31720@worldnet.att.net>`

```
NORENT is the option you need.  What is happening is that upon each entry into the
COBOL II program, you are starting the program in an initial state. The OS/VS
COBOL's Working Storage is maintained between calls.  Checking for a first time
flag maintained in the calling program will fail, as your table does not contain
valid data.

To paraphrase IBM:

COBOL programs that are compiled RENT behave differently
under the VS COBOL II run time.

For VS COBOL II and IBM COBOL programs that are run under Language Environment
Release 5 or later
 the same copy of WORKING-STORAGE is used for each call. And, the program
 is entered in its last-used state, unless there is an intervening CANCEL.

Under VS COBOL II, different copies of WORKING-STORAGE is used for each...

Regards,
John

…[19 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OSVS Cobol vs COBOL II under MVS

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369DA7F9.F9E40324@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<8O1n2.45$O45.223@testbox> <77iasf$d9l@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <Lh3n2.50$O45.228@testbox> <369D691F.E4E31720@worldnet.att.net> <369D6E36.886023BD@ix.netcom.com> <369D7578.81D51B4E@worldnet.att.net> <369d8789.98789356@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On 14 Jan 1999 04:40:13 GMT, Arnold Trembley
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks!

There's really two questions here.  If you want to retain the contents
of working-storage in the COBOL subprogram across CALL statements, then
it looks like you need NORENT (non-re-entrant).  The RES/NORES option
just determines whether the program requires a runtime library (RES) or
will have the COBOL runtime routines statically linked into it.  But you
no longer have the choice of RES/NORES with COBOL for MVS and VM, you
have to use the runtime library.

Generally RES,NORENT is fine for batch COBOL.  We had a bad experience
with an OS/VS COBOL subprogram that was converted to COBOL II.  As a
COBOL II program, we compiled it with the RES option, so it required
access to the COBOL II runtime library.  But the old OS/VS COBOL program
was compiled NORES, and it was called dynamically by an assembler main
program in another department.  The first time that program called the
COBOL II version, it abended because it had no COBOL runtime
environment.

I have my favorite compile time options, so here they are (some will
disagree with my choices):

X(FULL)    The Cross-reference will include non-referenced items
OFFSET     Offset list, so you can relate an offset addres to a COBOL
line number
TRUNC(OPT) For my money, the closest approximation to the OS/VS
"NOTRUNC" option
NUMPROC(NOPFD)  Maximum sign fixup, slightly inefficient, but prevents
more S0C7 abends            than NUMPROC(MIG) does.
RES	   COBOL library routines are invoked at runtime
NORENT     Generate non-re-entrant code

For CICS programming, there are some differences:
LIST       Generate a full disassembly, rather than an OFFSET list
RENT	   Generate re-entrant code (required for CICS)
AMODE(31) AND RMODE(ANY) for virtual storage constraint relief.

There's been some debate here in the past on TRUNC(OPT) and
NUMPROC(NOPFD) but I use them for both batch and CICS without any
problems.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OSVS Cobol vs COBOL II under MVS

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369D9496.9F7DC631@att.net>`
- **References:** `<8O1n2.45$O45.223@testbox> <77iasf$d9l@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <Lh3n2.50$O45.228@testbox> <369D691F.E4E31720@worldnet.att.net> <369D6E36.886023BD@ix.netcom.com> <369D7578.81D51B4E@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Arnold Trembley wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> I'm trying to remember now if a COBOL II program has to be RENT in order
> to run above the line.  I've never seen a COBOL II program too big to
> fit in 16 megabytes, but a really large table could do that.

No, these are independent variables. RENT determines how Working-Storage
is managed, while AMODE determines its addressability (there's no
problem, BTW, with making your programs AMODE(ANY) & RMODE(24)).

Bill Lynch
```

Folks,  several rather critical corrections to this thread  (unless stated
otherwise, this is for VS COBOL II  - LE and COBOL for this-and-that have
slightly different rules and regulations)

1) MOST CRITICAL
   "last used state" has NOTHING to do with RES, RENT, or related options.
It has to do with "Is this program a main program?  Has it been canceled?
Does it have the 'IS INITIAL' attribute'"  - All of these have to do with
application (not just individual source code) issues.

2) RENT requires RES; there is no corresponding "requirement" for NORES if
you are NORENT (i.e. NORENT, RES, is perfectly valid).

   NOTE WELL: RENT was most frequently used with VS COBOL II (non-CICS) in
order to get your application above the 16M line.  If (as indicated in the
original question), you are increasing the size of a COBOL table, check out
whether or not you have enough "private" space below the 16M line - if you
want to use NORES and/or NORENT.

3) If you are NORES, not only are you due for serious problems going to LE,
you also may NOT use any dynamic CALLs (either via the DYNAM compiler option
or by the CALL identifier syntax).

4) In order to get a COBOL subprogram to "maintain" data (in Working-Storage
Section) from invocation to invocation (under a non-COBOL driver) you MUST
use one of the techniques that identify the COBOL program as a subprogram
rather than a main program. - e.g.
     ILBOSTP0 (called by driver)
     IGZERRE  (called by driver)
     COBOL stub (calling the non-COBOL driver)

5) If you have a FORTRAN driver (rather than Assembler, PL/I, etc), please
see the topic
  "Calls between VS COBOL II and FORTRAN" (viewable on the web at

http://ppdbooks.pok.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/IGYA1101/3.9.2
..3
before trying to handle this

6)  IBM has *NEVER* even hinted that you can recompile an OS/VS COBOL
program with VS COBOL II and "blindly" expect it to work the same.  There
are lots AND LOTS of issues involved with this.  IBM has migration guides
(and there are non-IBM books - like mine) dealing with this.  Please do not
try this and expect a "simple" process.

7) NORES and CMPR2 are compiler options that do solve "some" minor migration
options - but NORES has already disappeared in the current strategic COBOL
products (COBOL for this-and-that under LE) and CMPR2 is not strategic (and
is dropped from VisualAge for COBOL).  Please do NOT try and use these as
anything but very temporary "circumvention's" (not fixes) to individual
problems.  Allocate the time/resources as soon as possible to FIX the
underlying problem - if/when you use one of these.

8) Other compiler options to look at include (when doing migrations)
    NUMPROC(MIG)
    TRUNC(???)  - nothing works exactly like OS/VS COBOL (watch out for
performance ramifications too)
    WSCLEAR (VS COBOL II) and STORAGE (LE) - run-time, not compile-time
options
    OPT  - (you can lose literals from working-storage)

9) Static link-edits (as opposed to NCAL or dynamic COBOL calls) *can*
impact the retention of data across CALLs - but they still don't make a
subprogram into a main program.

   ***

Good luck!

  I still think the best (possible) circumvention for the originally
reported problem, is to create a VS COBOL II stub to call the FORTRAN
driver.  This should avoid any source code changes and SHOULD solve the
"last used state" problem.  However, I have never tried this with a FORTRAN
driver, so some problems may still remain.
```

## Thread: Problems with C/C++ Cobol DLL call — 6 msgs

### Problems with C/C++ Cobol DLL call

- **From:** Johan Palmæus <pjohan@hal.sparta.lu.se>
- **Date:** 1999-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369C91F7.B6E3A4A3@hal.sparta.lu.se>`

```

Hi, I have some problems calling a Cobol DLL. The problem is that
I'm importing it from a C++ program and I don't really know Cobol and
the guy who wrote the DLL doesn't know C++... I hope some Cobol AND C++
guru can help me...(The cobol DLL was made with Netexpress and the
program is compiled in Microsoft Visual C++ version 6)

The function I call in the DLL is called "SYDENTRY", and the Cobol-guy
says that in Cobol he would have called it this way:

CALL "SYDENTRY" USING XX-PARAMETER.

where XX-PARAMETER is described as:

01 XX-PARAMETER.
   05  FORNAMN   PIC  X(35).
   05  EFTNAMN   PIC  X(35).
   05  GADRNA    PIC  X(35).
   05  POSTNR    PIC  X(09).
   05  ORTNAMN   PIC  X(35).
   05  LANDKOD   PIC  X(03).
   05  KONKOD    PIC  X(01).
   05  PERSNR    PIC  X(10).
   05  STTS      PIC  X(02).
   05  MATCH     PIC  X(37).

I have no problems loading the Cobol-DLL (with LoadLibrary and
GetProcAddress), and finding the function but I there is a strange
problem when using it. When I import it I define the function in C(++)
as

const matchCodeParameterSize = 202;
typedef void (*pointerToMatchCode) (char
message[matchCodeParameterSize]);

The DLL reads inputdata from the first 8 variables and write returndata
to the last two (STTS & MATCH). I but the imput data in the big
char-array where message[0-34] is the first entry, message [35-69] is
the next one etc.

Mostly this works but there are som problems:

1) If I compile in VC++ in debugmode I get a notify that "The value of
ESP was not properly saved accross a function call. This is usually a
result of calling a function declared with one calling convention with a
function pointer declared with a different calling convention". But
mostly it works if I just press "Ignore". When compiling releasecode
theres no such warnings (and mostly the code works ok except from 2)
below)

2) Sometimes I get strange results like other variables being written
over when calling the DLL. But by just moving the place of the call the
problems disappear and everything works ok again.

I suppose the problem is something about the datadefinition and the
size. Since I usually get correct result it is not completely wrong but
obviously something is bad in the memory management...

Are there anyone out there wo can help me with this?
```

#### ↳ Re: Problems with C/C++ Cobol DLL call

- **From:** "Carsten A. Arnholm" <arnholm@online.no>
- **Date:** 1999-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77j74c$at$1@readme.online.no>`
- **References:** `<369C91F7.B6E3A4A3@hal.sparta.lu.se>`

```
Johan Palm�us wrote in message <369C91F7.B6E3A4A3@hal.sparta.lu.se>...
>
>Hi, I have some problems calling a Cobol DLL. The problem is that
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>as

> const matchCodeParameterSize = 202;
> typedef void (*pointerToMatchCode) (char
> message[matchCodeParameterSize]);


First, I don't know COBOL at all, but I know Fortran and they are generally
considered the same generation. From the (Swedish!) COBOL and the C++ code
it looks like what you are trying to pass as a parameter is this (expressed
in C++):

struct xx_parameter {
   char EFTNAMN[35];
   char GADRNA[35];
   char POSTNR[9];
   char ORTNAMN[35];
   char LANDKOD[3];
   char KONKOD;
   char PERSNR[10];
   char STTS[2];
   char MATCH[37];
};

You need to figure out the following:
1. Which calling convention do you need to use?
2. How must the parameter be passed, bu value or reference?

here are a few C++ prototype declarations you could try

extern "C" {
    typedef void __cdecl (*funcptr1)(xx_parameter& by_ref);
    typedef void __cdecl (*funcptr2)(xx_parameter   by_val);  // <--
equivalent to yours?
    typedef void __stdcall (*funcptr3)(xx_parameter& by_ref);
    typedef void __stdcall (*funcptr4)(xx_parameter   by_val);
}

see also my website for advice on mixed C++/Fortran programming

Carsten A. Arnholm
arnholm@online.no
http://home.sol.no/~arnholm/
```

#### ↳ Re: Problems with C/C++ Cobol DLL call

- **From:** "Martijn Lievaart" <nobody@orion.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-01-13T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<77i5u6$7tv@news3.euro.net>`

>
>Hi, I have some problems calling a Cobol DLL. The problem is that
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This is really off-topic in all the newsgroups you posted to, as it is
implementation specific.

You will need to read the manual for the COBOL compiler, maybe contact the
manufacturer. The information should be there. Then you'll have to look up
calling dll's in the MSVC on-line help, looking specifically for calling
dll's written in other languages.

Actually it shouldn't be to hard. If you've still got questions after that,
you'll stand a much better chance for answers in a microsoft specific
newsgroup for VC.

HTH,
Martijn
```

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

Hi. I've briefly scanned the code you posted and there is no glaringly
obvious error in it. However, the one piece of information that is
relevant to the error you are mentioning (possible call convention
mismatch) has not been given.
Can you post compilable, linkable cut-downs of the actual code, and
instructions on the exact compile-time and link-time options you use ?
I realise that this may not actually show the problem any more, but
it will give us a hint as to whether your call conventions are
consistent.

Cheers, Kev.

Some of the answer to this may depend on which COBOL compiler is being used.
In this case it's NetExpress (some version).  I would check with someone
that knows NetExpress from MicroFocus and see if that version supports C++
the way it was intended to be used.

Just for your information, that MF program could be converted into Fujitsu
COBOL 4.0 and then C++ could use it. I know that Fujitsu COBOL 4.0 works
with C++ and VB seamlessly even with a debugger.  :)

Regards,
Gary Roush

## Thread: Please help me!!!! — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Please help me!!!!

- **From:** "Sally Hall" <Hallsframpton@btinternet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77ium4$6nc$1@plutonium.btinternet.com>`

```
Hello Guys!

 I've been given this procedure in Pascal to put into a proper program.

 It's designed for a salesman who wants to know how much commission he can
earn on selling different products. If the product is in category A, he
charges 0.5% commission. If it's in category B, he can charge 1% commission.
If it's in category C, he can charge 2% commission. If it's in category D,
he can charge 2.5% commission.

Here's the procedure code:

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
SALES-SEG.
    ACCEPT PRODUCT=CODE.
    ACCEPT SALE=VALUE.
COMM-SEL.
    IF PRODUCT-CODE NOT = "A" GO TO COMM-OR-2.
      MUTLIPLY 0.005 BY SALE-VALUE GIVING COMMISSION,
    GO TC COMM-END.
COMM-OR-2.
    IF PRODUCT-CODE NOT = "B" GO TO COMM-OR-3.
      MULTIPLY 0.01 BY SALE-VALUE GIVING COMMISSION,
    GO TO COMM-END.
COMM-OR-3.
    IF PRODUCT-CODE NOT = "C" GO TO COMM-OR-4.
      MULTIPLY 0.02 BY SALE-VALUE GIVING COMMISSION.
    GO TO COMM-END.
COMM-OR-4.
      MULTIPLY 0.025 BY SALE-VALUE GIVING COMMISSION.
COMM-END.
    DISPLAY CONFIRMATION. STOP RUN.
SALES-END.

Thanks ever so much!!!!



Tony Hall

(please send your reply to 056540@gloscat.ac.uk as well as
tonyhall@btinternet.com)
```

#### ↳ Re: Please help me!!!!

- **From:** Alex Romaniuk <ales.romaniuk@zag.si>
- **Date:** 1999-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369E28E5.7D91@zag.si>`
- **References:** `<77ium4$6nc$1@plutonium.btinternet.com>`

```
Sally Hall wrote:
> PROCEDURE DIVISION.
> SALES-SEG.
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> tonyhall@btinternet.com)

There could be more efficient code like:

 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
 SALES-SEG.
     ACCEPT PRODUCT-CODE.
     ACCEPT SALE-VALUE.
     EVALUATE PRODUCT-CODE
        WHEN  "A"    MULTIPLY 0.005 BY SALE-VALUE GIVING COMMISSION
        WHEN  "B"    MULTIPLY 0.01  BY SALE-VALUE GIVING COMMISSION
        WHEN  "C"    MULTIPLY 0.02  BY SALE-VALUE GIVING COMMISSION
        WHEN  "D"    MULTIPLY 0.025 BY SALE-VALUE GIVING COMMISSION
        WHEN OTHER DISPLAY "WHAT IS WRONG ???"
     END-EVALUATE
     DISPLAY CONFIRMATION. STOP RUN.
 SALES-END.

or in Pascal

   READLN(PRODUCT-CODE);
   READLN(SALE-VALUE);
   CASE PRODUCT-CODE OF
      'A': COMMISSION := SALE-VALUE * 0.005 ;
      'B': COMMISSION := SALE-VALUE * 0.01 ;
      'C': COMMISSION := SALE-VALUE * 0.02 ;
      'D': COMMISSION := SALE-VALUE * 0.025 ;
      else
         WRITELN('WHAT IS WRONG ???');
   END ;
   WRITELN(CONFIRMATION);
```

#### ↳ Re: Please help me!!!!

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5Tvn2.3243$hE2.20528302@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<77ium4$6nc$1@plutonium.btinternet.com>`

```
>PROCEDURE DIVISION.
>SALES-SEG.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>SALES-END.


First, for a little criticism...never ever use GO TO statements.

But maybe try this:

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01  COMMISSION-RATE-DATA.
    05 FILLER       PIC X(22)
       VALUE '0005  0010  0020  0025'.
01  FILLER REDEFINES COMMISSION-RATE-DATA.
    05 COMMISSION-RATE OCCURS 4 TIMES
                    PIC 9V999.
01  COMMISSION-SUB  PIC 999.

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
SALES-SEG.
     ACCEPT PRODUCT-CODE
     ACCEPT SALE-VALUE
     COMPUTE COMMISSION-SUB = FUNCTION ORD(PRODUCT-CODE)
                              - ORD('A') +1
     IF COMMISSION-SUB > 0 AND < 5 THEN
        MULTIPLY COMMISSION-RATE(COMMISSION-SUB)
              BY SALE-VALUE
          GIVING COMMISSION
     ELSE
        DISPLAY "WHAT IS WRONG ???"
     END-IF
     DISPLAY CONFIRMATION
     STOP RUN
     .
```

<a id="t-08fa99a969"></a>
## Thread: Microfocus COBOL linking problem — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Microfocus COBOL linking problem

- **From:** bterzic@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77iluo$290$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Hello,

I'm just completing a COBOL project and it seems to work fine except for one
little snag. I'm using the MicroFocus COBOL compiler/linker and I'm working
in a Win98 environment (in a dos-box). I use the following compile and link
command line: To compile: COBOL prog.cbl /NESTCALL To link: LINK prog.obj
adis.obj adisinit.obj adiskey.obj extfh.obj cobintfn.obj

Thiw works and I'm able to run my program without any problems on _my
computer_, which has COBOL installed of course. To be able to run it on a
different computer though it seems I have to link in LCOBOL.LIB but this is
were the problem lies. When I do this with this command line:

LINK prog.obj adis.obj adisinit.obj adiskey.obj extfh.obj
cobintfn.obj,,,LCOBOL.LIB,,

(all on one line of course)
Then the program crashes (Windows gives an 'performed illegal operation')
whenever I try to write/delete a record in a file.

If anybody has any ideas as to why it does this and more importantly how I
could solve this it would be much appreciated.

Thanks in advance,

Boris Terzic
ntropy@dma.be

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus COBOL linking problem

- **From:** "kennet" <kennet@post11.tele.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77j6bd$189o$1@news-inn.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<77iluo$290$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I'm not sure of this, since I'm using OS/2, and possibly another linker. But
if I want an executable to be able to run on a PC without the runtime
environment installed, all I have to do is use the link option -t. This
makes the linking process considerably slower, but it generates a standalone
program.

Don't you have a similar option?

Kennet

#### ↳ Re: Migration tool for moving to LE?

- **From:** Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369e8424$2$tfs$mr2ice@news>`
- **References:** `<77imgo$2r5$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In <77imgo$2r5$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>, on 13 Jan 1999 at 17:52,
tony.granata@gecapital.com said:

>  I was wondering if anyone out there knows of a tool that will scan
>COBOL source and determine what changes will be necessary so that the
>module(s) in question will compile under the LE environment.  Of
>particular interest is a tool that will do this for both COBOL II and
>pre-COBOL II source modules.

There is about a dozen different tools that convert OS/VS COBOL (or DOS/VS
COBOL) to the current levels of COBOL (OS/390, VM and VSE).  Some of these
tools can also handle the conversion from VS COBOL II Release 2 (CMPR2) to
full ANSI 85 standard.  

If you don't use CMPR2 in COBOL II, converting the code to a more recent
level of COBOL is only a matter of changing two reserved words (one is
FUNCTION, the other is quite obscure and I can't think of it at the
moment).  There could be run-time problems when going from COBOL II to
COBOL for MVS though, but it's a different story.

Our company (AMS) provides conversion services (mostly VSE/MVS and COBOL)
using our own tools, which have been designed to perform "en masse", as
opposed to more traditional tools which process one program at a time. 
This allows us to convert, compile and link-edit up to 2000 programs an
hour: give us an entire inventory (1000s of programs, COPY books, etc) on
wednesday night, and your programmers can have a complete, consistent, set
of converted source and load-modules ready for testing thursday morning.

I you have any question, don't hesitate to contact me.

 Gilbert Saint-flour
 Technical Manager
 Automated Migration Services
 http://ams.atstaffing.com/ams/ams.html
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: User-defined functions in micro-focus cobol 3.4?

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1999-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369f74fc.192071544@news.enterprise.net>`
- **References:** `<77jm21$63r$1@winter.news.rcn.net> <VXHn2.175$c92.459634@news4.mia>`

```
On Fri, 15 Jan 1999 14:06:45 GMT, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:

>
>I use MF COBOL 3.2, and have heard that it supports user written
>functions.  However, I can't find reference to it in the manuals,
>or in the sample code supplied by Micro Focus.  If somebody knows
>for sure, I would like to know too.

Are you not confusing it with installable functions? (INSTALLF style)

These let you install assembler functions and use them like they were
runtime routines.

It's not the same as functions in C or Pascal. In COBOL I usually use
the often maligned ENTRY statement for that.

eg.
working-storage section.
01 stuff  	pic(x).

linkage section.
01 a               pic x(10).
01 b               pic x(10).
01 c               pic x.
procedure division.
   if stuff = 'Y'
       display 'You've already done stuff'
   else
       move 'Y' to stuff
   end-if
   exit program.
   stop run.

entry 'func-a' using a,c.
    perform do-a
    exit program.

entry 'func-b' using a, b,c.
    perform do-b
    exit program.

do-a section.
    display a
    move 'a' to c.

do-b section.
    display a
    display b
    move 'b' to c.



or something like that (ie. I haven't compiled that so it may not work
;-) )

The calling program needs to call the program then it can call the
entry points unless your using DLL's
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: User-defined functions in micro-focus cobol 3.4?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-15T00:00:00

Shaun C. Murray wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>>I use MF COBOL 3.2, and have heard that it supports user written
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>runtime routines.

Not knowingly. :-)  There have been newsgroup discussions about creating
callable subroutines and functions in COBOL.  At some point I believe
someone stated that MF COBOL has the facility (non standard of course),
to create user written functions in COBOL.  It is possible that I do not
remember correctly (my memory is getting older like the rest of me :),
or that the poster was mistaken, or that it is in a different version of
MF COBOL than I have.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: User-defined functions in micro-focus cobol 3.4?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77oeun$osq@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<77jm21$63r$1@winter.news.rcn.net> <VXHn2.175$c92.459634@news4.mia> <369f74fc.192071544@news.enterprise.net> <g5Ln2.521$c92.559779@news4.mia>`

```
I wish the original person who asked the question would "jump in" and
explain what they really want.

1)  If you are using "function" just because you aren't used to the term
"sub-program" - then every COBOL compiler supports this (many/most allow
interlanguage "Calls" so the caller can be C and the callee COBOL or vice
versa)

2) If by "function" you mean that the "called thing" must RETURN a value,
then Micro Focus, IBM, Fujitsu, CA (and probably several others) - ALL have
an extension to allow a RETURNING option on an EXIT PROGRAM statement.

3) If you are looking at a way to create a "user-defined" function that can
be used in COBOL in syntax like:

  Move Function I-wrote-it-myself (parm1 parm2) to Somewhere

then this feature WAS introduced by Micro Focus quite a while ago - and will
be in the next Standard.  Look in the MF documentation under such topics as
"user-defined functions" and "function proto-type".  I can't remember which
release introduced it - but if you can't find it, I'll check with my MF
sources to get more detail.

4) If you are talking about "installation" options, then we need a lot more
information on what you really want.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: User-defined functions in micro-focus cobol 3.4?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** renfrew@erols.com (Dave Miner)
- **Date:** 1999-01-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77p7aj$ooa$1@winter.news.rcn.net>`
- **References:** `<77jm21$63r$1@winter.news.rcn.net> <VXHn2.175$c92.459634@news4.mia> <369f74fc.192071544@news.enterprise.net> <g5Ln2.521$c92.559779@news4.mia> <77oeun$osq@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`

```
My humblest apologies to Mr Klein for being unclear.  I've always thought of functions and 
subprograms as two different animals, but had I shown an example of what I meant it would have 
avoided the confusion.

I want to be able to write my own function, in Micro-Focus COBOL version 3.4, to operate just 
like an intrinsic function.  Here is an example:  
               MOVE FUNCTION MYFUNC (ARG1 ARG2 ARGn) TO WHATEVER.  

Now to follow up further with a bit more info about what got me onto this track..... 

I was browsing a website containing a really excellent Report Writer tutorial (see 
"http://www.spc-systems.com/refce/cobrwr.htm") that eventually segued into a discussion about 
user-written COBOL functions.  These functions are apparently invoked just like the pre-canned 
intrinsic functions are.  After reading about this, and naively presuming that this was a 
cobol-standard feature, I excitedly wrote up my own simple function program -- but when I checked 
my MF manuals to figure out how to link it to a calling program I found absolutely no references 
to user-written functions, nor about Function Control Areas.  I've been clinically depressed ever 
since.  
										
(By the way, if you would like to see the user-written function discussion, go to 
"http://www.spc-systems.com/refce/cobrwr.htm", select "Special Topics", and then select 
"Developing User-written Functions".)

The Report Writer tutorial also described how to conditionally generate detail lines, but my 
hopes were similarly dashed when I found no references to "PRESENT WHEN" in my MF-cobol manuals. 
 But I still think it's a great tutorial.  Even though some of its clauses aren't universal, it 
still explains the basic Report Writer concepts many times better than the MF manuals do.  It 
also includes some working examples.  I highly recommend it. 

Thanks,
Dave Miner
renfrew@erols.com



In article <77oeun$osq@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>, wmklein@nospam.netcom.com says...
>
>I wish the original person who asked the question would "jump in" and
…[57 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: User-defined functions in micro-focus cobol 3.4?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77pa3n$g8s@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<77jm21$63r$1@winter.news.rcn.net> <VXHn2.175$c92.459634@news4.mia> <369f74fc.192071544@news.enterprise.net> <g5Ln2.521$c92.559779@news4.mia> <77oeun$osq@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <77p7aj$ooa$1@winter.news.rcn.net>`

```
Both USER-DEFINED functions and all the PRESENT WHEN stuff *will* be in the
next COBOL Standard (assuming it ever gets done and doesn't change).

"User-defined functions" are available with Micro Focus COBOL - but I think
(if my memory serves me correctly) they were still "early release" in the
3.4 product (which is so old that I actually still worked for them when it
was supported).

I'll pass this on to MF people, but you should check:

A) in your Early-Release dox/disk

B) see if Function-ID works in the Identification Division of your program
when you run with the highest MF(nn) directive - and might try the
early-release directive as well.

As my memory goes for the 3.4 product, you needed to define your functions
with each compile (probably via a COPY statement) - but I may not remember
the full syntax or rules any more.
```
