```
-->"http://www.spc-systems.com/refce/cobrwr.htm") that eventually segued into a
discussion about 
-->user-written COBOL functions.  These functions are apparently invoked just like
the pre-canned 
-->intrinsic functions are.  After reading about this, and naively presuming that
this was a 
-->cobol-standard feature, I excitedly wrote up my own simple function program -- but
when I checked 
-->my MF manuals to figure out how to link it to a calling program I found absolutely
no references 
-->to user-written functions, nor about Function Control Areas.  I've been clinically
depressed ever 
-->since.  
-->										
-->(By the way, if you would like to see the user-written function discussion, go to 

"http://www.spc-systems.com/refce/cobrwr.htm"

--> select "Special Topics", and then select 
-->"Developing User-written Functions".)
-->
-->The Report Writer tutorial also described how to conditionally generate detail
lines, but my 
-->hopes were similarly dashed when I found no references to "PRESENT WHEN" in my
MF-cobol manuals. 
--> But I still think it's a great tutorial.  Even though some of its clauses aren't
universal, it 
-->still explains the basic Report Writer concepts many times better than the MF
manuals do.  It 
-->also includes some working examples.  I highly recommend it. 
-->
-->Thanks,
-->Dave Miner
-->renfrew@erols.com

Thanks for the link.  We are about to go into report writer in earnest (use it in
programs from the next one on.)  This reference looks pretty complete. For me is
extremely timely!  (I'll share the url with the class.)   TS


"Come to think of it, there are already a million monkeys on a million
 typewriters, and Usenet is NOTHING like Shakespeare." Blair Houghton
*********No generalization is wholly true, not even this one**********
++++++****tauceti****@****abraxis****.****com****for***email****++++++
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: User-defined functions in micro-focus cobol 3.4?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<781mpp$62@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<77jm21$63r$1@winter.news.rcn.net> <VXHn2.175$c92.459634@news4.mia> <369f74fc.192071544@news.enterprise.net> <g5Ln2.521$c92.559779@news4.mia> <77oeun$osq@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <77p7aj$ooa$1@winter.news.rcn.net>`

```
I have checked with the MF people - and they do NOT think that user-defined
functions were in the 3.4 product.  You might find (early release) support
for "vocabularies" - which would provide similar (but certainly NOT
identical) functionality.  The recommendation from MF is to wait for an
upcoming (no guarantee when) release that WILL support user-defined
functions in the manner that is defined in the (draft) Standard - hopefully
before the draft Standard changes its definition!

P.S.  The 3.4 product really is pretty old.  If you are looking for "new and
exciting" COBOL syntax, I would sure think that you might want to move to
one of the newer products/releases.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: User-defined functions in micro-focus cobol 3.4?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1999-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36a49e2b.530294653@news.enterprise.net>`
- **References:** `<77jm21$63r$1@winter.news.rcn.net> <VXHn2.175$c92.459634@news4.mia> <369f74fc.192071544@news.enterprise.net> <g5Ln2.521$c92.559779@news4.mia> <77oeun$osq@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <77p7aj$ooa$1@winter.news.rcn.net> <781mpp$62@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`

```
On Tue, 19 Jan 1999 04:29:26 -0600, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:


>P.S.  The 3.4 product really is pretty old.  If you are looking for "new and
>exciting" COBOL syntax, I would sure think that you might want to move to
>one of the newer products/releases.
>

1996 isn't that old but more significantly it's the only 16bit product
that is still supported by Micro Focus. (at least till June 1999
anyway)

There are still quite a few DOS based systems and Win3.1 systems that
rely on that or an earlier compiler. Architecturally, I have a couple
of systems that won't work on anything other than a 16bit compiler
without major work but those are not your usual COBOL DOS apps. Not
too many use XM API calls and dig about inside the concurrency calls
in the run-time for instance.

There is no point in upgrading to a Win32 platform for these apps
because the application in which they are used does not warrant a GUI
application. DOS is also significantly more robust than Windows.

So, I think it's a bit of a shame Micro Focus have left the low
overhead compiler market but that's progress I guess.
```

```
>Shaun C. Murray wrote:
>>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>In this case it's change, but not progress.

Unfortunately, the conventional wisdom is that people who are still
running Win 3.1 or MS-DOS are too cheap to spend much money on software.
*Nobody* is maintaining their 16-bit software.  Netscape writes their
software on Unix, then ports it to other platforms, but they think the
porting job is over when the program just barely runs.  Netscape 4 uses
some file access techniques that are disasterously slow on a 16-bit OS.
Their defense is that I should be happy they have a 16-bit version at all.
```

```
> 
> There is no point in upgrading to a Win32 platform for these apps
> because the application in which they are used does not warrant a GUI
> application. DOS is also significantly more robust than Windows.

I recently developed a new system for a client where he
specified dumb terminals as a requirement for the
warehouse users.  I used Multiuser DOS, Wyse-60s and
SP2 DOS version.  This gives multiple sessions on each
terminal if required, a text mode GUI, and can be 
easily made to run using a Windows GUI or Unix.

It runs fast and the users don't spend half the day
playing solitaire or changing the screen savers to
pornographic pictures.

> So, I think it's a bit of a shame Micro Focus have left the low
> overhead compiler market but that's progress I guess.

Still, the existing compilers won't stop working (I hope).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: User-defined functions in micro-focus cobol 3.4?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** renfrew@erols.com (Dave Miner)
- **Date:** 1999-01-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7882m9$8em$1@winter.news.rcn.net>`
- **References:** `<77jm21$63r$1@winter.news.rcn.net> <VXHn2.175$c92.459634@news4.mia> <369f74fc.192071544@news.enterprise.net> <g5Ln2.521$c92.559779@news4.mia> <77oeun$osq@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <77p7aj$ooa$1@winter.news.rcn.net> <781mpp$62@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`

```
		
I am in absolute and total agreement with the suggestion to upgrade to a 
newer compiler, but convincing my co-workers and my management of that 
is quite another matter.  Since so many kind people from the newsgroups 
have been giving me this sage-but-unfortunately-inapplicable advice, I 
will now try to kill many birds with one stone by elaborating here why 
we're stuck with level 3.4.....

Our system is of a dos-driven structure, and we stayed with it even after 
we changed our OS to Win95 a couple of years ago.  Our programs are 
written in a combination of three versions of MF cobol -- 3.1, 3.2 and 
3.4.  Since the folks at Micro-Focus have advised us that the former two 
versions are not Y2k-compatible, we have been in the process of 
converting all of the old versions up to level 3.4.

As a result of a few of our departmental Y2k meetings, our group has 
decided that we will stick with level 3.4 until sometime after the year 
2000.  I begged and pleaded with them to upgrade us to either 4.0 or 
NetExpress 2.0, but they want us to stay at our present level because 
they feel that moving us up to a 32-bit compiler during the Y2K crunch 
period would unnecessarily complicate our transition.  They politely 
listened to my objections, but they remain unconcerned that level 3.4 
will become an unsupported product as of June 1999.

My biggest hope is that they are right and that I am wrong.  My 
next-biggest hope is that we find out sooner as opposed to later about 
why sticking with 3.4 would be a bad choice.  If anyone out there is 
aware of any problems with 3.4, particularly y2k-related problems, 
please let me know.  In fact I will repost this request under the new 
heading  "MF Cobol 3.4: Any Y2K problems? ".  

Okay, back to the original subject.....  

I guess I can still do what I need to do via called subprograms -- its 
just that a simple function would have been so much cleaner, easier to 
review, and easier for the next guy to do maintenance on.  My sincerest 
thanks to all of you who went out of your way to look into this matter, 
and for the alternative suggestions.

Dave


In article <781mpp$62@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>, wmklein@nospam.netcom.com says...
>
>I have checked with the MF people - and they do NOT think that user-defined
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>Dave Miner wrote in message <77p7aj$ooa$1@winter.news.rcn.net>...

[snipped]

>>I want to be able to write my own function, in Micro-Focus COBOL version
>>3.4, to operate just like an intrinsic function.  Here is an example:
>>               MOVE FUNCTION MYFUNC (ARG1 ARG2 ARGn) TO WHATEVER.

[snipped]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: User-defined functions in micro-focus cobol 3.4?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78972p$c3t$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<77jm21$63r$1@winter.news.rcn.net> <VXHn2.175$c92.459634@news4.mia> <369f74fc.192071544@news.enterprise.net> <g5Ln2.521$c92.559779@news4.mia> <77oeun$osq@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <77p7aj$ooa$1@winter.news.rcn.net> <781mpp$62@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <7882m9$8em$1@winter.news.rcn.net>`

```

Dave Miner wrote in message <7882m9$8em$1@winter.news.rcn.net>...
>
[...]
>
>Our system is of a dos-driven structure, and we stayed with it even after
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>will become an unsupported product as of June 1999.

I have to agree with the group. Firstly, 4.0 will also be unsupported
as of June 30, 1999; therefore that path would not satisfy your desire
for a supported product. Secondly, NetExpress, AIUI, is a very
different product than Workbench; therefore you would be involved
in learning a new product that "would unnecessarily complicate" the
transtion. Thirdly, the new standard is still in flux which means that,
in using features of the proposed COBOL standard, you could be
required to make source code changes with each update to
NetExpress -- IMO it is better to wait.

As for support, I have found that the products from
Professional COBOL 1.1 (1984) through COBOL WB 3.2 (1994)
have had very few problems that actually required support.  I cannot
imagine that using 3.4 for another year or two, without support,
would be a problem.

>My biggest hope is that they are right and that I am wrong.  My
>next-biggest hope is that we find out sooner as opposed to later about
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>heading  "MF Cobol 3.4: Any Y2K problems? ".

I suggest you visit the Micro Focus web site to read their description
about Y2K compliance and to see the list of Y2K compatible products.
This list includes 3.4.20 as Y2K compatible.

Start at:
< http://www.microfocus.com/products/y2kcomp.htm >

The last products in any line tend to have the fewest problems.
WB 3.4 is the end of the line for MF 16-bit COBOL. It is reasonable
to believe that WB 3.4 would have fewer problems than NetExpress,
which is still being "improved." (AIUI, NE 3.0 is in beta.)

Based upon your earlier description of using a DOS-structure,
I would suggest that WB 3.4 is a better fit the NE 2.0, which is
intended for Windows-based enterprise development.

Although these are not the answers you want, they, at least, add
support toward your "biggest hope."
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: User-defined functions in micro-focus cobol 3.4?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-01-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36a9dc3e.381957905@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<77jm21$63r$1@winter.news.rcn.net> <VXHn2.175$c92.459634@news4.mia> <369f74fc.192071544@news.enterprise.net> <g5Ln2.521$c92.559779@news4.mia> <77oeun$osq@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <77p7aj$ooa$1@winter.news.rcn.net> <781mpp$62@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <7882m9$8em$1@winter.news.rcn.net> <78972p$c3t$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
On Fri, 22 Jan 1999 01:49:36 -0500, "Rick Smith"
<ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:


>The last products in any line tend to have the fewest problems.
>WB 3.4 is the end of the line for MF 16-bit COBOL. It is reasonable
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>support toward your "biggest hope."

Rick, 

Is there an update later than 3.4.24?  I wish MF would release one
before dropping support.  You see, where I worked before we were
running an updated (later than 3.4.24) version of EXTFH and REBUILD.
Why?  The one that came in 3.2 - thru 3.4.24 would ocasionally burp
when running a Dos Application on a Windows 95 workstation on a
network - Novell, NT, Win 95 Peer to Peer - didn't matter.  What would
happen is that a file using MicroFocus compression would become
corrupt and report a file size on the GIGABYTES - on machines with
less than a gig of hard disk!  We worked for quite some time with
support in Philly and the developers in England to get this fixed -
and were running that fixed code when I left.  But you won't get that
fix - or any later ones - in 3.4.24.  I know you don't work for MF,
but perhaps someone there who reads this will make the suggestion for
one last update release before the death of the product.
```

## Thread: What is the equivalent function in Cobol — 4 msgs

### What is the equivalent function in Cobol

- **From:** "Pedro Tavares" <pedrotavares@cmvm.pt>
- **Date:** 1999-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77kvs4$m8u$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
What's the equivalent  C function - fflush() in Cobol?
Thanks

Pedro Tavares
Lisbon, Portugal
```

#### ↳ Re: What is the equivalent function in Cobol

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1999-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369e1087.100818809@news.freeserve.net>`
- **References:** `<77kvs4$m8u$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
On Thu, 14 Jan 1999 14:49:14 -0000, "Pedro Tavares"
<pedrotavares@cmvm.pt> wrote:

>What's the equivalent  C function - fflush() in Cobol?

There isn't one. You don't need one. WRITE is all you need unless you
are doing something with a peculier compilers byte-stream I/O
functions perhaps.

What are you doing that you think you need to be sure that data has
been flushed from the buffers?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What is the equivalent function in Cobol

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1999-01-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990125172955.21395.00002781@ng133.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<369e1087.100818809@news.freeserve.net>`

```
Actually 

   CLOSE filename.

should flush the buffers. And the syntax

    CLOSE ALL.

is available on most compilers.


In online environments you need to trigger a syncpoint to get the data
management subroutines to flush buffers (that will commit all i/o not just DASD
related).

Best Wishes

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

```

formula:        _________A _________

A = amount of loan
n = number of payments
i = interest rate for one month    (yearly interest rate / 12)
```

```
On 14 Jan 1999 18:59:22 GMT, lewisf01@aol.com (LEWISF01) wrote:

>Does anyone know how to write one compute statement for this?

No idea. Your formula doesn't seem to follow any mathematical notation
I know. I'd also suggest that putting it in one compute statement is a
bad idea. Split it up into simple statements. It'll be more accurate,
easier to understand and probably faster to execute.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL FOMULA

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77vtk8$4fr@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<369e4c67.116146199@news.freeserve.net> <19990116154432.12052.00001700@ng-fb1.aol.com> <36a365fc.450375245@news.enterprise.net>`

```
I am truly "out of the loop" when it comes to terms such as "amortization"
and "present value" - but (as stated in an earlier part of this thread),
isn't this related to "present-value" and "net present value" - in which
case the single statement solution SHOULD involve the intrinsic function
that does this for you.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL FOMULA

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1999-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2225095E49182A77.F97C05614EBF86BD.16913DA264B2A224@library-proxy.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<369e4c67.116146199@news.freeserve.net> <19990116154432.12052.00001700@ng-fb1.aol.com> <36a365fc.450375245@news.enterprise.net> <77vtk8$4fr@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
On Mon, 18 Jan 1999 12:13:43 -0600, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> enlightened us:

>I am truly "out of the loop" when it comes to terms such as "amortization"
>and "present value" - but (as stated in an earlier part of this thread),
>isn't this related to "present-value" and "net present value" - in which
>case the single statement solution SHOULD involve the intrinsic function
>that does this for you.

Amortization is the process you use to calculate a fixed loan payment
given the principle, interest rate and term of the loan.  Present
value relates to money accruing interest or money that is held in an
annuity.  The definition and formula for present value can be
expressed as:

The total present amount (P) which will supply an annuity (N) at the
end of each year for (n) years beginning one year hence (assuming that
in successive years the amount not yet paid out earns interest at rate
(r), compounded annually) is,

1)  Calculate simple annuity (sn) at rate (r) = ((1+r)**n-1)/r
2)  Calculate annuity (an) at r = ((1-(1+r)**-n)/r
3)  Calculate Present Value  P =( N(sn))/(1 + r)**n 
      which should equal N(an)

As you can see, this is much more complex then amortization.

Cheers,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

It may be that your sole purpose in life is simply to serve as a warning to 
others.

 Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL FOMULA

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<782lce$kb7$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<369e4c67.116146199@news.freeserve.net> <19990116154432.12052.00001700@ng-fb1.aol.com> <36a365fc.450375245@news.enterprise.net> <77vtk8$4fr@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <2225095E49182A77.F97C05614EBF86BD.16913DA264B2A224@library-proxy.airnews.net>`

```

SkippyPB wrote in message
<2225095E49182A77.F97C05614EBF86BD.16913DA264B2A224@library-proxy.airnews.ne
t>...
>On Mon, 18 Jan 1999 12:13:43 -0600, "William M. Klein"
><wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> enlightened us:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>given the principle, interest rate and term of the loan.  Present
[...]
>
>As you can see, this is much more complex then amortization.
>
I think that what William M. Klein is looking for is:

    compute periodic-payment rounded =
        (function annuity ( periodic-interest number-of-payments )
            * priniciple ) + 0.005
    end-compute

This will give the payment required to amortize a loan. The "rounded"
and "+ 0.005" cause the last payment to be less than the periodic
payment. (US dollars assumed.)
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>

```
I can't make heads or tails of your notation, but if that is the formula
I think it is, try two COMPUTE statements:

COMPUTE F-EXP = (1 + I) ** N.     Either define F-EXP as a float or
provide many decimal places on both sides of the decimal point.

COMPUTE = A * (F-EXP - 1)   /
                          (I * F-EXP) .

Of course, you could substitute (1 + I) ** N for F-EXP in the second
compute statement, but why crunch this number twice?  You could reduce
the formula so F-EXP only appears once, but let's leave that to you as
an algebra exercise.

LEWISF01 wrote:

> Does anyone know how to write one compute statement for this?
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> email me at lewisf01@aol.com
```

```
COMPUTE = should have been COMPUTE P =.  However, that was the wrong
formula.  I had used P to mean 'present value' and A was the payment
amount.  Solve for A and it should fly.

Sorry 'bout that.  My apologies.
```

<a id="t-153dde3f2d"></a>
## Thread: Perform...Until question — 13 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Perform...Until question

- **From:** rwaltei1@nycap.rr.ocm (Rich)
- **Date:** 1999-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369e8eff.4900536@news.nycap.rr.com>`

```

When using a perform until, does it perform first, then check the
condition? like a Do..while construct. Or does it check first.

I looked in two books and found they contradicted themselves.

I know that there is a 'with test before' and a 'with test after' but
what is the default when these clauses arent used.

Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Perform...Until question

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77m6c7$k4k$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<369e8eff.4900536@news.nycap.rr.com>`

```
Using the default of 'test before' it's really a DO WHILE, similar to the DO
WHILE construct in PL/I. This means that it's tested at the top and may not
execute at all. However, using the 'test after' guarantees that it will be
executed at least once, which may or may not be desirable.

HTH....

Cheers,

WOB

Rich wrote in message <369e8eff.4900536@news.nycap.rr.com>...
>
>When using a perform until, does it perform first, then check the
>condition? like a Do..while construct. Or does it check first.


<Rest Snipped>
```

- **Message-ID:** `<77mbku$bap$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<369e8eff.4900536@news.nycap.rr.com>`

```

perform never gets done.
```

#### ↳ Re: Perform...Until question

- **From:** "Gary Roush" <support@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77ov1c$np2$1@remarQ.com>`
- **References:** `<369e8eff.4900536@news.nycap.rr.com>`

```
'test before' is the default in all cases. There may be some that do a run
through any way the first time, but that's a baddie.  In all cases, the
Until clause is what determines whether to execute the perform or not and
when it is to end.  The 'test after' always ensures the perform runs at
least once before it is checked.

Gary
```

#### ↳ Re: Perform...Until question

- **From:** "Rob Annandale" <rob_aNOSPAM@unipharm.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369f6655$0$26980@fountain.mindlink.net>`
- **References:** `<369e8eff.4900536@news.nycap.rr.com>`

```
You can stipulate when you want the perform to check the condition.
e.g.

perform test after varying xx from 1 by 1 until...

or

perform test before varying xx from 1 by 1 until...

AcuCobol-85.

Rob Annandale

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: Perform...Until question

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rzAn2.3275$hE2.20710465@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<369e8eff.4900536@news.nycap.rr.com>`

```
>When using a perform until, does it perform first, then check the
>condition? like a Do..while construct. Or does it check first.


When in doubt run a test. Books only tell syntax and what the author is
thinking about at the time. I always find it better to run a test, if time
allows.


       MOVE 'NOT FINISHED' TO WS-INDICATOR
       DISPLAY 'INDICATOR SAYS : ' WS-INDICATOR
       IF WS-INDICATOR = 'FINISHED    ' THEN
          DISPLAY 'LOOP SHOULD NOT BE EXECUTED.'
       END-IF
******* THIS LOOP WILL BE EXECUTED
       PERFORM UNTIL WS-INDICATOR = 'FINISHED    '
          ADD 1 TO WS-COUNTER
          DISPLAY 'LOOP COUNT ' WS-INDICATOR
          IF WS-COUNTER > 5 THEN
             MOVE 'FINISHED    ' TO WS-INDICATOR
          END-IF
       END-PERFORMOVE 'NOT FINISHED' TO WS-INDICATOR

       DISPLAY 'INDICATOR SAYS : ' WS-INDICATOR
       IF WS-INDICATOR = 'FINISHED    ' THEN
          DISPLAY 'LOOP SHOULD NOT BE EXECUTED.'
       END-IF
******* THIS LOOP WILL NOT BE EXECUTED
       PERFORM UNTIL WS-INDICATOR = 'FINISHED    '
          ADD 1 TO WS-COUNTER
          DISPLAY 'LOOP COUNT ' WS-INDICATOR
          IF WS-COUNTER > 5 THEN
             MOVE 'FINISHED    ' TO WS-INDICATOR
          END-IF
       END-PERFORMM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Perform...Until question

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36A4DCE1.10EA04@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<369e8eff.4900536@news.nycap.rr.com> <rzAn2.3275$hE2.20710465@storm.twcol.com>`

```
Jeff wrote:

> >When using a perform until, does it perform first, then check the
> >condition? like a Do..while construct. Or does it check first.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> allows. (8< Example)

Thus revealing what syntax and semantics the author of the particular compiler
is thinking about at the time. But when it is the compiler it is to be used,
such a test gives indeed useful information.

TTBSF









Trying To Be Smart Frog
```

#### ↳ Re: Perform...Until question

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36A4E1A6.A2A5D670@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<369e8eff.4900536@news.nycap.rr.com>`

```
Rich wrote:

> When using a perform until, does it perform first, then check the
> condition? like a Do..while construct. Or does it check first.

1) As others pointed out, it checks first, in COBOL.
2) This is to ensure compatibility with earlier COBOL standards (COBOL
'74, '68 etbackera). There was no option "AFTER/BEFORE", but an explicit
definition of being tested "before" in the LRM.
3) But "perform first, then check" is not always "like a Do..while
construct"! The exact behaviour of a "while construct" or "until
construct" is language dependent:
In PL/1: while tests before (terminating when false), until tests after
(terminating when true)
In COBOL: while does not exist, until test before or after depending on
these words (both terminating when true)
In C/C++: while tests before or after, depending on statement, i.e.
while-statement or do..while-statement (both terminating when false).
In Pascal it is like in PL/1. (N. Wirth called PL/1 the second best
constructed comp.lang)!

I think you meant a do-while-construct from C(C++), but as a former PL/1
and C++ programmer I know this to be notoriously misunderstood / mixed
up.

> I looked in two books and found they contradicted themselves.

Then one of them is at fault. Which were they, which one said "after"?

> I know that there is a 'with test before' and a 'with test after' but
> what is the default when these clauses arent used.

I like it as good habit to always specify BEFORE of AFTER in new programs
(don't touch old code when it works).

> Thanks.

You're welcome

The Frog
```

---

## Thread: How set window title in Win32 console app Need help — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How set window title in Win32 console app Need help

- **From:** "J���rgen Ibelgaufts" <ibelgaufts@gfc-net.de>
- **Date:** 1999-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369DEA44.751A9573@gfc-net.de>`

```
Hi,

I have the following problem. When I start a new Windows NT console window I can
do this with the windows command "start". This command allows me to set the title
of the window (for example type 

  start "ABC"

in your console window and a new window will open which has the title "ABC" on the
title bar and the button down there in the start menu bar.

But as soon I invoke a Cobol program (for example XYZ.EXE), the title will first
change to "ABC - XYZ) and then to "XYZ".

Does anybody have a workaround or a method to set the window title from cobol?

Thanks in advance

Juergen Ibelgaufts
```

#### ↳ Re: How set window title in Win32 console app Need help

- **From:** "David Sands" <davs@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77uusl$cb2@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<369DEA44.751A9573@gfc-net.de>`

```
Hi Jurgen,

You can do this using a standard Resource File and the Resource compiler.

The Resource file should be something like:-

#include "DISPLAY.H"
#define _MFRES_INC_
/* Micro Focus Resource File. */
/* Version 4.00.017 */
STRINGTABLE LOADONCALL DISCARDABLE
BEGIN
   100 "This is the Title"
END
#include <mfrcwin.h>
/*MF_BITMAP*/
/*MF_CURSOR*/
/*MF_DIALOG*/
/*MF_ICON*/
ID_DPS  ICON PRELOAD DISCARDABLE "dps.ico"
/*MF_MENU*/
/*MF_RCDATA_END*/

This sets the standard title text and also associates an ICON with the
application.

Hope this help.
David.
```

## Thread: PC Printer Controls for Cobol? — 4 msgs

### PC Printer Controls for Cobol?

- **From:** "BH" <bhaworth@avalon.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77m5om$a25$1@sword.avalon.net>`

```
Could use some help in coding commands for an Epson PC printer to print
compressed lines.
Trying to get as much school work done at home as possible.
I'm using Fujitsu's Ver 3 compiler.
Brad
```

#### ↳ Re: PC Printer Controls for Cobol?

- **From:** "_DEFAULT" <_DEFAULT@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77nvqr$313m$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<77m5om$a25$1@sword.avalon.net>`

```
You can try Jeff's suggestion using hex '0F'. Most Epson printers use this
code for condensed mode which should give you about 132 characters per line
on 8.5" paper.  The Fujitsu compiler may reset the printer and override your
controls, however.  I've also used the old DOS DEBUG program to set up
control codes and  set the printer using a TYPE  PRTRESET > PRN from DOS,
where PRTRESET is the name of the file I created using DEBUG.  A list of the
most frequently used printer commands might be found at www.epson.com. Good
luck.

Denny
```

#### ↳ Re: PC Printer Controls for Cobol?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369ec510.7469883@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<77m5om$a25$1@sword.avalon.net>`

```
On Thu, 14 Jan 1999 19:31:45 -0600, "BH" <bhaworth@avalon.net> wrote:

>Could use some help in coding commands for an Epson PC printer to print
>compressed lines.
>Trying to get as much school work done at home as possible.
>I'm using Fujitsu's Ver 3 compiler.
>Brad

You need to set the runtime option for the font size - you won't be
able to just send the compressed print code and get it to print
compressed.
```

#### ↳ Re: PC Printer Controls for Cobol?

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IOAn2.3276$hE2.20718825@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<77m5om$a25$1@sword.avalon.net>`

```
>Could use some help in coding commands for an Epson PC printer to print
>compressed lines.
>Trying to get as much school work done at home as possible.
>I'm using Fujitsu's Ver 3 compiler.


On my old Commodore 128/64 you sent hex codes to the printer to change
fonts/sizes/super/subscript type as well as wide/underline/bold.

You might try printing something from a filler with hexadecimal codes in it:

       01  PRINTER-CODE  PIC X(003) VALUE SPACES.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.

       0000-MAIN.
           MOVE '7F20' TO PRINTER-CODE
           WRITE PRINTER-RECORD FROM PRINTER-CODE
           STOP RUN
           .


This would send the character codes 127 and 32 to the printer record. The
same as :

Print #1,chr(127);chr(32);

in BASIC.

Of course you would have to have some kind of manual which tells you the
printer codes. Most of the early printer drivers put the printer in graphics
mode through Windows to do the fancy WYSIWYG stuff that the modern word
processors produce. But this would require generating a bitmap, converting
it into the priters bitmap format, then dumping to the printer. I wrote
something to do this once and it is very ugly.

Try inquiring with the manufacturer of the printer. They would be your best
bet on getting the hex codes for changing type faces and styles.
```

- **From:** "Mark Byrne" <yia08@dial.pipex.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77kfqj$5qa$1@taliesin.netcom.net.uk>`

```
Anyone help???

I've got a few files in .dbf format (I think they're produced by a Clipper
application) which I need to be able to use in COBOL - Fujitsu and Casegen.
Can anyone tell me how to convert them to ASCII Line sequential files
please?

Thanks...
```

#### ↳ Re: Data conversion anyone?

- **From:** "Rech Informatica Ltda" <rechinfo@nh.conex.com.br>
- **Date:** 1999-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<t9mn2.592$kl.13183@newsfeed.slurp.net>`
- **References:** `<77kfqj$5qa$1@taliesin.netcom.net.uk>`

```
Use the program DBU.EXE of Clipper.

Example:
DBU FILE1.DBF
Type Esc.
Type F6-Utility - Copy
File:             File1.txt
While:          .not. deleted ()
Scope:         SDF
Type Ok.
This should work.

Rovani Marcelo Rech
Rech Inform�tica Ltda.

Mark Byrne escreveu na mensagem <77kfqj$5qa$1@taliesin.netcom.net.uk>...
>Anyone help???
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Data conversion anyone?

- **From:** Scott Ramey <scottr@bdssoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369DFCB8.815DB40A@bdssoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<77kfqj$5qa$1@taliesin.netcom.net.uk>`

```
Mark Byrne wrote:
> 
> Anyone help???
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks...

If you have dBASE (most any version from dBASE-III on), or FoxPro (pretty much any version) you
should be able to create a SDF file with the copy command as in:

USE <file name>
COPY TO <output file> TYPE SDF

The output will be straight text, each field set to the width as defined in the dbf file.  Decimal
points are explicit for numeric fields.  If you can't get your hands on dBASE or FoxPro then use the
Clipper DBU utility.
```

### COBOL

```
>I am looking for Cobol By Microfocus version 4.000 any help ?

Micro Focus COBOL V4.0 includes the object oriented syntax. You can't
get a version 4.0 without it. You of course don't have to use that
syntax.

Try www.mfltd.co.uk although I don't know if MF still sell 4.0.
Support for it runs out in June 1999 btw.
```

```
Large manufacturing company located in Cleveland, OH seeks a senior level
mainframe programmer for contract work. The project includes planning,
controlling and analyzing user requirements and formatting those requirements
into work plans in connections with the Human Resource Information System.
The contract is expected to last 1-1 1/2 years. Required technical skills
include: COBOL, JCL, DB2, VSAM, IMS, MVS-JCL, MVS-TSO, Easytrieve, Panvalet
and Librarian. Excellent verbal and written communication is required along
with at least two years of U.S. experience. Sorry, no sponsorship is
provided.

Location: Cleveland, OH
Hourly rate: $50+
Length: 1-1.5 years

For more information please contact:

Candy Taylor
```

<a id="t-4fa2d36a88"></a>
## Thread: Compiler names — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Compiler names

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369E51E7.DC32ADD8@home.com>`

```
A few years back I was in a shop which went from Univac to DEC.  The
COBOL we used was changed along with everything else.  It wasn't until I
worked at an IBM shop again that I learned that the DEC COBOL was COBOL
II.  Knowing that may have been useful in job hunting.

Does anybody have a list of various COBOL versions used by various
manufacturers with ways to determine which versions were comparable to
which other versions?

(My current shop has COBOL 68, COBOL 72, & COBOL 370, but as far as I
can tell, no COBOL II (they only switched to be Y2K compatible, and
COBOL II is not Y2K compatible)).

Some shops use names such as COBOL 82/89 and COBOL 2000.  Others user or
did use their own names or implied names (Honeywell COBOL around 1974,
etc).  Others have different COBOLS for different sized machines
(Burroughs).
```

---

<a id="t-6c4d72febb"></a>
## Thread: Initializing 3-d Table? — 1 msgs

### Re: Initializing 3-d Table?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77lkrt$erj@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<369b90af.5665969@news> <369c6df8.14705415@news3.ibm.net> <369e4e89.6823352@news>`

```
IBM did some APAR fixes for INITIALIZE performance several years ago.  If
you are running on a current or currently maintained IBM mainframe COBOL,
you shouldn't see real serious problems with INITIALIZE performance.  If you
do, I think IBM would like to hear about this ASAP.  There are (I think)
some problems still in cases where you have an ODO (Occurs Depending ON) -
but I don't recall the details on this.

If you are still running an early version of VS COBOL II without much
maintenance applied, then YES, you will see serious performance hits with
INITIALIZE.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: credit card validation

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36A06299.303C15E2@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<369F9F4B.D91@erols.com> <77ojda$6ok$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
WOB@my-dejanews.com wrote:
> 
> In article <369F9F4B.D91@erols.com>,
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> future maybe?

That's definitely a risk.  I don't think you will find any employee of a
credit card association posting the algorithm in a usenet newsgroup.

A couple of years ago I saw a routine posted in BASIC in a DOS or
Windows newsgroup.  Looked like it would probably work.  

The requestor wanted to put the logic into a POS card swipe device he
was programming.  But the credit card associations will probably tell
you to transmit the card number and let the system tell you if it's a
bad card.

The check digit algorithm is a relatively poor security feature, and I
believe the original intent was to detect digit transposition for
keypunched cardnumbers.

If you really want to know how to do it, call up Visa, MasterCard,
Discover or Amex and ask them.  They might tell you if you give them a
good enough reason.  
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: credit card validation

- **From:** bwspoor@fridaycs.win-uk.net (Brian W Spoor)
- **Date:** 1999-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<47@fridaycs.win-uk.net>`
- **References:** `<369F9F4B.D91@erols.com> <77ojda$6ok$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36A06299.303C15E2@worldnet.att.net>`

```
 
In article <36A06299.303C15E2@worldnet.att.net>, Arnold Trembley (arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net) writes:
>WOB@my-dejanews.com wrote:
>> 
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Certainly in the UK the banks and credit card associations will not
release this information - when I spoke to my bank manager about
this, he came back and told me that the technical people would not
even tell him how it worked.

I required the information for an online order processing system
taking orders via the telephone. The idea was to do basic card
validation at the order entry stage to guard against the operator
transposing/missing digits.

The answer from the card company (visa) was to phone the number
through for authorisation - not practical when you have a caller on
the line wanting to place an order, even with an electronic card
terminal - it's needs a line, which in the UK are expensive
compared to the USA.

The system is designed for the orders to be stored, passed in a batch
to a supervisor to get the authorisations and then release the
orders for picking/dispatch. Bigger systems do this automatically,
but this was written for a small company, just starting up - hence
the simple 'batch' approach.

I did manage to obtain the information (not for all cards) I
needed from a colleague who knew my reasons for wanting it, and
it has trapped a few operator errors saving callbacks to customers,
although not does not guarantee a valid card or that the
transaction will be approved. 

For security reasons, I am not willing to pass this on to anybody,
unless I know them, trust them and regard the reason as being valid
- so please no email requests they will be ignored.

Brian


-----------------------------------------------------------
Brian W Spoor MBCS
Chartered Information Systems Practitioner
Friday Computer Services          Phone: +44-(0)1803 852625
bwspoor@fridaycs.co.uk            Fax:   +44-(0)1803 854926
-----------------------------------------------------------
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: credit card validation

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Karl Wagner" <kewagner@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ke1p2.269$my.1383@news.rdc1.on.wave.home.com>`
- **References:** `<369F9F4B.D91@erols.com> <77ojda$6ok$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36A06299.303C15E2@worldnet.att.net> <47@fridaycs.win-uk.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-rGZtbylpSQqd@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <780s70$sc8$1@news.igs.net>`

```

Donald Tees wrote in message <780s70$sc8$1@news.igs.net>...
>Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The Canadian social insurance number (SIN) is a 9 digit number where the
first 8 digits are random and the last digit is the checksum. The checksum
is derived using a MOD10 algorithm, as follows:

1) Add up digits 1,3,5 and 7 giving subtotal-1
2) **this is the tricky part** Add the sum of the digits of the result of
doubling digits 2,4,6 and 8 giving subtotal-2.
3) Add subtotal-1 and subtotal-2
4) Divide the total by 10.
5) Subtract the remainder by 10 giving the checksum.

For example:
  Given 450680772

1)  4 + 0 + 8 + 7 = 19 (subtotal-1)
2)  5 + 5 = 10 --> 1 + 0 = 1
     6 + 6 = 12 --> 1 + 2 = 3
     0 + 0 = 0
     7 + 7 = 14 --> 1 + 4 = 5
     1 + 3 + 0 + 5 = 9 (subtotal-2)

3)  19 + 9 = 28
4)  28 / 10 = 2 r 8.
5) 10 - 8 = 2 (checksum)

>
```

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>

```
Brooks Hagenow <bhagenow@tcei.com> wrote in article
<36A6B139.6A8C60C1@tcei.com>...
> [previous postings:]
> > > ...
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> couldn't be the check digit.

That's just my point.  I didn't doubt his math in the example, I simply
considered another example where step 4 _would_ give a remainder of 0, and
hence step 5 would give 10, which has, as you also noticed, 2 digits.

Karl Wagner replied in another posting that it was assigned to a variable
that was declared as PIC 9, so it would be truncated to 0.  I believe the
algorithm is supposed to give a checksum of 0 if step 4 gives 0. So it's
just a funny way of programming it.

Gunnar.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: credit card validation

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36A49811.96A59C82@home.com>`
- **References:** `<369F9F4B.D91@erols.com> <77ojda$6ok$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36A06299.303C15E2@worldnet.att.net> <47@fridaycs.win-uk.net>`

```
There are several reasons for allowing the credit card company to
perform the validation, but the best one is so that they can change the
algorithm without you having to re-write your code.

Look at the phone company for example.
In the Denver area, we now have to enter area code for all local calls. 
Let's say you wrote a program which sees the area code and adds a 1
prefix to call, or sees the area code - recognizes it as the calling
area code and drops it.

Or the PBX which won't let you dial long distance calls except (800) and
has to be reprogrammed to accept 888.

Things change.  Where they can change, try to narrow down the
responsibility for making the changes.

Another thing - I often see people doing more editing than is
necessary.  If a key has to be numeric, sure a COBOL numeric check is
more efficient than calc'ing to the database.  But as I said, things
change.  When business needs say that key can now be alphanumeric, do
you want to change the code?  And when 99% of the time people enter the
key numerically - where's the efficiency in checking it 100% of the
time?

Maybe you can check it after it fails - only if you can give the user a
better hint on what's wrong by doing so.
```

```
at all).  The algorithm will determine solely whether the number is formatted
properly so that it *could* be a valid number.

John Baker
```

```
30XX - MVS plateform). I need to create a unique key for each record of

langage; I don't have a C compiler in my environment. Thank's
```

#### ↳ Re: hash routine writing in cobol

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<369fd5a0.0@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<77o3ma$o78$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
akana@my-dejanews.com wrote in message <77o3ma$o78$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>Good Morning I'm looking for a hash routine writen in Cobol (Mainframe IBM
>30XX - MVS plateform). I need to create a unique key for each record of
>differents tables, by using a hash routine wich, using differents
information
>of each table, can generate a a unique value. Please let me know if someone
>can help me. PS. all the Hash routine that i've found in internet are in C
>langage; I don't have a C compiler in my environment. Thank's


Hashing and generating a unique key are not exactly the same operation.
To get a unique key, you first compute a hash value, then divide that by a
prime number somewhat larger than the maximum number of items you
are processing. Then you insert the item and its key in the table. If you
get a collision (you will!), you must calculate another hash key and prope
again. This is standard hashing theory, and I assume that you know all
that.

Now to the hash value calculation. This is surprisingly difficult. The main
problem is to convert the characters of a data item to numbers so you
can compute on them. Cobol does not really have the concept of characters
as bit patterns. There is also the question of character set used (e.g.
EBCDIC
or ASCII or Unicode or ...) and of general portability. Converting
characters
to (bit) numbers could be done by redefinition of binary numbers, by using
a function (if your compiler supports it), and other such (mostly
non-portable)
tricks.

The following code shows a PORTABLE way of computing a hash-value
that gives the same result with all compilers on all platforms. It can
easily
be generalized to use more than space, A-Z, and 0-9.

000100 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000200 PROGRAM-ID.    HASHPK.
000300
000400 AUTHOR.        LEIF SVALGAARD.
000500 DATE-WRITTEN.  99/01/15
000600     -REVISED:  99/01/15.
000700
000800 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000900
001000 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
001100 SOURCE-COMPUTER. PORTABLE.
001200 OBJECT-COMPUTER. PORTABLE.
001300
001400 DATA DIVISION.
001500
001600 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
001700
001800 01  THE-STRING.
001900     02  THE-STRING-CHAR         PIC X      OCCURS 80 TIMES.
002000
002100 01  THE-STRING-VALUES.
002200     02  THE-STRING-VALUE        PIC 99     OCCURS 80 TIMES.
002300
002400 01  THE-STRING-HANDLING.
002500     02  STRING-PTR              PIC S9(3)  COMP.
002600     02  STRING-1.
002700         03  STRING-1-CHAR                  OCCURS 80 TIMES.
002800             04  STRING-1-DIGIT  PIC 9.
002900     02  STRING-2.
003000         03  STRING-2-CHAR                  OCCURS 80 TIMES.
003100             04  STRING-2-DIGIT  PIC 9.
003200
003300     02  CALCULATED-KEY          PIC 9(18).
003400     02  CALCULATED-KEY-PARTS    REDEFINES  CALCULATED-KEY.
003500         03  CALCULATED-KEY-HIGH PIC 9(02).
003600         03  CALCULATED-KEY-LOW  PIC 9(16).
003700
003800 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
003900 BEGIN-OF-PROGRAM.
004000     MOVE SPACES TO THE-STRING
004100     ACCEPT THE-STRING
004200     MOVE THE-STRING TO STRING-1
004300     INSPECT STRING-1 REPLACING
004400       ALL " " BY "0" ALL "A" BY "0" ALL "B" BY "0" ALL "C" BY "0"
004500       ALL "D" BY "0" ALL "E" BY "0" ALL "F" BY "0" ALL "G" BY "0"
004600       ALL "H" BY "0" ALL "I" BY "0" ALL "J" BY "1" ALL "K" BY "1"
004700       ALL "L" BY "1" ALL "M" BY "1" ALL "N" BY "1" ALL "O" BY "1"
004800       ALL "P" BY "1" ALL "Q" BY "1" ALL "R" BY "1" ALL "S" BY "1"
004900       ALL "T" BY "2" ALL "U" BY "2" ALL "V" BY "2" ALL "W" BY "2"
005000       ALL "X" BY "2" ALL "Y" BY "2" ALL "Z" BY "2" ALL "0" BY "2"
005100       ALL "1" BY "2" ALL "2" BY "2" ALL "3" BY "3" ALL "4" BY "3"
005200       ALL "5" BY "3" ALL "6" BY "3" ALL "7" BY "3" ALL "8" BY "3"
005300       ALL "9" BY "3"
005400
005500     MOVE THE-STRING TO STRING-2
005600     INSPECT STRING-2 REPLACING
005700       ALL " " BY "0" ALL "A" BY "1" ALL "B" BY "2" ALL "C" BY "3"
005800       ALL "D" BY "4" ALL "E" BY "5" ALL "F" BY "6" ALL "G" BY "7"
005900       ALL "H" BY "8" ALL "I" BY "9" ALL "J" BY "0" ALL "K" BY "1"
006000       ALL "L" BY "2" ALL "M" BY "3" ALL "N" BY "4" ALL "O" BY "5"
006100       ALL "P" BY "6" ALL "Q" BY "7" ALL "R" BY "8" ALL "S" BY "9"
006200       ALL "T" BY "0" ALL "U" BY "1" ALL "V" BY "2" ALL "W" BY "3"
006300       ALL "X" BY "4" ALL "Y" BY "5" ALL "Z" BY "6" ALL "0" BY "7"
006400       ALL "1" BY "8" ALL "2" BY "9" ALL "3" BY "0" ALL "4" BY "1"
006500       ALL "5" BY "2" ALL "6" BY "3" ALL "7" BY "4" ALL "8" BY "5"
006600       ALL "9" BY "6"
006700
006800     PERFORM GET-STRING-VALUES
006900       VARYING STRING-PTR FROM 1 BY 1
007000         UNTIL STRING-PTR > 80
007100
007200     DISPLAY THE-STRING-VALUES
007300
007400     MOVE ZEROES TO CALCULATED-KEY
007500     PERFORM CALCULATE-THE-KEY
007600       VARYING STRING-PTR FROM 80 BY -1
007700         UNTIL STRING-PTR < 1
007800
007900     DISPLAY CALCULATED-KEY
008000     STOP RUN
008100     .
008200
008300
008400 GET-STRING-VALUES.
008500     IF STRING-1-CHAR (STRING-PTR) IS NUMERIC
008600         COMPUTE THE-STRING-VALUE (STRING-PTR) =
008700                   STRING-1-DIGIT (STRING-PTR) * 10
008800                 + STRING-2-DIGIT (STRING-PTR)
008900     ELSE
009000         COMPUTE THE-STRING-VALUE (STRING-PTR) = 37
009100     .
009200
009300
009400 CALCULATE-THE-KEY.
009500     COMPUTE CALCULATED-KEY = CALCULATED-KEY * 11
009600               + THE-STRING-VALUE (STRING-PTR)
009700
009800     IF CALCULATED-KEY-HIGH > ZEROES
009900         COMPUTE CALCULATED-KEY = CALCULATED-KEY / 101
010000     .
```

<a id="t-90fa4dea6d"></a>
## Thread: MF COBOL PIC 9 comparison problem. — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF COBOL PIC 9 comparison problem.

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpdi.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<916423848.26012.0.nnrp-10.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
Does anybody know of a MF setting that would alter the way that PIC 9 fields
would be processed?

I've always understood that comparing 2 PIC 9 fields would always do a
numeric comparison regardless of the length.  However I have had a bug
reported in MF Cobol that doesn't do this. E.g.  We have a structure which
in part looks something like this:

01  WS02-FIELDS.
       05  WS02-OCCURS OCCURS 350.
             10  WS02-DECIMALS        PIC 9(01).
             10  WS02-FLD-SIZE           PIC 9(02).


With code  like:

IF WS02-FLD-SIZE (SUB1) < WS02-DECIMALS (SUB1)
        PERFORM FLD-SIZE-ERROR.

We get an error condition whenever the first byte of the PIC 9(02) field is
less than the PIC 9(1) field so 14 is considered less than 2, i.e. it seems
to be doing a character compare not a numeric one.

Can anybody explain this?  Is there a known rule in COBOL or Micro Focus
COBOL

The identical code works fine in AS400 and WANG COBOL.

In the meantime I'm arranging additional tests.

BTW we have been using AS400 COBOL for c 9 years, WANG COBOL for c 18 years
and MF COBOL for c 5 years but we have recently changed our MF version from
3.0 to 4.0.  Also the MF Cobol is running on hp unix.

TIA

Rick
```

#### ↳ Re: MF COBOL PIC 9 comparison problem.

- **From:** "Gary Roush" <support@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77ofqu$4au$1@remarQ.com>`
- **References:** `<916423848.26012.0.nnrp-10.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
Rick,

I think it works on the mainframe because the processing of data is
different where data on the PC is use differently so you have to do things a
little differently and more specific.

You may have to redefine so that you are comparing one-byte or two byte
fields instead of comparing 2 bytes to a 1 byte field, especially when it is
numeric.

If you want a sample, e-mail me and I'll give you one.

Regards,
Gary

<916423848.26012.0.nnrp-10.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>...

fields
>would be processed?
>
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: MF COBOL PIC 9 comparison problem.

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-01-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36a00f90.92090332@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<916423848.26012.0.nnrp-10.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
On Fri, 15 Jan 1999 18:01:18 -0000, "Rick Price"
<rick@hpdi.demon.co.uk> wrote:

>Does anybody know of a MF setting that would alter the way that PIC 9 fields
>would be processed?
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Can you post the ACTUAL if?  and the Fields?  What I suspect - an old
(pre 3.2.5) version of MF COBOL - compiled as a GNT where the table
crosses a segment boundary.  Move the table to the top of working
storage and see what happens.  

Second possibility - Field being treated as reference modified -
that's why I wanted to see the actual compare.


>We get an error condition whenever the first byte of the PIC 9(02) field is
>less than the PIC 9(1) field so 14 is considered less than 2, i.e. it seems
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MF COBOL PIC 9 comparison problem.

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpdi.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<916765871.5512.0.nnrp-07.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<916423848.26012.0.nnrp-10.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk> <36a00f90.92090332@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane

Thanks for your 2 replies.  Apologies.  I've probably wasted your time.  I
got this thrown at me Friday night after being out of the office for a
couple of days and I didn't have the full facts from the developer.  In fact
the compare always failed when the 2 digit value was < 10 i.e. when it
started with zero.  E.g. it would  say 09 was less than 2 but would always
know that a 2 digit number 10 or above was higher than a 1 digit number
(which of cource it always is so it doen't really mean that the compile was
working in this case).  However if I'd known this I would have suspected a
compile bug.

In fact you are right  about the GNT.  When we tested it using the INT then
everything was hunky dory.  However the compiler version is MF COBOL for
unix 4.0 not pre 3.2.5.  We did move the table to the top of working storage
but that didn't correct things.

We wrote simple programs to try to isolate the problem but they always
worked.  In fact we tried so many things that the programmer  finally
swapped the IF statement
from IF 2digits < 1digit to if 1digit > 2digits.  This worked in both the
INT and GNT versions and recognised 9 as > 08 and 8 < 09.

All very worrying.  We will plod on.

Thanks again for your help

Rick

from

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF COBOL PIC 9 comparison problem.

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-01-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36a53b09.78499096@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<916423848.26012.0.nnrp-10.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk> <36a00f90.92090332@news1.ibm.net> <916765871.5512.0.nnrp-07.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
On Tue, 19 Jan 1999 17:14:01 -0000, "Rick Price"
<rick@hpdi.demon.co.uk> wrote:


>In fact you are right  about the GNT.  When we tested it using the INT then
>everything was hunky dory.  However the compiler version is MF COBOL for
>unix 4.0 not pre 3.2.5.  We did move the table to the top of working storage
>but that didn't correct things.
>

I've seen this behavior more than once between INT and GNT's.  (MF
swears the end results are the same though).  Sometimes it's the other
way around - the INT fails and the GNT works <G>.  The software where
I used to work was a mishmash of INT's and GNTs.  We were continually
fighting problems where we had a problem in the GNT so decided to make
the program an INT from them on.  Well, if you issue a CALL "PROGRAM"
and you don't specify INT or GNT - it loads the GNT first.  So
leftover GNT's would kill us sometimes.  What a mess.
```

<a id="t-49ecd3de53"></a>
## Thread: [o]: JCL question — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### [o]: JCL question

- **From:** "kennet" <kennet@post11.tele.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77oa0h$5qeq$1@news-inn.inet.tele.dk>`

```
I'm trying to write a procedure in JCL but I am having problems with the
parameters. Here's a simple example:

// write     proc line=''
// step1   exec db2ucaf,pgmname=writeit,param=&line
// etc.

Here the called procedure "db2ucaf" is our standard JCL for running load
modules, and writeit is just a PL/1 program that prints the content in
param. So the procedure can simply print a line of text.

Now, if I what to write "Hello" on the screen, I can just do this:

//  exec   write,line=hello

And it works fine. However, if I need to write "Hello, world", I'm having
problem with the comma. So I tried

//  exec   write,line='hello, world'

But this doesn't work. However triple-quotes works:

//  exec   write,line='''hello, world'''

But that's not the way I wanna do it! I also tried recoding the procedure as

// write     proc line=''
// step1   exec db2ucaf,pgmname=writeit,param='&line'
// etc.

but then &line is not recognised as a symbolic parameter, and the program
simply prints the string '&line'.
Is there a way to pass parameters with spaces and commas without having to
use triple-quotes?

Regards,
Kennet
```

#### ↳ Re: [o]: JCL question

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1999-01-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990116142026.25453.00001673@ng133.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<77oa0h$5qeq$1@news-inn.inet.tele.dk>`

```
I have another way of looking at this, as opposed to a proposed direct
solution.

Your problem is that your style involves a PROC invoking another PROC.  It
would not be correct to say that that is a no-no, but it is discouraged. 
The biggest reason does not relate directly to your current considerations with
resolving symbolics, but relates to the difficulty of specifying restarts.  A
step which has a stepname invokes a proc, the proc has a stepname, and if it
invokes a proc you have a proc-proc stepname to contend with. Restart syntax is
not flexible, it allows only for stepname and procstepname.  (and only the
stepname within the last proc can be referenced as the procstepname).

Thus specifying parm overrides or dd statement overrides is confusing, tough
and sometimes impossible when a proc invokes a proc. This problem is usually
faced when you can least afford it, an emmergency abend system down, in the wee
hours of the morning when there are few application programmers and few system
programmers available.

But really procs within a proc are just _almost_ a no-no. Because there are
shops that do it anyway, and some amelioration can be had by making the outer
proc single-stepped (a severe limitation). For example your proc as posted has
only one step in the outer proc, which may work for you, but provides very
limitted future flexibility.

JCL was invented long before recursive functional languages became all the
craze.  JCL is obtusedly flatlanded in cousciousness.
Getting parms from the top level of a job stream into a proc is straight
forward, passing them deeper is infact not what the language was intended for.
Yes folks do it to many layers deep, it's just that that is the wrong path with
this tool.

Some thoughts relating to simplifying this (that is flattening it).  

One approach is to proliferate the bottom level proc into each of its versions
(as opposed to dynamically generating it at proc execution time). Your initial
approach is more artistic, but in situations in which there are not realy too
many different basic low level procs, proliferating them as mostly hard coded
procs is a solution to the limitations of JCL.

If you have the right JES you can SET symbolics that could be used in a PROC
name in the high level job JCL to pick the correct proc for execution.

For example if you had a DB2UW PROC that executed your WRITEIT program, you
could code this:

// SET SPECXYZ=W 
//WRITE1 EXEC DB2U&SPECXYZ

this will execute proc DB2UW and that proc would have 

//WHATEVER EXEC WRITEIT

This should ease the specification of the text parameters.


Another way to look at this is that as you veer away from simplistic parm
values (only the complex ones engage the quoting issue), you are nolonger
dealing with parms but instead data.

In the situation you describe, many shops will not try to get the text in via
parms. Utilities like WRTIEIT are coded to access data files for headers, tags
or elaborate control parms. A prestep is needed to jam the text onto this
requisite file. This is controversial because everyone just wants a simple way
to do things and two steps is not desirable when one step ought to be enough.
But JCL does have limitations which bring these alternatives into view. 

There is a basic fact in your post. You know the text value up at the jobstream
level, and are having trouble getting it all the way down to the specific EXEC
that invokes the presenter WRITEIT.  What some shops will do is have you put
that text on a data card in the _job_ JCL lib, which is just JCL and can
therefore tollerate DD * input and /* input termination. (procs do not allow
that of course, but JCL does).

Atleast as far as your example is concerned, these text items are job specific,
not PROC specific. This distinction can be used powerfully. Job streams can
have SYSIN DD *, even if submitted from a PDS with a name like *.PROC or
*.PROCLIB.  It is the manner of invocation that makes the difference. A
corollary is that a proc invoked with EXEC PROCNAME or EXECUTE PROC=PROCNAME
cannot have DD* input even if brought in, via JCLLIB control from
GIZMO.NOTPROC.YOURNODE.
It is the manner of invocation that determines it.

Yet another possibility, is to fold all this back together, and, keeping your
PROCs one layer deep, simply insert a //utilddnm DD * to the correct step for
WRITEIT or whichever, and bring the text directly into the program.

Alternatively, if your text is not job specific, but application specific, then
place the text into parm file PDSes, and bring it in explicitly with a DD in
the proc just like the DD * approach. Obviously you could resolve the member
name, and all or parts of the PDS DSN with symbolics.

In short, another way to look at this is that the complex text has provided you
with a symptom of a problem.  Either you are trying to go too deep in your proc
layers, as JCL infact was not designed for this (it was designed for a flat
world, and has been extended strangely). Or your text interface, via parms, was
conceived for simple matter, and you are finding you want more; which will flow
better through data files or PDSes, than through parms.

Hope this helps even though it is not directly responsive.




"kennet" <kennet@post11.tele.dk>
On Fri, 15 Jan 1999 21:53:48 +0100
posted

<<
I'm trying to write a procedure in JCL but I am having problems with the
parameters. Here's a simple example:

// write     proc line=''
// step1   exec db2ucaf,pgmname=writeit,param=&line
// etc.

Here the called procedure "db2ucaf" is our standard JCL for running load
modules, and writeit is just a PL/1 program that prints the content in
param. So the procedure can simply print a line of text.

Now, if I what to write "Hello" on the screen, I can just do this:

//  exec   write,line=hello

And it works fine. However, if I need to write "Hello, world", I'm having
problem with the comma. So I tried

//  exec   write,line='hello, world'

But this doesn't work. However triple-quotes works:

//  exec   write,line='''hello, world'''

But that's not the way I wanna do it! I also tried recoding the procedure as

// write     proc line=''
// step1   exec db2ucaf,pgmname=writeit,param='&line'
// etc.

but then &line is not recognised as a symbolic parameter, and the program
simply prints the string '&line'.
Is there a way to pass parameters with spaces and commas without having to
use triple-quotes?

>>


Best Wishes,


Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: [o]: JCL question

- **From:** "kennet" <kennet@post11.tele.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77qr4i$3o14$1@news-inn.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<77oa0h$5qeq$1@news-inn.inet.tele.dk> <19990116142026.25453.00001673@ng133.aol.com>`

```

RKRayhawk skrev i meddelelsen
<19990116142026.25453.00001673@ng133.aol.com>...
>[...a lot of good stuf...]
>Hope this helps even though it is not directly responsive.
>


Thank you very much for your answer. Indeed, it does help to
understand some of the background for JCL.
I've been a PC programmer so far, and have little knowledge
of the mainframe world, esp. the limitations. It does seem,
that I'm trying to do something with JCL that it is not really intended
for. I'll consider using another approach.

Thank you.
Kennet
```

#### ↳ Re: JCL question

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8oUo2.5793$hE2.23593709@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<77oa0h$5qeq$1@news-inn.inet.tele.dk>`

```
>Here the called procedure "db2ucaf" is our standard JCL for running load
>modules, and writeit is just a PL/1 program that prints the content in
>param. So the procedure can simply print a line of text.

Sounds more like a PROC used to execute DB2 programs. I don't usually use
these unless there is actually DB2 in the program you are trying to execute.
If there is no DB2, CICS, or IMS in the program you can run it by:

//STEP001  EXEC PGM=WRITEIT
//STEPLIB  DD DSN=TSO.PROD.LOADLIB,DISP=SHR
//*above dd contains load module WRITEIT
//SYSIN    DD *
Hello, world
/*
//SYSOUT   DD SYSOUT=*
//*sometimes you need the following in case of an abend
//SYSUDUMP DD SYSOUT=*
//SYSABEND DD SYSOUT=*

or

//STEP001  EXEC PGM=WRITEIT
//STEPLIB  DD DSN=TSO.PROD.LOADLIB,DISP=SHR
//*above dd contains load module WRITEIT
//*the following dsn contains your string "Hello, world"
//SYSIN    DD DSN=TSO.PROD.PARMLIB(LINE),DISP=SHR
//SYSOUT   DD SYSOUT=*
//*sometimes you need the following in case of an abend
//SYSUDUMP DD SYSOUT=*
//SYSABEND DD SYSOUT=*



>
>Now, if I what to write "Hello" on the screen, I can just do this:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>problem with the comma. So I tried

The problem would be the comma tells jcl you are passing another parameter.
Thus if in your program you were expecting 2 vars X and Y then your parm
below would pass X='hello' Y='world'.

>
>//  exec   write,line='hello, world'
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>//  exec   write,line='''hello, world'''

The triple quotes tells JCL you physically want to pass a "'" as part of the
parameter and use the first "'" as a delimeter.


>// write     proc line=''
>// step1   exec db2ucaf,pgmname=writeit,param='&line'
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>use triple-quotes?

If you have to do this within a proc try using a file containing the string
you wish to pass down, typically stored in a PARM lib or a CONTROL lib:
// write     proc line=''
// step1   exec db2ucaf,pgmname=writeit,param='&line'
// sysin   dd dsn=tso.prod.parmlib(line),disp=shr

where the dsn tso.prod.parmlib(line) would contain any characters you need
to pass to your COBOL progrm. Then inside the program you can ACCEPT each
line in the SYSIN DD, in this case:
tso.prod.parmlib(line)
which would contain something like:
"JCL DOES NOT LIKE A ' CONTAINED ON A LINE OR A , CONFUSES IT."


If you assign the parm card to something else besides SYSIN you can OPEN it
and READ it like any other file.
```

```
I have just received a phone call from a colleague in TWA telling me that last
night Rex Widmer was killed in an auto accident.

I did not know Rex well, but we had talked and shared technical titbits. And he
has helped countless people on this, and other, usenet newsgroups. He will be
missed, I know, by many people in this business.

Don't know what else to say. Just wanted you all to know.

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

```
I worked with Rex on and off for 8 years.  He was always a great source of
information and learning.  Many hours were spent listening to his insights and
overall knowledge of how and why things worked the way they did.  Upon leaving
my former position I stated how grateful I was to have had an opportunity to
learn from the very best.  Rex, I appreciate all you did and I will miss your
vast knowledge.

The following is information published in the Kansas City Star:

REX C. WIDMER Rex C. Widmer, 49, Shawnee, KS, passed away January 14, 1999,
north of Freeburg, MO, as the result of injuries sustained in an automobile
accident. Funeral services will be 11 a.m. Tuesday, January 19, at Spengel-
Boulanger Funeral Home, Highland, IL (888) 654-7234; burial in St. Joseph
Catholic Cemetery, Highland. Friends may call 4-8 p.m. Monday at the funeral
home. Memorial contributions may be made to the University of Missouri-Rolla,
and may be sent in care of the funeral home.

Highland IL. is approximately an hour outside of St. Louis.

…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: Sad news about Rex Widmer

- **From:** tim.oxler@NOSPAMteo-computer.com (Tim Oxler)
- **Date:** 1999-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36a49c28.3222537@news.teo-computer.com>`
- **References:** `<19990115095418.03250.00001230@ng123.aol.com>`

```

This was in the St. Louis Post-Dispatch yesterday (1-18-99)

http://www.stlnet.com/postnet/news/pdtoday.nsf/caa8f7cdae14badf862566a0005ca738/c9118d1829589b01862566fd00267084?OpenDocument

Rex C. Widmer 

Monday, January 18, 1999

A Metro East native who wrote software that identifies computer
problems associated with the year 2000 bug was killed Thursday night
(Jan. 14, 1999)   when he drove his car off a rural highway and struck
a tree near Jefferson City, authorities said.

Rex C. Widmer, a native of Highland, died after visiting a friend's
son at the University of Missouri at Rolla, where Mr. Widmer earned a
bachelor of science degree in computer science in 1972. Police said
the cause of the crash on Missouri Highway 63, almost a mile north of
Highway E, is unknown.

Mr. Widmer, 49, of Shawnee, Kan., had worked with computers at TWA for
25 years. He then traveled the world as a computer expert as demand
for his programming skills increased.

Six years ago, Mr. Widmer wrote a program called Portfolio Analyzer,
which allows a personal computer to scan the language in an IBM
mainframe. His friends said there was little demand for the program
until firms began buying it to update their mainframes to recognize
dates starting Jan. 1, 2000.

Mr. Widmer's company, Edge Information Group of Des Plaines, Ill.,
ended up selling more than $2 million worth of the software.

Mr. Widmer pledged 1 percent of the software's gross revenue to
Missouri-Rolla.

"He used to say we're in the rabbit-pulling business," longtime
business partner Carl Gehr said. "People would have a problem, and
we'd go in and pull a rabbit out of his hat."

Among the survivors are his parents, Cletus M. and Jane L. Widmer of
Highland; a sister, Kim M. Widmer of Columbia, Ill.; a brother, Rad M.
Widmer of St. Louis; and his former wife, Donna L., of Kansas City.

Visitation will be from 4 to 8 p.m. today and 8 to 11 a.m. Tuesday at
Spengel-Boulanger Funeral Home, Highland, with the funeral service
immediately following. Burial will be at St. Joseph Catholic Cemetery,
Highland.




Tim Oxler
TEO Computer Technologies Inc.
http://www.teo-computer.com
Take the Y2k Quiz
http://www.teo-computer.com/dev/quiz.html
```

```
-->> Plutonius

-->Bill Klein

If that is the case, here's one:
(Unattributable)

How chickens cross a road..

 Fort� Chicken:
    Will cross the road in August. No, September. October for sure.

 OS/2 Chicken:
    It crossed the road in style years ago, but it was so quiet
    that nobody noticed.

 Win 95 Chicken:
    You see different colored feathers while it crosses, but cook
    it and it still tastes like ... chicken.

 Win NT Chicken:
    In the interests of maintaining the high level of security and due the 
    business nature of the platform, there is no chicken, there is no road.
   (Recommend consulting 3rd party vendors.)  See MS KB article Q/1/2/3/4/56/7
    regarding use of 16 bit chickens and river crossings.  You must have admini-
    strator priviledges if you are a chicken.

 Microsoft Chicken (TM):
    It's already on both sides of the road.  And it just bought the
    road.

 Linux Chicken:
    Read Chicken_Road_Crossing Mini-HOWTO.  (Available at Sunsite)
    This version may not be implemented in Kernels prior to 4.01.365.111.98x,
    use at your own risk.  (Drivers for Chicken .9 are under development)

 OOP Chicken:
    It doesn't need to cross the road, it just sends a message.

 Assembler Chicken:
    First it builds the road ...

 C Chicken:
    It crosses the road without looking both ways.

 C++ Chicken:
    The chicken wouldn't have to cross the road, you'd simply refer
    to him on the other side.

 VB Chicken:
    USHighways!TheRoad.cross (aChicken)

 Delphi Chicken:
    The chicken is dragged across the road and dropped on the other
    side.

 Java Chicken:
    If your road needs to be crossed by a chicken, the server will
    download one to the other side. (Of course, those are chicklets)

 Web Chicken:
    Jumps out onto the road, turns right, and just keeps on running.

 Gopher Chicken:
    Tried to run, but got flattened by the Web chicken.

 Telnet Chicken:
    Please enter Chicken Name:
    Please enter Road Name:
    Is this an ANSI Chicken?   (Y)

 Newton Chicken:
    Can't cluck, can't fly, and can't lay eggs, but you can carry it
    across the road in your pocket!

 Tucows Chicken:
    Plenty of chickens cross roads daily!  Read what other users say.

 FTP Search v7.2, Trondheim, Norway Search Results: Chicken:
    Chicken:  no chickens found
    Road:  14,277 entries found
    Crossing:  no hits, try boolean?

 Cray Chicken:
    Crosses faster than any other chicken, but if you don't dip it
    in liquid nitrogen first, it arrives on the other side fully
    cooked.

 Quantum Logic Chicken:
    The chicken is distributed probabalistically on all sides of the
    road until you observe it on the side of your course.

 Lotus Chicken:
    Don't you *dare* try to cross the road the same way we do!

 Mac Chicken:
    No reasonable chicken owner would want a chicken to cross the
    road, so there's no way to tell it to.

 Al Gore Chicken:
    Waiting for completion of NCI (National Chicken-crossing
    Infrastructure) and will cross as soon as it's finished, assuming
    he's re-elected and the Republicans don't gut the program.

 COBOL Chicken:
    0001-CHICKEN-CROSSING.
       IF NO-MORE-VEHICLES THEN
          PERFORM 0010-CROSS-THE-ROAD
          VARYING STEPS FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL
             ON-THE-OTHER-SIDE
             MOVE ROAD-CROSSED TO CHICKEN
       ELSE
          GO TO 0001-CHICKEN-CROSSING
       END-IF.
    0002-CHICKEN-COOKING.
       IF CHICKEN = ROAD-CROSSED
          PERFORM 0020-WRING-NECK UNTIL SW01-DEAD= 'Y'
          PERFORM  0030-PLUCK-FEATHERS THROUGH
                                 0090-PUT IN POT  
        END-IF.
```

## Thread: RPG vs. COBOL — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`AS/400, iSeries, RPG`](topics.md#as400)_

### RPG vs. COBOL

- **From:** "Jon" <jon_w_graham@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77o8rl$gdv$1@news1-alterdial.uu.net>`

```
I'm looking for a little advice.  I earned a Bachelors Degree in Computer
Information Systems from DeVry in 1995.  Since then I have had no computer
programming jobs.  I've worked at the same company for 6 years working in
their Tech Support Department and now the Quality Assurance Dept.  I have
the opportunity to take a position in the same company working with an
AS/400 using RPG and CL (control language?).

I'm wondering if anyone has thoughts towards whether this is a good move.

Can this give me helpful experience towards someday becoming a COBOL
programmer?

If you were an employer looking for a COBOL programmer with experience,
would RPG experience be suitable?

Thanks in advance.

Jon
```

#### ↳ Re: RPG vs. COBOL

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-01-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36a01104.92462785@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<77o8rl$gdv$1@news1-alterdial.uu.net>`

```
On Fri, 15 Jan 1999 14:35:55 -0600, "Jon" <jon_w_graham@hotmail.com>
wrote:

>I'm looking for a little advice.  I earned a Bachelors Degree in Computer
>Information Systems from DeVry in 1995.  Since then I have had no computer
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Take it.  RPG/400 is not your fathers RPG.

>I'm wondering if anyone has thoughts towards whether this is a good move.
>
>Can this give me helpful experience towards someday becoming a COBOL
>programmer?

See if the machine HAS COBOL on it - many do, and sometimes it's not
used cause there's not a COBOL programmer there.  After getting the
job show what you can do in RPG and then show what you can do in COBOL
- this is your chance to get your foot in the door - take it.

>
>If you were an employer looking for a COBOL programmer with experience,
>would RPG experience be suitable?
>

Real world business programming experience is ALWAYS worth something.
```

```
!@#$%^&&**(

Another foot of snow!  I just walked across the street to
the store.  It took 10 minutes.  My car is buried so deep
I cannot see it and I have a 1 o'clock appointment. It is
ten below zero.  Shit shit shit.

Recruiters:  anywhere warm! any rate! as soon as possible!
Coders:  Five grand for the first Cobol program that can
shovel snow.
```
