The more they see the better.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Asking for COBOL code with control breaks (big)

- **From:** "Alan Russell" <RUSSELAH@apci.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78205p$5if@netnews1.apci.com>`
- **References:** `<19990117164746.18393.00001853@ng-cd1.aol.com> <T_No2.2928$gd3.1980825@news1.mia>`

```
While I don't normally disagree with Judson, I'd like to add my .02 here.
The following is an extract from my doctoral dissertation
(http://members.aol.com/AHRussell/dissert.htm) dealing with the subject of
"control breaks".


RULE 11 - Use the Balanced Line algorithm for file "matching" instead of the
key matching algorithm.

This same historical context of comparing keys is the basis for the common
method of performing "control breaks". This is generally done with an IF
statement at the beginning of the main processing paragraph. If there are
multiple level of breaks, the rule is to test for a "break" in the major
control field first, the intermediate control field next, and the minor
control field last, of course remembering to include execution of the minor
break and the intermediate break in the major break logic, and to include
the minor break logic in the intermediate break logic.

Again, if we look at the underlying structure of the data being processed, a
better way to accomplish this is apparent. A file is not, for example,
simply composed of records for different divisions, stores, and departments.
Rather, the file is an ordered list of divisions, each division is an
ordered list of stores, each store is an ordered list of departments, and
each department has multiple detail records within it. The file can be
depicted logically as follows (the "[...]" is a repetition indication):

Company [Division [Store [Department [Details]]]]

The corresponding report might have the following logical structure:

Company [Division [Store [Department [Details] Department total] Store
total] Division total] Company total

We can align these structures as follows:

    Co [Div [St [Dept [Det]         ]         ]          ]
    Co [Div [St [Dept [Det]  total]  total]  total]  total

From this structure, two things are apparent - (1) there is no structure
clash between the two structures, and (2) rather than a "break", groupings
are composed both of things which come before the details (e.g. printing the
store name) and things which come after the details (e.g. printing store
totals). Pseudo-COBOL code to handle this situation is below.

             .
             .
             PERFORM COMPANY-INITIALIZATION.
             PERFORM PROCESS-DIVISION
                 UNTIL NO-MORE-DATA.
             PERFORM PRINT-COMPANY-TOTALS.
             .
             .
             .
         PROCESS-DIVISION.
             PERFORM DIVISION-INITIALIZATION.
             MOVE IN-DIVISION TO CURRENT-DIVISION.
             PERFORM PROCESS-STORE
                 UNTIL NO-MORE-DATA
                    OR IN-DIVISION NOT = CURRENT-DIVISION.
             PERFORM PRINT-DIVISION-TOTALS.
             .
             .
             .
         PROCESS-STORE.
             PERFORM STORE-INITIALIZATION.
             MOVE IN-STORE TO CURRENT-STORE.
             PERFORM PROCESS-DEPARTMENT
                 UNTIL NO-MORE-DATA
                    OR IN-DIVISION NOT = CURRENT-DIVISION
                    OR IN-STORE NOT = CURRENT-STORE.
             PERFORM PRINT-STORE-TOTALS.
             .
             .
             .
         PROCESS-DEPARTMENT.
             PERFORM DEPARTMENT-INITIALIZATION.
             MOVE IN-DEPT TO CURRENT-DEPT.
             PERFORM PROCESS-DETAIL
                 UNTIL NO-MORE-DATA
                    OR IN-DIVISION NOT = CURRENT-DIVISION
                    OR IN-STORE NOT = CURRENT-STORE
                    OR IN-DEPT NOT = CURRENT-DEPT.
             PERFORM PRINT-DEPARTMENT-TOTALS.
             .
             .
             .
         PROCESS-DETAIL.
             .
             .
             PERFORM READ-INPUT-FILE.

Several advantages come from this revised structure. (1) Performing things
at the beginning of a "group" (e.g. skipping to a new page) is just as
simple as performing things at the end of a "group" (e.g. printing group
totals). In fact, things like zeroing out totals is a natural thing to do in
the group initialization routine, rather than remembering to do them in the
global program initialization routine as well as after each total has been
printed out. (2) "Rolling totals" upward is a fairly easy thing to do. The
"last time" break at the end of the file no longer requires additional code.
(3) The complexity introduced by testing for control breaks with IF
statements has been eliminated. It is interesting to note that the
"mainline" logic that accumulates grand totals is almost always coded using
this logic structure, even when the sub-totals are done using a different
structure.


RULE 12 - Use the PERFORM UNTIL structure instead of the historical control
break structure.


The previous example also illustrates another common coding structure - the
so-called "triform program".4 This is a simple extrapolation of the fact
that most programs

process repeating groups of data. Therefore, it is almost obvious that the
matching program logic to process this type of data is:


(1) Do whatever needs to be done at the beginning of a group.

(2) Process each element of the group in a loop structure.

(3) Do whatever needs to be done at the end of a group.


Therefore, we can expect most programs to have a main logic paragraph that
looks like:



             PERFORM xxxxx-INITIALIZATION.
             PERFORM xxxxx-PROCESS
                 UNTIL terminating-condition.
             PERFORM xxxxx-TERMINATION.

In fact, the multi-level control program illustrated earlier is just several
of these structures nested within one another. There are however, two points
of contention about this structure. (1) If the xxxxx-INITIALIZATION or
xxxxx-TERMINATION procedures are sufficiently small, it is sometimes
suggested that they be coded "in-line", that is to simply code them in place
of the PERFORM noted. (2) The words INITIALIZATION and TERMINATION may be
objectionable to some. However, apart from these two minor points, if most
of our programs were to be structured this way, then they could be more
easily understood by others. The author does not object to either of these
points.

Alan Russell, PhD, CCP
Work - Russelah@apci.com
Home - AHRussell@computer.org
Home page - http://members.aol.com/ahrussell
-------------------------------------------------------
The views expressed are mine alone and do not necessarily reflect those of
my employer

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Asking for COBOL code with control breaks (big)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Alan Russell" <RUSSELAH@apci.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<782fts$bmr@netnews1.apci.com>`
- **References:** `<78205p$5if@netnews1.apci.com> <19990119102235.12145.00003175@ng140.aol.com>`

```

S Comstock wrote in message <19990119102235.12145.00003175@ng140.aol.com>...
>Alan,
>
>Thanks for the insight. I, too, teach control break logic in my COBOL
course,
>generally using the traditional approach.
>
>Your notes have given me a new way to look at the logic. I'll spend some
time
>mulling it over, of course, and I may not adopt it. But it looks
interesting
>and it's always good to consider new ways of doing things.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>USA

An interesting sidelight to using the type of control "break" and file
"matching" logic is that it eliminates many of the IF statements used to
make the traditional methods work.  By eliminating IF statements and
"switches", the logic is simpler, easier to understand and debug, and easier
to test.  If you told me to write a program which had as input an inventory
master file and two transaction files (one with adds/changes/deletes and
another one with issues/receipts) and two output reports (in addition to the
new master), one report of the transactions and one with a new master
contents (3 control totals), with "traditional" logic that would be a VERY
complex program to get right.  But by using the "balanced line algorithm"
and the revised control logic, I can predict ahead of time how many IF
statements it will take.  The number is (a) two simple IF's for coordinating
the files [n-1], (b) one for the master report [linecount/pagebreak], (c)
one for each type of error condition [and any "not found on master" or
"duplicate add" errors are just one more type of error condition.  That's
all.  There would be NONE in the main logic stream.

To write a program which "matches" say 5 files would normally take 32 IF
statements (2**5) each of which would have 4 AND/OR components [total of
over 100 lines of code just to do the matching].  Using the "balance line"
or coordinated key method it will take 4 simple IF statements and no AND/OR
components.  Tell me which one you'd rather write?  I have successfully
written a 7-file match program which ran the first time.  As good as I am
with COBOL, to use the key "match" logic I might still be working on it
today and still not be sure if it was working correctly.

Alan Russell, PhD, CCP
Work - Russelah@apci.com
Home - AHRussell@computer.org
Home page - http://members.aol.com/ahrussell
-------------------------------------------------------
The views expressed are mine alone and do not necessarily reflect those of
my employer
```

```
Bravo!  The structure of the process is determined by the structure
of the underlying data.  I encourage anyone, to whom this is
unfamiliar, to spend a little time with Alan's example.  It's not just
about control breaks. It is, rather, the natural way to process any
structured data.  Your efforts will be well repaid.

Mike
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Asking for COBOL code with control breaks (big)

- **From:** John Piggott <John_Piggott@compuserve.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36A58FCD.44B6@compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<19990117164746.18393.00001853@ng-cd1.aol.com> <T_No2.2928$gd3.1980825@news1.mia>`

```
Codetrader wrote:

>Would anyone be kind enough to send me (or post) any COBOL code that has
>examples of control breaks (plus multilevel).  I am a student and can use any
>good tips/code/info on literature about breaks and tables. Thanks in advance.

Below I've re-coded Judson McClendon's coded example using the Report 
Writer syntax.  This kind of application is pure torture in vanilla 
COBOL.  With the RW syntax, note that:

--  all the moving, printing, and line-counting drop out
--  no Fillers needed, and no PIC if you have a Value
--  you know what each part does because of the TYPE and where it goes 
because of the LINE and COL(UMN) clauses
--  the entire control-break areas and code vanish
--  to total things you just write SUM xxx !

I've left in much of Judson's model code, such as accessing the data 
base, so I couldn't test the program, but it's pretty close.  The 
"counting" could probably be done with RW code too.

My code incorporates some of the new standard, so if you're using a 
compiler with the 68, 74 or 85 standard syntax, you need to read the 
Reference to get the syntax differences.

000100 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000200*
000300*PROGRAM-ID.    DE/DE/90100.
000400*
000500*        ************************************
000600*        *                                  *
000700*        *       JUDSON D. MCCLENDON        *
000800*        *       SUN VALLEY SYSTEMS         *
000900*        *       4522 SHADOW RIDGE PKWY     *
001000*        *       PINSON, AL 35216-2192      *
001100*        *          205/680-0460            *
001200*        *                                  *
001100*        *  MODIFIED TO USE REPORT WRITER   *
001100*        *         BY JOHN PIGGOTT          *
001100*        *    (PIGGOTT@SPC-SYSTEMS.COM)     *
001200*        *                                  *
001300*        ************************************
001400*
001500 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
001600*
001700 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
001800*
001900 FILE-CONTROL.
002000*
002100     SELECT WORK-FILE    ASSIGN TO DISK
002200                         RESERVE 1 AREA.
002300*
002400     SELECT SORT-FILE    ASSIGN TO SORT.
002500*
002600     SELECT PRINT-FILE   ASSIGN TO PRINTER.
002700*
002800 DATA DIVISION.
002900*
003000 FILE SECTION.
003100*
003200*
003300*
003400******************************************************************003500*                                                                *
003600*                     W O R K   F I L E                          *
003700*                                                                *
003800******************************************************************003900*
004000 FD  WORK-FILE
004100     RECORD CONTAINS 29 CHARACTERS
004200     BLOCK CONTAINS 180 RECORDS
004300     VALUE OF TITLE IS "DE/DE/90100/DSK1 ON WORK".
004400*
004500 01  WORK-RECORD.
004600     03  WORK-WORK-LOC           PIC  9(04) COMP.
           03  WORK-VISIT-DATE         PIC  X(8).
005200     03  WORK-VISIT-NBR          PIC  9(04) COMP.
005300     03  WORK-SEQ-NBR            PIC  9(04) COMP.
005400     03  WORK-PAT-NBR            PIC  9(06) COMP.
005500     03  WORK-CHARGE-CODE        PIC  9(06) COMP.
005600     03  WORK-TOOTH-FROM         PIC  X(03).
005700     03  WORK-TOOTH-TO           PIC  X(03).
005800     03  WORK-SURFACES           PIC  X(03).
005900     03  WORK-QTY                PIC  9(02) COMP.
006000     03  WORK-STAFF-NBR          PIC  9(04) COMP.
006100     03  WORK-BILL-CODE          PIC  9(01).
006200*
006300*
006400*
006500******************************************************************006600*                                                                *
006700*                     S O R T   F I L E                          *
006800*                                                                *
006900******************************************************************007000*
007100 SD  SORT-FILE.
007200*
007300 01  SORT-RECORD.
007400     03  SORT-WORK-LOC           PIC  9(04) COMP.
007500     03  SORT-VISIT-DATE.
007600         05  SORT-VISIT-DT-CC    PIC  9(02) COMP.
007700         05  SORT-VISIT-DT-YY    PIC  9(02) COMP.
007800         05  SORT-VISIT-DT-MM    PIC  9(02) COMP.
007900         05  SORT-VISIT-DT-DD    PIC  9(02) COMP.
008000     03  SORT-VISIT-NBR          PIC  9(04) COMP.
008100     03  SORT-SEQ-NBR            PIC  9(04) COMP.
008200     03  SORT-PAT-NBR            PIC  9(06) COMP.
008300     03  SORT-CHARGE-CODE        PIC  9(06) COMP.
008400     03  SORT-TOOTH-FROM         PIC  X(03).
008500     03  SORT-TOOTH-TO           PIC  X(03).
008600     03  SORT-SURFACES           PIC  X(03).
008700     03  SORT-QTY                PIC  9(02) COMP.
008800     03  SORT-STAFF-NBR          PIC  9(04) COMP.
008900     03  SORT-BILL-CODE          PIC  9(01).
009000*
009100*
009200*
009300******************************************************************009400*                                                                *
009500*                    P R I N T   F I L E                         *
009600*                                                                *
009700******************************************************************009800*
009900 FD  PRINT-FILE
010000     VALUE OF TITLE IS "DE90100LST1"
           REPORTS ARE DENTAL-REPORT EMPTY-FILE-REPORT.
      *
015800/*****************************************************************015900*                                                                *
016000*                        DATA-BASE SECTION                       *
016100*                                                                *
016200******************************************************************016300*
016400 DATA-BASE SECTION.
016500*
016600 DB  DEDABA.
016700*
016800 01  DE-FAMILY.
016900 01  DE-PATIENT.
017000 01  DE-APPOINTMENT.
017100 01  DE-VISIT.
017200 01  DE-ACTIVITY.
017300 01  DE-STAFF.
017400 01  DE-CHARGE.
017500 01  DE-LOCATION.
017600*
017700 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
017800/*****************************************************************017900*                                                                *
018000*                W O R K I N G   S T O R A G E                   *
018100*                                                                *
018200******************************************************************018300*
018400 77  WS-END-FLAG                 PIC  9(01)       COMP VALUE 0.
018500*
018600 77  WS-APP-PRESENT-FLAG         PIC  9(01)       COMP VALUE 0.
018700 77  WS-VIS-PRESENT-FLAG         PIC  9(01)       COMP VALUE 0.
018800 77  WS-ACT-PRESENT-FLAG         PIC  9(01)       COMP VALUE 0.
025500*
025600*
025700*
025800******************************************************************025900*                                                                *
026000*                 T O T A L   H O L D   A R E A                  *
026100*                                                                *
026200******************************************************************026300*
026400 01  TOTAL-HOLD-AREA.
026900     03  THA-LEVEL-2.
027300         05  THA-2-APP-KEPT      PIC  9(04)        VALUE 0.
027400         05  THA-2-APP-LATE      PIC  9(04)        VALUE 0.
027500         05  THA-2-APP-BROKEN    PIC  9(04)        VALUE 0.
027600         05  THA-2-APP-WALKIN    PIC  9(04)        VALUE 0.
027700         05  THA-2-APP-REPLACE   PIC  9(04)        VALUE 0.
028700*
028800*
028900*
029000******************************************************************029100*                                                                *
029200*                 H O L D   W O R K   A R E A                    *
029300*                                                                *
029400******************************************************************029500*
029600 01  HOLD-WORK-AREA.
029700     03  HW-HOLD-WORK-LOC        PIC  9(04) COMP.
029800     03  HW-HOLD-BEG-DATE.
029900         05  HW-HOLD-BEG-DT-CC   PIC  9(02) COMP.
030000         05  HW-HOLD-BEG-DT-YY   PIC  9(02) COMP.
030100         05  HW-HOLD-BEG-DT-MM   PIC  9(02) COMP.
030200         05  HW-HOLD-BEG-DT-DD   PIC  9(02) COMP.
030300     03  HW-HOLD-END-DATE.
030400         05  HW-HOLD-END-DT-CC   PIC  9(02) COMP.
030500         05  HW-HOLD-END-DT-YY   PIC  9(02) COMP.
030600         05  HW-HOLD-END-DT-MM   PIC  9(02) COMP.
030700         05  HW-HOLD-END-DT-DD   PIC  9(02) COMP.
030800*
       LINKAGE SECTION.
031000*
031100******************************************************************031200*                                                                *
031300*                  W F L   P R I N T   W O R K                   *
031400*                                                                *
031500******************************************************************031600*
031700 01  WFL-PRINT-PARAMS.
031800     03  WPP-NUM-PARAM           PIC  9(06).
031900     03  WPP-BEG-DATE.
032000         05  WPP-BEG-DT-CC       PIC  9(02).
032100         05  WPP-BEG-DT-YY       PIC  9(02).
032200         05  WPP-BEG-DT-MM       PIC  9(02).
032300         05  WPP-BEG-DT-DD       PIC  9(02).
032400     03  WPP-END-DATE.
032500         05  WPP-END-DT-CC       PIC  9(02).
032600         05  WPP-END-DT-YY       PIC  9(02).
032700         05  WPP-END-DT-MM       PIC  9(02).
032800         05  WPP-END-DT-DD       PIC  9(02).
032900     03  WPP-NUM-PARAM2          PIC  9(06).
036400*
      *
      ******************************************************************
      *                                                                *
      *                  R E P O R T   L A Y O U T S                   *
      *                                                                *
      ******************************************************************
      *
       REPORT SECTION.
      *
       RD  DENTAL-REPORT
      ** HERE ARE THE CONTROLS LISTED FROM HIGHEST TO LOWEST:
           CONTROLS ARE WORK-WORK-LOC
                        WORK-VISIT-DATE
                        WORK-PAT-NBR
                        WORK-VISIT-NBR
                        WORK-STAFF-NBR
                        WORK-SEQ-NBR
           PAGE LIMIT 58  FIRST DETAIL 6.
      *
       01  TYPE PAGE HEADING.
         02  LINE 1.
           03  COL 1     VALUE "DATE".
           03  COL + 2   PIC  99/99/99 FUNCTION MDATE.
           03  COL + 2   PIC  99":"99  FUNCTION TIME.
           03  COL 29    VALUE "DENTAL HEALTH SYSTEM".
           03  COL 57    VALUE "DE90100LST1   PAGE ".
           03  COL + 2   PIC  Z(04)   SOURCE PAGE-COUNTER.
         02  LINE 2.
           03  COL 26    VALUE "PATIENT ACTIVITY BY LOCATION".
         02  LINE 4.
           03  COL 1     VALUE "LOCATION".
           03  COL + 2   PIC  Z(04)    SOURCE DELOC-LOC-CODE.
           03  COL + 2   PIC  X(50)    SOURCE DELOC-LOC-NAME.
           03  COL + 3   VALUE "DATE".
           03  COL + 2   PIC  XX"/"    SOURCE WORK-VISIT-DATE (5:2).
           03  COL + 1   PIC  XX"/"    SOURCE WORK-VISIT-DATE (3:2).
           03  COL + 1   PIC  XX       SOURCE WORK-VISIT-DATE (7:2).
      *
       01  VISIT-DATE-HEAD  TYPE CONTROL FOOTING FOR WORK-VISIT-DATE.
      *
       01  PATIENT-HEAD  TYPE CONTROL HEADING FOR WORK-PAT-NBR.
         02  LINE + 2.
           03  COL 1     VALUE "PATIENT".
           03  COL + 2   PIC  Z(06)    SOURCE WORK-PAT-NBR.
           03  COL  2    PIC  X(30)    SOURCE DEPAT-PAT-NBR.
           03  COL + 3   VALUE "PHONE".
           03  COL + 2   PIC  X(16)    SOURCE DEFAM-HOME-PHONE.
         02  LINE + 1.
           03  COL 3     VALUE
               "  CODE TOOTH   SURFAC DESCRIPTION--------- QY  RPUS  STD
      -        " FEE MCAI FEE STAFF".
      *
       01  VISIT-ENTRY  TYPE DETAIL.
         02  LINE + 1.
           03  COL 1     PIC  ZZ9999   SOURCE WORK-CHARGE-CODE.
           03  COL + 2   PIC  X(03)    SOURCE WORK-TOOTH-FROM.
           03  COL + 1   VALUE "-".
           03  COL + 1   PIC  X(03)    SOURCE WORK-TOOTH-TO.
           03  COL + 2   PIC  X(06)    SOURCE WORK-SURFACES.
           03  COL + 2   PIC  X(20)    SOURCE DECHG-DESC.
           03  COL + 2   PIC  Z(02)    SOURCE WORK-QTY.
           03  PL1-RPUS  COL + 2   PIC  ZZ.ZZ
                    SOURCE DECHG-TIME(1) * WORK-QTY ROUNDED.
           03  PL1-STD-FEE   COL + 2   PIC  ZZZZZ.ZZ
                    SOURCE DECHG-STD-FEE * WORK-QTY ROUNDED.
           03  PL1-MCAID-FEE COL + 2   PIC  ZZZZZ.ZZ
                    SOURCE DECHG-MCAID-FEE * WORK-QTY ROUNDED
                    PRESENT WHEN WORK-BILL-CODE = 2.
           03  COL + 2   PIC  Z(04)    SOURCE WORK-STAFF-NBR.
      *
       01  TYPE CONTROL FOOTING FOR WORK-PAT-NBR.
         02  LINE + 2  COL 31         VALUE
               "RPUS     STD FEE   MCAID FEE".
         02  LINE + 1.
           03  COL RIGHT 24           VALUE "* DAY * TOTAL".
           03  PL2-RPUS  COL + 3      PIC  Z,ZZZ.ZZ   SUM PL1-RPUS.
           03  PL2-STD-FEE COL + 3    PIC  ZZZ,ZZZ.ZZ SUM PL1-STD-FEE.
           03  PL2-MCAID-FEE COL + 3  PIC  ZZZ,ZZZ.ZZ SUM PL1-MCAID-FEE.
         02  LINE + 2.
           03  COL 4     VALUE "APPOINTMENTS  KEPT".
           03  PL3-TOT-KEPT   COL + 1  PIC  Z(04)
                         SOURCE THA-2-APP-KEPT.
           03  COL + 3   VALUE "LATE".
           03  PL3-TOT-LATE   COL + 2  PIC  Z(04)
                         SOURCE THA-2-APP-LATE.
           03  COL + 3   VALUE "BROKEN".
           03  PL3-TOT-BROKEN COL + 2  PIC  Z(04)
                         SOURCE THA-2-APP-BROKEN.
           03  COL + 3   VALUE "WALKIN".
           03  PL3-TOT-WALKIN COL + 2  PIC  Z(04)
                         SOURCE THA-2-APP-WALKIN.
           03  COL + 3   VALUE "REPLACE".
           03  PL3-TOT-REPLACE COL + 2 PIC  Z(04)
                         SOURCE THA-2-APP-REPLACE.
      *
       01  TYPE CONTROL FOOTING FINAL.
         02  LINE + 2  COL 31    VALUE
               "RPUS     STD FEE   MCAID FEE".
         02  LINE + 1.
           03  COL RIGHT 24      VALUE "* GRAND TOTAL".
           03  COL + 3   PIC  Z,ZZZ.ZZ       SUM PL2-RPUS.
           03  COL + 3   PIC  ZZZ,ZZZ.ZZ     SUM PL2-STD-FEE.
           03  COL + 3   PIC  ZZZ,ZZZ.ZZ     SUM PL2-MCAID-FEE.
         02  LINE + 2.
           03  COL 4     VALUE "APPOINTMENTS  KEPT".
           03  COL + 1   PIC  Z(04)   SUM  PL3-TOT-KEPT.
           03  COL + 3   VALUE "LATE".
           03  COL + 2   PIC  Z(04)   SUM PL3-TOT-LATE.
           03  COL + 3   VALUE "BROKEN".
           03  COL + 2   PIC  Z(04)   SUM PL3-TOT-BROKEN.
           03  COL + 3   VALUE "WALKIN".
           03  COL + 2   PIC  Z(04)   SUM PL3-TOT-WALKIN.
           03  COL + 3   VALUE "REPLACE".
           03  COL + 2   PIC  Z(04)   SUM PL3-TOT-REPLACE.
      *
      *
       RD  EMPTY-FILE-REPORT.
      *
       01  EMPTY-FILE-WARNING   TYPE DE.
         02  LINE + 1  COL 5  VALUE "*** NO RECORDS FOUND ***"
      *
038200/*****************************************************************038300*                                                                *
038400*              P R O C E D U R E   D I V I S I O N               *
038500*                                                                *
038600******************************************************************038700*
038800 PROCEDURE DIVISION              USING WFL-PRINT-PARAMS.
038900*
       DECLARATIVES.
       GET-PATIENT-DETAILS SECTION.
           USE BEFORE REPORTING PATIENT-HEAD.
       D00000-PAT-HD.
058800     FIND DE-PATIENT VIA DEPATPATSET
058900         AT (DEPAT-PAT-NBR = WORK-PAT-NBR).
059100     FIND DE-FAMILY VIA DEFAMFAMSET
059200         AT (DEFAM-FAM-NBR = DEPAT-FAM-NBR).
       COUNT-VISITS SECTION.
           USE BEFORE REPORTING VISIT-DATE-HEAD.
       D00100-VDAT-HD.
074100*
074200*  ** COUNT APPOINTMENTS **
074300*
074400     MOVE 1 TO WS-APP-PRESENT-FLAG.
074500     FIND DE-APPOINTMENT VIA FIRST DEAPPLOCDATESET
074600         AT (DEAPP-WORK-LOC = HW-HOLD-WORK-LOC)
074700             AND
074800            (DEAPP-APP-DATE = CBA-PREV-VISIT-DATE)
074900         ON EXCEPTION
075000             MOVE 0 TO WS-APP-PRESENT-FLAG.
075100     PERFORM 029210-ACCUM-APPOINTMENTS
075200        THRU 029210-EXIT
075300         UNTIL (WS-APP-PRESENT-FLAG = 0).
075400*
075500*  ** COUNT VISITS **
075600*
075700     MOVE 1 TO WS-VIS-PRESENT-FLAG.
075800     FIND DE-VISIT VIA FIRST DEVISLOCSET
075900         AT (DEVIS-WORK-LOC = HW-HOLD-WORK-LOC)
076000             AND
076100            (DEVIS-VISIT-DATE = CBA-PREV-VISIT-DATE)
076200         ON EXCEPTION
076300             MOVE 0 TO WS-VIS-PRESENT-FLAG.
076400     PERFORM 029220-ACCUM-VISITS
076500        THRU 029220-EXIT
076600         UNTIL (WS-VIS-PRESENT-FLAG = 0).
083200*
083300*         A C C U M U L A T E   A P P O I N T M E N T S
083400*
083500 029210-ACCUM-APPOINTMENTS.
083600*

083800         ADD 1 TO THA-2-APP-KEPT
083900     ELSE
084000     IF (DEAPP-STATUS = "L")

084200     ELSE

084700         AT (DEAPP-WORK-LOC = HW-HOLD-WORK-LOC)
084800             AND

085000         ON EXCEPTION
085100             MOVE 0 TO WS-APP-PRESENT-FLAG.
085200*
085300 029210-EXIT.
085400     EXIT.
085600*
085700*               A C C U M U L A T E   V I S I T S
085800*
085900 029220-ACCUM-VISITS.
086000*

086300     ELSE

#### ↳ Re: Asking for COBOL code with control breaks

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dZTq2.929$fz.1806706@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<19990117164746.18393.00001853@ng-cd1.aol.com>`

```
>Would anyone be kind enough to send me (or post) any COBOL code that has
>examples of control breaks (plus multilevel).  I am a student and can use
any
>good tips/code/info on literature about breaks and tables. Thanks in
advance.


Been writing, but mostly maintaining code for 4 yrs now. I have found that
using utilities are the best way to go...unless the user is unorganized and
cannot explain the control break logic properly enough for it to be
simplified. What does this all mean?

SyncSort/DFSORT reports use control break logic in a much simpler manner,
the only problem is you need to know the offset of the CONTROL FIELDS within
the file. Thus if you have the following offsets:

FIELD1 position 1 format character
FIELD2 position 10 format character
FIELD3 position 30 format signed zoned decimal

A syncsort control card file for a control break report would look like:

 SORT FIELDS=(001,010,CH,A,
              010,020,CH,A,
              030,015,ZD,A)
 SUM FIELDS=(030,015,ZD)
 OUTFIL FILES=01,
    OUTREC=(001,010,
            1X,
            010,020,
            1X,
            030,015,
            EDIT=(III,III,III,III,IIT)),
    HEADER2=(&TIME,
             20:C'PAGE HEADER',
             32:&PAGE),
    SECTIONS(001,010,
             HEADER3=(C'1ST LEVEL BREAK FOR',
                      001,010),
             010,020,
             HEADER3=(C'2ND LEVEL BREAK FOR',
                      010,020))


This would produce a report something like:
12:00:00           PAGE HEADER  00000001
1ST LEVEL BREAK FOR A111111111
2ND LEVEL BREAK FOR A22222222222222222222
A111111111 A22222222222222222222 111,111,111,111,111
2ND LEVEL BREAK FOR B22222222222222222222
A111111111 B22222222222222222222 111,111,111,111,111
2ND LEVEL BREAK FOR C22222222222222222222
A111111111 C22222222222222222222 111,111,111,111,111
1ST LEVEL BREAK FOR B111111111
2ND LEVEL BREAK FOR A22222222222222222222
B111111111 A22222222222222222222 111,111,111,111,111
2ND LEVEL BREAK FOR B22222222222222222222
B111111111 B22222222222222222222 111,111,111,111,111
2ND LEVEL BREAK FOR C22222222222222222222
B111111111 C22222222222222222222 111,111,111,111,111


Short and sweet. Many levels of control breaks can be executed in this
manner as well as different sort orders, multiple reports from the same
data, selection and omission logic and summation and elimination of
duplicate records.

Other utilities which are more like a program language are EZtrieve and
DYL280 (which can even use COBOL copybooks).

With all of these utilities I haven't had to write a control break program
in COBOL in my working career.

EXTRACT FILTER SORT REPORT break your control break logic into at least 2 of
these steps and the program will be much easier to understand.

But that's just my opinion, I may be wrong.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Asking for COBOL code with control breaks

- **From:** fyaeger5@vnet.ibm.com
- **Date:** 1999-01-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78i607$q1g$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<19990117164746.18393.00001853@ng-cd1.aol.com> <dZTq2.929$fz.1806706@storm.twcol.com>`

```
In article <dZTq2.929$fz.1806706@storm.twcol.com>,
  "Jeff" <a@a.com> wrote
>...
> SyncSort/DFSORT reports use control break logic in a much simpler manner,
> the only problem is you need to know the offset of the CONTROL FIELDS within
> the file.
>...

With DFSORT R14, you can actually map your fields and constants with symbols
and use them in DFSORT and ICETOOL statements, so you don't have to remember
the field offsets or constant values.  Similar to using symbols in COBOL
programs.  This makes the control break logic even easier to deal with.

For more information on DFSORT R14, Symbols and OUTFIL, visit the DSORT home
page at URL:

http://www.ibm.com/storage/dfsort/

Frank Yaeger - DFSORT Team (Specialties: Symbols, Y2K, OUTFIL, ICETOOL)



-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Asking for COBOL code with control breaks

- **From:** Frank Yaeger <fyaeger@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36AE7DAF.5BC4B956@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<19990117164746.18393.00001853@ng-cd1.aol.com> <dZTq2.929$fz.1806706@storm.twcol.com>`

```


Jeff wrote:

> SyncSort/DFSORT reports use control break logic in a much simpler manner,
> the only problem is you need to know the offset of the CONTROL FIELDS within
> the file.

With DFSORT R14, you can actually map your fields and constants with symbols and
use them in DFSORT and ICETOOL statements, so you don't have to remember the
field offsets or constant values.  Similar to using symbols in COBOL programs.
This makes the control break logic even easier to deal with.

For more information on DFSORT R14, Symbols, OUTFIL, etc., visit the DFSORT home
page at URL:

http://www.ibm.com/storage/dfsort/

Frank Yaeger - DFSORT Team (Specialties: Y2K, Symbols, ICETOOL, OUTFIL :-)
fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Asking for COBOL code with control breaks

- **From:** John Piggott <John_Piggott@compuserve.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36AF7C83.17D7@compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<19990117164746.18393.00001853@ng-cd1.aol.com> <dZTq2.929$fz.1806706@storm.twcol.com> <36AE7DAF.5BC4B956@ibm.net>`

```
Doing control breaks with the COBOL Report Writer syntax takes a bit 
longer to code but it's very readable and concise, and I think the main 
advantage is that you don't need to switch programming languages and 
split up the application into separate chunks.

You put the clause e.g. CONTROLS ARE STATE COUNTY CITY etc. in the RD and 
each control group can be defined wth LINE and COL clauses e.g.

  01  TYPE CF  FOR STATE.     (CF means "CONTROL FOOTING")
     03  LINE + 2.
         05  COL 11   "Here's the Sales Total".
         05  COL 23   PIC ZZZZ9.99  SUM OF SALES-FIGURE.

I don't know how other non-COBOL systems compare, but one nice thing is 
that, if all your total lines have a similar layout, you can combine 
several levels into one, e.g.

  01  TYPE CF FOR STATE, COUNTRY, CITY.

and it automatically rolls forward subtotals into totals etc.

Frank Yaeger wrote:
> 
> Jeff wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com
```

innocent new programmer that was away for a minute to get some coffee.

<a id="t-c7b0e448e2"></a>
## Thread: linking problem with fujitsu 3.0 compiler — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### linking problem with fujitsu 3.0 compiler

- **From:** smiley@izap.com (smiley)
- **Date:** 1999-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36a187e6.1789324@news.izap.com>`

```
hi.  i just installed fujitsu COBOL compiler 3.0.  when i compile it
it's fine.  but when i try to LINK my *.OBJ file into an executable
(*.EXE) it gives me this error:

	LINK:  fatal error LNK1561: entry point must be defined.

does anyone know what this means?  why is fujitsu compilers so much
more complicated than Micro Focus?

smiley
```

#### ↳ Re: linking problem with fujitsu 3.0 compiler

- **From:** "IAN STODDART" <Ian@stoddart60.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be41ef$f5e705e0$a919883e@stodfamily.u-net.com>`
- **References:** `<36a187e6.1789324@news.izap.com>`

```
Most newcomers to Fujitsu encounter this.  When compiling, select options,
add, select "Main" then add.  Now select "Compile program as a main
program" you should now be ok.  The default compile option is subprogram ! 
I have found I need to set this option for each new folder I work in.
If you search the Fujitsu web site there are a series of tutorial's which
get us newbies up and running.  They cover Compile,link,execution --
Editing -- Using the Debugger (again will not work unless you set some
options) -- Runtime filenames -- The file utility.
It is well worth checking these pages out.

smiley <smiley@izap.com> wrote in article
<36a187e6.1789324@news.izap.com>...
> hi.  i just installed fujitsu COBOL compiler 3.0.  when i compile it
> it's fine.  but when i try to LINK my *.OBJ file into an executable
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: linking problem with fujitsu 3.0 compiler

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36a213dc.68325897@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36a187e6.1789324@news.izap.com>`

```
On Sun, 17 Jan 1999 06:54:03 GMT, smiley@izap.com (smiley) wrote:

>hi.  i just installed fujitsu COBOL compiler 3.0.  when i compile it
>it's fine.  but when i try to LINK my *.OBJ file into an executable
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>more complicated than Micro Focus?

The compiler is actually not any more complex!  What you have to do is
indicate that the program is a "MAIN" program.  The Fujitsu Default is
to compile as a sub program.  The simplest way is to insert a line at
the top of the program, case sensitive, starting in column 8 that
says:

@OPTIONS MAIN
```

#### ↳ Re: linking problem with fujitsu 3.0 compiler

- **From:** gkr@adtools.com (Gary Roush)
- **Date:** 1999-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36a2e28f.29088259@slcnews.urjet.net>`
- **References:** `<36a187e6.1789324@news.izap.com>`

```
Smiley,

The answers given in previous messages are correct. MAIN must be
issued when compiling a program if it IS the main program. Once you
understand this, then it is very easy knowing what the purpose of MAIN
is. It's a common occurence that new programmers forget. It is no more
complicated than MicroFocus. Quite easy to do actually. 

We have an updated V3 COBOL that can be downloaded free from the
website at: http://www.adtools.com/  Simply notice the V3 COBOL
Starters Kit link and start downloading. It is 11 MB long so take note
of that. Also know that the system V3 would be running on needs to be
a 32-bit system such as Windows 95/98/NT. If you want more features
than what you have in V3, you may look up V4 and see what has been
added. 

If you have further questions on using Fujitsu COBOL V3 or V4, feel
free to ask us. 

Regards,
Gary Roush
Fujitsu COBOL Support
mailto:gkr@adtools.com,support@adtools.com


On Sun, 17 Jan 1999 06:54:03 GMT, smiley@izap.com (smiley) wrote:

>hi.  i just installed fujitsu COBOL compiler 3.0.  when i compile it
>it's fine.  but when i try to LINK my *.OBJ file into an executable
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

<a id="t-541a831604"></a>
## Thread: Populating Pull Down Lists From Data With Netexpress — 3 msgs

### Populating Pull Down Lists From Data With Netexpress

- **From:** nick@asheath.demon.co.uk (Nick & Carol Lucas-White)
- **Date:** 1999-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36a2144f.1656445@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
I'm told by my programmer that it's not possible to fill the details
of a pull-down list with data "on the fly" with NetExpress/Dialog. Is
this true ? I'd have thought this was a fairly fundamental
requirement. If it can't do this how could it handle, for instance, a
list of file names etc. that is dynamic information. 

Any information, as always, gratefully received.
```

#### ↳ Re: Populating Pull Down Lists From Data With Netexpress

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36a29a43.102737504@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36a2144f.1656445@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
On Sun, 17 Jan 1999 17:08:20 GMT, nick@asheath.demon.co.uk (Nick &
Carol Lucas-White) wrote:

>I'm told by my programmer that it's not possible to fill the details
>of a pull-down list with data "on the fly" with NetExpress/Dialog. Is
>this true ? I'd have thought this was a fairly fundamental
>requirement. If it can't do this how could it handle, for instance, a
>list of file names etc. that is dynamic information. 

Not true.  You can do this on the fly.  It's a little "tricky"
involving tables and some Dialog code, but it can be done.  There's
several caveats about the starting item etc etc.  I must tell you that
where I last worked we strugled with several methods before finding
one that worked satisfactorily, but it CAN be done.
```

#### ↳ Re: Populating Pull Down Lists From Data With Netexpress

- **From:** "S. Sharifi" <ssharifi@atlanta.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77vsub$nmu$1@east44.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<36a2144f.1656445@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
You can Insert and delete items at run time using dialog functions
INSERT-LIST-ITEM or INSERT-MANY-LIST-ITEMS and DELETE-LIST- ITEM. The data
source can be an array or a single field where the data items are separated
by the hexadecimal characters 0A.

Nick & Carol Lucas-White wrote in message
<36a2144f.1656445@news.demon.co.uk>...
>I'm told by my programmer that it's not possible to fill the details
>of a pull-down list with data "on the fly" with NetExpress/Dialog. Is
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Any information, as always, gratefully received.
```

For those who have not heard by other avenues, Rex Widmer was killed on

some of his time to help the school.

time.

 Spengel - Boulanger Funeral Home
 1501 Ninth Street
 Highland, Illinois 62249
The service is expected to last approximately 35 minutes.  Burial will be at
St. Joseph's Cemetary following the funeral and will conclude approximately
12:30PM.

Highland, IL is about 30 miles East of STL airport.

H 618 654-6506
W 618 654-6465

Donations can be sent to the University of Missouri - Rolla Alumni
Association.

We are all still in a state of shock about this, but if you would like any
further information, we will try to get it for you.  You can contact me at
the phone numbers or address below.

Rex was an important part of the lives of all who came in contact with him.
His friendship and contributions will be greatly missed.

Carl

<a id="t-133f98bf14"></a>
## Thread: fujitsu v3 — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### fujitsu v3

- **From:** "Mark Reid" <markmreid@tinet.ie>
- **Date:** 1999-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77tqp7$l4a$1@spock.tinet.ie>`

```
I have a program with the following sentence " ACCEPT W-FIRST-NUMBER WITH
CONVERSION" .  When I compile, I'm told "WORD CONVERSION IS UNDEFINED".  If
I define it , I then get "THERE IS WORD CONVERSION WHERE STATEMENT SHOULD
APPEAR".  Any ideas, anyone, as to why the compiler won't accept
"conversion". CK
```

#### ↳ Re: fujitsu v3

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36a29c65.103284015@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<77tqp7$l4a$1@spock.tinet.ie>`

```
On Sun, 17 Jan 1999 23:11:19 -0000, "Mark Reid" <markmreid@tinet.ie>
wrote:

>I have a program with the following sentence " ACCEPT W-FIRST-NUMBER WITH
>CONVERSION" .  When I compile, I'm told "WORD CONVERSION IS UNDEFINED".  If
>I define it , I then get "THERE IS WORD CONVERSION WHERE STATEMENT SHOULD
>APPEAR".  Any ideas, anyone, as to why the compiler won't accept
>"conversion". CK

Sounds like you are converting a VMS or VAX COBOL program - just leave
the WITH CONVERSION off.
```

#### ↳ Re: fujitsu v3

- **From:** gkr@adtools.com (Gary Roush)
- **Date:** 1999-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36a2e6f1.30210465@slcnews.urjet.net>`
- **References:** `<77tqp7$l4a$1@spock.tinet.ie>`

```
Hi Mark,

For your information, there is no such command as CONVERSION in
Fujitsu COBOL. That may be a command in another program language like
maybe MicroFocus. You do not need to use CONVERSION. 

Are you accepting data from the screen input? ACCEPT W-FIRST-NUMBER is
all you need and when the program runs, and comes to that point, it
will stop and request information be typed such as a number and then
hit return and that will be entered into the W-FIRST-NUMBER field.

Hope this helps.

Regards,
Gary Roush
Fujitsu COBOL Support
mailto:gkr@adtools.com,support@adtools.com


On Sun, 17 Jan 1999 23:11:19 -0000, "Mark Reid" <markmreid@tinet.ie>
wrote:

>I have a program with the following sentence " ACCEPT W-FIRST-NUMBER WITH
>CONVERSION" .  When I compile, I'm told "WORD CONVERSION IS UNDEFINED".  If
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Regards,
Gary Roush
Fujitsu COBOL Support
mailto:gkr@adtools.com,support@adtools.com
```

<a id="t-cfd6faa1af"></a>
## Thread: Multithreading and ProCobol — 1 msgs

### Re: Multithreading and ProCobol

- **From:** "Henrik Paludan-Moerk" <hpmpcmag@inet.uni2.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.databases.oracle.server,comp.lang.cobol,comp.programming.threads
- **Message-ID:** `<01be4270$5dbeac60$cad08e81@hpm.kreativ-it.dk>`
- **References:** `<75f5p8$bl7$1@newton.pacific.net.sg> <76vi88$28p$1@hermes.is.co.za> <3693dd6c.16491780@netnews> <772839$7ov$1@hermes.is.co.za> <77498b$r2o@espresso.cafe.net>`

```
> Actually I can easily imagine languages that might not be good for
> threading. Take, for instance, some language like BASIC that has no
> concept of a local variable. Or, more specifically, a language that
> has only static storage.

What? No local variables in Basic? that does indeed depend on which flavor
we talk about; one of the most common ones, visual basic, has them.
Also, elderly BASIC implementations has them, if you want to use so-called
SUBS (procedures) in your program, you have to declare the variables you
want to be global, as global.

I think BASIC could well do threading, but we'd need some new verbs for it
- just as with COBOL..

My best,
HenrikPM
```

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)

John:

Jeff is absolutely correct that you can get the Fujitsu compiler from
adtools, BUT if you're teaching yourself COBOL, I suggest that you buy
a book called, "Teach Youself COBOL in 24 Hours" authored by Mr. Thane
Hubbell.

You'll accomplish two things...

1.  You'll get a great manual which will assist you in a step-by-step
approach...and

2.  You'll also get the free Fujitsu compiler with the book on CD-ROM.

It's generally a lot faster turnaround on getting the book as well.

You can buy it from a number of on-line book buying services (amazon).
The ISBN number is:

0-672-31453-3

### call cobol sub from Visual basic

- **From:** "m" <m_peled@usa.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,alt.comp.lang.visualbasic.ver3,alt.online-service.microsoft,comp.databases.oracle.misc,comp.databases.oracle.server,comp.lang.basic.visual.database,comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol,microsoft.public.il.hebrew.vb,microsoft.public.vb.con

```
Hello,
I want to know how can i run cobol subrotine from VB6

my connections DB is ORACLE ,
the cobol function exist at another computer (outside the DB computer)

what version of ORACLE i need for it ?


Are somebody have same or similar problem.
Please, send me email on mailto:m_peled@usa.net
```

#### ↳ Re: call cobol sub from Visual basic

- **From:** "Billy Verreynne" <vslabs@onwe.co.za>
- **Date:** 1999-01-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,alt.comp.lang.visualbasic.ver3,alt.online-service.microsoft,comp.databases.oracle.misc,comp.databases.oracle.server,comp.lang.basic.visual.database,comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol,microsoft.public.il.hebrew.vb,microsoft.public.vb.con
- **Message-ID:** `<78a19b$no9$1@hermes.is.co.za>`
- **References:** `<77vhp6$scg$1@news.netvision.net.il>`

```
m wrote in message <77vhp6$scg$1@news.netvision.net.il>...
>I want to know how can i run cobol subrotine from VB6
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>what version of ORACLE i need for it ?


Oracle Distributed Parallel Server v19.x (ODPS). You will also need the
Oracle's Distributed Virtual Machine Environment 2.1 (Ora DVME) and you
will have to install the Oracle COBOL RTS (Run Time System) for JAVA
(which will ship according to my sources will ship with Oracle v18.x and
up).

The only problem is of course VB6. By the time that Oracle v19.x is
released, VB and Microsoft will only be a vague memory of something that
went horribly wrong back in the late 20th century.
```

<a id="t-e76a3466c4"></a>
## Thread: OO CObol — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### OO CObol

- **From:** marsht1@diebold.com
- **Date:** 1999-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7801rq$6hk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
All:

I've been reading about the OO Extensions for MVS COBOL. Before we dive in
to try it, we have some questions:

1. How difficult is it to set up the environment to support it?

2. Where can I find a reference to set up the environment?

3. I see that it uses SOM, but I seem to recall seeing somewhere that IBM is
not going to be supporting SOM in the near future. Is this true?

4. Can C++ classes written for MVS extend COBOL classes, and Vice-Versus?

5. Is anyone out there using it?

6. What are your experiences with it?

7. Are there recommended standards for class names, method names, etc?


TIA!

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: OO CObol

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<780e4d$8d4@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7801rq$6hk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Replies (indicated with ">>" from my usually reliable sources)

Bill,

  I have answered some of the questions below.  You can take
1 and 2, I couldn't tell if he was asking about LE or setting up SOM.

From: IBM Mainframe Discussion List [mailto:IBM-MAIN@BAMA.UA.EDU] On
Behalf Of Marsh, Thomas S.
Sent: Monday, January 11, 1999 8:12 AM
To: IBM-MAIN@BAMA.UA.EDU
Subject: OO COBOL

All:

I've been reading about the OO Extensions for MVS COBOL. Before we dive in
to try it, we have some questions:

1. How difficult is it to set up the environment to support it?

>> LE and SOM are easy to install, but moving all of your applications
>> to LE can be easy or hard depending on your particular applications.

2. Where can I find a reference to set up the environment?

>> It depends if you mean SOM or LE or OS/390 or ?

3. I see that it uses SOM, but I seem to recall seeing somewhere that IBM
is not going to be supporting SOM in the near future. Is this true?

>> SOM is no longer strategic, but IBM COBOL will continue to run even
>> after SOM is no longer supported.  We may include SOM or a SOM
>> replacement with COBOL or change underlying support, but it will
>> not impact customer code that was written for SOM originally.

4. Can C++ classes written for MVS extend COBOL classes, and Vice-Versus?

>> Yes, as long as the C++ classes are written using SOM.  C++ supports
>> both SOM and non-SOM OO.

5. Is anyone out there using it?

>> Yes.

6. What are your experiences with it?

>> Any volunteers for this one?

7. Are there recommended standards for class names, method names, etc?

>> No, I don't think so.

TIA!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OO CObol

- **From:** marsht1@diebold.com
- **Date:** 1999-01-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<787ht1$m8a$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<7801rq$6hk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <780e4d$8d4@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In regards to questions 1 and 2, I guess I was speaking about SOM. We are
currently running LE.

TIA

<a id="t-74912aa53d"></a>
## Thread: OldBOL to NewBOL - <IBM> COBOL Migration Tutorial — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### OldBOL to NewBOL - <IBM> COBOL Migration Tutorial

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78064e$ada@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
I have just heard from Micro Focus publishing that they are about to throw
out several hundred copies of my (now out of date) book on migrating from
OS/VS COBOL (DOS/VS COBOL and early versions of VS COBOL II) to the "newer"
VS COBOL II and COBOL/370 products (not COBOL for this-and-that - but with
some discussion of LE).  About half of the book concerns now UNsupported
versions of Micro Focus COBOL Workbench (and its use in mainframe
development).

HOWEVER,  I think that much of the IBM mainframe COBOL stuff is still of
some interest.  Therefore, if you were to contact

Linda Rodenspiel (of Micro Focus Publishing)
   at
(650) 404-7366
   or
lr@microfocus.com

You might be able to get copies of this "historical" document at a bargain
price.  Legal and other restrictions MIGHT require them to quote you the
"published" price - but I can't see why they wouldn't accept offers for
close to the shipping price - just to get it off their hands.
```

## Thread: Micro Focus user groups, periodic post — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Micro Focus user groups, periodic post

- **From:** "Stephen D. Burns" <stephen.d.burns2@boeing.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36A39917.CAB454CF@boeing.com>`

```
If you have any interest in Micro Focus (soon to be Merant) products,
you should look into joining a user group near you.  You can find more
information and an online registration form at
http://www.microfocus.com/partners/  Or pick  'Partners and user groups'
in the navigation bar.  

There are user groups in many major cities, with more forming all the
time.  If you wish to start one in your area, Micro Focus will help you
do it.  Instructions can be found in the web page or by contacting Laura
Wang at lyw@microfocus.com
```

<a id="t-9c82fb92ab"></a>
## Thread: winhelp in MF cobol — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### winhelp in MF cobol

- **From:** "WerewolF" <werewolf@anti-social.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<780f7j$3gh$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
Does anyone know how can I call winhelp in MF cobol but executing the entry
in the topic I want?

A solution for this would be much appreciated.

Here is the program.
Thank you

       SPECIAL-NAMES.
           call-convention 11 is winapi.
       DATA DIVISION.
       Working-Storage Section.

       01 WS-WinExec-Parameters.
          03 WS-WinExec-Command-Line         pic x(128).
          03 WS-WinExec-Window-State         pic 9(004) comp-5.
          03 WS-WinExec-Return-Code          pic 9(004) comp-5.
       01 WS-PTR                             PROCEDURE-POINTER.
       01 WS-COUNTER                         PIC 9(3).
******************************************************
       LINKAGE SECTION.
       COPY FSTLINK.CBL.
      *--------------------------------------------------
       PROCEDURE DIVISION USING COM-AREA COM-AREA-S COM-AREA-T.
      *--------------------------------------------------
           SET WS-PTR TO ENTRY"KERNEL".
           initialize WS-WinExec-Parameters.
           MOVE 1    TO WS-WINEXEC-WINDOW-STATE.
           STRING "WINHELP.EXE"
                            DELIMITED BY SIZE
                  " "         DELIMITED BY SIZE
                   "praia.hlp "   DELIMITED BY SPACES
****************  " 4125 "         DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 X"00"        DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 INTO WS-WINEXEC-COMMAND-LINE
           END-STRING.
***********DISPLAY WS-WINEXEC-COMMAND-LINE
           CALL Winapi "WINEXEC"
                    USING BY REFERENCE WS-WINEXEC-COMMAND-LINE
                          BY VALUE WS-WINEXEC-WINDOW-STATE
                      RETURNING   WS-WINEXEC-RETURN-CODE
           END-CALL.
       R-FIM.
            EXIT PROGRAM.
```

#### ↳ Re: basic cobol

- **From:** "kennet" <kennet@post11.tele.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<77vlqs$49h0$1@news-inn.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<77tvjq$3cc$1@netnews.upenn.edu>`

```
This program has input a and b and output c. It calculates the sum c=a+b.

Kennet

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.      SUMMER.
       AUTHOR.          KENNET ANDERSEN.

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.

       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

       LINKAGE SECTION.

       01 A                 PIC 9(7).
       01 B                 PIC 9(7).
       01 C                 PIC 9(7).

       PROCEDURE DIVISION USING A B C.

       MAIN.

           ADD A TO B GIVING C

           EXIT PROGRAM.
           STOP RUN.

<a id="t-df78fbb1b9"></a>
## Thread: Please read, I need advice — 16 msgs

### Please read, I need advice

- **From:** "John Adams" <jadams18@idirect.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be43fd$3da0fc60$63d1a1d1@johnadam>`

```
	I am in the midst of my second semester in my first year of a two-year
computer programming course.  This semester contains a beginner COBOL
course, of which I am doing very well in.  I have one of the highest, if
not the highest GPA's in my program.  I really love programming both in
COBOL and C (later on we will be learning VBasic, Java, and several
database related subjects).  I have a work term coming up in May and
another later on.  I think now would be an excellent time to choose a
career direction because I can get the experience from my two work terms
and choose my elective courses to fit my goal.  My college is one of either
a few or none in my area that teaches COBOL and it has an advanced COBOL
elective that I can take as well.  I have also helped create a personnel
data base at a major organization in my grade 12 co-op term using MS
Access.  My problem is that I am not aware what is required in education
and experience, and what the future will hold.  This is why I come to you
for advice.

	Running in my head are some choices:
1)  Learn object oriented programming in the form of C++ and JAVA
2)  Learn VBasic and/or concentrate on database programming
3)  Take the advance course in COBOL and try for work term experience and
live my life as a COBOL programmer (hopefully get a job before fourth and
final semester, which starts May 2000)

	Is COBOL going to be a language which will command a high salary in the
future.  I know the language will be around for a long long time and that
less and less people are learning it and less and less schools are teaching
it.  It might be highly employable niche, even after Y2K.  

	I would like to have your advice/opinion on what I have said and the
following questions
1) What do I need to get into COBOL programming?
2) Will it provide a better than average to high salary in the future?
3) How would I go about getting an entry level position in COBOL
programming

	Most important to me though is to have some contacts in Ontario that I can
speak with and learn from.  I really look forward to your help.  My e-mail
address is jadams18@idirect.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Please read, I need advice

- **From:** carloss940@aol.com (CarlosS940)
- **Date:** 1999-01-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990119210403.04931.00000951@ng126.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<01be43fd$3da0fc60$63d1a1d1@johnadam>`

```
learn C++; most professional software engineer use C++.  Also learn as much as
possible about Database as almost every computer program has a database or
something similar built into it.  

I myself learning web programming in a commuity college. I also need to learn
C++ and database programming.

Carlos A. Santos            
E-mail to: CarlosS940@aol.com
Life is wonderful only if you know what it means! : )
```

- **Date:** 1999-01-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36A55459.8538CDD7@att.net>`

> 
> learn C++; most professional software engineer use C++.

This might be true for the toy computers, but is not true (in my
experience) on IBM mainframes.

>  Also learn as much as
> possible about Database as almost every computer program has a database or
> something similar built into it.

I agree that it's definitely a strategic skill to learn at least one of
the major databases systems - but whatever do you mean by "almost every
computer program has a database or something similar built into it"?

Bill Lynch
```

#### ↳ Re: Please read, I need advice

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-01-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36a58004.2750575@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<01be43fd$3da0fc60$63d1a1d1@johnadam>`

```
On 19 Jan 1999 17:41:43 +0500, "John Adams" <jadams18@idirect.com> wrote:

>	Running in my head are some choices:
>1)  Learn object oriented programming in the form of C++ and JAVA
>2)  Learn VBasic and/or concentrate on database programming
>3)  Take the advance course in COBOL and try for work term experience and
>live my life as a COBOL programmer 
Yes, but not necessarily in that sequence <g>  In my experience (mainframe) The best order
would be
0) Learn Assembler (not to program in it, but to learn about the underlying structure of
the environment)
1)  Learn COBOL
2)  Learn Databases (like DB2, DLI, DATACOM, ADABAS, in this sequence)
3) Learn CICS  (you might exchange 2 and 3)
4) Learn VBasic or any other front-end programming language, possibly JAVA
5) If you still have time on your hands, learn C++ (not that you will ever use it.....)

with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Please read, I need advice

- **From:** swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1999-01-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<907BE2C0EFD9E0F2.3F7BCCA6DBCD1038.3708BE634485C7AA@library-proxy.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<01be43fd$3da0fc60$63d1a1d1@johnadam> <36a58004.2750575@news3.ibm.net>`

```
On Wed, 20 Jan 1999 08:10:49 GMT, vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
enlightened us:

>On 19 Jan 1999 17:41:43 +0500, "John Adams" <jadams18@idirect.com> wrote:
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>(BSP GmbH)

From a mainframe perspective, I agree with Volker's answers.  But you
also need to look at your non-computer courses.  You also need to
decide (if you can) what kind of programming you'd like to do and take
elective courses that will give you some background.  For example, if
you decide you want to do business programming (banks, insurance
companies, brokerages etc.), you should take at least two or three
accounting courses to get a solid background in finances.  Programming
for a bank and understanding debits/credits as they relate to deposit
and/or loan systems makes the programming a little easier to figure
out.  If you're more interested in systems programming, then you'll
want to take more then one semester of Assembler and you should take
whatever courses the college offers in this area.  You didn't mention
what university you are attending, but from my experience, it is only
engineering colleges that have the systems type courses.  There is
also scientific programming you might be interested in.  For this you
should probably take some higher math courses and chemistry.  Remember
that the electives you want to take should enhance you knowledge in
the area you plan on working in.

HTH and Good Luck.


          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

It may be that your sole purpose in life is simply to serve as a warning to 
others.

 Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Please read, I need advice

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-01-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36a63084.88614127@netnews>`
- **References:** `<01be43fd$3da0fc60$63d1a1d1@johnadam> <36a58004.2750575@news3.ibm.net> <907BE2C0EFD9E0F2.3F7BCCA6DBCD1038.3708BE634485C7AA@library-proxy.airnews.net>`

```
'Twas Wed, 20 Jan 1999 16:36:19 GMT, when swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>From a mainframe perspective, I agree with Volker's answers.  But you
>also need to look at your non-computer courses.  

I agree.  If you want to be successful in business programming, you should
study accounting, business management, and English.

I have a friend who deliberately avoided taking a composition class in
high school, then got a BS in CompSci without taking an English class or
even a class with essay tests (you could do that in the 1970s).  Now she's
always complaining about writing test plans, documentation, etc, because
she's always told to write it over.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Please read, I need advice

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Jan Lend <janlend@notayahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36A6AEF5.ADC80DBB@notayahoo.com>`
- **References:** `<01be43fd$3da0fc60$63d1a1d1@johnadam> <36a58004.2750575@news3.ibm.net> <907BE2C0EFD9E0F2.3F7BCCA6DBCD1038.3708BE634485C7AA@library-proxy.airnews.net> <36a63084.88614127@netnews>`

```
I think Java should be a top choice - it will teach you the
object-oriented paradigm, and a language that is poised to be dominant
relatively soon. (C and C++ will force you to do too much of the job
that rightfully belongs to compiler writers, and get in the way of your
conceptual learning.)

Cobol, of course, is a bias of everyone in this group, and is a very
good, disciplined, business language, although Computerworld says that
demand for cobol-ers will decline after Y2K. Of course, supply is
retiring and dying too, so unemployment is unlikely :).

For pure fun, try Rexx, Netrexx, or Object Rexx.

As with the other correspondents, I suggest ensuring that your studies
are well-rounded. The coming net world might suggest learning about
graphic presentation, etc.  Good programming is perhaps 50% good design
and code, and 50% people-skills.

Good luck.

Jan Lend

 

Randall Bart wrote:
> 
> 'Twas Wed, 20 Jan 1999 16:36:19 GMT, when swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Please read, I need advice

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<S0Fp2.23$jE1.22046@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<01be43fd$3da0fc60$63d1a1d1@johnadam> <36a58004.2750575@news3.ibm.net> <907BE2C0EFD9E0F2.3F7BCCA6DBCD1038.3708BE634485C7AA@library-proxy.airnews.net> <36a63084.88614127@netnews> <36A6AEF5.ADC80DBB@notayahoo.com>`

```
Jan Lend wrote:
>
>I think Java should be a top choice - it will teach you the
>object-oriented paradigm, and a language that is poised to be dominant
>relatively soon. [snip]

Or subside into oblivion.  The jury is still out on Java, and will be for
some time.  Pascal was hot for a while, but now is almost irrelevant. :-)

>Good programming is perhaps 50% good design and code, and 50% people-skills.

For a programmer, in amount of time spent, I somewhat agree (maybe 80%/20%).
But in terms of importance, good programming is absolutely essential, while
people skills are only desirable.  (E.g. given only the choice between a
techie nerd who can program like blazes but must be locked in a cage, or
someone who has brilliant people skills but never saw a computer, which would
you choose to write your software?) ;-)

But for a systems analyst your percentages are much closer. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Please read, I need advice

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** dssmith@NA2.US.ML.COM
- **Date:** 1999-01-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78ak93$cs4$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<01be43fd$3da0fc60$63d1a1d1@johnadam> <36a58004.2750575@news3.ibm.net> <907BE2C0EFD9E0F2.3F7BCCA6DBCD1038.3708BE634485C7AA@library-proxy.airnews.net> <36a63084.88614127@netnews> <36A6AEF5.ADC80DBB@notayahoo.com> <S0Fp2.23$jE1.22046@news4.mia>`

```
Suggestion for two areas of study:

    Server Side: Concentrate on "Component Technology"
                  (Java/Corba - UNIX and/or C++,ATL/MTS/DCOM - NT)
                  XML over HTTP (embedded RPC in XML eliminate Corba/DCOM)
                  SQL, stored procedures and DB transactions
    Client Side: DHTML/DOM and Scripting

All of the above software can be purchased for less than $2000.
You can become an expert!

IBM/CICS/COBOL/BAL/DB2 can only be learned in the workplace.

I have 7 years of big blue experience and 9 years of distributed experience.
Go for the distributed side.  It is much more dynamic and interesting, even
aggravating but never boring.  The mainframe is much more static/stagnant.

Good Luck, Dean Smith



In article <S0Fp2.23$jE1.22046@news4.mia>,

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Please read, I need advice

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36A7396E.11366A3E@home.com>`
- **References:** `<01be43fd$3da0fc60$63d1a1d1@johnadam> <36a58004.2750575@news3.ibm.net> <907BE2C0EFD9E0F2.3F7BCCA6DBCD1038.3708BE634485C7AA@library-proxy.airnews.net> <36a63084.88614127@netnews> <36A6AEF5.ADC80DBB@notayahoo.com>`

```
Jan Lend wrote:
> 
> I think Java should be a top choice - it will teach you the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> conceptual learning.)

I just got my masters with a heavy emphasis on OO.  I just hope I have
the discipline to continue to play with Java at home.  At my age, I
expect that there will be no problem to stay with COBOL until retirement
though, and I prefer COBOL.  But I spent a lot of money and time to
avoid being hurt to badly if I am wrong!

There is an old adage that we should avoid being the first to jump into
a new technology or the last to leave the old technology.  I think this
is 100% wrong for programmers.  If you are the first in or the last to
leave, you will be ahead on the supply curve.  Instead of competing with
a majority of programmers, you will be in demand.  Just make sure you
have your parachutes ready.
```

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

learning assembler at the beginning is going to prevent the person
from  wanting to complete their training. I'd go fo COBOL first, then
C instead of assembler, then databases, then front end languages.

I believe in learning C instead of assembler basically because it is
soooo amazingly close to assembler its unreal, plus its slightly
portable.

C has bit shift functions, pointers, register variables, and inline
macros which I have not found in other languages.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Please read, I need advice

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36AE91CD.3D46CF4D@att.net>`
- **References:** `<01be43fd$3da0fc60$63d1a1d1@johnadam> <36a58004.2750575@news3.ibm.net> <36b2236d.2857857@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <36ad30b7.600224191@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> No one quote me on this.  I'm still trying to hide from it.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> class.  This is NOT necessarily a BAD thing.

I went to work for IBM as an SE in the 60's and the first language we
were taught was Fortran. I asked the instructor why Fortran and was told
they did Fortran first because they could cover enough in one session so
we'd be able to run class problems (after 5:30, of course). I think
Assembler was next. They also covered COBOL (on a 1401!) and PL/I. I'm
sure there are better first languages now, but I still believe their
thinking was sound. Maybe they do REXX first now?

Bill Lynch
```

<a id="t-66ba054685"></a>
## Thread: Please could you help — 7 msgs

### Please could you help

- **From:** "Colin Birch" <colin@birch1959.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<782o7i$ugs$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Please help me write a procedure division or give me any advice for the
following problem
A program iS required to validate batches of transactions that are held on a
disk file Each batch should consist of up to 100 transaction records
followed by a batch control record Each field of the transaction record
needs to be
validated and hash totals of all the numeric fields calculated

If the accumulated hash totals match those of the batch control record and
there are no invalid records and the number of transaction records in the
batch does not exceed 100, then the batch is to be classed as valid All
valid records are to be written to a valid file An erroneous batch is to be
printed out on an error report that contains appropriate headings, with each
batch starting on a new page It is also required that the accumulated hash
totals for that batch are printed after each invalid batch A separate disk
file is to be used as temporary storage of a batch until the validity of the
batch is ascertained
  Thank you

PS. I am new to news groups so I hope I don't upset anyone with my ignorance
```

DD

```
If you're planning on working as a COBOL programmer oneday, this exercise
will be great to develop your skills.
This exercise is not difficult, and you should not be intimidated by it.

The only other advice I can give you is to draw a flowchart first
and then code your program according to the way you designed your flowchart.

Rob Annandale
Vancouver, BC

#### ↳ Re: Please could you help

- **From:** Scott Ramey <scottr@bdssoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36A54E97.FFE6AD86@bdssoftware.com>`

```
You'll probably get a lot of advice on this topic, most of which is not what you want to hear.  Like
one party said, flowchart the problem.  I might add, do some pseudo code in english.  If you cant
scrawl out your program in english you'll never be able to do it in COBOL (or BAL, or C, or RPG, or
anything for that fact).
```

Colin Birch wrote in message <782o7i$ugs$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>...
>Please help me write a procedure division
>This is my second attempt at this problem.       PROCEDURE DIVISION.

       MAIN-CONTROL.
          PERFORM INITIALISE
          PERFORM TRANS-PROCESS UNTIL NO-MORE-RECORDS
          PERFORM TEMP-VALIDATE
          PERFORM CLOSE-DOWN
          STOP RUN.

       INITIALISE.
          OPEN INPUT TRAN-FILE
               OUTPUT TEMP-FILE VALID-FILE PRINT-FILE
          PERFORM HEADINGS.

       TRANS-PROCESS.
          READ TRAN-FILE AT END
             MOVE 'YES' TO EOF
             NOT AT END
             EVALUATE TYPE-T
                WHEN 'T' PERFORM TRANS-PROCESS-2
                WHEN 'B' PERFORM HASH-PROCESS
             END-EVALUATE
          END-READ.
       TRANS-PROCESS-2.
          IF REC-PART NOT NUMERIC
             OR REC-SUP NOT NUMERIC
             OR REC-DATE NOT NUMERIC
             OR REC-QUANT NOT NUMERIC
                MOVE 'Y' TO W-ERROR-FLAG
          ELSE
             ADD 1 TO REC-COUNT
             ADD REC-PART TO W-HASH-PART
             ADD REC-SUP TO W-HASH-SUP
             ADD REC-DATE TO W-HASH-DATE
             ADD REC-QUANT TO W-HASH-QUANT
             WRITE TEMP-REC FROM TRANS-REC
          END-IF.

       HASH-PROCESS.
          IF HASH-PART NOT NUMERIC
            OR HASH-SUP NOT NUMERIC
            OR HASH-DATE NOT NUMERIC
            OR HASH-QUANT NOT NUMERIC
               MOVE 'Y' TO W-ERROR-FLAG
          ELSE
               WRITE TEMP-REC FROM TRANS-REC
          END-IF.

       TEMP-VALIDATE.
          CLOSE TEMP-FILE
          OPEN INPUT TEMP-FILE
             READ TEMP-FILE AT END
             MOVE 'YES' TO EOF
             MOVE 'N' TO W-ERROR-FLAG
             NOT AT END
                IF W-HASH-PART = HASH-PART
                   AND W-HASH-SUP = HASH-SUP
                   AND W-HASH-DATE = HASH-DATE
                   AND W-HASH-QUANT = HASH-QUANT
                   AND REC-COUNT <= 100
                   WRITE VALID-REC FROM TEMP-REC
                   CLOSE TEMP-FILE
                   OPEN OUTPUT TEMP-FILE
                      MOVE ZERO TO HASH-PART
                      MOVE ZERO TO HASH-SUP
                      MOVE ZERO TO HASH-DATE
                      MOVE ZERO TO HASH-QUANT
                ELSE
                   MOVE 'Y' TO W-ERROR-FLAG
                END-IF.

       PRINT-DETAIL.
          IF W-ERROR-FLAG = 'Y'
             OR REC-COUNT > 100
             IF W-DETAIL-LINE NOT = SPACES
                MOVE REC-PART TO INVAL-PART
                MOVE REC-SUP TO INVAL-SUP
                MOVE REC-DATE TO INVAL-DATE
                MOVE REC-QUANT TO INVAL-QUANT
                ADD HASH-PART TO HASH-PART-TOT
                ADD HASH-SUP TO HASH-SUP-TOT
                ADD HASH-DATE TO HASH-DATE-TOT
                ADD HASH-QUANT TO HASH-QUANT-TOT
                WRITE PRINT-REC FROM W-DETAIL-LINE
                WRITE PRINT-REC FROM W-TOT-LINE
             END-IF
          END-IF.

       HEADINGS.
          WRITE PRINT-REC FROM W-HEADING AFTER PAGE
          WRITE PRINT-REC FROM W-HEADINGS-2 AFTER 1.

       CLOSE-DOWN.
          IF REC-COUNT > THAN ZERO
             OR W-ERROR-FLAG = 'Y'
             PERFORM PRINT-DETAIL
          ELSE
             CLOSE TRAN-FILE TEMP-FILE VALID-FILE PRINT-FILE
          END-IF.
I can't work out why HASH-FIELDS holds SPACES in the ADD W-HASH-PART TO
HASH-PART etc.when I put 'VALUE ZERO' in the DATA DIVISION. Any advice on
the logic of the program would be greatly appreciated.

THANKS.
```

+          IF REC-PART NOT NUMERIC

+             OR REC-QUANT NOT NUMERIC

+             ADD REC-QUANT TO W-HASH-QUANT
+             WRITE TEMP-REC FROM TRANS-REC

+            OR HASH-DATE NOT NUMERIC

+                IF W-HASH-PART = HASH-PART
+                   AND W-HASH-SUP = HASH-SUP
+                   AND W-HASH-DATE = HASH-DATE
+                   AND W-HASH-QUANT = HASH-QUANT

+                   CLOSE TEMP-FILE

+                      MOVE ZERO TO HASH-PART

+                WRITE PRINT-REC FROM W-DETAIL-LINE
+                WRITE PRINT-REC FROM W-TOT-LINE

## Thread: COBOL Program To Do Bar Codes On OS2 — 4 msgs

### COBOL Program To Do Bar Codes On OS2

- **From:** dcantua@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7832li$quj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
We are running our system on OS/2 Warp 3, using MicroFocus COBOL 3.2.  And we
need to print out some bar codes on Okidata Okipage 10i laser printer.  We are
having problem getting the printer to translate the characters to a bar code.
Can someone help us out.

Thank You,
Daniel G. Cantua

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

```

> We are running our system on OS/2 Warp 3, using MicroFocus COBOL 3.2.  And we
> need to print out some bar codes on Okidata Okipage 10i laser printer.  We are
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own

 Some OKI printers can print bar codes if you select the right font - I am not
sure if Okipage 10i is this case. If yes, then you only send escape seqence to
switch the printer and it works. If not, it will not be easy...
hope this helps Mirek Chocholous
