<a id="t-412e813a1b"></a>
## Thread: [LONG][Not urgent] Annoying problem regarding invalid keys. — 17 msgs

### [LONG][Not urgent] Annoying problem regarding invalid keys.

- **From:** "kayin/ka khiong kwok" <kayin@mpx.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-01-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<786qd0$1ji$1@news1.mpx.com.au>`

```
Hi, thanks for taking the time out to read this. I'm just wondering if
someone can point out where I went wrong with the code. Sorry about the
space used, it's a tad rude I know.

There's some weird bits and pieces in there that I used to try to debug the
bloody thing. As I noted, it's not urgent cause it's only a pet project that
I'm working on (while I try to find work), so if can help out, many thanks.
The main problem is that I have a PIC 9(03) key field that keeps coming up
with an invalid key error. I'd understand if the problem was that the entry
already exists in the indexed file (Main.dat) but I've written code to read
the file and they're not in there. The code's okay until it reaches a two
digit key field, that is, as the key move from "9" to "10" then the code
start going funny, and refuse to accept "10" as a key. Any help provided
would be appreciated (and I've made a few "no-no"s but I've only really just
begun so bear with me). Again, thanks for your time and patience.

Cheers,

Ka.


==== Code starts here ====

        IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
        PROGRAM-ID. PROCESS-EXERCISE-RECORDS.
      * AUTHOR. KA KHIONG KWOK.
      * DATA SOURCE. EXERCISE.NSF (LOTUS NOTES)
      * PURPOSE. EXPORT DATA FROM NOTES. PERFORM BATCH WORK ON DATA.

      * THINGS TO CONSIDER:
      *                         MULTI PAGE REPORTING.
      *                         PROVIDE OPTION FOR ENTERING FILE NAME.
      *                         FIX UP SOME OF THE SUCKY SECTION DUMMY.

      * ORDER OF PROCESSING.
      * 1) READ EXERCISE TYPE FILE TO DETERMINE WHETHER THERE
      * ARE NEW EXERCISES.
      * 2) PROCESS EXPORTED FILE1.
      * 3) PROCESS EXPORTED FILE2.

        ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
        INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
        FILE-CONTROL.
            SELECT ET-EXERCISE-TYPE ASSIGN TO "EXTYPES.DAT"
              ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
            SELECT EM-EXERCISE-MAIN ASSIGN TO "MAIN.DAT"
              ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
              ACCESS IS SEQUENTIAL
              RECORD KEY IS EM-EXERCISE-KEY.

        DATA DIVISION.
        FILE SECTION.
        FD  ET-EXERCISE-TYPE.
        01  ET-EXERCISE-TYPE-RECORD.
            02      ET-EXERCISE-KEY         PIC X(03).
            02      FILLER                  PIC X(17).
            02      ET-EXERCISE-NAME        PIC X(20).
            02      ET-EXERCISE-COMMENTS    PIC X(20).

        FD  EM-EXERCISE-MAIN.
        01  EM-EXERCISE-MAIN-RECORD.
            02      EM-EXERCISE-KEY         PIC X(03).
            02      EM-EXERCISE-NAME        PIC X(20).
            02      EM-EXERCISE-TALLY       PIC 9(03).

        WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
        01  100-USER-FILE-NAME              PIC X(20).
        01  100-ZEROES-SUPRESSION           PIC ZZ9.
        01  100-TRUE                        PIC X(01) VALUE "T".
        01  100-FALSE                       PIC X(01) VALUE "F".
        01  100-END-OF-FILE-FLAG            PIC X(01).
            88  100-END-OF-FILE             VALUE "T".
        01  100-WRITE-MAIN-RECORD           PIC X(60).
        01  100-COUNT-DELETED-RECORDS       PIC 9(03).
        01  100-COUNT-NUMBER-OF-RECORDS     PIC 9(03).


        PROCEDURE DIVISION.
        100-MAIN.
            MOVE ZEROES TO 100-COUNT-DELETED-RECORDS
            MOVE ZEROES TO 100-COUNT-NUMBER-OF-RECORDS
            PERFORM 200-READ-DEFAULT-FILE
            STOP RUN
            .


        200-READ-DEFAULT-FILE.
            OPEN INPUT ET-EXERCISE-TYPE
            OPEN OUTPUT EM-EXERCISE-MAIN
            PERFORM 300-GET-EXERCISE-RECORD
            PERFORM UNTIL 100-END-OF-FILE
                IF ET-EXERCISE-TYPE-RECORD = SPACES
                  DISPLAY "SPACES"
                ELSE
                  ADD 1 TO 100-COUNT-NUMBER-OF-RECORDS
                  DISPLAY 100-COUNT-NUMBER-OF-RECORDS
                  PERFORM 300-WRITE-MAIN-RECORD
                END-IF
              PERFORM 300-GET-EXERCISE-RECORD
            END-PERFORM
            CLOSE ET-EXERCISE-TYPE
            CLOSE EM-EXERCISE-MAIN
            .


        300-GET-EXERCISE-RECORD.
            MOVE 100-FALSE TO 100-END-OF-FILE-FLAG
            READ ET-EXERCISE-TYPE
              AT END MOVE 100-TRUE TO 100-END-OF-FILE-FLAG
            END-READ
            DISPLAY "=== Start ==="
            DISPLAY ET-EXERCISE-TYPE-RECORD
            .

        300-WRITE-MAIN-RECORD.
            MOVE ET-EXERCISE-KEY TO EM-EXERCISE-KEY
            MOVE ET-EXERCISE-NAME TO EM-EXERCISE-NAME
            MOVE ZEROES TO EM-EXERCISE-TALLY
            DISPLAY "OTHER KEY:" EM-EXERCISE-KEY
            DISPLAY EM-EXERCISE-NAME
            DISPLAY EM-EXERCISE-TALLY
            WRITE EM-EXERCISE-MAIN-RECORD
              INVALID KEY PERFORM 400-DISPLAY-BAD-KEYS
            END-WRITE
            .

        400-DISPLAY-BAD-KEYS.
            DISPLAY "=== bad keys ==="
            DISPLAY ET-EXERCISE-KEY
            DISPLAY EM-EXERCISE-KEY

==== Code ends here ====

       .
```

#### ↳ MOVE 100-TRUE TO 100-END-OF-FILE-FLAG.

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36A737FE.5697F06F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<786qd0$1ji$1@news1.mpx.com.au>`

```
>         01  100-TRUE                        PIC X(01) VALUE "T".
>         01  100-FALSE                       PIC X(01) VALUE "F".
>         01  100-END-OF-FILE-FLAG            PIC X(01).
>             88  100-END-OF-FILE             VALUE "T".

I always hated this construct.  While newer COBOLs allow one to set
100-END-OF-FILE to true making this even worse, but I never cared for
it. 

When you do maintenance, MOVE "T" to 100-END-OF-FILE-FLAG is just as
clear, and we don't have to double check the value of "T".

I can see an argument for using 100-TRUE.  If you try to move 100-YES to
the value, you get a syntax error.   But using the SET command is more
clear and we don't have to even look at working storage when we see the
code.

I just wish we could SET 100-END-OF-FILE TO FALSE before performing
until 100-END-OF-FILE.  But moving space or "F" to 100-END-OF-FILE-FLAG
is clear - I wouldn't double check their values while reading the code!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MOVE 100-TRUE TO 100-END-OF-FILE-FLAG.

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-01-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36a7bc69.242715806@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<786qd0$1ji$1@news1.mpx.com.au> <36A737FE.5697F06F@home.com>`

```
On Thu, 21 Jan 1999 07:21:50 -0700, Howard Brazee
<NOSPAMbrazee@home.com> wrote:

>>         01  100-TRUE                        PIC X(01) VALUE "T".
>>         01  100-FALSE                       PIC X(01) VALUE "F".
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>is clear - I wouldn't double check their values while reading the code!

I think the problem with the "set to false" construct (which IS in the
next standard - so far) is determining what value should be in the
100-End-Of-File-Flag field.

When I want to do what you are talking about I either "Initialize
100-End-Of-File-Flag" or I declare another 88 level - Not-End-Of-File.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Set to false

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36A88BE1.F04F9191@home.com>`
- **References:** `<786qd0$1ji$1@news1.mpx.com.au> <36A737FE.5697F06F@home.com> <36a7bc69.242715806@news1.ibm.net>`

```
> I think the problem with the "set to false" construct (which IS in the
> next standard - so far) is determining what value should be in the
> 100-End-Of-File-Flag field.

Yeah, I had thought about that.  The compiler could check to see if
space is a valid "true", if not, then move space there, if not, try "F",
if that doesn't work, give a syntax error.  I wonder what the next
standard will do.
 
> When I want to do what you are talking about I either "Initialize
> 100-End-Of-File-Flag" or I declare another 88 level - Not-End-Of-File.

The trouble with this is that we STILL have to double check Working
Storage to make sure someone didn't accidently cut and paste the wrong
88 to the wrong switch.

The following will work until someone does some maintenance which resets
SW-EOF-FILE1-SWITCH, then it will break.  You have to read
Working-storage to discover the problem.   A SET TO FALSE would be
simpler.

10   SW-EOF-FILE1-SWITCH      PIC X   VALUE 'F'.
  88 SW-EOF-FILE1                     VALUE 'T'.
10   SW-EOF-FILE2-SWITCH      PIC X   VALUE 'F'.
  88 SW-EOF-FILE2                     VALUE 'T'.
  88 SW-NOT-EOF-FILE1                 VALUE 'F'.

SET SW-NOT-EOF-FILE1  TO TRUE
PERFORM 1000-FILE1-ROUTINE UNTIL SW-EOF-FILE1.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Set to false

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-01-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78a681$db0$1@callisto.clark.net>`
- **References:** `<786qd0$1ji$1@news1.mpx.com.au> <36A737FE.5697F06F@home.com> <36a7bc69.242715806@news1.ibm.net> <36A88BE1.F04F9191@home.com>`

```
In article <36A88BE1.F04F9191@home.com>,
Howard Brazee  <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com> wrote:
>> I think the problem with the "set to false" construct (which IS in the
>> next standard - so far) is determining what value should be in the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

No idea here... consider the following:

01  VSAM-STAT       PIC XX VALUE SPACES.
    88  GOOD-IO     VALUE ZEROES.
    88  END-OF-FILE VALUE '10'.
    88  DUPE-KEY    VALUE '22'.
    88  NOT-FND     VALUE '23'.

... among many others, of course; a well-crafted FILE STATUS copybook is a
joy to behold.

What value would VSAM-STAT contain after a SET END-OF-FILE TO FALSE?

DD
```

```
COBOL Frog (Huib Klink) wrote:

> docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Not '10'.

But serious: The new standard proposes:

88 conditionName VALUE {xxx [THRU yyy]}... WHEN SET TO FALSE zzz

with all known variations of VALUES, THROUGH etc. WHEN, SET and TO are
optional words, FALSE is the only one needed.

So you could code:
    88 END-OF-FILE VALUE '10' FALSE 'DD'. *> :)

The Frog
```

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36AC872F.9972A4B5@home.com>`
- **References:** `<786qd0$1ji$1@news1.mpx.com.au> <36A737FE.5697F06F@home.com> <36a7bc69.242715806@news1.ibm.net> <36A88BE1.F04F9191@home.com> <36a9de63.382507565@news1.ibm.net>`

```
> Why not use my other suggestion:
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Would that Eleviate teh maint  problems?

Looking at this, I can assume that SW-EOF-FILE1 and SW-EOF-FILE-SWITCH
are related.  But we all know the perils of assuming.  

So I have to double check to see if this does what I think it does.

The following doesn't require any assumptions.
INITIALIZE SW-EOF-FILE1 TO TRUE.
PERFORM 1000-FILE1-ROUTINE UNTIL NOT SW-EOF-FILE1.

or possibly (next release)
INITIALIZE SW-EOF-FILE1 TO FALSE.
PERFORM 1000-FILE1-ROUTINE UNTIL SW-EOF-FILE1.
```

#### ↳ Re: [LONG][Not urgent] Annoying problem regarding invalid keys.

This is coming from a mainframe programmer, not a PC guy, but..I
looked at your code and it appears to be okay.  You didn't show your
data files, so I can only guess at this.  Try sorting in ascending
sequence on the key field your input file before running your program.
As I remember, invalid key on a write is triggered by an out of
sequence key or a duplicate key.

HTH

Regards,

On Thu, 21 Jan 1999 20:05:33 +1100, "kayin/ka khiong kwok"
<kayin@mpx.com.au> enlightened us:

>Hi, thanks for taking the time out to read this. I'm just wondering if
>someone can point out where I went wrong with the code. Sorry about the
…[133 more quoted lines elided]…
>

         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

It may be that your sole purpose in life is simply to serve as a warning to 
others.

 Steve

#### ↳ Re: [LONG][Not urgent] Annoying problem regarding invalid keys.

- **From:** veronem@aol.com (Veronem)
- **Date:** 1999-01-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990121152524.04796.00000818@ng-ch1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<786qd0$1ji$1@news1.mpx.com.au>`

```

>Subject: [LONG][Not urgent] Annoying problem regarding invalid keys.
>From: "kayin/ka khiong kwok" <kayin@mpx.com.au>
>Date: 1/21/99 3:05 AM Central Standard Time
>Message-id: <786qd0$1ji$1@news1.mpx.com.au

>        WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
>        01  100-USER-FILE-NAME              PIC X(20).
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Just a couple of suggestions.  I generally use numbers as above to distinguish
paragraphs.  I  tend to prefix my working storage fields with WS (EX:
WS-COUNT-NUMBER-OF-RECORDS), it makes it easier to find the fields and
paragraphs in the code.

>        01  100-TRUE                        PIC X(01) VALUE "T".
>        01  100-FALSE                       PIC X(01) VALUE "F".
>        01  100-END-OF-FILE-FLAG            PIC X(01).
>            88  100-END-OF-FILE             VALUE "T".

If you initialize 100-END-OF-FILE-FLAG in working storage, then you will not
have to move 100-FALSE to it as below . 100-END-OF-FILE-FLAG 's value will not
change again until end of file is reached, so to 
move 100-FALSE to it on every read is redundant.
      01  100-END-OF-FILE-FLAG            PIC X(01)              VALUE "F".
            88  100-END-OF-FILE             VALUE T".

>300-GET-EXERCISE-RECORD.
>            MOVE 100-FALSE TO 100-END-OF-FILE-FLAG
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>            DISPLAY ET-EXERCISE-TYPE-RECORD

I also agree with Howard Brazee it is much easier to read the code when at end
you move "T" instead of true.  When programs become quite lengthy, you don't
want to have to go thru the code over and over to identify what each field is
set to.  This isn't as important in code you write and maintain, because you
will be aware of you own practices.  But in the real world other people will
pick up your code to read and modify and you really don't want to piss off your
fellow workers.  .  Even if you are the only one to look at your code, think of
looking at it 6 months after you wrote and you have no idea what it's supposed
to do.  One simple rule to remember when you are writing code is to make it as
straight forward as possible.

Well so much for my 2 cents.  

As far as you write problem.  You stated that the key is defined as 9(3) in the
file.  In you FD you define it as X(3).  If the field is _10 or 10_ , the
spaces is not numeric.   The file is probably looking for 010.  Check your to
see if key is numeric.   You may have to do some redefines if this is the case.

Hope this helps,
Mary Veronesi
```

## Thread: help in cobol — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### help in cobol

- **From:** "WerewolF" <werewolf@anti-social.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<787t1o$gol$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
Does anybody can help me!
I want know what parameters must I put when calling "winhelp" to make the
entry in a topic of the help.
```

#### ↳ Re: help in cobol

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-01-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36A85895.3E4C704B@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<787t1o$gol$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
WerewolF wrote:

> Does anybody can help me!
> I want know what parameters must I put when calling "winhelp" to make the
> entry in a topic of the help.

The following is from the Win32 SDK online books. You should get that
yourself.
Here I only give plain text by copy/paste, so good luck

<start copy/paste>
The WinHelp function starts Windows Help (WINHELP.EXE) and passes additional
data indicating the nature of the help requested by the application.
BOOL WinHelp(
    HWND hWndMain, // handle of window requesting Help
    LPCTSTR lpszHelp, // address of directory-path string
    UINT uCommand, // type of Help
    DWORD dwData  // additional data
   );

Parameters
hWndMain
Identifies the window requesting Help. The WinHelp function uses this handle
to keep track of which applications have requested Help. If the uCommand
parameter specifies HELP_CONTEXTMENU or HELP_WM_HELP, hWndMain identifies the
control requesting Help.
lpszHelp
Address of a null-terminated string containing the path, if necessary, and
the name of the help file that WinHelp is to display.
The filename may be followed by an angle bracket (>) and the name of a
secondary window if the topic is to be displayed in a secondary window rather
than in the primary window. The name of the secondary window must have been
defined in the [WINDOWS] section of the Help project (.HPJ) file.
uCommand
Specifies the type of help requested. For a list of possible values and how
they affect the value to place in the dwData parameter, see the Remarks
section.
dwData
Specifies additional data. The value used depends on the value of the
uCommand parameter. For a list of possible values, see the Remarks section.

Return Values
If the function succeeds, the return value is nonzero.
If the function fails, the return value is zero.
Remarks
Before closing the window that requested Help, the application must call
WinHelp with the uCommand parameter set to HELP_QUIT. Until all applications
have done this, Windows Help will not terminate. Note that calling Windows
Help with the HELP_QUIT command is not necessary if you used the
HELP_CONTEXTPOPUP command to start Windows Help
The following table shows the possible values for the uCommand parameter and
the corresponding formats of the dwData parameter:
uCommand Action dwData
HELP_COMMAND Executes a Help macro or macro string.  Address of a string that
specifies the name of the Help macro(s) to run. If the string specifies
multiple macro names, the names must be separated by semicolons. You must use
the short form of the macro name for some macros because Windows Help does
not support the long name.
HELP_CONTENTS Displays the topic specified by the Contents option in the
[OPTIONS] section of the .HPJ file. This command is for backward
compatibility. New applications should provide a .CNT file and use the
HELP_FINDER command. Ignored, set to 0.
HELP_CONTEXT Displays the topic identified by the specified context
identifier defined in the [MAP] section of the .HPJ file.  Unsigned long
integer containing the context identifier for the topic.
HELP_CONTEXTMENU Displays the Help menu for the selected window, then
displays the topic for the selected control in a pop-up window.  Address of
an array of double word pairs. The first double word in each pair is a
control identifier, and the second is a context number for a topic.
HELP_CONTEXTPOPUP Displays the topic identified by the specified context
identifier defined in the [MAP] section of the .HPJ file in a pop-up window.
Unsigned long integer containing the context identifier for a topic.
HELP_FINDER Displays the Help Topics dialog box. Ignored; set to 0.
HELP_FORCEFILE Ensures that Windows Help is displaying the correct help file.
If the incorrect help file is being displayed, Windows Help opens the correct
one; otherwise, there is no action.  Ignored, set to 0.
HELP_HELPONHELP Displays help on how to use Windows Help, if the WINHLP32.HLP
file is available. Ignored, set to 0.
HELP_INDEX Displays the topic specified by the CONTENTS option in the
[OPTIONS] section of the .HPJ file. This command is for backward
compatibility. New applications should use the HELP_FINDER command. Ignored,
set to 0.
HELP_KEY Displays the topic in the keyword table that matches the specified
keyword, if there is an exact match. If there is more than one match,
displays the Index with the topics listed in the Topics Found list box.
Address of a keyword string. Multiple keywords must be separated by
semicolons.
HELP_MULTIKEY Displays the topic specified by a keyword in an alternative
keyword table.  Address of a MULTIKEYHELP structure that specifies a table
footnote character and a keyword.
HELP_PARTIALKEY Displays the topic in the keyword table that matches the
specified keyword, if there is an exact match. If there is more than one
match, displays the Topics Found dialog box. To display the Index without
passing a keyword, you should use a pointer to an empty string.  Address of a
keyword string. Multiple keywords must be separated by semicolons.
HELP_QUIT Informs Windows Help that it is no longer needed. If no other
applications have asked for Help, Windows closes Windows Help.  Ignored, set
to 0.
HELP_SETCONTENTS Specifies the Contents topic. Windows Help displays this
topic when the user clicks the Contents button if the help file does not have
an associated .CNT file.  Unsigned long integer containing the context
identifier for the Contents topic.
HELP_SETPOPUP_POS Sets the position of the subsequent pop-up window. Address
of a POINTS structure. The pop-up window is positioned as if the mouse cursor
were at the specified point when the pop-up window is invoked.
HELP_SETWINPOS Displays the Help window, if it is minimized or in memory, and
sets its size and position as specified.  Address of a HELPWININFO structure
that specifies the size and position of either a primary or secondary Help
window.
HELP_TCARD Indicates that a command is for a training card instance of
Windows Help. Combine this command with other commands using the bitwise OR
operator.  Depends on the command with which this command is combined.
HELP_WM_HELP Displays the topic for the control identified by the hWndMain
parameter in a pop-up window. Address of an array of double word pairs. The
first double word in each pair is a control identifier, and the second is a
context identifier for a topic.

See Also
HELPWININFO, MULTIKEYHELP
<end copy/paste>
```

### MVS, JCL, VSAM, and CICS...

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-01-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<786390$tg4@lotho.delphi.com>`

```

I have come to the conclusion that tis is an evil combination
for an ex-UNIX weenie. :) 

Actually, I have little problem using VSAM files with JCL
under MVS, but I do have a problem with the only way I have
figured out to get alternate indexes setup.  

(BTW: I can do this procedure using AMS or File Aid, but
I usually use FIle Aid because it is easier to mess around with
when experimenting....) 

I'm definatley looking for an eaiser way, and some advice on how to get
CICS to behave with alternate indicies around as well. :) 

Step 1. Create a VSAM cluster with a normal key in it.

Step 2. Use a COBOL progra, with no alternate keys in the 
select statement to write a primer record into the file

Step 3. Define the alternate indicies I want

Step 4. Define a path to the alternate indicies I want
   (note: I've been using some descriptive name .AIX for the indicies,
          and the same descriptive name with .PATH for the paths, both
          behind the high level qualifier and vsam cluster name.)

Step 5. Go back and modify the COBOL select to add the alternate keys

Step 6. Go back and modify the allocations in the JCL to 
        include the path for each alternate index (file1, file2, file3,
        etc.) 

Step 7. Go back and build the alternate indicies

Step 8. At last, I can use the cotten pick dataset!

As for using it in CICS, I'm stilla bit baffled. DO I have to 
define each path in the FCT? Or must they be pre-allocated somehow? 

Thanks in advance for any advice. :) 
Yours,
-Paul
```

#### ↳ Re: MVS, JCL, VSAM, and CICS...

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-01-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36a6a061.169993012@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<786390$tg4@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
On 21 Jan 1999 02:26:40 GMT, paulr@bix.com (paulr) wrote:

... proper steps deleted...

There are some variations on those steps allowed (ie you could define
all the alternate indexes before loading the file) - but those steps
have to be done.

>As for using it in CICS, I'm stilla bit baffled. DO I have to 
>define each path in the FCT? Or must they be pre-allocated somehow? 

The alternate indexes look like separate files to CICS - so yes you
have to define them all on the "fct".
```

#### ↳ Re: MVS, JCL, VSAM, and CICS...

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-01-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36a704cb.1394204@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<786390$tg4@lotho.delphi.com>`

```

>As for using it in CICS, I'm stilla bit baffled. DO I have to 
>define each path in the FCT? 

Yes, each path is sen as a separate file in CICS, and needs its own file definition in FCT
or CEDA


BTW, the steps to create a AIX/PATH ar ecorrect, as far as I see it

with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

#### ↳ Re: MVS, JCL, VSAM, and CICS...

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-01-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be4591$36dadc80$43a201c3@vaagshaugen>`
- **References:** `<786390$tg4@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
paulr <paulr@bix.com> wrote in article <786390$tg4@lotho.delphi.com>...
> ...
> Actually, I have little problem using VSAM files with JCL
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> when experimenting....) 
 
The 8-step procedure you describe does the neccessary steps, but you can do
it all with a few AMS commands in a single job step, supposing that you
have the records available in a sequential dataset (sorted on primary key).
 No JCL DD neccessary.

1. DEFINE the base cluster
2. REPRO from the sequential dataset into the base cluster
3. DEFINE the alternate indices and the paths
4. BLDINDEX from the base cluster into each path (or alternate index)

> As for using it in CICS, I'm stilla bit baffled. DO I have to 
> define each path in the FCT? Or must they be pre-allocated somehow? 

After the file has been loaded you can use it with CICS.  Each path is seen
as a separate file by CICS, so each path that you intend to use must be
defined in the FCT.  Otherwise the UPGRADE option will see to that each
index is synchronized as records are written to the base cluster.

Gunnar.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MVS, JCL, VSAM, and CICS...

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<788o9n$hk@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<786390$tg4@lotho.delphi.com> <01be4591$36dadc80$43a201c3@vaagshaugen>`

```
Actually,  I think you could skip all but the original "define" in AMS and
use the COBOL (or LE) run-time option of AIXBLD - described in the LE
manual, thusly

"AIXBLD invokes the access method services (AMS) for VSAM indexed and
relative data sets (KSDS and RRDS) to complete the file and index definition
procedures for COBOL programs. "
```

#### ↳ Re: MVS, JCL, VSAM, and CICS...

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36A7E7EE.F583DA2@att.net>`
- **References:** `<786390$tg4@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
paulr wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> Step 1. Create a VSAM cluster with a normal key in it.
> 
> Step 2. Use a COBOL progra, with no alternate keys in the
> select statement to write a primer record into the file

Use IDCAMS to REPRO a record in (you can use FileAid to create a flat
file with one record and all fields initialized any way you want)

> Step 3. Define the alternate indicies I want

I'd do the BLDINDEXes here (what are you waiting for?)

> Step 4. Define a path to the alternate indicies I want

I don't, and recommend that you don't, define the PATHs here (do them
after the BLDINDEXes)

>    (note: I've been using some descriptive name .AIX for the indicies,
>           and the same descriptive name with .PATH for the paths, both
>           behind the high level qualifier and vsam cluster name.)

I would guess almost all of us do this, too

> Step 5. Go back and modify the COBOL select to add the alternate keys

Now you don't need to modify the program, write it to do what you want
from the beginning. I don't have any COBOL programs involved in
reorganizing or defining VSAM files. You will, too, soon enough.:-)

> Step 6. Go back and modify the allocations in the JCL to
>         include the path for each alternate index (file1, file2, file3, etc.)

What do you mean by "allocations"? And which JCL are we talking about
here?

> Step 7. Go back and build the alternate indicies
> 
> Step 8. At last, I can use the cotten pick dataset!

I'm sure once you get some more experience you'll understand the flow &
streamline things, e.g., develop JCL that will REPRO the data to a flat
file, DELETE & DEFINE the cluster, REPRO the flat file into the new
cluster, define the AIXes, build the AIXes, define the PATHs. You can
put an IEFBR14 step before the REPRO step to delete the previous flat
file (if it exists). If you do this you can have another IEFBR14 as the
last step which deletes the flat file (assuming you want to free the
DASD). The advantage of the first IEFBR14 is that you can always
allocate the flat file (,CATLG) and be confident that it will run.
You're building a rudimentary tool, here.

> As for using it in CICS, I'm stilla bit baffled. DO I have to
> define each path in the FCT? Or must they be pre-allocated somehow?

Each PATH is a CICS file name, so it has to have an FCT entry. Are you
responsible for the JCL for your test region?

Bill Lynch
```

## Thread: Dotyczy: Dlugoterminowej wspólpracy — 5 msgs

### Dotyczy: Dlugoterminowej wspólpracy

- **From:** Juergen Luebeck <jluebeck@berlin.snafu.de>
- **Date:** 1999-01-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<m2zp7c4d9r.fsf@Turing.telecomputer.de>`

```
Szanowni Panstwo,

w celu konserwacji raz rozwoju posiadanego przez nas oprogramowania
szukamy dlugoterminowego partnera do wspólpracy na bazie umowy
projektowej lub w formie Joint-Venture. Firma nasza specializuje sie
wylacznie w systemach dla Urzedów Administracji Panstwowej
(województwa, miasta, gminy) swiadczac od ponad 20 lat swoje uslugi
w zakresie doradztwa, szkolenia, konserwacji oprogramowania, hotline.
Przez ten okres osiagnelismy w paru dziedzinach wiodaca pozycje w
Niemczech.

1. Zakres wspólpracy:

- glówny profil to konserwacja posiadanego oprogramowania
- sporadycznie, tworzenie nowych pojedynczych modulów.

2. Srodowisko programowe, srodowisko pracy.
Systemy napisana sa w jezyku programowania Cobol przy pomocy wlasnych
narzedzi programowych. (Kompilator firmy AcuCorp, USA, narzedzia
programowe to ogólnie uzywane elementy jak: Biblioteki systemowe,
maski ekranów, makro, oraz generator tabel decyzyjnych). Systemem
operacyjnym stosowanym w fazie programowania jest Unix firmy SCO. Nie
stanowi to jednak warunku koniecznego. W terenie instalujemy nasze
systemy praktycznie na wszystkich produkowanych maszynach wlacznie z
Siemensem BS2000 oraz IBM ES390 czyli:

IBM: AS/400, RS6000
SNI/Siemens: wszystkie MX-maszyny oraz RM-maszyny
Bull: Escala-maszyny
DEC: Alpha-maszyny
Olivetti: LSX-maszyny
PC'S: Novell, NT, Win95, Client/Server

Jako bazy danych oferujemy : Isam, Oracle, Informix, MS SQL-Server.

3. Dalsze kroki.

Od 01.02.99 planujemy przeprowadzenie rozmów w Polsce majacych okreslic
charakter wspólpracy, doswiadczenia i mozliwosci partnera, wybór
projektu pilotujacego etc.

Jezeli jestescie Panstwo zainteresowani wspólpraca, prosimy o kontakt
z nasza firma w Köln. Chetnie udzielimy równiez Panstwu wszelkich
dodatkowych informacji.

Telecomputer GmbH
Stolberger Str. 5
50933 Köln
Fon ++49 (02 21) 95 15 04-0
Fax ++49 (02 21) 95 15 04 92

mailto:ikol@telecomputer.de
http://www.telecomputer.de

Z up. Janusz Smiglak
```

## Thread: COBOL prog making a dynamic (non-DLL) call to a c function — 5 msgs

### COBOL prog making a dynamic (non-DLL) call to a c function

- **From:** colin.d.chapman@ac.com
- **Date:** 1999-01-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<787ei4$j4j$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I'm trying to make a dynamic (non-DLL) call from a COBOL module  to a C
module passing (BY REFERENCE) some linkage area that the C module will update
and the results will be seen in the COBOL module.  How do I do this?  The
only way I can get the COBOL module to see the C module is by putting in
main() in the C module.  The problem with this is that main() only accepts
argc and argv as arguments.  I can't "update" these and see the results in
the COBOL module.  If I try and call the C functions directly, my COBOL
program abends with "UNKNOWN MODULE".

I'm using COBOL for OS/390 and C for OS/390.  I have exhaustively gone through
IBM book manager books on ILC, C/C++ for OS/390 and COBOL for OS/390.  They
make it seem so simple.  Are they using DLL examples?  Are they static link
examples?  Is it possible to do this with a dynamic, non-DLL, call?

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL prog making a dynamic (non-DLL) call to a c function

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-01-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be4570$384ff350$43a201c3@vaagshaugen>`
- **References:** `<787ei4$j4j$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36a74cf5.0@news3.ibm.net>`

```
Leif Svalgaard <lsvalg@ibm.net> wrote in article
<36a74cf5.0@news3.ibm.net>...
> sorry that I cannot help here, but the very question is scary:
> DLLs in OS/390??

Yes, it's true!

COBOL support for generating and using dynamic link libraries (DLLs) was
introduced in the latest version, IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM Version 2
Release 1 (Version 1 was called IBM COBOL for MVS & VM).

DLL support is available for applications running under OS/390, in batch,
TSO,  CICS, and IMS environments.  DLL support is not available under
VM/CMS.  The DLL mechanism is common between COBOL, C, and C++.

Refer to COBOL for OS/390 & VM Programming Guide SC26-9049-01.

Gunnar Opheim.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL prog making a dynamic (non-DLL) call to a c function

```
Colin,

Just a thought/suggestion:-

Can you get a static call to work ok?
If so what about "COB-1"    making a dynamic call to "COB-2"
(where COB-2 is simply a small stubb program (no logic in it).
And "COB-2"  has a  static call to "C-Module".

COB-1    ---..>dynamic-call--->>>    (COB-2stub / C-Module)

I don't know what your desired effect is (or problem you want to overcome), maybe
this will give you what you want?

I had an inter-language problem a few months ago and this gave me what I desired.

Good luck
Phil

-------------------------------------------------

<a id="t-82c617fa5a"></a>
## Thread: COBOL for OS/390 calling a C for OS/390 function — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: COBOL for OS/390 calling a C for OS/390 function

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<788e4f$ot@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<787ebs$j1d$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <787fqe$pm8$1@news08.btx.dtag.de> <7880ee$486$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Colin,
  I got some answers (and more questions) from my "usually reliable
sources".  Let me know if this helps.


Bill,

  There must be something wrong with our books, because you can never call a
C subprogram if it has been marked as MAIN.  With C a program is predestined
to be either a SUB or a MAIN, but it can't be both.  I am a little confused
about 'calling a function directly'...does he feel that that is different
from 'making a dynamic (non-DLL) call from a
COBOL module to a C module'?

  ILC works great, and many customers have done it successfully. In fact,
with the newer COBOLs you can make the COBOL program look like a C program
for '#pragma-less ILC'.  Since Colin did not mention #pragma, nor whether he
is using C or C++ (different rules, same compiler) I wonder if he is aware
of the C/C++ differences.  The old way to have a C program mixed with COBOL
(and it still works) is to use '#pragma linkage
COBOL' in the C program, and force the C program to follow 'normal' linkage
rules.
Nowadays there is another choice of using new COBOL language features
(USING BY VALUE, RETURNING, etc) and avoiding the #pragma statements in
the C programs.  Now for C++, (which uses EXTERN statements rather than
#pragma) he could use EXTERN COBOL or the new COBOL features.  In all cases
he has to designate the C or C++ program as a MAIN or SUB, and you can't
call a MAIN
from another program, because then it is a SUB!

  So, the questions are:
  1) C or C++?
  2) #pragma-less or EXTERN-less?
  3) Can Colin get a C program to call a C program successfully?
  4) Does he know about how to get his C function as non-DLL,
      or is he maybe getting DLL behavior accidentally?
  5) If C++, does he know about SOM C++ versus non-SOM C++?
     (Only SOM C++ can work with COBOL)
```

If you're getting funny data, try displaying the record and a
redefinition of the fields you're interested in.  It could be that your
fields are slightly off.  If they get more off as you read new records,
then your record size is wrong.
```

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k) · [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF Cobol 3.4: Any Y2K problems?

- **From:** renfrew@erols.com (Dave Miner)
- **Date:** 1999-01-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7882td$8em$2@winter.news.rcn.net>`

```
     
I would really appreciate hearing from anyone about any known or
suspected Y2K limitations in version 3.4 of the MF cobol compiler.  
My company is planning to stay with this version until sometime 
after the Y2k threshold, but I am deeply concerned about having 
to rely on an unsupported product.  If I could show my management 
any known or substantially potential risks associated with this 
version, then I could probably convince them to bite the bullet 
to upgrade.  


Thanks,

Dave Miner
renfrew@erols.com
```

01 DIV-RESULT          PIC 9(9)V9999.
01 DIV-RED REDEFINES DEV-RESULT.
      03 DIV-WHOLE      PIC 9(9).
      03 DIV-DEV             PIC V9999.


Then in your code:

    COMPUTE DIV-RESULT = SOME-YR / 4.
    IF DIV-DEC NOT = ZERO
   etx
etc

##### ↳ ↳ Re: mod equivalent in cobol

- **From:** dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 1999-01-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78fi8d$178_003@news.netdirect.net>`
- **References:** `<j4Tp2.1911$PD.11130759@news.magma.ca> <36a82258.544306@netnews>`

```
In article <36a82258.544306@netnews>,
   Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart) wrote:
+'Twas Fri, 22 Jan 1999 04:24:47 GMT, when "�" <no@spam.com> illuminated
+comp.lang.cobol thusly:
+
+>Hi, i'm trying to calculate leap years and need to know the " mod "
+>equivalent in COBOL for the remainder.  Any help would be greatly
+>appreciated ;)
+
+COMPUTE REMAINDER = FUNCTION MOD (YYYY, 4)
+
Close, but no cigar. "REMAINDER" is a reserved word, and this won't
compile. Besides, older compilers don't support intrinsic functions. 
This should work on any compiler:

DIVIDE 4 INTO yyyy GIVING xyz REMAINDER re-mainder.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: mod equivalent in cobol

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78d79a$ra8$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<j4Tp2.1911$PD.11130759@news.magma.ca> <36a9dbaf.381814714@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Steve,

    Try this:

    03    WS-HALFWORD    PIC S9(04)     BINARY.
    03    WS-REMAINDER   PIC S9(04)     BINARY.
    03    WS-RESULT           PIC S9(04)     BINARY.
    03    WS-LEAP-IND        PIC    X(01).
            88    WS-LEAP-YR                          VALUE 'Y'.
    03    WS-DATE                PIC    9(07).
    03    WS-DATE-X            REDEFINES WS-DATE.
            05    WS-DATE-CCYY
                                                PIC   9(04).
            05    FILLER              REDEFINES WS-DATE-CCYY.
                    07      FILLER    PIC    9(02).
                    07      WS-DATE-YY
                                                PIC    9(02).
            05    FILLER              PIC    9(03).

    MOVE 1999023                       TO   WS-DATE.
    MOVE WS-DATE-CCYY        TO    WS-HALFWORD.
    MOVE SPACE                        TO    WS-LEAP-IND.
    DIVIDE    WS-HALFWORD    BY    +400
                                                        GIVING    WS-RESULT
                                                        REMAINDER
WS-REMAINDER.

    IF    WS-REMAINDER IS NOT EQUAL TO ZERO
            MOVE    WS-DATE-YY    TO    WS-HALFWORD
            DIVIDE    WS-HALFWORD    BY +4
                                                        GIVING    WS-RESULT
                                                        REMAINDER
WS-REMAINDER
            END-IF.

    IF    WS-REMAINDER IS EQUAL TO ZERO
            SET    WS-LEAP-YR       TO TRUE
            END-IF.

===============================================================

The first DIVIDE determines CCYY as a leap-year (like 2000). Note that CCYY
leap-year compliance occurs only every 400 years. If the remainder is not
zero, then issue the DIVIDE again, using only the YY as the divisor. YY
compliant leap-years for the 21st century begins with 2000 and progress
forward every 4 years, Hence, the need for the two DIVIDES.

BTW, I prefer using BINARY when performing this type of calculation. You
could substitute PACKED-DECIMAL or DISPLAY. It's your call.

HTH....

WOB,
Atlanta

>>Hi, i'm trying to calculate leap years and need to know the " mod "
>>equivalent in COBOL for the remainder.  Any help would be greatly
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: mod equivalent in cobol

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-01-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be47d6$765aace0$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`
- **References:** `<j4Tp2.1911$PD.11130759@news.magma.ca> <36a9dbaf.381814714@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell <redsky@ibm.net> wrote in article
<36a9dbaf.381814714@news1.ibm.net>...
> >Hi, i'm trying to calculate leap years and need to know the " mod "
> You probably really want the remaider and not the MOD.

And what is the difference in this context?
For positive years - none.
For negative years (BC), try -5 as an example:
FUNCTION REM: -1, i.e. "one year before leap year"
FUNCTION MOD: +3, i.e. "three years after leap year"
which is effectively the same.

Gunnar.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: mod equivalent in cobol

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)

- **Message-ID:** `<36aba85e.499767707@news1.ibm.net>`

and they are different.  If one starts using them interchangably, the
proper meaning may become lost.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: mod equivalent in cobol

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36ad291e.598279063@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<j4Tp2.1911$PD.11130759@news.magma.ca> <36a9dbaf.381814714@news1.ibm.net> <01be47d6$765aace0$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <36aba85e.499767707@news1.ibm.net> <01be4887$46fdb710$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`

```
On 25 Jan 1999 18:25:07 GMT, "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
wrote:


>Just out of curiosity: what is your argument for "the test calls for
>remainder and not mod"?

I'm on relatively weak ground -- However, 

Leap years are those whos numbers are evenly divisible by 4 and NOT
evenly divisible by 100 except when they are evenly divisible by 400.
By divisible we mean the division leaves no REMainder.  That's the
basic reason.  The second is the general misunderstanding that is
propogated by use of MOD instead of remainder.  Yes MOD and REM give
the same result most of the time, but the MOD (Modulus) of two numbers
is entirely different than the REM of the specified division.

Modulus:  Same as Absolute Value: for a complex number, computed by
adding the squares of each part and taking the positive square root of
each sum.  (Ex The Modulus of a + bi = The Square root of (a squared +
b squared).  Second meaning:  a given quantity wich gives the same
remainder when it is the divisor of two quantities (How it is used in
Function MOD).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: mod equivalent in cobol

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)

- **Message-ID:** `<36ad9756.102986297@netnews>`

'Twas Tue, 26 Jan 1999 04:44:51 GMT, when redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>Yes MOD and REM give
>the same result most of the time, but the MOD (Modulus) of two numbers
>is entirely different than the REM of the specified division.

Someone please correct me if I'm wrong, but I think they're only the same
when the numerator is positive, because it depends on the signs.  If both
numbers are positive, MOD and REM are the same.  The sign of MOD is always
the sign of the divisor.  The sign of REM is the product of the two signs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: mod equivalent in cobol

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-01-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36b75b5b.87170454@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<j4Tp2.1911$PD.11130759@news.magma.ca> <36a9dbaf.381814714@news1.ibm.net> <01be47d6$765aace0$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <36aba85e.499767707@news1.ibm.net> <01be4887$46fdb710$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <36ad291e.598279063@news1.ibm.net>`

```
On Tue, 26 Jan 1999 04:44:51 GMT, redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:


>
>Modulus:  Same as Absolute Value: for a complex number, computed by
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Function MOD).


????? Could you say that again ??????

According to my sources (whixh is me, but I have to admit that it is about 20 years ago
since I studied mathematics) the MODULUS m of a number N to the reference k is the
equivalence set of all numbers m where N = k*x + m for some integer x.  Usually, all the
elements in this equivalence set are "equated" to the smallest, nonnegative number.

Example:  The modulus of 12 to the reference number 5 is the following set: 
{...,-12,-8,-3,2,7,12,17,....}, or

2 = MOD(12,5)
-3 = MOD(12,5) as well

The ABSOLUTE VALUE of a COMPLEX (or REAL) number has nothing to do with the MODulus

(at least I firmly believe in what I said above, but haven't really taken any effort to
dig out my old math books to verify <s>)


with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: mod equivalent in cobol

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "kennet" <kennet@post11.tele.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<790soe$4cf8$1@news-inn.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<j4Tp2.1911$PD.11130759@news.magma.ca> <36a9dbaf.381814714@news1.ibm.net> <01be47d6$765aace0$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <36aba85e.499767707@news1.ibm.net> <01be4887$46fdb710$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <36ad291e.598279063@news1.ibm.net> <36b75b5b.87170454@news3.ibm.net> <36b3a563.1023371559@news1.ibm.net>`

```

Thane Hubbell skrev i meddelelsen <36b3a563.1023371559@news1.ibm.net>...
>On Sat, 30 Jan 1999 20:37:25 GMT, vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
>wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>I got it from Websters! <G>

As with many words "modulus" has many meanings in mathematics. For a complex
number z=a+ib it is true that mod(z) = squareroot(a*a + b*b). So for a real
number a we have mod(a)=squareroot(a*a) which is equal to the absolute value
of a.

However, modulus also has a different meaning for integers, and this is the
following: for integers a and b,

a mod b

(also written "mod(a b)") is the integer c in the interval 0,1,2,...,b-1
such that there exists another integer d, so that a = d *b + c. So it is a
bit like remainder. For positve numbers, it is precisely the same as
remainder.
This is the usual meaning when you use the function mod in any computer
language (that I know).

This is probably not very clear, but the point is this:
Websters is correct, it's just reffering to another meaning of the word
"modulus".

Regards,
Kennet
```

Thane Hubbell <redsky@ibm.net> wrote in article
<36ad291e.598279063@news1.ibm.net>...
> On 25 Jan 1999 18:25:07 GMT, "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Function MOD).

I am aware of those two definitions, and even a third one concerning math:
the number by which a logarithm to one base is multiplied to give the
corresponding logarithm to another base.

However the MOD function as defined in COBOL bears a strong resemblance to
REM as far as positive numbers are involved.  And that was the original
theme of this thread.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: mod equivalent in cobol

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1999-01-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1999Jan24.214510.19488@giant>`
- **References:** `<j4Tp2.1911$PD.11130759@news.magma.ca> <01be47d6$765aace0$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`

```
In article <01be47d6$765aace0$0100007f@vaagshaugen>,
"Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no> wrote:
>
> In article <36a9dbaf.381814714@news1.ibm.net>,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> For negative years (BC), try -5 as an example:

Please note that positive years BC and negative years AD are *NOT* the
same thing because of the discontinuity caused by the fact that there is
no year zero.  The year I BC is immediately followed by the year AD I. 
The leap year V BC is AD -IV, and conversely the leap year AD IV is -V
BC.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: mod equivalent in cobol

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: mod equivalent in cobol

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-01-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be488c$9348b6b0$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`
- **References:** `<j4Tp2.1911$PD.11130759@news.magma.ca> <01be47d6$765aace0$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <1999Jan24.214510.19488@giant> <78h38o$ne5$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote in article
<78h38o$ne5$1@news.igs.net>...
> I was always under the impression that there were no
> leap years 2000 years ago.

Sorry that I have to disappoint you.  The Julian calendar was introduced in
the year 46 BC in the Roman Empire and included the use of leap years.  The
year 46 BC was called the 'annus confusionis' since it lasted 445 days to
make up for errors made since year 153 BC when Jan 1. was introduced as the
start of the year.  The first year after this, 45 BC, was a leap year, and
every 4th year after that is a leap year.  Until the Gregorian calendar
revised the Julian in 1582 and introduced the additional rule that even
hundred years should be divisable by 400 to be a leap year.  To account for
the errors so far, Oct 5. to 10. was skipped that year.  This goes for the
Roman Empire, other countries switched to the Gregorian calendar much
later: in my country, Norway, 1700, in England 1752, Sweden 1753, Russia
after the revolution 1917, to mention a few.  Some countries still use the
Julian calendar, and others use quite different calendars.

OB.Cobol: If you are confident that your programs won't ever have to
support the year 2100 (or 1900), you can safely forget Gregorius' rule and
program leap year as the years divisable by four.

Gunnar Opheim.
```

#### ↳ Re: mod equivalent in cobol

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YwTq2.926$fz.1793467@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<j4Tp2.1911$PD.11130759@news.magma.ca>`

```
>Hi, i'm trying to calculate leap years and need to know the " mod "
>equivalent in COBOL for the remainder.  Any help would be greatly
>appreciated ;)


First to calculate a leap year lets explain a little. A leap year is any
year divisible by 4 and not divisible by 100. if divisible by 100, must be
divisible by 400. Thus:

       01  LEAP-YEAR-VARS.
           05 LY-YEAR PIC 9(04).
           05 FILLER REDEFINES LY-YEAR.
              10 LY-CC PIC 9(02).
              10 LY-YY PIC 9(02).
           05 CC-MOD-4 PIC 9(02).
           05 YY-MOD-4 PIC 9(02).
           05 TEMP-Q   PIC 9(02).
           05 LEAP-YEAR-STATUS PIC X(01) VALUE 'N'.
              88 NOT-LEAP-YEAR VALUE 'N'.
              88 IS-LEAP-YEAR  VALUE 'Y'.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       .......
           MOVE GIVEN-YEAR TO LY-YEAR
           DIVIDE LY-CC BY 4 GIVING TEMP-Q REMAINDER CC-MOD4
           DIVIDE LY-YY BY 4 GIVING TEMP-Q REMAINDER YY-MOD4
           IF LY-YY = 00 THEN
              IF CC-MOD-4 = 00 THEN
                 SET IS-LEAP-YEAR TO TRUE
              ELSE
                 SET NOT-LEAP-YEAR TO TRUE
              END-IF
            ELSE
              IF YY-MOD-4 = 00 THEN
                 SET IS-LEAP-YEAR TO TRUE
              ELSE
                 SET NOT-LEAP-YEAR TO TRUE
              END-IF
            END-IF
            .

Now if you want to do something like making an array from 1 to 100 and have
the values in the array determine if it is a leap year you could eliminate
the computation and if logic:

       01  LEAP-YEAR-DATA.
      *                               00000000001111111111
      *                               01234567890123456789
           05 FILLER PIC X(00) VALUE '10001000100010001000'.
           05 FILLER PIC X(00) VALUE '10001000100010001000'.
           05 FILLER PIC X(00) VALUE '10001000100010001000'.
           05 FILLER PIC X(00) VALUE '10001000100010001000'.
           05 FILLER PIC X(00) VALUE '10001000100010001000'.
       01  FILLER REDEFINES LEAP-YEAR-DATA.
           05 FILLER OCCURS 100 TIMES.
              10 FILLER PIC X(01).
                 88 IS-IDX-LEAP-YEAR VALUE '1'.

Then using the previous example:
           MOVE GIVEN-YEAR TO LY-YEAR
           SET NOT-LEAP-YEAR TO TRUE
           IF IS-IDX-LEAP-YEAR (LY-CC + 1) THEN
              SET IS-LEAP-YEAR TO TRUE
           ELSE
              IF IS-IDX-LEAP-YEAR (LY-YY + 1) THEN
                 SET IS-LEAP-YEAR TO TRUE
              END-IF
           END-IF
           .

There may even be a simpler way to do this, but I can't think f it at the
time.
```

---

## Thread: How to ACCEPT a signed float numeral — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to ACCEPT a signed float numeral

- **From:** panlinda@hotmail.com
- **Date:** 1999-01-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78a2dm$rte$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Hi, pals,

I am a beginer on COBOL. I wrote an exercise as follows. I want to
use ACCEPT statement to accept a numeral from terminal or console.
The numeral format is S9(3)V99. But each time the program recieved
the data as a string, not a numeral as I need. For example, I want
the program read +26.68, but it read as '+26.6'. So experts, please
tell me how to define this kind of numeric format. Please write to
me. Thank you.

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. EX-DATA2.
       AUTHOR. XXXX.
       INSTALLATION. XXXXX.
       DATE-WRITTEN. 01/22/1999.
       DATE-COMPILED. 01/22/1999.
       SECURITY. PUBLIC.

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       SOURCE-COMPUTER. XXXXX.
       OBJECT-COMPUTER. XXXXX.
      *
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       77   A       PIC S9(3)V99.
       ...
       COMMON-STORAGE SECTION.
       ...
      *
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       S1.  DISPLAY 'PLEASE INPUT A DATA TYPED S9(3)V99: '
             UPON CONSOLE.
            ACCEPT A FROM CONSOLE.
            DISPLAY 'A=', A UPON CONSOLE.
      * ...
            STOP RUN.

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: How to ACCEPT a signed float numeral

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-01-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36A8C898.C15A940D@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<78a2dm$rte$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I'll give you ea break:

1st - you need to know how S999V99 is stored in internal memory

2nd - you need to ensure that a conforming bit pattern is entered on the
screen to ACCEPT it unconverted into the variable
OR
2nd - you need to accept another format (f.e. a more or less user friendly
format that allows for signs and thouands separators (i.e. commas) anf
decimal points (dot) a.s.o) and convert that to S999V99. This needs checks
for valid data of course.

Why step 1?
Because to understand the need for choosing the 2nd 2nd option mentioned
above.

Why choose the convert step and not the direct step as 2nd?
Because that is really user friendly and as such the only ACCEPTable way to
design an interface. And because that method is the one that checks for
errors.

On most platforms the sign of a S9(n) field is stored along with one of the
digits in one machine position (byte). The resulting bit pattern of that
combined digit and sign should be entered when it is unconvertedly moved
(ACCEPTed) into the variable and thus hope that the wanted value for that
digit plus sign as a one-character bit pattern is available on your
keyboard. In EBCDIC for example the characters A thru I are availabel for
+1 thru +9 and J thru R for their negative counterparts. A 0 (zero digit)
with + of - sign is then a { or } respectively. Up till now I never saw a
user document that correctly, understandably and user friendly explained
these matters to a non-computer-technics-trained end-user.

So go for option 2 and desig some program to ACCEPT anPICxxxxx field long
enough to contain a lot of digits and character dots, commas and +/-
characters. Let that program allow and check for a valid human readable
numeric value give some nice error texts when not valid and convert to
internally stored numeric when right. That kind of code has been made many
times by others, but is a great exercise for you.

Perhaps if you ask him kindly, comp.lang.cobol contributor Judson McClendon
will eventually give you such code. I believe he has some reusable stuff
for that, if I recall my memories well.

Good programming!

The Frog

P.S: COMMON-STORAGE? That's non-standard. What compiler do you use?

panlinda@hotmail.com wrote:

> Hi, pals,
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> me. Thank you.

8<

>        DATA DIVISION.
>        WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>              UPON CONSOLE.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to ACCEPT a signed float numeral

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-01-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36A8C90B.A13CA0E4@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<78a2dm$rte$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36A8C898.C15A940D@IMN.nl>`

```
B.T.W:

S999V99 is NOT float but fixed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to ACCEPT a signed float numeral

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78ljc1$4e9@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<78a2dm$rte$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36A8C898.C15A940D@IMN.nl> <78li6j$iab$1@spock.tinet.ie>`

```
Simon,
  I really do NOT mean to discourage you from using this newsgroup. You have
shown what you coded and explained your confusion.  What I would suggest is
that you check DejaNews.  This very question is asked (and answered) about
once a month.  I think the latest answer was about a week ago.

Bottom-Line:

   ACCEPT input and DISPLAY output fields are almost alphanumeric or
possibly numeric-edited.  They are almost never "numeric"

  You need to "evaluate" the field that comes in to determine what sign
information is included and what space padding exists at the beginning and
end of the input.  (Periods are always "explicit"  PIC "." and not "implicit
PIC "v".

Do check your input for the possibility of non-numeric data.
```

```
I'm writing a COBOL CICS program that accepts dates from the user in
mm-dd-yyyy format.  Is there a way to code a function or CALL statement to
validate the input string as a valid date (i.e. 02-31-2000 or 14-22-1999

#### ↳ Re: Do I have a valid date?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
I haven't used it AND it requires the "latest and greatest" IBM compiler
INCLUDING the MLE (Millennium Language Extensions) - but there is an IBM
extension of a
   DATEVAL (similar to the Standard NumVal function)
intrinsic function that will do exactly this.  I suggest that you check to
see if it is available at your shop - and in your environment.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Do I have a valid date?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36b11c88.857239741@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<78a4p6$u2v$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <78a88u$o3@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Here's another way (Ansi 85 COBOL, with '89 extensions) to validate a
date.

The Working-Storage.

01  Date-Work-Area.
      03  Date-To-Check.
            05  D-Year Pic 9(4).
            05  D-Month Pic 99.
            05  D-Day Pic 99.
      03  Date-To-Check-N Redefines Date-To-Check Pic 9(8).
      03  Rem-1 Pic 9(4).
      03  Valid-Flag Pic X value Spaces.
            88  Date-Invalid Value "I".

The Procedure Division.

Move Space to Valid-Flag
Evaluate D-Month Also D-Day
   When 1 Also 1 thru 31
   When 3 Also 1 thru 31
   When 4 Also 1 thru 30
   When 5 Also 1 Thru 31
   When 6 Also 1 thru 30
   When 7 Thru 8 Also 1 Thru 31
   When 9 Also 1 thru 30
   When 10 Also 1 thru 31 
   When 11 Also 1 thru 30
   When 12 Also 1 thru 30
       Continue
   When 2 Also 1 thru 29
       If D-Day = 29
          Compute Rem-1 = Function Rem (Date-To-Check 4)
          If Rem-1 = Zeros 
             Compute Rem-1 = Function Rem (Date-To-Check 100)
             If Rem-1 = Zeros
                Compute Rem-1 = Function Rem (Date-To-Check 400)
                If Rem-1 = Zeros
                   Continue
                Else
                   Set Invalid-Date to True
                End-if
             End-if 
          Else
             Set Date-Invalid to True
          End-if
       End-if
       Continue
   When Other
       Set Date-Invalid To True
End-Evaluate
.

Short and sweet. <G>  (But you do need ANSI 85 COBOL with the 89
extensions).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Do I have a valid date?

- **From:** NoLonger@ix.netcom.com (OldUncleMe)
- **Date:** 1999-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36b1cfc3.3020035@news>`
- **References:** `<78a4p6$u2v$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <78a88u$o3@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <36b11c88.857239741@news1.ibm.net>`

```
On Fri, 29 Jan 1999 02:40:38 GMT "redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)" posted to
"comp.lang.cobol" about: "Re: Do I have a valid date?" :

-->Here's another way (Ansi 85 COBOL, with '89 extensions) to validate a
-->date.
-->
-->The Working-Storage.
-->
-->01  Date-Work-Area.
-->      03  Date-To-Check.
-->            05  D-Year Pic 9(4).
-->            05  D-Month Pic 99.
-->            05  D-Day Pic 99.
-->      03  Date-To-Check-N Redefines Date-To-Check Pic 9(8).
-->      03  Rem-1 Pic 9(4).
-->      03  Valid-Flag Pic X value Spaces.
-->            88  Date-Invalid Value "I".
-->
-->The Procedure Division.
-->
-->Move Space to Valid-Flag
-->Evaluate D-Month Also D-Day
-->   When 1 Also 1 thru 31
-->   When 3 Also 1 thru 31
-->   When 4 Also 1 thru 30
-->   When 5 Also 1 Thru 31
-->   When 6 Also 1 thru 30
-->   When 7 Thru 8 Also 1 Thru 31
-->   When 9 Also 1 thru 30
-->   When 10 Also 1 thru 31 
-->   When 11 Also 1 thru 30
-->   When 12 Also 1 thru 30
-->       Continue
-->   When 2 Also 1 thru 29
-->       If D-Day = 29
-->          Compute Rem-1 = Function Rem (Date-To-Check 4)
-->          If Rem-1 = Zeros 
-->             Compute Rem-1 = Function Rem (Date-To-Check 100)
-->             If Rem-1 = Zeros
-->                Compute Rem-1 = Function Rem (Date-To-Check 400)
-->                If Rem-1 = Zeros
-->                   Continue
-->                Else
-->                   Set Invalid-Date to True
-->                End-if
-->             End-if 
-->          Else
-->             Set Date-Invalid to True
-->          End-if
-->       End-if
-->       Continue
-->   When Other
-->       Set Date-Invalid To True
-->End-Evaluate
-->.
-->
-->Short and sweet. <G>  (But you do need ANSI 85 COBOL with the 89
-->extensions).  

That is succinct.  I'm curious tho, what part of the sample depends on '89
extensions?  Every thing I could think of to look up is in my references, which I
believe are '85 standard; not sure about the Fujitsu Language Reference--which lists
the CONTINUEstatement where our text, "Cobol from Micro to Mainframe" has it in the
language reference, but has no explanation or index entry (typical for this book....)
Thanks, TS



"Come to think of it, there are already a million monkeys on a million
 typewriters, and Usenet is NOTHING like Shakespeare." Blair Houghton
*********No generalization is wholly true, not even this one**********
++++++****tauceti****@****abraxis****.****com****for***email****++++++
```

<a id="t-e8805019af"></a>
## Thread: Cobol calling PL1 — 8 msgs

### Cobol calling PL1

- **From:** "Fabricio Ortiz Jr" <fortiz@us.ibm.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36a9446c.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Hi,

    Can anybody post an example of a Cobol program calling a PL1 program?
I'm able to do that but performance wise the program takes too long loading
and unloading the PL1 modules. Is there any way to get around this?

Thanks,
Fabricio
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol calling PL1

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78bp1q$njj@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36a9446c.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
What version and release of PL/I and COBOL are you using - and are you using
LE as your run-time library.  (All of this assuming you are talking about an
IBM mainframe environment).

There were issues about applying later (about 1992) maintenance to Pl/I V1.5
(as I recall) to insure that the correct version of ILBOSTP0 was used by the
PL/I run-time software.  If you are using the LE run-time library, then this
shouldn't be your problem - but other issues might be relevant.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol calling PL1

- **From:** "Fabricio Ortiz Jr" <fortiz@usa.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VNnq2.1415$If7.2744344@newshog.newsread.com>`
- **References:** `<36a9446c.0@news1.ibm.net> <78bp1q$njj@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`

```
I'm running this on an IBM mainframe environmente I will check both versions
and post it here later... I assume thay are very current.
Both programs are working but my problem is that this COBOL program is
calling 5 PL1 programs for each record it processes. This call is taking
alot of time. I ran a performance check on the programs and it showed 95
percent used in WAIT time for these modules. So what I want to know is if
there is a way to make these PLI libraries/programs resident.

Thanks
William M. Klein wrote in message <78bp1q$njj@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>...

using

an

V1.5

the
>PL/I run-time software.  If you are using the LE run-time library, then
this
>shouldn't be your problem - but other issues might be relevant.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

loading
>>and unloading the PL1 modules. Is there any way to get around this?
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol calling PL1

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78d97t$5fo@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36a9446c.0@news1.ibm.net> <78bp1q$njj@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <VNnq2.1415$If7.2744344@newshog.newsread.com>`

```
Sorry, if I wasn't clear(er) in my earlier post.  The issues related to
maintenance and releases DIRECTLY ties into the speed of switching from
COBOL to PL/I "environments".  It is entirely possible that what is
happening is that both function correctly - but that each time a PL/I
subroutine is called a "new" environment needs to be built (and subsequently
torn down).  This is a known type of problem and can be related to solutions
that IBM provided at later releases.

Question:  Does your shop have Omagemon or some other analyzer tool?  If so,
you (or your systems people) may want to run a test to determine exactly
WHAT routine is in a wait state.  (This may have been what you were
referring to as a "performance check".)  If the routine's name starts with
ILBO or IGZ, then it is probably a COBOL routine; if it starts IBM, then it
is a PL/I routine; if it starts CEE, then it is an LE routine; and if starts
with something else - then it is probably an in-house routine.

Please do keep the newsgroup informed if you find the origin of the problem.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol calling PL1

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Fabricio Ortiz Jr" <fortiz@usa.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IrIq2.208$Ai.524130@newshog.newsread.com>`
- **References:** `<36a9446c.0@news1.ibm.net> <78bp1q$njj@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <VNnq2.1415$If7.2744344@newshog.newsread.com> <78d97t$5fo@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`

```

JFBUCAL  .PL/ILIB         IBMBIEP1 INTERLANGUAGE HOUSEKEEP   000000   4.00  4.00

JFBU9AL                                                      000680   2.00  2.00
JFBUAAL                                                      000200   2.00  2.00

William M. Klein wrote in message <78d97t$5fo@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>...

>maintenance and releases DIRECTLY ties into the speed of switching from
…[67 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol calling PL1

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78ga82$oj0@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36a9446c.0@news1.ibm.net> <78bp1q$njj@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <VNnq2.1415$If7.2744344@newshog.newsread.com> <78d97t$5fo@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <IrIq2.208$Ai.524130@newshog.newsread.com>`

```
OK,  I think this helps.

One person has already suggested that you put a PL/I "stub" at the top of your application (in other words, a PL/I that does NOTHING but call your COBOL driver).  This might help.

Also, from the fact that all your ILC (Inter-Language CALL) libraries seem to be "IBMxxx" routines, I would GUESS that you don't have LE installed.  Please do follow up on my earlier suggestion and find out what release and version you have available at your shop for
   COBOL
   PL/I
   LE

It may be that simply by STEPLIBing to a newer run-time library, your performance problems will go away.  If not, a recompile (of the PL/I or the COBOL or both) will solve the problem.

Finally,  I know that each of the COBOL programming guides from the mid-80's on has included a section on ILC.  I am fairly certain that the PL/I manuals do to.  Once you know what compiler(s) you are using, check the relevant manuals for a topic on "creating and maintaining a PL/I environment".  (I know where the documentation is on creating a COBOL environment to maintain across returns to PL/I - but I don't know where the documentation is on maintaining the PL/I environment - which seems to be what you need.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol calling PL1

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "John Melcher, Jr." <John_Melcher_Jr@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36ad25aa.0@news.ic.net>`
- **References:** `<36a9446c.0@news1.ibm.net> <78bp1q$njj@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <VNnq2.1415$If7.2744344@newshog.newsread.com> <78d97t$5fo@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <IrIq2.208$Ai.524130@newshog.newsread.com>`

```
The ugly solution to this is to write a PL/I main program which calls your
COBOL program (and passes the PARM field, if applicable). This will cause
COBOL to run "in the PL/I environment", and dispense with all of the PL/I
environment setup & teardown.

#### ↳ Re: Help

- **From:** JeffreyFarkas@earthlink.net (Jeff Farkas)
- **Date:** 1999-01-22T00:00:00

In article <78docq$a5j$1@news5.svr.pol.co.uk>, 
colin@birch1959.freeserve.co.uk says...
> I have put  a problem on this newsgroup and all I have got is abuse. We all
> had to start somewhere.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Hi Colin,

    Well, if I recall it seems that members of this 
group  did not think that you had put in an adequate 
amount of effort into the problem before you posted a 
question to this group. The rule of thumb is the more 
effort that you put into a problem before you post a 
question to this group the more likely it is that you 
will get some help. And of course the opposite is also 
true, no effort on your part will result in no help from 
the group. 

    If you don't belive me then just check out the FAQ 
and you should find a similar answer there as well.

    I am sure that if you put a little more effort into 
the problem in question and then re-post any questions 
you may have I think you may receive better response.



Jeff

Jeffreyfarkas@earthlink.net
```

#### ↳ Re: Help

- **From:** Jan <jan@no.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36AA7A9A.37B06EB8@no.com>`
- **References:** `<78docq$a5j$1@news5.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
You presented a program and did not show that you had attempted to
compile it, nor state what the problem, if any, might particularly be. 
Do you expect that everyone else is going to attempt to do with your
program what you apparently have not?

My suggestion, which I thought helpful, was to have separate EOF flags
for each file, so that checking the EOF flag will actually let you know
just which file it is that has been read completely.  

So go and compile your program; fix the syntax errors (or ask here for
interpretation of the reported errors); then run the program and come
back with your results. 

Everyone has to start somewhere, true, but they have to start !!

Hang in there, it will begin to make more sense.

Jan

Colin Birch wrote:
> 
> I have put  a problem on this newsgroup and all I have got is abuse. We all
> had to start somewhere.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol

The corrected information that I received from Micro Focus follows:

"Actually, we (Micro Focus) don't have anything on the web site regarding
the Visual COBOL product line (we have some in the works, however).  The
sales and support of the product is handled out of New Hampshire.  The Sales
number is 1-800-231-6342 and the Support number is 1-800-447-4627.

As to the Y2K issue, there are new versions of the products that are fully
Y2K compliant, which are available as upgrades to the previous product
versions.

For Europe, Sales and Support of the Visual COBOL product line is handled
out of the Dortmund Germany office - +49 231 75850"

 ***

Sorry for my original misdirection
```

I know nothing about security access codes.
Can anyone tell me about the following.
What do they mean, and how does on achieve that particular clearance.
I found these access codes on a Canadian Recruiters form.

The job applicant was to choose his security access level from one of the
following.

- Top Secret
- ISSA
- DISCO
- TS/SBI
- Polygraph
- Secret
- TS/SCI
______________________________________________
Robert B. Annandale

"Love all, trust a few and do wrong to none..."
- William Shakespeare

There is no single terminology.
The military used to (when I was in it) have the following levels:
unrestricted
restricted
confidential
secret
top secret
cosmic


Getting a high clearance takes up to a year and may cost $100,000

<a id="t-ba64a12ba5"></a>
## Thread: NetExpress Ver 2.0 to 3.0 upgrade problem — 1 msgs

### NetExpress Ver 2.0 to 3.0 upgrade problem

- **From:** vgibson@westga.edu (Vince Gibson)
- **Date:** 1999-01-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36a89308.10015221@news.westga.edu>`

```
I have received and installed NetExpress V3.0 on my development
machine (In addition to V2.0. )

I opened and recompiled all of my GUI application programs developed
with 2.0, in version 3.0.

I then installed the ver 3.0 runtime on a client machine.

I moved copies of my (newly recompiled under 3.0) applications to the
server.

I created a shortcut to my main program on the client and called it�It
would not work.

I then copied all of the runtime files from the client machine into
the application directory on the server where the called .EXE's and
GS's are located. I then tried it again.

My initial login program comes up and runs OK. Once the user id and
password are validated, the program then does a call as follows to the
main menu program:

CALL-MENU SECTION.
          MOVE "J:\FPO\fpomenu.exe" to Command-Line
          MOVE 128 TO Command-Line-Len
          MOVE 0 TO Status-Code
          CALL "CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT" USING
               BY REFERENCE Command-Line
               BY VALUE Command-Line-Len
               BY REFERENCE Run-Unit-ID
               BY VALUE Stack-size
               RETURNING Status-code
          END-CALL
          .

It then returns the following message:

 


Question: What exactly do I have to do to get the programs compiled in
V3.0 to work?


I then attempted to call the v2.0 version of the application and it
won't work either. Now the client machine with the new runtime will
not run either version of the application.

I looked in the "readme" file which came with the software, which
states that all code is compatible and that all I had to do was to
recompile any applications developed in 2.0 NetExpress, in NetExpress
3.0.

I recompiled the program under 3.0 and installed the new runtime on
the client. If this stuff is "backward compatible" as advertised, the
old version should still work, as well as the new.

The setup under the v2.0 is this:

The .EXE, .GS, and v2.0 runtime files are located on a network server
in an application directory. The client PC's have the v2.0 runtime
installed on them. I have a shortcut on the client PC which points to
the .EXE on the server. The user clicks on the shortcut, and the
program runs.

I setup is the same under v3.0 except that the 3.0 runtime on the
client is calling a 2.0 developed, 3.0 recompiled program located on
the server.

What am I missing?
```
