<a id="t-802588dbf4"></a>
## Thread: Help with Batch-Validation — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help with Batch-Validation

- **From:** "Colin Birch" <colin@birch1959.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78ajj7$bap$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Please could you tell me if the program below works,If it doesn't any advice
where Iam going wrong would be greatly appreciated.
A program is required to validate batches of transactions that are held on a
disk file Each batch should consist of up to 100 transaction records
followed by a batch control record Each field of the transaction record
needs to be
validated and hash totals of all the numeric fields calculated

If the accumulated hash totals match those of the batch control record and
there are no invalid records and the number of transaction records in the
batch does not exceed 100, then the batch is to be classed as valid All
valid records are to be written to a valid file An erroneous batch is to be
printed out on an error report that contains appropriate headings, with each
batch starting on a new page It is also required that the accumulated hash
totals for that batch are printed after each invalid batch A separate disk
file is to be used as temporary storage of a batch until the validity of the
batch is ascertained
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.                BATCH-VALIDATION.

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
          SELECT OPTIONAL TRAN-FILE ASSIGN TO 'TRANS.DAT'
             ORGANIZATION LINE SEQUENTIAL.
          SELECT TEMP-FILE ASSIGN TO 'TEMP.DAT'
             ORGANIZATION LINE SEQUENTIAL.
          SELECT VALID-FILE ASSIGN TO 'VALID.DAT'
             ORGANIZATION LINE SEQUENTIAL.
          SELECT PRINT-FILE ASSIGN TO PRINTER.

       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD TRAN-FILE.
       01 TRANS-REC.
          03 TYPE-T            PIC X.
          03 REC-PART          PIC 9(8).
          03 REC-SUP           PIC 9(6).
          03 REC-DATE          PIC 9(6).
          03 REC-QUANT         PIC 9(5).

       01 HASH-REC.
          03 TYPE-B            PIC X.
          03 HASH-PART         PIC 9(8).
          03 HASH-SUP          PIC 9(8).
          03 HASH-DATE         PIC 9(8).
          03 HASH-QUANT        PIC 9(8).

       FD TEMP-FILE.
       01 TEMP-REC.
          03 T-TYPE-T          PIC X.
          03 TEMP-PART         PIC 9(8).
          03 TEMP-SUP          PIC 9(6).
          03 TEMP-DATE         PIC 9(8).
          03 TEMP-QUANT        PIC 9(5).

       FD VALID-FILE.
       01 VALID-REC.
          03                   PIC X.
          03 VAL-PART          PIC 9(8).
          03 VAL-SUP           PIC 9(6).
          03 VAL-DATE          PIC 9(6).
          03 VAL-QUANT         PIC 9(5).

       FD PRINT-FILE LINAGE IS 60 LINES.
       01 PRINT-REC            PIC X(80).

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01 HASH-PART-TOT        PIC 9(8) VALUE ZERO.
       01 HASH-SUP-TOT         PIC 9(8) VALUE ZERO.
       01 HASH-DATE-TOT        PIC 9(8) VALUE ZERO.
       01 HASH-QUANT-TOT       PIC 9(8) VALUE ZERO.

       01 EOF                  PIC XXX VALUE 'NO '.
          88 NO-MORE-RECORDS           VALUE 'YES'.


       01 W-HASH-FILE.
          03 W-HASH-TYPE       PIC X VALUE 'B'.
          03 W-HASH-PART       PIC 9(8) VALUE ZERO.
          03 W-HASH-SUP        PIC 9(8) VALUE ZERO.
          03 W-HASH-DATE       PIC 9(8) VALUE ZERO.
          03 W-HASH-QUANT      PIC 9(8) VALUE ZERO.

       01 W-HEADING.
          03                   PIC X(20) VALUE SPACES.
          03                   PIC X(30) VALUE
               '***** ERROR REPORT *****'.

       01 W-HEADINGS-2.
          03                   PIC X(5) VALUE SPACES.
          03                   PIC X(7) VALUE 'PART NO'.
          03                   PIC X(8) VALUE SPACES.
          03                   PIC X(11) VALUE 'CUS/SUP NO.'.
          03                   PIC X(8) VALUE SPACES.
          03                   PIC X(12) VALUE 'DATE SUP/REC'.
          03                   PIC X(8) VALUE SPACES.
          03                   PIC X(8) VALUE 'QUANTITY'.

       01 W-TOT-LINE.
          03                   PIC X(5) VALUE SPACES.
          03                   PIC X(8) VALUE 'PART TOT'.
          03 PRINT-PART-TOT    PIC X(10) VALUE SPACES.
          03                   PIC X(7) VALUE 'SUP/TOT'.
          03 PRINT-SUP-TOT     PIC X(10) VALUE SPACES.
          03                   PIC X(8) VALUE 'DATE TOT'.
          03 PRINT-DATE-TOT    PIC X(10) VALUE SPACES.
          03                   PIC X(10) VALUE 'QUANTITY'.
          03 PRINT-QUANT-TOT   PIC X(10) VALUE SPACES.

       01 W-DETAIL-LINE.
          03                   PIC X(5) VALUE SPACES.
          03 INVAL-PART        PIC X(8) VALUE SPACES.
          03                   PIC X(7) VALUE SPACES.
          03 INVAL-SUP         PIC X(6) VALUE SPACES.
          03                   PIC X(13) VALUE SPACES.
          03 INVAL-DATE        PIC X(6) VALUE SPACES.
          03                   PIC X(14) VALUE SPACES.
          03 INVAL-QUANT       PIC X(5) VALUE SPACES.

       01 REC-COUNT            PIC 999 VALUE ZERO.

       01 W-ERROR-FLAG         PIC X VALUE 'N'.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.

       MAIN-CONTROL.
          PERFORM INITIALISE
          PERFORM TRANS-PROCESS UNTIL NO-MORE-RECORDS
          PERFORM CLOSE-DOWN
          STOP RUN.

       INITIALISE.
          OPEN INPUT TRAN-FILE
               OUTPUT TEMP-FILE VALID-FILE PRINT-FILE
          PERFORM HEADINGS.

       TRANS-PROCESS.
          READ TRAN-FILE AT END
             MOVE 'YES' TO EOF
             NOT AT END
             EVALUATE TYPE-T
                WHEN 'T' PERFORM TRANS-PROCESS-2
                WHEN 'B' PERFORM HASH-PROCESS
             END-EVALUATE.

       TRANS-PROCESS-2.
          IF REC-PART NOT NUMERIC
             OR REC-SUP NOT NUMERIC
             OR REC-DATE NOT NUMERIC
             OR REC-QUANT NOT NUMERIC
                MOVE 'Y' TO W-ERROR-FLAG
          ELSE
             ADD 1 TO REC-COUNT
             ADD REC-PART TO W-HASH-PART
             ADD REC-SUP TO W-HASH-SUP
             ADD REC-DATE TO W-HASH-DATE
             ADD REC-QUANT TO W-HASH-QUANT
             WRITE TEMP-REC FROM TRANS-REC
          END-IF.

       HASH-PROCESS.
          IF HASH-PART NOT NUMERIC
            OR HASH-SUP NOT NUMERIC
            OR HASH-DATE NOT NUMERIC
            OR HASH-QUANT NOT NUMERIC
               MOVE 'Y' TO W-ERROR-FLAG
               PERFORM PRINT-DETAIL
          ELSE
                  PERFORM TEMP-VALIDATE
          END-IF.
       TEMP-VALIDATE.
          CLOSE TEMP-FILE
          OPEN INPUT TEMP-FILE
             READ TEMP-FILE AT END
             MOVE 'YES' TO EOF
             NOT AT END
                IF REC-COUNT <= 100
                   AND W-HASH-SUP = HASH-SUP
                   AND W-HASH-PART = HASH-PART
                   AND W-HASH-DATE = HASH-DATE
                   AND W-HASH-QUANT = HASH-QUANT
                   WRITE VALID-REC FROM TEMP-REC
                ELSE
                   MOVE 'Y' TO W-ERROR-FLAG
                   PERFORM PRINT-DETAIL
                END-IF
                   CLOSE TEMP-FILE
                   OPEN OUTPUT TEMP-FILE
                      MOVE ZERO TO REC-COUNT
                      MOVE ZERO TO HASH-PART
                      MOVE ZERO TO HASH-SUP
                      MOVE ZERO TO HASH-DATE
                      MOVE ZERO TO HASH-QUANT.

       PRINT-DETAIL.
          IF W-ERROR-FLAG = 'Y'
             OR REC-COUNT > 100
             IF W-DETAIL-LINE = SPACES
                MOVE REC-PART TO INVAL-PART
                MOVE REC-SUP TO INVAL-SUP
                MOVE REC-DATE TO INVAL-DATE
                MOVE REC-QUANT TO INVAL-QUANT
                ADD HASH-PART TO HASH-PART-TOT
                ADD HASH-SUP TO HASH-SUP-TOT
                ADD HASH-DATE TO HASH-DATE-TOT
                ADD HASH-QUANT TO HASH-QUANT-TOT
                WRITE PRINT-REC FROM W-DETAIL-LINE
                WRITE PRINT-REC FROM W-TOT-LINE
             END-IF
          END-IF.

       HEADINGS.
          WRITE PRINT-REC FROM W-HEADING AFTER PAGE
          WRITE PRINT-REC FROM W-HEADINGS-2 AFTER 1.

       CLOSE-DOWN.
          IF REC-COUNT > THAN ZERO
             OR W-ERROR-FLAG = 'Y'
             PERFORM PRINT-DETAIL
          ELSE
             CLOSE TRAN-FILE TEMP-FILE VALID-FILE PRINT-FILE
          END-IF.

Thank you.
```

## Thread: VA Cobol and running external batch programs — 1 msgs

### VA Cobol and running external batch programs

- **From:** klm@shaw.wave.ca
- **Date:** 1999-01-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<788tco$tcc$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I'm busy porting code from MF Cobol to VA Cobol under OS/2.  There are various
programs which used the MF function x'91' subtype 35 to run an external rexx
program to perform some file printing.

I cannot find anything comparable under VA Cobol.  I'd like to use something
which would work across both OS/2 and Windows, but I guess I could use the
OS/2 System calls (such as DosStartProgram) to perform this task.

Any ideas?

	-klm.

http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu

- **From:** Michael Entwistle <mikent@san.rr.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36AAB569.81237BD2@san.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<78b4v2$aok$1@spock.tinet.ie> <78cbc6$oth$1@news.igs.net> <78dnad$n20$1@spock.tinet.ie>`

```
The file is an input file.  The program does not create it.  The program is
assuming the file is on disk.

Mark Reid wrote:

> Still having problems with this program. Why would the file not exist?  I've
> typed SELECT EMPLOYEE-FILE ASSIGN TO "EMPLOY.DAT".
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#a5f7TnS#GA.294@upnetnews03>`
- **References:** `<78b4v2$aok$1@spock.tinet.ie> <78cbc6$oth$1@news.igs.net> <78dnad$n20$1@spock.tinet.ie> <36AAB569.81237BD2@san.rr.com>`

```
COBOL programs don't "assume" anything!  They believe everything that the
programmer tells them is true.  If a program is told a defined file exists,
it will justifiably express its displeasure upon finding that it has been
lied to.

Michael Entwistle wrote in message <36AAB569.81237BD2@san.rr.com>...
>The file is an input file.  The program does not create it.  The program is
>assuming the file is on disk.

wouldn't

but

…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu

- **From:** GRZNAR <grznar@omega.uta.edu>
- **Date:** 1999-01-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pine.OSF.3.96.990124145246.3160B-100000@omega.uta.edu>`
- **References:** `<78b4v2$aok$1@spock.tinet.ie> <78cbc6$oth$1@news.igs.net> <78dnad$n20$1@spock.tinet.ie>`

```
Here's a (very) long shot for you.
Perhaps previously you had included the path with EMPLOY.DAT which pushed
the (erroneous) period after the quote mark into column 73.  In which case
the compiler would ignore the period and read the ORGANIZATION blah blah
blah properly.  When you removed the path for EMPLOY.DAT that brought the
period into area B, and now the compiler is trying to interpret your entry
as two different statements.

Well, I warned you at the beginning this was a very long shot.

John Grznar
UTA

On Sat, 23 Jan 1999, Mark Reid wrote:

> Still having problems with this program. Why would the file not exist?  I've
> typed SELECT EMPLOYEE-FILE ASSIGN TO "EMPLOY.DAT".
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> recognise the word ORGANIZATION, although it had previously.  Please help!
```

## Thread: Fujitsu v.3: Entry point of subprogram? — 12 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu v.3: Entry point of subprogram?

- **From:** NoLonger@ix.netcom.com (OldUncleMe)
- **Date:** 1999-01-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36a94663.49538893@news>`

```
Ok, I'm lost:  we're using a subprogram for most of the processing in this
assignment.  I'm trying to compile it using the Fuji v.3 Programming Staff suite.
Compilation goes great, but on linking the subprogram a number of questions come up
(in my mind) and also the linker quits with this error: 

       LINK : fatal error LNK1561: entry point must be defined

The Language reference from F. states:

-->21. To transfer control to the beginning of the procedure
-->division of the called program, specify the name of the
-->called program as literal-1 or identifier-1. You may want
-->to transfer control to entry point other than the beginning
-->of the procedure division. If so, specify the secondary
-->entry name specified in the ENTRY statement as literal-1
-->or identifier-1.

I wish to use the beginning of the procedure division as the entry point, so:

(from the subprogram)
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.  DATEDIFF IS INITIAL.
                 <...>
       PROCEDURE DIVISION 
               USING lw02-date-one lw02-date-two lw03-diff lw04-msg.
       1000-main section.

(from the main program)
                 call 'DATEDIFF'
                  using ww02-date-one ww02-date-two ww03-diff ww04-msg  

Am I missing something basic?  I have the compiler option set to NOMAIN for the
subprogram, and MAIN for the calling prog.  I've tried ALPHAL, NOALPHAL, a lot of
other seemingly unrelated options, no dice.  The Fujitsu reference and all other
sources I've seen say that the primary entry point is established by default as the
PROCEDURE DIVISION USING ....   point.  So I can't understand the linker's reported
error message.

Is there a limitation to this suite that does not allow linking of subprograms?  I
appreciate any help on this.   Should I link the subprogram as a dll or an exe file?
Should I then rename either of these to remove the file extension, .dll or .exe in
order to have a consistent name for the calling program?  Should I use the full path
to the file thus produced or put it in the same directory with the calling program
and depend on the code to find it?  Compiled separately like this, is the subprogram
"external" or "internal"?  Seems there are some differences in what can be done
between these two types.  Should I compile the called program as type MAIN and link
it as a  .DLL?  Both are written in COBOL.

I extend, also, my thanks for all the comments on the INITIALIZE  3 dimensional table
thread.  Finished the program and it runs like a champ.  I went with the group move 
( move zeros to wt01-sale-data )  for this table:

       01 wt01-sale-data.
           02 wt01-stores      occurs 5  times.
             04 wt01-months    occurs 13 times.
               06 wt01-depts   pic 9(9)V99 occurs 6 times.

TS


"Come to think of it, there are already a million monkeys on a million
 typewriters, and Usenet is NOTHING like Shakespeare." Blair Houghton
*********No generalization is wholly true, not even this one**********
++++++****tauceti****@****abraxis****.****com****for***email****++++++
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu v.3: Entry point of subprogram?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78bpl3$nvv@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36a94663.49538893@news>`

```
After a quick review, it LOOKS like you have done everything correctly.  A
couple of POSSIBLE (but unlikely) places to look:

1)  Did you compile the subprogram first, so when you tried to compile and
link the main, it could find the subprogram's executable? (and are they in
the same directory)

2)  You don't show that you have defined all those USING items from your
PROCEDURE DIVISION USING statement in the subprogram - in its LINKAGE
SECTION.  Just "matching the names" doesn't work - you need to have LINKAGE
SECTION items for all the passed parameters in the subprogram.  Just like
you have to define them in the Working-Storage in the CALLing program. (I
can't believe that the message you report would relate to this)

3) Do you happen to have any data items defined with the EXTERNAL attribute?
With some compilers (but I don't think Fujitsu), this can cause linker
problems.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu v.3: Entry point of subprogram?

- **From:** NoLonger@ix.netcom.com (OldUncleMe)
- **Date:** 1999-01-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36a9e97c.2310641@news>`
- **References:** `<36a94663.49538893@news> <78bpl3$nvv@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`

```
On Sat, 23 Jan 1999 00:19:13 -0600 ""William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>"
posted to "comp.lang.cobol" about: "Re: Fujitsu v.3:  Entry point of subprogram?" :

-->After a quick review, it LOOKS like you have done everything correctly.  A
-->couple of POSSIBLE (but unlikely) places to look:
-->
-->1)  Did you compile the subprogram first, so when you tried to compile and
-->link the main, it could find the subprogram's executable? (and are they in
-->the same directory)
-->
-->2)  You don't show that you have defined all those USING items from your
-->PROCEDURE DIVISION USING statement in the subprogram - in its LINKAGE
-->SECTION.  Just "matching the names" doesn't work - you need to have LINKAGE
-->SECTION items for all the passed parameters in the subprogram.  Just like
-->you have to define them in the Working-Storage in the CALLing program. (I
-->can't believe that the message you report would relate to this)
-->
-->3) Do you happen to have any data items defined with the EXTERNAL attribute?
-->With some compilers (but I don't think Fujitsu), this can cause linker
-->problems.

-->Bill Klein

1.  I have compiled the two programs in both orders, either one first.  It's the
linking of the subprogram, all in the same directory, that fails.

2. The working storage and linkage sections are carefully matched, I believe.  As
this is the first time I've ever done this, I spent a lot of time trynig to make it
right, which is of course no guarantee:

<calling program (Main)>

       01 ww02-date-one.
           02 ww02-date1-mm   pic 9(2).
           02 ww02-date1-dd     pic 9(2).
           02 ww02-date1-yy     pic 9(4).
       01 ww02-date-two.
           02 ww02-date2-mm  pic 9(2).
           02 ww02-date2-dd    pic 9(2).
           02 ww02-date2-yy    pic 9(4).
       01 ww03-diff                  pic 9(18) value zeros.
       01 ww04-msg                pic x(40) value spaces.
                  <...>
                 call 'DATEDIFF.EXE'
                  using ww02-date-one ww02-date-two ww03-diff ww04-msg  

<called program (Sub)>

       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

                    <...>

       LINKAGE SECTION.
       01 lw02-date-one.
           02 lw02-date1-mm   pic 9(2).
           02 lw02-date1-dd   pic 9(2).
           02 lw02-date1-yy   pic 9(4).
       01 lw02-date-two.
           02 lw02-date2-mm  pic 9(2).
           02 lw02-date2-dd    pic 9(2).
           02 lw02-date2-yy    pic 9(4).
       01 lw03-diff                  pic 9(18).
       01 lw04-msg               pic x(40).
      *....................

       PROCEDURE DIVISION using lw02-date-one lw02-date-two
              lw03-diff lw04-msg.

3.  I don't have any data items defined as EXTERNAL.  I'm not quite sure even what
that means.  Seems like it would allow other programs/subprogs to access the data
items in memory locations assigned by the program with the EXTERNAL dataname?  Would
this then allow other progs to change that value, and then the main prog would
continue to operate on the data name's data that now has a different value?  This is
different from passing data as a 'Parameter' between programs??

I'm a bit new to this multiple--programming, but it looks very interesting.  Ideally
I would like to write, say, Visual Basic front ends to modules (libraries?) of
functions and processes in other more appropriate languages.  Not an original idea
but very powerful.  Every great journey starts with a first step (and a bruised can?
hah)

  TS
```

```
On Sat, 23 Jan 1999 06:18:52 -0500 ""Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>" posted to

Here's the entire Linker output, even after the above change it's the same:

-->NMAKE : fatal error U1077: 'J:\COBOL\FUJITSU\PCOBOL32\LINK' : return code '0x68'
-->Stop.
-->Microsoft (R) 32-Bit Incremental Linker Version 3.00.5270
-->Copyright (C) Microsoft Corp 1992-1995. All rights reserved.

-->/OUT:J:\SKULWERK\COBOL-3\PROG-3\FUJI\DATEDIFF.EXE
-->J:\SkulWerk\Cobol-3\Prog-3\Fuji\DATEDIFF.OBJ
-->J:\COBOL\FUJITSU\PCOBOL32\PROJECT.RES
-->J:\COBOL\FUJITSU\PCOBOL32\F3BICIMP.LIB
-->J:\COBOL\FUJITSU\PCOBOL32\LIBC.LIB
-->J:\COBOL\FUJITSU\PCOBOL32\KERNEL32.LIB
-->J:\COBOL\FUJITSU\PCOBOL32\USER32.LIB
-->LINK : fatal error LNK1561: entry point must be defined
-->Linking files ended.
-->Please close the window.

The linker looks like it found all the libraries and other support files it needed.
I have compiled, linked and run, with success other programs from Cobol source on
this same setup, so it is a known operable system.

Thanks for looking it over, TS

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu v.3: Entry point of subprogram?

```
Try to simplify the problem to:

000100 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000200 PROGRAM-ID.  PROG1.
000300 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000400 DATA DIVISION.
000410 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
000420 01  PARM PIC X(10) VALUE "HELLO".
000500 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
000600 MAIN-PROGRAM.
000700      CALL "PROG2" USING PARM
000800      STOP RUN
000900      .

000100 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000200 PROGRAM-ID.  PROG2.
000300 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000400 DATA DIVISION.
000410 LINKAGE SECTION.
000420 01  PARM PIC X(10).
000500 PROCEDURE DIVISION USING PARM.
000600 SUB-PROGRAM.
000700      DISPLAY PARM
000800      EXIT PROGRAM
000900      .
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu v.3: Entry point of subprogram?

- **From:** <@usaor.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ASlq2.183$1g3.75200@news.sgi.net>`
- **References:** `<36a94663.49538893@news>`

```

OldUncleMe wrote in message <36a94663.49538893@news>...
>Ok, I'm lost:  we're using a subprogram for most of the processing in this
>assignment.  I'm trying to compile it using the Fuji v.3 Programming Staff
suite.
>Compilation goes great, but on linking the subprogram a number of questions
come up
>(in my mind) and also the linker quits with this error:
>
>       LINK : fatal error LNK1561: entry point must be defined
>
A 1561 is usually caused when a program is compiled as MAIN and linked as
sub.

Compile Calling program with MAIN switch set and link as EXE.
Compile Called program with NOMAIN and link as dll.

Joe Hunter
```

That works, only after moving the two source files to a new directory.  Now the main
program won't link....  They're in the same directory, but the reference is suddenly
'unresolved'.  I've tried recompiling with every change I could think of to make it
work.  Renaming the DATEDIFF.DLL file to _DATEDIFF , and all sorts of combinations,
putting these variously named files in the path in c:\windows, etc.: the linker just
won't bite.

Here is the output from the linker, used on the main program, (which did link at one
time before, and nothing has changed in the file, except I have tried to change the
call statement to the same names as the file name of the called prog...):

=====================================================================

ved externals

The linker seems to be renaming the file to _name in the 
(unresolved external symbol _DATEDIFF.DLL) statement in the output report above.

prog's source code, both, etc.  Thanks again for suggestions, TS

Here is a summary of the suite that was filed:

NEW YORK--(BUSINESS WIRE)--Jan. 20, 1999-- 

The following was announced by the law firm of Weiss & Yourman: 

The law firm of Weiss & Yourman commenced a class action lawsuit
yesterday in the United States District Court for the Southern
District of New York (99 CIV 0373) against Micro Focus Group PLC
("Micro Focus" or the "Company") MIFGY and certain individuals
associated with the Company. The Class on whose behalf plaintiff
has initiated this litigation includes investors who acquired the
American Depository Shares ("ADS") of Micro Focus as a result of the
merger of Intersolv Inc. into Micro Focus on or about September 24,
1998, as well as on behalf of all those who purchased or otherwise
acquired Micro Focus ADSs between June 17, 1998 and
November 12, 1998 (the "Class Period"). 

The complaint alleges that defendants violated the federal securities
laws (Sections 11, 12(2) and 15 of the Securities Act of 1933, and
Sections 10(b) and 20(a) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934) by
misrepresenting or failing to disclose, in a series of false and
misleading statements and omissions, material information
concerning retention of key personnel critical to the success of the
Company's North American Y2K products and operations. As a result of
these false and  misleading statements and omissions, the price of
Micro Focus' ADSs was artificially inflated during the Class Period. 

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

It may be that your sole purpose in life is simply to serve as a warning to 
others.

 Steve

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Do I have a valid date?

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-01-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36acc49e.49177098@netnews>`
- **References:** `<78adcv$6dd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36a8bf6b.2288175@netnews> <78bvg3$d36@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <36ac21dc.7667475@netnews> <78iea4$741@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
'Twas Mon, 25 Jan 1999 12:48:26 -0600, when "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> illuminated alt.cobol thusly:

>On what compiler is documented as working?

>>>>Shuffle your digits arround into YYYYMMDD, then use FUNCTION
>>>>INTEGER-OF-DATE (YYYYMMDD).  If the date is invalid, the fucntion will
>>>>return zero.

On Unisys A Series Cobol85, INTEGER-OF-DATE and INTEGER-OF-DAY return the
value zero if the argument isn't a valid date later than 1600-12-31.  This
is documented behavior.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Do I have a valid date?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78ijpo$nb9@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<78adcv$6dd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36a8bf6b.2288175@netnews> <78bvg3$d36@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <36ac21dc.7667475@netnews> <78iea4$741@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <36acc49e.49177098@netnews>`

```
From the current IBM LRM (and I believe that this is a DIRECT QUOTE from the
current ANSI/ISO Standard),

"If the values assigned as arguments for a function do not comply with
specified constraints, the returned value is undefined."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Do I have a valid date?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36ace210.56685785@netnews>`
- **References:** `<78adcv$6dd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36a8bf6b.2288175@netnews> <78bvg3$d36@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <36ac21dc.7667475@netnews> <78iea4$741@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <36acc49e.49177098@netnews> <78ijpo$nb9@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`

```
'Twas Mon, 25 Jan 1999 14:22:09 -0600, when "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> illuminated alt.cobol thusly:

>From the current IBM LRM (and I believe that this is a DIRECT QUOTE from the
>current ANSI/ISO Standard),
>
>"If the values assigned as arguments for a function do not comply with
>specified constraints, the returned value is undefined."

But it *should* be defined, and if you do define it, what value can you
end up with other than zero?  A well described number space might include
a not-a-number value, but Cobol doesn't have such a concept.

Again I ask:  Can you tell me a compiler where my technique doesn't work?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Do I have a valid date?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78jg5c$4hh@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<78adcv$6dd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36a8bf6b.2288175@netnews> <78bvg3$d36@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <36ac21dc.7667475@netnews> <78iea4$741@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <36acc49e.49177098@netnews> <78ijpo$nb9@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <36ace210.56685785@netnews>`

```
I know that at various release/maintenance levels of IBM's mainframe
compiler, it has NOT worked.  This was the origin of the original ANSI
interpretation request that cleared up the difference between "results are
undefined" and "the returned value is undefined".  (See substantive change
potentially effecting documented in the ANSI/ISO Amendment 2 - Corrections
Amendment)

Please, PLEASE, don't suggest that people rely on any value that HAPPENS to
be returned for this situation - unless YOUR compiler does document what
value will be returned.   I know that Micro Focus and IBM (both Workstation
and Mainframe) do NOT define what value will be returned - and if it happens
to be zero today, there is certainly no guarantee that it will be tomorrow.

NOTE PARTICULARLY - that the results are STILL undefined in the draft of the
next Standard - although an exception could be used for testing for this
situation.
```

---

<a id="t-efd178ddc4"></a>
## Thread: Do I have a valid date? — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### Re: Do I have a valid date?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78c1u3$f21@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<78adcv$6dd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Note: The following assumes that from the reference to COBOL and CICS, that
you are running on an IBM mainframe.  I am even writing it for OS/390 or
MVS - but think there are comparable VSE solutions also.

I provided one solution (using the new IBM extension of a DateVal intrinsic
function) to this problem if you have the MLE add-on to COBOL for
this-and-that - but there is another option that you can use - assuming you
have the LE run-time libraries available (this will even work if your
program is compiled with VS COBOL II - I think)

Please see
  http://www2.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/ceea305/3%2e5%2e24
for complete reference material on the CEEDAYS callable routine.

Special notes for those wondering if 01-02-2000 is Jan 2 or Feb 1 - by
"simply" switching the LE VString-PICSTR, you can easily change the mapping
of your input data - without actually having to "reformat" your input.


Given Working-Storage

01  Full-Date-in.
    05   MM-in   Pic 9(02).
    05                Pic X(01) Value "-".
    05   DD-in   Pic 9(02).
    05                 Pic X(01) Value "-".
    05   YY-in     Pic 9(04).

01   LE-Parameters.
    05    Dynamic-Call-Name     Pic X(07)   Value "CEEDAYS".
    05    LILIAN-Date                  PIC S9(9) BINARY.
     05 FC.
                10  Condition-Token-Value.
                COPY  CEEIGZCT.
                    15  Case-1-Condition-ID.
                        20  Severity    PIC S9(4) BINARY.
                        20  Msg-No      PIC S9(4) BINARY.
                    15  Case-2-Condition-ID
                              REDEFINES Case-1-Condition-ID.
                        20  Class-Code  PIC S9(4) BINARY.
                        20  Cause-Code  PIC S9(4) BINARY.
                    15  Case-Sev-Ctl    PIC X.
                    15  Facility-ID     PIC XXX.
                10  I-S-Info            PIC S9(9) BINARY

01    VString-Date-In.
                02  Vstring-length-DI      PIC S9(4) BINARY.
                02  Vstring-text-DI.
                    03  Vstring-char-DI    PIC X
                                OCCURS 0 TO 256 TIMES
                                DEPENDING ON Vstring-length-DI.
01     VString-PICSTR.
                02  Vstring-length-PS      PIC S9(4) BINARY.
                02  Vstring-text-PS.
                    03  Vstring-char-PS    PIC X
                                OCCURS 0 TO 256 TIMES
                                DEPENDING ON Vstring-length-PS.

      ...
Procedure Division.
   ....

    Move 10 to Vstring-length-PS
    Move "MM-DD-YYYY" to Vstring-char-PS
    Move 10 to Vstring-length-DI
    Move Full-Date-In to Vstring-text-DI
    Call Dynamic-Call-Name
          Using     VString-PICSTR
                         VString-Date-In
                        LILIAN-Date
                        FC

*  Note when running under CICS, change the following DISPLAY statements to
*  appropriate CICS commands

          On Exception Display "Are you Certain that you are running under
LE?"
          Not On exception
                If Not CEE000
                    Display "This was an invalid Date"
                Else
                    Display "Date was valid with a Lilian value of "
Lilian-Date
                End-IF
    End-Call
```

<a id="t-2c4f42c840"></a>
## Thread: advice needed... — 11 this month / 25 total

### advice needed...

- **From:** "hyun sayers" <lee@kimchie.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78fukt$oen$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Hi all to you programming experts.

ok.. i really want to learn the  programming language but i'm not surewhich
one to learn, as i keep getting conflicting advices as to which language to
learn.
I'm told that cobol is a good one to learn however i find that there's
not many places that teaches cobol and also i would like to know
whether this will lead me to a good employment.

Other people tells me visual basics is good to learn as microsoft
products all wriiten in v.basics.

I'm getting a bit confused as to which one to learn as i want to
change my career in to  computing.

So if you have any advice please e-mail me at
lee@kimchie.freeserve.co.uk

Many thanks to you all.

Lee.
```

#### ↳ Re: advice needed...

- **From:** "Steve Beattie" <steve@sbeattie.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78g0gi$mj8$1@news5.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<78fukt$oen$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Lee,
I'll probably get a hard time for this, but if I were you I'd stay away from
COBOL, however this is my main programming language.  Go for something like
VB, or Java even.  As you implied, most colleges aren't teaching COBOL
anymore (this could mean that COBOL programmers will be worth more, if there
is a lack of skilled COBOL people coming into the industry).

If you learn VB it's easier to migrate to other GUI programming languages,
plus theres VB for applications.  Learning a GUI language will mean that you
are more likely to be able to write usefull applications in your spare time
(if you're into that sort of thing).  And before anyone says I'm Bill Gates
in disguise, I'm not pro MicroSoft.

Anyway, I hope this helped, Steve.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: advice needed...

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-01-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36ab8a14.492013185@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<78fukt$oen$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <78g0gi$mj8$1@news5.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
On Sun, 24 Jan 1999 20:37:52 -0000, "Steve Beattie"
<steve@sbeattie.freeserve.co.uk> wrote:

>Lee,
>I'll probably get a hard time for this, but if I were you I'd stay away from
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

One can easily and quickly create GUI programs in COBOL using a
variety of methods.  GUI requirements should not be a reason to avoid
COBOL.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: advice needed...

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-01-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36ac8337.3365314@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<78fukt$oen$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <78g0gi$mj8$1@news5.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
"Steve Beattie" <steve@sbeattie.freeserve.co.uk> wrote:

>Lee,
>I'll probably get a hard time for this, but if I were you I'd stay away from
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>is a lack of skilled COBOL people coming into the industry).

Steve:

It seems as if the above paragraph contradicts itself.  In the first
sentence, you provide advice to Lee that he should stay away from
COBOL.  In your last statement, you suggest that COBOL programmers may
make more money, which may provide a significant incentive for Lee to
learn COBOL.

Perhaps....and it is only a thought...Lee may be able to teach himself
BOTH VB and COBOL!  I realize that this may be a radical concept, but
if he were to learn BOTH programming languages, he would then be that
much MORE valuable in the marketplace.

Lee...buy Thane Hubbell's book, Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours.
Teach yourself COBOL if the colleges and universities continue to be
in denial.  Then take a course in VB.

As my friends at Nike say, "Just do it!"



>
>If you learn VB it's easier to migrate to other GUI programming languages,
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

#### ↳ Re: advice needed...

- **From:** "Jeffrey J. Parker" <jjp5421@garnet.acns.fsu.edu>
- **Date:** 1999-01-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78ovso$gqe$1@news.fsu.edu>`
- **References:** `<78fukt$oen$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Hyun,
    I am a student at Florida State University learning COBOL after learning
"C", and not enjoying either one of them. I have friends all over the
country (USA) at consulting firms looking for Java and C++ programmers for
top dollar. If I was you, I would concentrate on C++ and after that, you
could learn Java easily if needed.
    Another reason for C++ or Java, you usually get farther from the
mainframe room(what I consider a huge plus). I just went from Visual C++
(beautiful interface and speed) to IBM COBOL for AIX (command line ugly
piece of very hard working proven garbage). Believe me, I never thought I
would say it, but I miss Microsoft!
Hope I added a little to your confusion,
Jeff

PS-Check magazines like computer world to see what the languages people are
looking for are.
```

- **Date:** 1999-01-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36b32cb4.4114943@news.trxinc.com>`

<jjp5421@garnet.acns.fsu.edu> wrote:

>Hyun,
>    I am a student at Florida State University learning COBOL after learning
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…

As a second semester programming student, I can well understand your
confusion. I am in my second semester of COBOL and my first of RPG. I
have had a couple of experienced programmers explain to me that no
matter what direction you plan to go in COBOL is a good place to
start. I have learned the history and evolution of languages and now
understand what I am learning is not much a language but the art of
structure and logic.  Not to mention data representation along with
the how's and why's or computers. The result will be (If I survive
<S>) that languages such as C will come easier with a solid
understanding of COBOL. All opinions welcome.

Cyndy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: advice needed...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IRIs2.1343$Xl5.1344622@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<78fukt$oen$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <78ovso$gqe$1@news.fsu.edu> <36b32cb4.4114943@news.trxinc.com> <78vbrc$uhb$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
WOB@my-dejanews.com wrote:
>cyndyh@hotmail.com wrote:
>> >Jeff
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Every programmer should have a general understanding of ASSEMBLER.

I agree with you.  However, I have had the experience of teaching low-
level hardware/software concepts and assembly language to a number of
people.  It is my experience that, outside of assembly language, COBOL
is far and away the BEST first language to teach these same concepts.
It is easy, for example, to explain memory management concepts to a
COBOL programmer, because the way records are created in COBOL is a
small picture (no pun :) of how memory is allocated by the.  If there
is a worse first language than RPG, I have yet to see it.  An average
programmer with two or three years of RPG and nothing else, is likely
ruined for ever truly mastering other languages.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: advice needed...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WiYs2.1150$Fs.1245374@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<78fukt$oen$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <78ovso$gqe$1@news.fsu.edu> <36b32cb4.4114943@news.trxinc.com> <78vbrc$uhb$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <IRIs2.1343$Xl5.1344622@news1.mia> <790jfp$2ke$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote
>[snippage]
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>I have never had the pleasure of RPG beyond the occasional hack.

BASIC and FORTRAN are both bad, because they 'spontaneously generate'
variables on first use.  This promotes developing a truly wacky mental
paradigm of memory management. :-)  But RPG disassociates to a high
degree the procedural nature of programming.  I have worked with
programmers who programmed exclusively in RPG for two or three years
before being exposed to other languages.  None of them ever overcame
the thinking handicap which RPG imposed.  In my opinion, it should be
illegal to teach RPG to anyone before they have been exposed to more
conventional languages.  Something like "contributing to the mental
impairment of a programmer" or some such. ;-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: advice needed...

- **From:** "George McGinn" <gmcginn@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VBEs2.1472$fd.1159@news.rdc1.fl.home.com>`
- **References:** `<78fukt$oen$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <78ovso$gqe$1@news.fsu.edu>`

```
Hi My 2-cents worth -

It takes time and proficiency to get top dollar at any language.  I have
been programming and designing systems for 25+ years.

My suggestion is get proficient at both the Mainframe and PC Lan languages
and systems.  I am in both COBOL (Mainframe) and UNIX (Java, Perl and enough
C++ to make me dangerous!!!)

The reason is that in the last 2 years I have been asked to design and work
on systems that connect Mainframe CICS systems to UNIX applications, which
included web access to the mainframe data.  Being able to understand both
environments have not only helped me design the mainframe part, but I was
able to help with the UNIX piece (the UNIX design needs to take into account
the way the mainframe processes and presents the data).

Legacy systems are not going away because no PC to mid range hardware can
ever process large amounts of data like a mainframe.  So the trend is to add
the mainframe into the Tier of a LAN.

Again, my 2-cents worth!!  Good luck.
Jeffrey J. Parker wrote in message <78ovso$gqe$1@news.fsu.edu>...
>Hyun,
>    I am a student at Florida State University learning COBOL after
learning
>"C", and not enjoying either one of them. I have friends all over the
>country (USA) at consulting firms looking for Java and C++ programmers for
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-574eb10bd7"></a>
## Thread: did it come easy??? — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### did it come easy???

- **From:** Green <new@at.cbl>
- **Date:** 1999-01-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36AB5FB3.4260E202@at.cbl>`

```
Hello!,

 I am kinda posting an open question here. So far, I am planning on
going to school for buisness programing, Cobol is the main area of
study. I read some of the answer to problems people post, and am
impressed by the knowledge of the denizens of this area. It is a little
discouraging when I look out in the market, and it seems to be a dry
oasis so to speak.

 I am still pushing ahead, but I would like to know if while learning
your various programing languages, did it come easy? I remember many
years ago, while learning pascal, having some problem figuring out how
to sort! Some problems took me longer to understand than it did others.
On the other hand, I was able to pick up things better than the others.

I guess admist my ramblings, I would like to know...where you all whizes
while learning? Or did you go threw some bad markings, or question if
you were able to handle it all?
```

#### ↳ Re: did it come easy???

- **From:** "M.Kobobel" <classicmek@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78fpba$7l4$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<36AB5FB3.4260E202@at.cbl>`

```
<<where you all whizes
while learning? Or did you go threw some bad markings, or
question if
you were able to handle it all?>>

The first language is really tough.  You have to learn to
sweat the details, and pick nits at a very low level.  The
second language is a little easier, you have to learn that
the nits don't always have the same face.  With any luck at
all the first  two will be different enough that  every one
after that is pretty much just a matter of learning the
syntax - not a big deal.
```

#### ↳ Re: did it come easy???

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78gelt$anf$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36AB5FB3.4260E202@at.cbl>`

```
You have to remember that it is an exponential curve.  Your
first language is the toughest, by far.  The second is not quite
as bad.  By the third or fourth, you can pick up the basics in a few
days, and be fluent in a few months. Most of the regulars in this
NG have been coding for years and years, and most have at
least three or four languages under their belt.

You can be absolutely assured, however, that no one was born
being able to code. ;<)-(yet...maybe in a few million years).

As to your question, it was the sixties, and I find it hard to remember ...


Green wrote in message <36AB5FB3.4260E202@at.cbl>...
>Hello!,
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: did it come easy???

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36AC8188.EC41DFEC@home.com>`
- **References:** `<36AB5FB3.4260E202@at.cbl> <78gelt$anf$1@news.igs.net>`

```
One hard thing about learning languages is if you have created
unstructured programs in the past.  Even .BAT files count here.  

Knowing the value of structure and maintenance friendly programs, and
analysis techniques is the big difference between hobbyist programmers
and pros.  And it makes going from language to language much easier.
```

#### ↳ Re: did it come easy???

- **From:** "Jeffrey J. Parker" <jjp5421@garnet.acns.fsu.edu>
- **Date:** 1999-01-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78p0n9$h9d$1@news.fsu.edu>`
- **References:** `<36AB5FB3.4260E202@at.cbl>`

```
Hang in there, I am just starting out in COBOL after "C" in the Florida
State College of Business, the second language is much easier, but I am
nowhere near the rest of these guys. Maybe the third and fourth will be the
charm...
Keep Chugging,
Jeff
```

- **Message-ID:** `<36ABC60B.740F@netbox.com>`
- **References:** `<78gaoc$ocu$1@spock.tinet.ie>`

```
Mark Reid wrote:
> 
> Hi, I can't print from PSTAFF at the moment, despite it having done so
> before.  No problem with the printer because I've printed out other stuff.
> Error message coming up saying printer not connected  or insuficient work
> area (I think). Can anyone help?
It sounds like you are running under win 3.x. Try turning the printer
off and back on, maybe even exiting PSTAFF, and reopening file.  As a
last resort, you may have to reboot.  The system is telling you it
doesn't have enough resources to spool the job to the printer.  However,
another less resource-hungry app may successfully print.  Go figure. 
```

#### ↳ Re: Another Fujitsu Problem!

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78h32q$n4p$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<78gaoc$ocu$1@spock.tinet.ie>`

```
I do not know if this is related or not, but I have found two
bugs (the first is a fault rather than a bug) that have caused
me untold problems. The first is that Fujitsu *never* clean up
after themselves.  If you look in the TMP area that they
set in the autoexec, you will find a couple files for every compile
you have ever done.  I recently erased 450 megabytes out of
there.  Ignore it, and everything will eventually crash, as they
default to drive C.

The other is a bit more insidious.  First, they always initialize
data to nulls.  Now that should not really matter if the Cobol is
well written, and the standard DOES say that data need not
be initialized. (Though why nulls? Why not space?).  However,
internally, the code follows C conventions ... IE-a string ends
in a null.  So data moves of PICTURE X(50), if there is a null
in position 5, really only moves 4 bytes of data.  This does not
seem to happen when moving Cobol data to Cobol data, but it
*does* happen when moving data internally.  For example,
data between a Cobol program and an object, even if the
object is another Cobol program.  So be very careful with
any data that is used internally(like file id's, and printer specification
strings).


Mark Reid wrote in message <78gaoc$ocu$1@spock.tinet.ie>...
>Hi, I can't print from PSTAFF at the moment, despite it having done so
>before.  No problem with the printer because I've printed out other stuff.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-30e5dafeac"></a>
## Thread: b-color & f-color in Fujitsu — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### b-color & f-color in Fujitsu

- **From:** "GARWIG Ruddy" <ruddy.garwig@skynet.be>
- **Date:** 1999-01-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78emq3$mdc$1@news0.skynet.be>`

```
In the screen-section, I use a variable (b-color) after the instruction
background-color and foreground-color.
This variable (b-color) is declared pic S9(4) binary.
The diagnostic message is JMN 1121-S syntax of background-color clause is
invalide or symbolic constant b-color is undefined.

Why?
```

#### ↳ Re: b-color & f-color in Fujitsu

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xmEq2.1416$oi.1437810@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<78emq3$mdc$1@news0.skynet.be>`

```
GARWIG Ruddy wrote:
>In the screen-section, I use a variable (b-color) after the instruction
>background-color and foreground-color.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Why?

The phrase "symbolic constant b-color" is your clue.  Fujitsu apparently
requires a symbolic constant here, not a variable.  Check your manual or
help.  Next time, paste the actual code with error message in your post,
and we can give you a better response.  As it is, I have no clue as to
whether you may have made a syntax error of some kind, which is the other
possibility from the error message.  And don't 'tell' us how you declared
the variables, post the COBOL code.  Many errors are perceptual errors,
when you look right at the code and see what you expect to see, instead
of what is actually there.  Every experienced programmer is very familiar
with this phenomenon. :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: b-color & f-color in Fujitsu

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78ffet$mgv$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<78emq3$mdc$1@news0.skynet.be>`

```
You cannot set your colour dynamically in the screen section,
so the syntax is incorrect. The colour can be defined with a
*digit* or a symbolic constant, not a variable.

GARWIG Ruddy wrote in message <78emq3$mdc$1@news0.skynet.be>...
>In the screen-section, I use a variable (b-color) after the instruction
>background-color and foreground-color.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: b-color & f-color in Fujitsu

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78g9dm$nv5@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<78emq3$mdc$1@news0.skynet.be> <78ffet$mgv$1@news.igs.net>`

```
FYI,
  In the draft of the next COBOL Standard (as with several existing
compilers - but apparently not Fujitsu) there ARE ways to change
foreground/background color dynamically.  However, if you are using Fujitsu,
stick with what you have (as documented in your LRM)

NOTE: I have NOT verified that this isn't supported (yet) by Fujitsu, but am
assuming that Donald is correct.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: b-color & f-color in Fujitsu

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78gdtk$a4d$1@news.igs.net>`

There are ways to change the colour with many of the compilers
that I have used, but I have never yet used one that allowed
the "background color IDENTIFIER-NAME" methodology in
the screen section. I always thought it rather annoying, to
tell the truth.  The most current version of MF I have is 4,
and it does not, the Liant that I have says you cannot, though
I have not tried it, and the Fujitsu gives you an error message
when you try it, so I assume that is the case (you cannot look
it up, because the online documents ... ah shit ... nine books,
no index, no global search, no way of using any cross references
except paper and pen, and if you do spend three hours searching
and finding every occurrence of the word "colour", you then have to
go back and do the same for "color" and even then it will not
be there ...

Can you tell I have been doing a lot of Fujitsu work lately?
;<)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: b-color & f-color in Fujitsu

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78ggvb$s7@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<78emq3$mdc$1@news0.skynet.be> <78ffet$mgv$1@news.igs.net> <78g9dm$nv5@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com> <78gdtk$a4d$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald,
   When it comes to Accept/Display screen stuff, I always get confused which
is supported by X/Open, which is in the draft of the next Standard, and
which is in Micro Focus (all of which I have worked with).  I don't know
Fujitsu's support (although I do have a copy of their compiler in-house),
but you might look for one of the following:

1) Do you use a Screen Section entry with the Foreground/Background-Color
clause?  The next Standard will allow this to point to an identifier, not
just a literal, so you can change it from DISPLAY to DISPLAY or ACCEPT to
ACCEPT.
 (see also comment about MF below - in the middle of your comments)

2) Micro Focus (V4 is what I have an LRM handy for) only has integers for
FOREGROUND and BACKGROUND-COLOR on the DISPLAY statement *BUT* it includes
(format 3) a CONTROL is identifier phrase - which can "dynamically" modify
all the attributes specified in the CONTROL data-item described in the data
division section. (This includes - I think - colors)

3)  The draft Standard also has a new format of the SET statement which can
modify screen description attributes.  In the draft that I looked at
quickly, this did NOT include colors - but I don't know if this is
intentional or not (or the way the current draft looks).

   ***

I hope one of these provides you with some guidance where to look in the
Fujitsu documentation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: b-color & f-color in Fujitsu

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-01-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36abd604.511455660@news1.ibm.net>`

```

<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:


>2) Micro Focus (V4 is what I have an LRM handy for) only has integers for
>FOREGROUND and BACKGROUND-COLOR on the DISPLAY statement *BUT* it includes
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

## Thread: Microfocus Cobol Problem. — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Microfocus Cobol Problem.

- **From:** Stevie B <skb3@coventry.ac.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pine.OSF.3.91.990124142045.1773A-100000@godiva>`

```
Hello, I have a problem with one of my programs.  What I am trying to do 
is sort a file using a sort file giving my ouput file.  This is simple 
enough but when I so to process the output file to a Print file, i.e 
Headings, total records etc, I have problems.  

I have Microfocus for Unix and Dos, the Dos one processed the output file 
properly and will read my output file into the print file that I have set 
up.  When I use the Unix version it just believes the output file is 
empty and therefore It will not process any of the records and so I have 
no print file. 

Here is the code I am using:

      SORT SORT-FILE
            ON ASCENDING KEY SORT-STOCK-NUM
            USING ORDER-FILE
            GIVING SORTED-FILE.
       OPEN INPUT SORTED-FILE.
       READ SORTED-FILE INTO WS-ORDER-REC
            AT END MOVE "T" TO EOF-FLAG.

The unix version of Microfocus always processes the SORTED-FILE as being 
empty.  This means my it will not read naything into the working storage 
i.e WS-ORDER-REC.

If anyone could help, I would be so grateful.

Thanx in advance.
     

			     Stevie B
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus Cobol Problem.

- **From:** "IriePeer" <IriePeer@Hotmail.nospam.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be4873$ee2825c0$7b01a8c0@luc>`
- **References:** `<Pine.OSF.3.91.990124142045.1773A-100000@godiva>`

```
It may be easyer to use the SORT-FILE in your case :
A sorted file does not need to be opened. You get the records by the RETURN
command.

EG :

      SORT SORT-FILE
            ON ASCENDING KEY SORT-STOCK-NUM
            USING ORDER-FILE
            OUTPUT PROCEDURE IS MOVE-RECS.


MOVE-RECS.
       RETURN SORT-FILE
            AT END MOVE "T" TO EOF-FLAG.
       PERFORM UNTIL EOF-FLAG = "T"
             MOVE SORT-FILE-REC TO  WS-ORDER-REC
             RETURN SORT-FILE
                     AT END MOVE "T" TO EOF-FLAG
       END-PERFORM.
```

<a id="t-ea3b9a6089"></a>
## Thread: Help needed — 44 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help needed

- **From:** "Judy & Al" <al-judy@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78ii2r$5tj@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
I was being interviewed by a C,C++ Technical person today for a job as a
COBOL programmer.  We didn't speak the same language, but he asked me a
question that I couldn't answer and I was wondering if anyone out there
could give me an answer.

    The question:  how would you determine if a field contained a word that
was a palindrome (a word, phrase or sentence that reads that same forwards
and backwards, eg. mom, noon, A man a plan a canal panama)?

I sat there dumbfounded.  I haven't programmed in 3 years and am trying to
get back into a COBOL programming position.  I have a feeling, it won't be
there.  Any help on this would be great.  It's a matter of curiosity now.

Thanks!
Judy
```

#### ↳ Re: Help needed

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78iiue$mmt@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<78ii2r$5tj@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Assuming we don't have any space padding

    If Field-A = Function Reverse (Field-A)

should do it pretty quickly and easily.  (Obviously, you could subscript
backwards and forwards - if you don't have intrinsic functions - but I think
this is the "prettiest" way to do it.)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help needed

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-01-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36b861e6.88845603@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<78ii2r$5tj@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <78iiue$mmt@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`

```
On Mon, 25 Jan 1999 14:07:37 -0600, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>Assuming we don't have any space padding
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>this is the "prettiest" way to do it.)


Sorry, no can do:  If the input text is 

A man a plan a canal panama

The reverse function will not give the correct result.  I believe something like


ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
CONFIGURATION SECTION.
SPECIAL NAMES.
CLASS ignore-char IS '  '	, ',', '.', '?', '!'.	

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01 misc-flag PIC X VALUE 'P'.
    88 palindrom VALUE 'P'.
    88 no-palindron VALUE 'N'.
01 input-text PIC X(??)
01 stripped-text PIC X(??)
MOVE 1 to k
PERFORM VARYING I from 1 BY 1 UNTIL I > LENGTH OF INPUT-TEXT
  IF input-text(I:1) IS NOT ignore-char
      MOVE input-text(I:1) TO stripped-text(k:1)
      ADD 1 TO k
  END-IF
END-PERFORM
PERFORM VARYING I FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL I > k OR NO-PALINDROM
  COMPUTE TAIL = k - I + 1
  IF stripped-text(I:1) NOT EQUAL stripped-text(TAIL:1)
     SET NO-PALINDROM TO TRUE
  END-IF
END-PERFORM
    
would be a trifle better


with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

#### ↳ Re: Help needed

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-01-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78ijiu$kr5$1@callisto.clark.net>`
- **References:** `<78ii2r$5tj@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In article <78ii2r$5tj@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>,
Judy & Al <al-judy@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

[snippage]

>    The question:  how would you determine if a field contained a word that
>was a palindrome (a word, phrase or sentence that reads that same forwards
>and backwards, eg. mom, noon, A man a plan a canal panama)?

What an utterly *useless* exercise for a COBOL-jockey... what comes next,
the Towers of Hanoi?

Anyhow, the mechanism is (to me, that is) obvious, in NewBOL; the guts
of it would be reference-modification comparisons of characters, starting
at both ends of the field:

PERFORM VARYING SUB1 FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL SUB1 > LENGTH OF PAL-FLD
    IF PAL-FLD(SUB1:1) NOT = PAL-FLD((LENGTH OF PAL-FLD + 1) - SUB1:1)
        SET IS-NOT-PALINDROME TO TRUE
    END-IF
END-PERFORM.

... or something close to that.

(if I am wrong, of course, at least I hope to be wrong... beautifully!)

DD
```

- **From:** Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com

```
Doc -

No more useless for a cobol jock than any other language, except for the guy
writing the REVERSE intrinsic function.  I'll be charitable and guess the guy
asking the question wasn't really interested in palindromes (or even what
language-specific algorithms the candidate might carry in their head) but,
instead, wanted to get clue as to how the programming candidate approaches
problems.

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

In article <78kgbd$nfd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>,
 <Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com> wrote:
>Doc -
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>problems.

Silly me... of *course* there is that kind of subtlety, a question is
given which is completely inappropriate for the tool at hand (COBOL for
string-handling) and then, based on how the candidate does the equivalent
of performing mathematical operations with crescent-wrenches one
determines the suitability of said candidate to be a tour-guide.

Is making *much* sense, hu hu!

I'm all for determining problem-handling skills but please, let them be
*real* problems, ones applicable to the job the applicant is expected to
know and do.  My favorite is 'Here's a program skeleton.  The situation is
this: the Master File's corrupted; all entries for division A3 have spaces
in the ACCT-TOT field (which is, of course, COMP-3).  Here's an
update-file containing the *right* account totals for each account in
branch A3; write me a quick and dirty fix (which will, in all probability,
become one of the standard Master File Utilities so neatness *does*
count).

I'll be back in 15 minutes... no, make it 20, the Master File's not sorted
and based on the DMAP you'll have to code a SyncSort (no internals in this
shop).  Since you claim to have only five years' experience I'll make it
even easier by not stopping by every three minutes and asking 'Well,
what've you got so far?''

*That*, in my humble estimation, is a Tech Exam covering something
*applicable*.

DD

>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help needed

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78n5tc$tvm$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<78ii2r$5tj@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <78ijiu$kr5$1@callisto.clark.net> <78kgbd$nfd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <78kl83$5l6$1@callisto.clark.net>`

```
I guess it depends on who's being interviewd and what position was being
filled.  If the candidate claims a lot of experience and the job being filled
is 'experience required' then I agree, there should be some technically
relevant questions to see if the candidate is blowing smoke or really knows
his stuff.  If the candidate has little or no experience and the job is entry
level, I'd be more interested in HOW he thinks, or even *if* he can think. 
If he can think, I can teach him techniques.  If he can't think thru how a
problem might be approached then it doesn't matter if he has memorized some
algorithms.  Even for the experienced candidate I would, in addition to
relevant technical questions, ask some questions designed to see *how* he
answers rather than *what* he answers.

Opinions are my own and not necessarily those of my employer.

In article <78kl83$5l6$1@callisto.clark.net>,
  docdwarf@clark.net () wrote:
> In article <78kgbd$nfd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>,

that

> >> >and backwards, eg. mom, noon, A man a plan a canal panama)?

>I guess it depends on who's being interviewd and what position was being
>filled.  If the candidate claims a lot of experience and the job being filled

<Doc and Judson's weight lifting secretary stuff snipped>

My experience in landing my first programming job I believe was unique
as I've not encountered this method since that time.  I was applying
for an assembler programmer's position at this small company.  I'd
already had all my computer courses and was still in college taking
all the non-computer stuff needed for my degree.  The guy that passed
as the IT manager gave me a program listing of an approximately 50-60
line assembler program.  He told me there was a bug in the program,
even though it compiled and linked clean and gave me 20 minutes to see
if I could find the bug.  I did and I was hired.

Cheers,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Fun Fact:  When opossums are playing 'possum, they are not
"playing." They actually pass out from sheer terror. 


 Steve
```

<9D8C9DA7004708CC.A0AB9749387C4477.D24BE297C5ADB7C2@library-proxy.airnews.ne
t>...
<snip>
>My experience in landing my first programming job I believe was unique
>as I've not encountered this method since that time.  I was applying
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Steve

My experience getting my first computer job (as an operator back in 1964)
was similar.  I was asked if I'd ever heard of binary arithmetic.  When I
said yes, they gave me a simple binary sum to do.  In fact I'd only heard of
binary because my brother had some homework a couple of weeks before and I'd
helped him work out what it meant.  See it does pay to help others with
their homework.

I had no real idea about computers but read a lot of SF so it seemed like a
neat job.  However I was disappointed as the machine only filled up half a
wall and there were very few flashing lights.

BTW the machine was a RCA301 rebadged as an ICT 1500 and had 40k memory.
They were proud as it was the biggest in Britain.  Compiles took so long
that operators had a patch sheet and had to patch in lines of code by hand
(using octal via buttons) if known errors occurred.  That was how I learned
machine code programming.

Rick
```

#### ↳ Re: Help needed

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36ACD91A.8FB47E21@home.com>`
- **References:** `<78ii2r$5tj@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Judy & Al wrote:
> 
> I was being interviewed by a C,C++ Technical person today for a job as a
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> and backwards, eg. mom, noon, A man a plan a canal panama)?

Copy the word to a field which is redefined as an array of characters. 
Find out which is the last character of the word and do a character by
character compare, working foreward from 1 and backwords from this
character.  Don't continue once the two subscripts pass each other.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help needed

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36AE9444.66F913F6@att.net>`
- **References:** `<78ii2r$5tj@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <36ACD91A.8FB47E21@home.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
(snip)
> >     The question:  how would you determine if a field contained a word that
> > was a palindrome (a word, phrase or sentence that reads that same forwards
> > and backwards, eg. mom, noon, A man a plan a canal panama)?
> 
> Copy the word to a field which is redefined as an array of characters.

If you copy it backwards you can do a simple compare (assuming the
blanks are out).

> Find out which is the last character of the word and do a character by
> character compare, working foreward from 1 and backwords from this
> character.  Don't continue once the two subscripts pass each other.

Given the example, the first thing I'd do is to squeeze out the blanks,
then the REVERSE function (or my hand coded version of same).

Bill Lynch

To summarize:

1. eliminate blanks
2. copy backwards
3. compare the two fields
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help needed

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36AF1F39.2E8F8A43@home.com>`
- **References:** `<78ii2r$5tj@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <36ACD91A.8FB47E21@home.com> <36AE9444.66F913F6@att.net>`

```
William Lynch wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee wrote:
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> 3. compare the two fields

Blanks and punctuation.  Doing a character by character comparison you
can increment your pointer for non-valid characters.  As soon as a
non-match is over exit. 

The question was asked in a job interview, so I provided an answer which
would be easy to explain orally and which showed understanding of COBOL
skills.  That's why I picked an array of characters over the REVERSE
function.   A hand coded REVERSE function is almost my whole answer.
```

Judy, I am currently in the tedious process of interviewing for
one of those fabulous "opportunities" one hears about, and I would
simply KILL for an interviewer who would ask me a PROGRAMMING
question such as you were asked.  I keep being asked all these
"play compiler" sorts of questions when the job in question almost
certainly wants the person who can develop algorithms using
whatever language, in our case COBOL.  Don't get me wrong here, I
can do both; if not off the top of my head, I KNOW how to find
out.

Both Bill Kline and DD have adduced potential solutions, and both
are good enough at this craft to realize soonest that their
solutions are not complete: the spaces and punctuation in "a man a
plan ..." render their first go-around incorrect.  But they know
how to refine their algorithms to get the proper result.

Rick Anderson
Seattle
anderson  ï¿½atï¿½  pobox  ï¿½fullstopï¿½  com

PS -- I haven't the FAINTEST idea of how to do numerical
integration in ANY language!

------------------------------------------------------------

<al-judy@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

#### ↳ Re: Help needed

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78iq2q$938$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<78ii2r$5tj@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```

Judy & Al wrote in message <78ii2r$5tj@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>...
>I was being interviewed by a C,C++ Technical person today for a job as a
>COBOL programmer.  We didn't speak the same language, but he asked me a
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The work is done in and comments are interspersed in
the paragraph 'palindrome-test'.

This program was tested with Micro Focus WB 3.2.50.
-------------------

       program-id. palindrome.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01 a pic x(80).
       01 b pic x(80).
       01 palindrome-flag pic x.
         88 is-palindrome value "Y".
         88 is-not-palindrome value "N".
       01 a-ptr pic s9(4) comp.
       01 b-ptr pic s9(4) comp.
       procedure division.
           perform with test after until a = space
               display "Enter a phase for palindrome testing"
               display "or ENTER to quit!"
               move space to a
               accept a
               if a not = space
                   perform palindrome-test
                   if is-palindrome
                       display "The phase or word is a palindrome"
                   else
                       display "The phase or word is NOT a palindrome"
                   end-if
               end-if
           end-perform
           stop run.

       palindrome-test.
      * initialize
           set is-not-palindrome to true
           move space to b
           move 0 to b-ptr

      * copy alphabetic, non-space characters from a into b
      * making all characters upper-case

           perform varying a-ptr from 1 by 1
             until a-ptr > function length ( a )
               if a ( a-ptr : 1 ) alphabetic
                 and a ( a-ptr : 1 ) not = space
                   add 1 to b-ptr
                   move function upper-case ( a ( a-ptr : 1 ) )
                       to b ( b-ptr : 1 )
               end-if
           end-perform

      * copy the reverse of the sub string of significant characters
      * from b into a
      * b-ptr contains the number of significant characters

           move space to a
           move function reverse ( b ( 1 : b-ptr ) ) to a

      * compare the transformed strings

           if a = b
               set is-palindrome to true
           end-if
           .
---------------------
HTH
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

#### ↳ Re: Help needed

- **From:** JeffreyFarkas@earthlink.net (jeff farkas)
- **Date:** 1999-01-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.1116c468508e9b7a989680@news.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<78ii2r$5tj@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In article <78ii2r$5tj@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>, al-judy@ix.netcom.com 
says...
=>     The question:  how would you determine if a field contained a word that
=> was a palindrome (a word, phrase or sentence that reads that same forwards
=> and backwards, eg. mom, noon, A man a plan a canal panama)?
=> 
=> Thanks!
=> Judy


Hi Judy,

    Assuming that the word in question is all lower or upper case then 
you could just move the last character in the word to the first place of 
a new string. Let's say that you find out that the word is three 
characters long, mom. Moving the last character to the first position of 
a new string would let you spell the new version of the word backwards.

    Then you just compare the two. If they are the same you have a 
palindrome.

    I just wonder how often you would have to do this.

Jeff
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help needed

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78l1v5$hat@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<78ii2r$5tj@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <MPG.1116c468508e9b7a989680@news.earthlink.net> <36ad3220.600584905@news1.ibm.net> <MPG.111645caa020e44d989690@news.earthlink.net>`

```
I submitted a "simplistic" solution to this problem earlier, but I did want
to mention what would be needed to do a "more fool proof" solution - still
using the REVERSE intrinsic function.  (Pseudo-code rather than code
follows)

Do an ALPHABETIC class test to see if the item includes any punctuation,
If it does,
  THEN
    -- Create a temporary copy of the original data item by looping thru the
original field and "eliminating" any punctuation characters (for example,
commas) While looping tally any trailing spaces
  ELSE
    - Use INSPECT  for leading spaces of the "reverse" of the original data
item - to determine how many trailing spaces exist in the field.
  END-IF

Use REVERSE of the UPPER-CASE (or LOWER-CASE) version of the reference
modified field (truncating trailing spaces) to determine if the letters are
a  palindrome
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help needed

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36ae4ece.6768737@netnews>`
- **References:** `<78ii2r$5tj@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <MPG.1116c468508e9b7a989680@news.earthlink.net> <36ad3220.600584905@news1.ibm.net> <MPG.111645caa020e44d989690@news.earthlink.net> <78l1v5$hat@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`

```
'Twas Tue, 26 Jan 1999 12:36:12 -0600, when "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>Do an ALPHABETIC class test to see if the item includes any punctuation,
>If it does,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>  END-IF

I fail to see why you test for ALPHABETIC.  Looking at the examples
provided

>>> >=> and backwards, eg. mom, noon, A man a plan a canal panama)?

we note that "A man a plan a canal panama" is ALPHABETIC, yet we need to
squeeze out spaces.  We also need to make the "A" at the beginning match
the "a" at the end (we might run into "P" also), so the text needs to be
converted to a single case.  

So the solution is to copy the characters one at a time, converting them
all to the same case, throwing out everything that isn't alphabetic and
throwing out spaces as well.  In the course of doing this you should be
counting the length at the same time.  Then the actual test is something
like 

    IF  THE-FIELD (1 : THE-LENGTH) 
            = FUNCTION REVERSE (THE-FIELD (1 : THE-LENGTH))
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help needed

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78mgm1$558@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<78ii2r$5tj@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <MPG.1116c468508e9b7a989680@news.earthlink.net> <36ad3220.600584905@news1.ibm.net> <MPG.111645caa020e44d989690@news.earthlink.net> <78l1v5$hat@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <36ae4ece.6768737@netnews>`

```
The "if alphabetic" test was to handle such cases as
   "Able was I, ere I saw able"
where a comma exists in the input field.  If you are assured that this never
happens, then you wouldn't need to do it.
```
