###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help needed

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36aeccfa.36528585@netnews>`
- **References:** `<78ii2r$5tj@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <MPG.1116c468508e9b7a989680@news.earthlink.net> <36ad3220.600584905@news1.ibm.net> <MPG.111645caa020e44d989690@news.earthlink.net> <78l1v5$hat@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <36ae4ece.6768737@netnews> <78mgm1$558@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
'Twas Wed, 27 Jan 1999 01:53:25 -0600, when "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>The "if alphabetic" test was to handle such cases as
>   "Able was I, ere I saw able"
>where a comma exists in the input field.  If you are assured that this never
>happens, then you wouldn't need to do it.

 But after your alphabetic test, what do you know?  If it's alphabetic,
you need to squeeze out the spaces, make it all the same case, then
comapre to the reverse.  If it isn't alphabetic it's exactly the same
except you remove the non-alphabetic characters as well as spaces.  Why
would you write two different routines, ie, your saying that "A man, a
plan, a canal: Panama" and "A man a plan a canal Panama" should be handled
by different routines.  Why?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help needed

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78mshu$aoa@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<78ii2r$5tj@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <MPG.1116c468508e9b7a989680@news.earthlink.net> <36ad3220.600584905@news1.ibm.net> <MPG.111645caa020e44d989690@news.earthlink.net> <78l1v5$hat@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <36ae4ece.6768737@netnews> <78mgm1$558@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <36aeccfa.36528585@netnews>`

```

Randall Bart wrote in message <36aeccfa.36528585@netnews>...
>'Twas Wed, 27 Jan 1999 01:53:25 -0600, when "William M. Klein"
><wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>where a comma exists in the input field.  If you are assured that this
never
>>happens, then you wouldn't need to do it.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>by different routines.  Why?

I am not claiming that this is the best approach - what I was answering was
an earlier part of handling all the contingincies.  I *hate* having to loop
thru all the characters - when their are verbs to do what is needed.  In the
case where there is no punctuation issues, then

  INSPECT TALLYING (of the REVERSED item) can count all the leading
(originally trailing) spaces
     and
  Then just compare
      If  Function Reverse (Function Upper-Case (field-a:
length-without-spaces) =
                                           Function Uppder-Case (field-a:
length without-spaces)

to see if the two are equivalent.  In the case of punctuation, then yes, I
think you need to go "looping thru" the field to find and remove them.  My
GUESS is that if I had a real need to do this task, then if I was told that
the input MIGHT have punctuation, I would probably use the loop technique to
get rid of punctuation - and to count trailing spaces.  If I was
"guaranteed" that my input would never have punctuation, I would probably
use the INPSECT and REVERSE technqiue.   But the IF ALPHABETIC test was put
there "just in case" what I was told, turned out to be a lie. (and how many
times have we ALL run into that one!)

>
>--
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>l    |/ MS^7=6/28/107   http://members.aol.com/PanicYr00/Sequence.html
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help needed

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<D%Cr2.2322$VB.3389710@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<78ii2r$5tj@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <MPG.1116c468508e9b7a989680@news.earthlink.net> <36ad3220.600584905@news1.ibm.net> <MPG.111645caa020e44d989690@news.earthlink.net> <78l1v5$hat@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
>I submitted a "simplistic" solution to this problem earlier, but I did want
>to mention what would be needed to do a "more fool proof" solution - still
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>a  palindrome


As we discovered in an earlier thread, FUNCTION REVERSE seems to
be slow on many compilers.  I propose the following fairly robust
solution, which does not use FUNCTION REVERSE.  It recognizes
"Madam, I'm Adam." and 1234321 as palindromes.

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.    PALINDROME.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  REVERSE-AREA.
           03  RA-SUB                  PIC  9(04) COMP.
           03  RA-SUB1                 PIC  9(04) COMP.
           03  RA-SUB2                 PIC  9(04) COMP.
           03  RA-POS2                 PIC  9(04) COMP.
           03  RA-SIZE                 PIC  9(04) COMP.
           03  RA-TEXT                 PIC  X(50).
           03  RA-TEMP1                PIC  X(50).
           03  RA-TEMP2                PIC  X(50).
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       PALINDROME-TEST.
           DISPLAY "Palindrome test, enter text".
           ACCEPT RA-TEXT.
           MOVE 1                        TO RA-SUB1.
           MOVE FUNCTION LENGTH(RA-TEXT) TO RA-SUB2.
           MOVE 0                        TO RA-SIZE.
           PERFORM VARYING RA-SUB FROM 1 BY 1
                   UNTIL (RA-SUB > FUNCTION LENGTH(RA-TEXT) )
               IF (RA-TEXT(RA-SUB:1) NOT = SPACE)
                   AND
                  ( (RA-TEXT(RA-SUB:1) ALPHABETIC)
                     OR
                    (RA-TEXT(RA-SUB:1) NUMERIC) )
                   MOVE FUNCTION UPPER-CASE(RA-TEXT(RA-SUB:1) )
                                TO RA-TEMP1(RA-SUB1:1)
                                   RA-TEMP2(RA-SUB2:1)
                   ADD 1 TO RA-SUB1 RA-SIZE
                   SUBTRACT 1 FROM RA-SUB2
               END-IF
           END-PERFORM.
           IF (RA-SIZE ZERO)
               DISPLAY "No text"
           ELSE
               ADD 1 TO RA-SUB2
               IF (RA-TEMP1(1:RA-SIZE) = RA-TEMP2(RA-SUB2:RA-SIZE) )
                   DISPLAY "Text is a palindrome"
               ELSE
                   DISPLAY "Text is not a palindrome"
               END-IF
           END-IF.
```

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00

```
One big advantage of this approach is that when a requirement changes,
your fix will be easy and work.  If you used functions based upon
assumptions, a rewrite might be necessary.  My proposal was similar, it
did not create the two strings but simply stopped when a non-match
character was found.  Either way, maintenance is easy when someone
changes the requirements.

That is very important to me.

> As we discovered in an earlier thread, FUNCTION REVERSE seems to
> be slow on many compilers.  I propose the following fairly robust
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: Help needed

- **From:** yzhen@hpis.com
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78lran$sc8$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<78ii2r$5tj@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In article <78ii2r$5tj@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>,
  "Judy & Al" <al-judy@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
>     The question:  how would you determine if a field contained a word that
> was a palindrome (a word, phrase or sentence that reads that same forwards
> and backwards, eg. mom, noon, A man a plan a canal panama)?

test-begin.
  if pal-fld = spaces
     go to test-empty.

  move 1 to p1.
  move !length(pal-fld) to p2.
test-loop.
  perform with test before varying p1 by 1 until pal-fld(p1:1) not = space.
  perform with test before varying p2 by -1 until pal-fld(p2:1) not = space.
  if p1 >= p2
     go to test-true.

  if pal-fld(p1:1) not = pal-fld(p2:1)
     go to test-false.

  add 1 to p1.
  subtract 1 from p2.
  go to test-loop.

test-empty.
test-true.
test-false.


Sincerely,
Rex Young
yzhen@hpis.com

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help needed

- **From:** NoLonger@ix.netcom.com (OldUncleMe)
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36ae85fc.6589833@news>`
- **References:** `<78ii2r$5tj@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <78lran$sc8$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
On Wed, 27 Jan 1999 01:49:13 GMT "yzhen@hpis.com" posted to "comp.lang.cobol" about:
"Re: Help needed" :

-->test-begin.
-->  if pal-fld = spaces
-->     go to test-empty.
-->
-->  move 1 to p1.
-->  move !length(pal-fld) to p2.
-->test-loop.
-->  perform with test before varying p1 by 1 until pal-fld(p1:1) not = space.
-->  perform with test before varying p2 by -1 until pal-fld(p2:1) not = space.
-->  if p1 >= p2
-->     go to test-true.
-->
-->  if pal-fld(p1:1) not = pal-fld(p2:1)
-->     go to test-false.
-->
-->  add 1 to p1.
-->  subtract 1 from p2.
-->  go to test-loop.
-->
-->test-empty.
-->test-true.
-->test-false.
-->
-->
-->Sincerely,
-->Rex Young

This has been an interesting thread for (esp. for beginners.)  I follow your code,
assuming that the last three paragraphs, test-etc. are unfilled with final code.  But
I have not seen this sort of notation before:

>>>>>>>>>move !length(pal-fld) to p2.<<<<<<<<<<<

Is the exclam. a legitimate compilable feature of Cobol?  What does it do?  It seems
like you're using it to denote a function.  Are there more 'replacement' symbols such
as this?  TIA,                      TS


"Come to think of it, there are already a million monkeys on a million
 typewriters, and Usenet is NOTHING like Shakespeare." Blair Houghton
*********No generalization is wholly true, not even this one**********
++++++****tauceti****@****abraxis****.****com****for***email****++++++
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help needed

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OiLy8TnS#GA.308@upnetnews03>`
- **References:** `<78ii2r$5tj@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <78lran$sc8$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36ae85fc.6589833@news>`

```
Hopefully you won't see (or write) much of this sort of notation in the
future, either!  Since the advent of structured coding (i.e., coding without
GO TO constructs), such examples have been called UNstructured code.  I also
note that there is also a slight defect in the in-line PERFORM statements -
they have no END-PERFORM clauses.

#### ↳ Re: Help needed

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36AF6672.30B3EBE6@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<78ii2r$5tj@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
My own stab at it (we don't have functions on our compiler yet) is as follows:
"First accept the text into a character array specifying either 'upper' or
'lower'
so all characters would be converted to the same case.  In a perform loop from
1 to the
array size I test each character for being alphabetic and, if it was, increment

a second subscript and move it to a second character array.  That would remove
all spaces and punctuation as well as provide a count of the number of
characters.
A second subscript would be initialized to that count while half that count
would
be the upper value for another loop.  In the last perform loop, the character
in the
2nd array referenced by the primary subscript would be compared with the
character in that array referenced by the 2nd subscript.  If they matched, I
would
subtract 1 from the 2nd subscript for the next pass, if not I would set a flag
for a
'don't match' condition and exit the loop."

Judy & Al wrote:

> I was being interviewed by a C,C++ Technical person today for a job as a
> COBOL programmer.  We didn't speak the same language, but he asked me a
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

I'd have to question the usefulness of a book without ANSI 85 syntax
in it. My usual rule of thumb for an instant discard is if it has
intrinsic functions in it or not. I can't remember the book I read but
it had a chapter on 'The Year 2000' problem but didn't mention the
intrinsic functions at all. Doh!

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78idr1$d54$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
In this example:

 01 EC01 BLANK SCREEN BACKGROUND-COLOR 7 FOREGROUND-   COLOR 0.
    02 LINE 10 COLUMN 01 VALUE "DSAFGDSFGDSFGDSFGDSF".
    02 LINE 11 COLUMN 01 VALUE "DSFGHDSFGDSFGDSFGGDSFG".
    02 LINE 12 COLUMN 01 VALUE "DSFGHDSFGDSFGDSFGGDSFG".
    02 LINE 13 COLUMN 01 VALUE "DSFGHDSFGDSFGDSFGGDSFG".
    02 LINE 14 COLUMN 01 VALUE "DSFGHDSFGDSFGDSFGGDSFG".
    02 LINE 15 COLUMN 01 VALUE "DSFGHDSFGDSFGDSFGGDSFG".
    02 LINE 16 COLUMN 01 VALUE "DSFGHDSFGDSFGDSFGGDSFG".
    02 LINE 16 COLUMN 78 PIC X TO PARADA.
 01 EC02.
    02 BLANK LINE LINE 11.
    02 BLANK LINE LINE 13.
    02 BLANK LINE LINE 15.
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
 DATA-INPUT  SECTION.
       DISPLAY EC01.
       ACCEPT EC01.
       DISPLAY EC02.

I can�t  clear the lines 11, 13 and 15,  when execute DISPLAY EC02.

Anybody know why?

Many Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Blank Line in Fujitsu Cobol V4.

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<X7lr2.2587$oi.2789151@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<78idr1$d54$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
Euromercante wrote:
>In this example:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>I can�t  clear the lines 11, 13 and 15,  when execute DISPLAY EC02.

Try:

 01 EC02.
    02 LINE 11 COLUMN 01 BLANK LINE.
    02 LINE 13 COLUMN 01 BLANK LINE.
    02 LINE 15 COLUMN 01 BLANK LINE.
```

Ok, I have tried this for about a year, and I doubt I'll get any
takers, but I am a programmer with lots of education and no
experience. I am looking for a position in Baltimore. The last
position I had turned out the recruiter was fired, and I got 'the big
gooseegg' for my work of 3 months. I am simply looking for minimum
wage type work in the Baltimore area. I've been programming for 15
years, but only as a hobby. Anyone want to offer me a job, and test my
skills? I've gone through the yellow pages, and called up almost every
number under data, compu, prog, mainframe, and mini. The jobs just
aren't there. I have the skills, strategies, and even source
documenting my work. I just don't have the connections.

I will do y2k work, but I request that if the method is sliding
window, that a seperate module for subtract and add, and different
formats (julian(yyday), mmddyyyy, yyyymmdd, ect) be coded by someone
else.
(division of labor and all)


something simple like
call mmddyy-subtract		by reference
				mdy-variable
				mdy-variable-from
				mdy-variable-giving


Thanks for your time.

- **From:** Jan Lend <janlend@notayahoo.com>

One wonders just how sincere you are in your search. It is hardly
appropriate for an entry-level programmer to attempt to dictate the way
software will be developed by his prospective employer.

I would never recommend the hiring of someone showing such tendencies.
Entry-level supplicants need to show an absolute willingness to do
whatever is asked of them, grunt-work though it might be, since that
first foot-in-the-door is so difficult to find.

Just my 2 cents,

Jan Lend

G Moore wrote in message <36ae1d7b.1335776@nntp.ix.netcom.com>...
>Ok, I have tried this for about a year, and I doubt I'll get any
>takers, but I am a programmer with lots of education and no
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>documenting my work. I just don't have the connections.

Um, forgive me for asking this, but did you try the classified ads instead
of the yellow pages?  That's where I usually look for job offers.
I can't understand it, though.  You're not being able to find a job.  I
would think that if you're willing to work as a programmer for minimum wage,
heck, what is that now, $4.75 or $5.25, then you would be snapped up in a
second.

I'll admit that I would work for minimum wage only at a fast-food restaurant
(etc.).

As general advice, get someone who can write with good grammer to go over
your resume before you submit your resume.

I'm not doing a newsgroup slam over online grammar here, and am in no way
shape or from trying to make you feel assaulted, only wondering if your
resume is in the same state as this post.

I can almost view this post as an actual resume.  Really.  I mean it.  You
have come to a public forum with many professional COBOL programmers
respesenting many segements of the industry (some of these people have been
published in major commerical COBOL books).

I think you might have started out by not mentioning all the problems you've
had.  I wouldn't do it at a real job interview, and so it seems
inappropriate to do it online.

I could go on, but what's the point?  The atmosphere of comp.lang.cobol is a
business like one (with only occasional off topic posts that go off into
never-never land and external spams).  I'm going to loosely relate it to an
actual work environment where you might actually get an interview (ok, very
loosely).  Judging from the reaction of some of the other people here, it
makes me take to idle speculation about the sort of reaction you have
received from real job interviews.


I will ask you--and from me at least, I assure you that I am not joking or
baiting you--how your past job interviews have gone?  What did you wear?
Was the resume prepared by a professional (or at least professional
looking)?  Did you tell them how often you han't succeeded?  Did you make
suggestions about how you wanted to develop/program or about how you wanted
the prospective employer to do development?

I'd really like to know.  (No, I'm not joking or attacking you.)



>I will do y2k work, but I request that if the method is sliding
>window, that a seperate module for subtract and add, and different
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks for your time.

#### ↳ Re: programmer seeks entry level job in baltimore, maryland

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36ad2f41.599849505@news1.ibm.net>`

```
On Mon, 25 Jan 1999 06:26:30 GMT, gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com (G Moore)
wrote:


>I will do y2k work, but I request that if the method is sliding
>window, that a seperate module for subtract and add, and different
>formats (julian(yyday), mmddyyyy, yyyymmdd, ect) be coded by someone
>else.
>(division of labor and all)

*THIS* sums up your problem with obtaining employment - if I may be so
blunt.

Let's say that my boss came to me and said you will use such and such
a method for such and such a calculation.  Go back and find every
place you used your own code and make it conform to this standard.  I
would do it.  Without hesitation or question.  You see *I* work for
him.  I can SUGGEST methods and Ideas - but I can NEVER dictate terms,
methods, etc.  This is the essence of being an employEE. 

I can never say (nor would I) ---

Sorry boss, that work is beneath me, I won't do it.  I can' t even
say, Sorry boss, that's too simple, can we have someone else do it?
Even that suggestion leads to the conception -even if not true - if
Prima Donna - and no one wants to work with a Prima Donna.  (Please
forgive my spelling).
```

Ten years ago I was in your position, doing an intensive 4-week COBOL
training course with *no* prior experience of programming.  I didn't find
it easy to understand!  The book I bought to help me was 'COBOL
Programming' by Stern and Stern.  Can't remember the exact title, but Stern
and Stern is a canonical work, well-known to COBOL programmers.  The names
of the authors should be enough to locate it.

called Reflexion 4. I think this is a terminal emulation program. Does
anybody know anything about this?

Do I need to run this program?

## Thread: Unfeasibly large COBOL programmer salaries — 15 this month / 44 total

### Unfeasibly large COBOL programmer salaries

- **From:** "Rory Moore" <rory@hahn.co.za>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36af3b9e.0@news1.mweb.co.za>`

```
I have seen estimates of pay rates for COBOL programmers working on the Y2K
issue in the last six months of this year ranging from $500 per hour to
GBP9500 per day.  Are these estimates mere dingdong, or is there something
in them?

Rory
rory@hahn.co.za
```

#### ↳ Re: Unfeasibly large COBOL programmer salaries

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78nf36$uu5$1@callisto.clark.net>`
- **References:** `<36af3b9e.0@news1.mweb.co.za>`

```
In article <36af3b9e.0@news1.mweb.co.za>, Rory Moore <rory@hahn.co.za> wrote:
>I have seen estimates of pay rates for COBOL programmers working on the Y2K
>issue in the last six months of this year ranging from $500 per hour to
>GBP9500 per day.  Are these estimates mere dingdong, or is there something

There is nothing in my experience which reflects such a salary, no...
quite the reverse; I got a raise in rate on my last contract-change which
was from a Y2K project to non-Y2K work.

DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Unfeasibly large COBOL programmer salaries

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36AF50D6.7F707ACE@home.com>`
- **References:** `<36af3b9e.0@news1.mweb.co.za> <78nf36$uu5$1@callisto.clark.net>`

```
docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
> 
> In article <36af3b9e.0@news1.mweb.co.za>, Rory Moore <rory@hahn.co.za> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

Y2K work is rather boring and often farmed out to people who don't know
as much about the enterprise - contractors generally make more so Y2K
salaries may be exaggerated due to that.  The Y2K problem has increased
demand for programmers, but either Computerworld or PC WEEK had an
article recently about how people were predicting $100/hour which was
way off the mark.  I don't remember what the salaries they were
discussing, I think in the $50k range.

But to see current salaries, search pcweek or computerworld.  The
following is a current article:
http://www.computerworld.com/home/features.nsf/all/980907mgt
```

#### ↳ Re: Unfeasibly large COBOL programmer salaries

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v7Kr2.2412$VB.3640088@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<36af3b9e.0@news1.mweb.co.za>`

```
Rory Moore wrote:
>I have seen estimates of pay rates for COBOL programmers working on the Y2K
>issue in the last six months of this year ranging from $500 per hour to
>GBP9500 per day.  Are these estimates mere dingdong, or is there something
>in them?

I haven't seen anything near $500 an hour, but I just had three different
programmers turn me down when I offered them several months Y2K work at
$100 per hour, telecommuting from home!  I know for a fact that none of
them is making near that, and one of them was only 24 years old with a
year's programming experience and no degree.  I'm still trying to get my
jaw off the floor.

P.S. - Please don't send me job requests.  Like the Little Red Hen, in
disgust, I have decided to do it myself (and keep all the money). :-)
```

The estimates are bullshit. Name any company which pays the programmer
$500 an hour. They may set aside $500/ hour, but programmers never see
that, and I doubt recruiters see that. Doesn't matter, because I'll
never see that anyways. Hell, I tried to get paid for tutoring, no
dice. I wrote y2k fixes, and the recruiter mysteriously was already
fired. Maybe it's $500/ hour AFTER the project is done.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unfeasibly large COBOL programmer salaries

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MWJs2.1351$Xl5.1376067@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<36af3b9e.0@news1.mweb.co.za> <36b16be4.814098@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <78u6dp$vt$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`

```
darnold02@sprynet.com wrote:
>and i thought my company was bad charging $80 per hour programming fees
>while paying the programmers $15.00 per hour (tops). somebody's making
>a lot of money, but i'm pretty sure it isn't us programmers. : )

There are two sides to this, of course, and I have spent time on both
sides.  But I would have to say that if I was paying a programmer $15
and charging a client $80 for his time, I couldn't sleep.  'Fairness'
depends a lot on the specific contract, and who the programmer is.
For 1099 work around here, a contract is considered 'fair' when the
consulting firm gets 20%-40% and the programmer gets the rest.  I
usually get about 25%, sometimes only 10%, but never over 33%, when a
programmer works for me.  Even 25% may sound high to some people, but
consider that the programmer doesn't have to hunt down the business,
and takes virtually no legal risk.  Believe me, it is a serious thing
to put your neck (reputation, home, etc.) on the line for many hundred
thousand dollars, and know that it could be chopped off by a contract
programmer doing something stupid that you couldn't help and didn't
even know about.  When real estate sales people sell a house they
typically get only half what their firm is paid, and they do all the
work, even finding the sellers and the buyers.  I think the programmer
should get certainly more than half, but 90% is probably too much.  I
would have to think about taking on a contract again that I only got
10%.  The risk and trouble is just too great to make it worthwhile.
It would have to be programmers that I knew well and trusted, and even
then it would be 'iffy'.  If I subcontract to somebody else, I don't
care what they make, as long as what they pay me is reasonable.  If I
did not help get their contract for them, then I have no claim on that
money, other than to pay me what I agreed to work for.  What they get
is between them and the client.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unfeasibly large COBOL programmer salaries

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-01-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<790ku1$rnb@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
Listen to this man, he knows of what he speaks.  I just got
bit for over a grand on this issue. (*sigh*) Admittedly, it was
my own fault in a way. (Too much work came in too quickly to 
handle.) 

And I still owe some $$ out on this one. (*sigh*)

-Paul

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unfeasibly large COBOL programmer salaries

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<791uut$q3g$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36af3b9e.0@news1.mweb.co.za> <36b16be4.814098@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <78u6dp$vt$1@juliana.sprynet.com> <MWJs2.1351$Xl5.1376067@news1.mia> <790ku1$rnb@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
Yes, Judson's figures are about the same as I
would use.  One thing that affects rate quite a bit
is just who finances the deal.  Sometimes the programmer
gets paid when the timesheet is signed, and sometimes the
programmer gets paid when the end customer pays
the bill.  If the "agency" is paying the programmer
regularly, as soon as time sheets are signed, then
that is worth at least an additional 10-15%, regardless
of who got the contract originally.

(through an agency/consulting firm) are running around $50 to $55 per
hour.  Unfortunately, this rate range has remained rather "stagnant" for

$80 per hour by now, but that has yet to happen.

<a id="t-61ea8f2e96"></a>
## Thread: Opinion on Cobol Evolution. — 4 msgs

### Opinion on Cobol Evolution.

- **From:** "Carlos Garcia" <enigma@mail.telepac.pt>
- **Date:** 1999-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78ldkc$9cr$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
Hi everybody,

I have already ask this but i am going to ask it again.
I have hundreds of clients working with my programs developed with Micro
Focus Cobol/3 in DOS. I want to evolve to Windows 95 Programing. I already
worked with Visual Basic, Delphi and Visual Fox Pro and received a demo
version of Micro Focus NETEXPRESS. I must say this: Are they joking ? Is
this for seriou ? What is this ? Poor and slow Development capabilities.
Outrageous Price !!!. Report tools ? NONE !!! Object Oriented ?!?
With the new RAD tools existing like those mentioned before i can develop a
program in 5 days with NETEXPRESS i would surely take, for the same program
a Month !
I tried Fujitsu Cobol but didn't like either. Slow and not very Windows MDI
Oriented.
Now, with all this what do i do ? I would like to evolve to Visual Basic but
i wouldn't like to convert all my clients data files. Does anyone knowes of
any ODBC driver to Micro Focus ISAM files or OLE DB ? Anyone has any
sugestion on what to do ?
Please leave your opinion on this Matter in this Newsgroup.

Thanks and sorry for anything.
Carlos Garcia
Enigma@mail.telepac.pt
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<78ldkc$9cr$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```

>i wouldn't like to convert all my clients data files. Does anyone knowes of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Use the COBOL of your choice and use COBOL sp2 for your front end.  I
know I sound like a broken record on this, but even my die hard COBOL
mentor from the past has finally given in.  He had purchased Visual
Realia (I forgot to list that as a GUI solution in my post listing
them) and was sorely disappointed.  He tried and tried to make it
work, but development was just too slow.  Over the last two years (has
it been that long?) I have tried to get him to at least TRY Sp2.  He
did, he's hooked, he bought it and he's productive again.  This stuff
really works.

http://www.flexus.com is their web site - you can dload a version for
your current compiler and give it a shot.  (I don't work for them).
```

Hi Carlos

Take a look at "Casegen COBOL for Windows" at www.casegen.co.uk. We don't
have all the millions of features that VB has - but your programs will run
very fast and you will build them very quickly.

Casegen generates standard Ansi Cobol '85 including all the Windows calls
and is a completely open (Object Based) architecture using standard Cobol
(plus our EXEC GUI to make Windows calls easy). It is compiled and tested
using Fujitsu V3 (supplied) or Fujitsu V4 (from www.adtools.com).

We have full details plus testamonials available - just email us for
details.

Until February 7th 1999, Casegen is available for just 50 USD (30 GBP).
After that the price will rise to 80 USD. We have a new "professional"
version out soon which will be available as an upgrade to all our customers
for 50 USD.

Regards

Simon Cordingley

#### ↳ Re: Opinion on Cobol Evolution.

- **From:** "PAULO CRUZ" <scruz_paulo@mail.teleweb.pt>
- **Date:** 1999-01-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<792el5$3mq$1@fenix.maxitel.pt>`
- **References:** `<78ldkc$9cr$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
Hi Carlos
Working with cobol (chiefly RM/Liant tools-Unix+Dos+Windows environment) for
the last ten years and, although not being flooded by such an amount of
clients as yourself, I myself have been dealing with the same problems,
which amount to two:
1.GUI, for several years now I've given up all the accept/display stuff
thanks to a pannelling procedural tool/extension which allows you to design
controls (pulldown menus, radio&push-buttons, list boxes and the like, which
work alike in a character or graphical interface) and retrieve their events
for appropriate prodedures - but this is by no means a true Windows
solution (however it is a true event driven solution) , yet I like it very
much. Lately, cobol compiler producers have been releasing VB-like
development tools, which one tends to look at (at least I do) with some
suspicion. Other suppliers (like the previous posters) are also offering
tools for the development of true GUI, which one can 'paint' cobol apps
with, but if you choose to steer towards VB (at least for some purposes, as
I'm doing), then the second question arises...
2.ODBC, yes one can easily have cobol apps running and maintaining their
files and at the same time have VB client programs (or Access or Whatever)
accessing (and, why not ?, maintaining those files). In a mixed environment,
with different kind of needs on the various levels of users/managers having
hands on the sistem, I stand by such a mixture approach and am, right now in
the process of installing ODBC acess
to cobol files (they happen to be RM files but could as well be Microfocus'
or any other dialects'): the tool that allows this is Relativity. I already
tested it and so far it works wonderfully. Further development is on the
way, but as for now I'm happy with it.

As a conclusion on the topic and - maybe - lighting up some fire, I would
say that programmers usualy blame languages for their own inefficiencies and
the quest for a graphically assisted language is sometimes driven by the
need of hiding those weaknesses.

PSCruz
(p.s. as a newcomer to the NG, I hope my posting didn't fall off the rules)


<78ldkc$9cr$1@duke.telepac.pt>...
>Hi everybody,
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…

but
>i wouldn't like to convert all my clients data files. Does anyone knowes of
>any ODBC driver to Micro Focus ISAM files or OLE DB ? Anyone has any
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

           05  LastName    PIC X(10) VALUE "Anonymous".

When I am running the program with the following line, I get

#### ↳ Re: MVS Cobol help needed

- **From:** daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch
- **Date:** 1999-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78kk8j$r1b$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<78k49s$ee9$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In article <78k49s$ee9$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>,
  mfeinshtein@my-dejanews.com wrote:
> Dear sirs,
>
> I'm just new to Cobol.I have some problems with running a very simple COBOL
> program on MVS(OS/390).
>
<snip>
>        FD TestFile
>            RECORDING MODE IS F
>            BLOCK CONTAINS   5 RECORDS
>            RECORD CONTAINS 30 CHARACTERS.
<snip>
>            OPEN OUTPUT TestFile.
>            PERFORM WriteFourRecords.
<snip>
> //PHONLST  DD  DSNAME=MAXIM.PHONES,UNIT=SYSDA,VOL=SER=NAV003,
> //             SPACE=(TRK,(20,5,1)),DISP=(MOD,PASS,CATLG),
> //             DCB=(BLKSIZE=400),RECFM=F

I think that you rather get a System Abend 0C4 than anything else. Look at
SYSOUT and CEEDUMP, not at the Console!

First of all, it's a JCL-Problem: Do not try to write in a library without a
member-name! Do not try to write blocked in an unblocked file!

And before all: ALWAYS ask the file-status before you try to write in a file!

So change your code the following way:

BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS
...
OPEN OUTPUT TESTFILE
IF PHONEFILESTATUS NOT = 00
 THEN DISPLAY PHONEFILESTATUS
 ELSE
   PERFORM
   ....
END-IF
GOBACK.
//             SPACE=(TRK,(20,5)),DISP=(MOD,PASS,CATLG),
//             DCB=(LRECL=30,BLKSIZE=300),RECFM=FB

Delete your existing file first! If it still does not work (I tried it, it
worked at my installation) repost your problem and show us your entire SYSOUT!

Cheers

Daniel

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MVS Cobol help needed

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-01-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be4d5a$bab2fbc0$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`
- **References:** `<78k49s$ee9$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36AE1444.CA9BBC95@yahoo.com>`

```
Joseph Kohler <joe_kohler@yahoo.com> wrote in article
<36AE1444.CA9BBC95@yahoo.com>...
> 
> For one thing you compile a program names SEQWRITE and link-edit it to a
module
> names COBTEST.  I don't think you can do that and this MAY be causing
your
> problem.

No, that is OK and not the cause of the problem.  As others has pointed out
the messages given refers to an environment incompatibility, which leads to
the U4083 abend in LE.  Have the system programmer check the content of
CEE.SCEELKED and SYS1.SCEERUN.  When this has been resolved there is still
the error with RECFM and BLKSIZE which is incompatible between the program
and the JCL.  This must be resolved otherwise the file will not open
(status key 39), and the WRITE will not work (status key 48).

Gunnar.
```

#### ↳ Re: MVS Cobol help needed

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78l33h$i8g@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<78k49s$ee9$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
There have been several other replies to your question - all of which MAY be
real problems. HOWEVER, from the message you are getting, let me point out
the following problems:

A)  Do not name a "user" program "COBTEST".  This is the name of an (older)
IBM supplied utility - and you may run into some conflicts.

B) In your link-edit step, you have
   CEE.SCEELKED
but in your "go" step you have,
   SYS1.SCEERUN
Where one starts with "CEE" and the other starts with "SYS1", it is possible
(in fact likely) that they are not a "matching" set of libraries.  Verify
that they do match or try finding a SYS1.SCEELKED or a CEE.SCEERUN and see
if this helps.

C) The message that you are getting indicates that the "run-time"
environment support that you are using (SCEERUN) doesn't match the
environment where you are actually running.  My guess is that this is caused
by the absence of the "correct" boot-strap module (see above) but it COULD
also be caused by your shop's SCEERUN actually being the CICS run-time
library rather than the batch one.  I doubt this strongly, but if everything
else fails, ask whether this might be the case in your shop.

D) Finally, you mention "MVS(OS/39)".  Can you confirm that you are REALLY
running under OS/390 rather than MVS.  There are some (minor) differences in
how you should be specifying LE - if you are actually still using MVS rather
than OS/390.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: level numbers 77/78

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36B1C317.3780000@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<36AD9C41.578CD49B@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com> <36AE0CE8.6E38BD9A@home.com> <36AE0DC5.69844F77@home.com> <78s4fe$6tb$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
fneale@no1.com.au wrote:

> In article <36AE0DC5.69844F77@home.com>,
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> In fact, does anyone still use level 77?

I used 77 in regularly until about 10 years ago although it still is created
in a generated shell our shop uses.  Only use level 66 for an "after the fact"
redefinition of usually related fields necessary for debugging.  The record
layout is in a copy statement and the 66 level can be easily removed when a
program goes back into production.  My only occasion to use 78 is to hold a
count of the number of entries in a table defined later in the code.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: level numbers 77/78

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7Njs2.46$Fs.108988@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<36AD9C41.578CD49B@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com> <36AE0CE8.6E38BD9A@home.com> <36AE0DC5.69844F77@home.com> <78s4fe$6tb$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
fneale@no1.com.au wrote:
>
>Actually, that raises a point of curiosity. Does anyone out there
>actually use level 66?

Very infrequently.

>In fact, does anyone still use level 77?

Yes.  Although 01 is sufficient, most compilers I use handle an 01
level differently for storage and alignment purposes.  The Unisys
A Series stores 77 entries in the stack and 01 entries as an array.
On those machines, using a 77 is more efficient.  Other than that,
it is mostly a matter of habit; I prefer using 77 for autonomous
data items with a picture, and 01 for group items.  This provides
a visual clue which I like, and that was what they were created to
be.  I see no advantage in using 01 over 77 for autonomous data
items with a picture.
```

<a id="t-d228fb22c5"></a>
## Thread: REDEFINES with OCCURS — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### REDEFINES with OCCURS

- **From:** John Piggott <John_Piggott@compuserve.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36AE1EEA.5B4B@compuserve.com>`

```
I'm puzzled but the legality of following type of Data Division code:

  03  A   PIC 9  OCCURS 6.
  03  B  REDEFINES A   PIC X  OCCURS 6.

This seems to be valid on most compilers I used, e.g. OS/390 and most 
Win95 type platforms, so that I took it for granted this was valid until 
I ran into a compiler that doesn't accept it.  On checking the standards 
(right up to the new one) it doesn't seem to be valid after all.  Anyone 
know why it's accepted by so many?
```

#### ↳ Re: REDEFINES with OCCURS

- **From:** "kennet" <kennet@post11.tele.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78ksp1$57g2$1@news-inn.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<36AE1EEA.5B4B@compuserve.com>`

```

Kennet

John Piggott skrev i meddelelsen <36AE1EEA.5B4B@compuserve.com>...

>

##### ↳ ↳ Re: REDEFINES with OCCURS

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36AE0E3A.BA4AE862@home.com>`
- **References:** `<36AE1EEA.5B4B@compuserve.com> <78ksp1$57g2$1@news-inn.inet.tele.dk>`

```
Actually, not all compilers will even accept a redefines at the 01
level.  But they all accept redefines at 02-49 levels.

kennet wrote:
> 
> I thought all redefines had to be at the 01 level?
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> >  03  B  REDEFINES A   PIC X  OCCURS 6.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: REDEFINES with OCCURS

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<W2rr2.3600$t85.3085578@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<36AE1EEA.5B4B@compuserve.com> <78ksp1$57g2$1@news-inn.inet.tele.dk> <36AE0E3A.BA4AE862@home.com>`

```
Actually, ALL standard COBOL compilers accept redefines at the 01 level,
if the 01 level is under an FD, and the redefines is implicit.

When you are redefining for tables like this:

 01  TABLE-VALUES.
     03  FILLER     PIC  X(05) VALUE "?????".
     03  FILLER     PIC  X(05) VALUE "?????".
     03  FILLER     PIC  X(05) VALUE "?????".
     03  FILLER     PIC  X(05) VALUE "?????".
 01  TABLE-ELEMENTS REDEFINES TABLE-VALUES.
     03  TABLE-ELEMENT  OCCURS 5 TIMES
                        PIC  X(05).

you should *NOT* redefine at the 01 level, because the above
error (should only be 4 OCCURS) will pass with no syntax error.
Do the redefines at a subordinate level, like this:

 01  TABLE-STUFF.
     03  TABLE-VALUES.
         05  FILLER     PIC  X(05) VALUE "?????".
         05  FILLER     PIC  X(05) VALUE "?????".
         05  FILLER     PIC  X(05) VALUE "?????".
         05  FILLER     PIC  X(05) VALUE "?????".
     03  TABLE-ELEMENTS REDEFINES TABLE-VALUES.
         05  TABLE-ELEMENT  OCCURS 5 TIMES
                            PIC  X(05).

and the above error will get a syntax error.  The reason for this
is that there is no requirement that 01 level entries which are
redefined be the same size.  If your OCCURS or your PIC sizes in
the two sections do not match, the compiler will not tell you this.
But if you redefine at a subordinate level, the redefining entry
cannot be larger, and the compiler will flag the table if it is.
Unfortunately, COBOL 85 does allow the redefining entry to be
smaller.  But you at least get a one-way check, which is better
than no check at all.  I have seen many such program bugs get
overlooked when redefining at the 01 level for this purpose.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: REDEFINES with OCCURS

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78n6p3$ukj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I'm working on a pgm right now (under VS Cobol II) where the redefining entry
is smaller than the original, for ligitimate reasons.  It does accept it, but
kicks out an informational msg to at least bring it to your attention.

In article <W2rr2.3600$t85.3085578@news1.mia>,
  "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:
<snip>

> smaller.  But you at least get a one-way check, which is better
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Ed Stevens
Nissan Motor Mfg. Corp., USA

http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: REDEFINES with OCCURS

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IzGr2.2364$VB.3511018@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<36AE1EEA.5B4B@compuserve.com> <78ksp1$57g2$1@news-inn.inet.tele.dk> <36AE0E3A.BA4AE862@home.com> <W2rr2.3600$t85.3085578@news1.mia> <78n6p3$ukj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I'm glad to hear about the warning.  Note that you never *have* to
create a smaller redefining entry, that's what FILLER is for. :-)

All the compilers I ever used before COBOL 85 required the entries
to be the exact same size, unless the redefines was at the 01 level.
I don't have a COBOL 74 or earlier standards manual to check if this
was a standard.  Perhaps someone can tell us.  But to me, we have
regressed here.  I am more concerned about the protection awarded by
that restriction than about saving a little time by not having to
use a FILLER.  It has not been a month since the last time I found a
table redefines error (in a client's program) which was not caught
because they redefined at the 01 level.  In the code I modify as a
consultant, that particular bug pops up very often.  I find them
because I specifically watch for table redefines at the 01 level.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: REDEFINES with OCCURS

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com
- **Date:** 1999-01-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78ps2g$7pm$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<36AE1EEA.5B4B@compuserve.com> <78ksp1$57g2$1@news-inn.inet.tele.dk> <36AE0E3A.BA4AE862@home.com> <W2rr2.3600$t85.3085578@news1.mia> <78n6p3$ukj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <IzGr2.2364$VB.3511018@news4.mia>`

```
Judson -

Perhaps I'm getting rusty (well, no "perhaps" to it -- I've been writing very
little cobol the last few years, and even less on the MVS system) but I don't
see where a restriction (requireing the redefineing area to be the same size
as the original) provides any protection.  The original resereved the address
space for the data; the REDEFINES just maps it differently.  Now,if the
situation were reversed -- REDEFINES larger than the original -- I can see
all kinds of "opportunities."  That situation is the same as when a
linkage-section 01-level item is defined larger than the storage actually
allocated by the calling program.  I've seen this several times in CICS
programs, and the results are not pretty.

- Ed Stevens
Opinions are mine and not necessarily those of my employer.
In article <IzGr2.2364$VB.3511018@news4.mia>,
  "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:
> I'm glad to hear about the warning.  Note that you never *have* to
> create a smaller redefining entry, that's what FILLER is for. :-)
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Ed Stevens
Nissan Motor Mfg. Corp., USA

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: REDEFINES with OCCURS

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Hx0s2.10$OS5.13759@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<36AE1EEA.5B4B@compuserve.com> <78ksp1$57g2$1@news-inn.inet.tele.dk> <36AE0E3A.BA4AE862@home.com> <W2rr2.3600$t85.3085578@news1.mia> <78n6p3$ukj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <IzGr2.2364$VB.3511018@news4.mia> <78ps2g$7pm$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com wrote:
>Judson -
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>programs, and the results are not pretty.

Ed, it is very simple.  When you define a data item with values intended
for use in a table, then redefine that data item in order to create a
table of OCCURS items, the tables should be the same size for three good
reasons.  If the OCCURS is too small (or too many values), the redefining
entry will be able to access unitialized storage.  If the OCCURS is too
great (or too few values) there will be values inaccessible using the
redefining entries.  If either of the table elements, the ones with the
values, or the redefining ones referencing the values, are accidentally
declared to be the wrong size, then you get an even worse situation,
for the references are then skewed in relation to the data.  I have seen
this sort of error repeated many, many times in many, many programs.  It
is unavoidable, unless you never create tables this way, or you are
perfect and make no coding errors.  On vary large and complex tables, it
is very likely to happen.

Please note that all of these errors would be caught if the compiler gave
a syntax error when the redefining entry was not the same size.  Using
COBOL 74 or earlier, you could place the REDEFINES clause on a level 02
through 49 and the compiler would do just that.  But beginning with COBOL
85, the restriction was relaxed so the redefining entry could be smaller.
This only gives you that safeguard in one direction: if the error makes
the redefining entry larger.  Since you are as likely to be in error one
way as another, we have lost some safeguard against this kind of error.
However, people who use redefines at the 01 level for this purpose, have
thrown out all possible help the compiler would give in this case anyway.
That's why I recommend not to do it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: REDEFINES with OCCURS

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6A0s2.11$OS5.15423@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<36AE1EEA.5B4B@compuserve.com> <78ksp1$57g2$1@news-inn.inet.tele.dk> <36AE0E3A.BA4AE862@home.com> <W2rr2.3600$t85.3085578@news1.mia> <78n6p3$ukj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <IzGr2.2364$VB.3511018@news4.mia> <78ps2g$7pm$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <Hx0s2.10$OS5.13759@news3.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
>...  If the OCCURS is too small (or too many values), the redefining
>entry will be able to access unitialized storage.  If the OCCURS is too
>great (or too few values) there will be values inaccessible using the
>redefining entries. ...

Sorry, I got that backwards. :-(
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: REDEFINES with OCCURS

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: REDEFINES with OCCURS

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78q693$4et$1@news.igs.net>`

What I always understood, and have found in experience,
is that most redefines of unequal length will be allowed
provided that the record is not larger than the record
being redefined.  Not sure what the standard says, but
that seems reasonable to me.

## Thread: NEWLINE STATEMENT??? — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### NEWLINE STATEMENT???

- **From:** "Jeffrey J. Parker" <jjp5421@garnet.acns.fsu.edu>
- **Date:** 1999-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78m1ge$498$1@news.fsu.edu>`

```
Is there a new line statement in COBOL to help in formatting output? I am a
novice C programmer trying to figure out COBOL with limited docs. Thanks for
any help.
Jeff
```

#### ↳ Re: NEWLINE STATEMENT???

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78mh4n$5c6@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<78m1ge$498$1@news.fsu.edu>`

```
Can you give us a little more idea of what type of "formatting" help you
want.  Certainly at one extreme (and it is FAR from new) you could use the
full Report Writer feature.  If you are looking for "formatting" of
individual fields/lines, you may want to look at the (still not new)
   STRING, UNSTRING, INSPECT verbs

As I say, depending on what you want, you may find it in Standard COBOL.
(Occasional posts to this NG are of the type,  "how do you code the
following C code in COBOL" - so that may be your best way to get the best
answer.
```

#### ↳ Re: NEWLINE STATEMENT???

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36af1619@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<78m1ge$498$1@news.fsu.edu>`

```
Jeffrey J. Parker wrote in message <78m1ge$498$1@news.fsu.edu>...
>Is there a new line statement in COBOL to help in formatting output? I am a
>novice C programmer trying to figure out COBOL with limited docs. Thanks
for
>any help.


No newline statement. The concept of lines really does not exist in std
Cobol.
On most systems a newline is automatically issued after each DISPLAY
statement.
So:
DISPLAY "Hello"
DISPLAY "World"
 would output two lines. If you include NO ADVANCING, no newline is output,
so:
DISPLAY "Hello " NO ADVANCING
DISPLAY "World"
would output one line.
Good luck
```

#### ↳ Re: NEWLINE STATEMENT???

- **From:** John Piggott <John_Piggott@compuserve.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36AF76E5.7A75@compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<78m1ge$498$1@news.fsu.edu>`

```
Jeff:

With the latest level of the COBOL Report Writer syntax, you just code a 
blank LINE clause, e.g.

    05  LINE + 1.
    05  LINE + 1.
      07  COL 25  VALUE "Next Heading".

This is not the best example perhaps because you simply have coded a 
single LINE + 2.  But it's used in cases where the blank line is optional 
(use PRESENT WHEN condition) or when you want a blank line before a 
table, e.g.

    05  LINE + 2.
    05  LINE + 1  OCCURS  2 TO 10 TIMES DEPENDING ON ... .

Jeffrey J. Parker wrote:
> 
> Is there a new line statement in COBOL to help in formatting output? I am a
> novice C programmer trying to figure out COBOL with limited docs. Thanks for
> any help.
> Jeff
```

#### ↳ Re: NEWLINE STATEMENT???

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36af25ae.95778922@news.enterprise.net>`
- **References:** `<78m1ge$498$1@news.fsu.edu>`

```
On Tue, 26 Jan 1999 22:28:59 -0500, "Jeffrey J. Parker"
<jjp5421@garnet.acns.fsu.edu> wrote:

>Is there a new line statement in COBOL to help in formatting output? I am a
>novice C programmer trying to figure out COBOL with limited docs. Thanks for
>any help.

Depends on what you are doing. There isn't one as such. 

       display "Hello"

will display Hello and return to the next line. Use the 'no advancing'
clause to *not* get a new line. Sit down, take a deep breath, and dig
out your manual and look up the syntax for the 'DISPLAY' verb.

If you're formatting strings for dialog boxes or some other kind of
api style string then you create your strings in much the same way as
C with a null at the end.
```

#### ↳ Re: NEWLINE STATEMENT???

- **From:** "Jeffrey J. Parker" <jjp5421@garnet.acns.fsu.edu>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78nnk6$rv3$1@news.fsu.edu>`
- **References:** `<78m1ge$498$1@news.fsu.edu>`

```
Everyone, thank you for the help.
I ended up solving my problem simply by making a separate record for each
line (KISS Keep It So Simple), but my understanding of COBOL is progressing
quickly. Thanks for steepening the learning curve for me.
Thanks again everyone (gotta love the internet)!
Jeff
```

```

<jjp5421@garnet.acns.fsu.edu> wrote:

<a id="t-1bec6c6bc0"></a>
## Thread: Cobol programming here I come — 6 this month / 7 total

### Cobol programming here I come

- **From:** "twoplus" <twoplus@downcity.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36add28e.0@news.cyberzone.net>`

```
I am about to begin a 14 week Cobol training program.  As an unemployed
individual this opportunity has been presented and I am excited about the
prospect.  I am A+ certified and have been on computers since IIe days.  But
have no experience in programming.  I consider myself a quick learner.
However, I am concerned about learning programming.  I suppose it is normal
to be concerned about learning something new.

A specific computer company is sponsoring this training (in fact they have
helped to design the training program) and will interview (no guaranteed
jobs) all of the students with a B or better grade at course completion.
The first class of this type in this area (limited to 15 students per class)
had placement of 90%.

I guess I am asking how this sounds to those of you who are Cobol
programmers?  Can you really learn enough in 14 weeks to actually perform
the job.  I am only looking for an entry level position from this (foot in
the door).  Any advice would be greatly appreciated.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol programming here I come

- **From:** "JERRY GAILEY" <gaileyje@westinghouse.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be497d$1fd56150$5f295ca8@l1342576>`
- **References:** `<36add28e.0@news.cyberzone.net>`

```
Sounds like a great opportunity. I'm assuming that the class is 8 hours a
day, 5 days a week for 14 weeks. That being the case, the course should be
more than adequate in preparing you for an entry level programming
position. Good luck and enjoy it.

#### ↳ Re: Cobol programming here I come

- **From:** "kennet" <kennet@post11.tele.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78kskp$3b22$1@news-inn.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<36add28e.0@news.cyberzone.net>`

```
I don't think that the company expects you to be able to
start programming on your own. They probably just want to make
sure that you have some basic programming skill before you start.
You'll need to know the specifics of their particular installation, and
that may be a lot tougher than learning how to program. Also, if
you get employed in a bank or so, you'll need to know a lot about
the business part. Noone in their right mind would just give you
a 14 week Cobol training program, and then expect you to perform
on your own. If they spend a lot of money on giving you basic
Cobol skills I'm sure they're going to follow up on it once you
start working.

Where I work (a Danish insurance company) even semiskilled
programmers are not expected to do much entirely on their own
for at least 6 months or so.

Hey, and we're all still learning, right?

Kennet

#### ↳ Re: Cobol programming here I come

- **From:** Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78n58n$t8n$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<36add28e.0@news.cyberzone.net>`

```
You can definately learn enough in 14 weeks to perform well in an entry level
position.  That's actually pretty much how I got into the business.  I was out
of work and applied for a bunch of different jobs thru the state civil service
commission.  One of the positions was "Programmer Analyst Trainee" and the
requirements were a college degree (any field) and high score on an aptitude
test.  Most of the agencies I interviewd were actually looking for some
experience and using the position to get someone at a low salary, but one
agency actually had an in-house training course, which was really nothing more
than supervised self-study.  For 3 months I did nothing but study cobol, jcl,
vsam, and cics, and when finished was ready to go to work.

In article <36add28e.0@news.cyberzone.net>,
  "twoplus" <twoplus@downcity.net> wrote:
> I am about to begin a 14 week Cobol training program.  As an unemployed
> individual this opportunity has been presented and I am excited about the
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Ed Stevens
Nissan Motor Mfg. Corp., USA

<a id="t-bdbbbda458"></a>
## Thread: Computeach International cobol course verdict ? — 2 this month / 4 total

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Computeach International cobol course verdict ?

- **From:** "Carpe_Diem_UK" <john@jwmiller.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78lk63$23h$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
I am just about to embark on a 600 hour distant learning course provided by
a UK based company by the name of Computeach International, in the hope of
the C & G 424 qualification it provides will give me a gateway into the
programming industry.

Before I part with my hard earned cash, I was wondering if anybody out there
has had dealings with the course and if they could give me an idea if it is
worth while or alternatively suggest any other UK based cobol courses.
```

#### ↳ Re: Computeach International cobol course verdict ?

- **From:** Jan Lend <janlend@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36AFE541.7DD59F91@yahoo.com>`
- **References:** `<78lk63$23h$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Do a lot of research first - talk to previous graduates; the UK
equivalent of the Better Business Bureau (any complaints lodged about
the company?); get hiring agencies opinion of the school; do some
internet searches of the company; and lastly, determine if you are the
kind of self-starter that will complete this course in isolation.

Good luck!!

JL.

## Thread: PowerCobol vs. Casegen — 3 msgs

### PowerCobol vs. Casegen

- **From:** "Jerry Peacock" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78kikj$ca4$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Any suggestions on which to use? The first thing we've noticed is that
PowerCobol loads forms very slowly, but we've not experimented yet
with Casegen. Comments appreciated.
```

#### ↳ Re: PowerCobol vs. Casegen

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36ae5a89.676477752@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<78kikj$ca4$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
On Tue, 26 Jan 1999 08:17:41 -0600, "Jerry Peacock"
<bismail@bisusa.com> wrote:

>Any suggestions on which to use? The first thing we've noticed is that
>PowerCobol loads forms very slowly, but we've not experimented yet
>with Casegen. Comments appreciated.
>

These are all the methods I am aware of to achieve a GUI using COBOL -
some are compiler specific.

AcuCOBOL GT
Fujitsu PowerCOBOL
MicroFocus Dialog System
IBM VisualAge COBOL
Objective COBOL
COBOL WOW
Mpact CSD
ETK
Casegen
COBOL SP2
Windows API calls
Visual Basic or other Language front end calling COBOL

There may be more - if I missed any I'm sorry, but post about it so I
can add it to my list.

My personal favorite is COBOL Sp2.  Why?

Compiler independent, simple call interface.  FAST execution time,
royalty free application distribution, Thin client cabable with
minimal application modifications.

Of the two you are asking about, CASEGEN is closer to the Windows API
than Fujitsu Power COBOL, so it should be faster, however screen
layout and design is easier with Power COBOL.
```

#### ↳ Re: PowerCobol vs. Casegen

- **From:** "Simon Cordingley" <simonc@casegen.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36af8a0c.0@mercury.nildram.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<78kikj$ca4$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Hi Jerry

First of all can I confess that I work for Casegen Systems?

We get this question lots of times and try hard to answer it truthfully.

If you need lots of different controls or lots of features, then PowerCobol
is hard to beat, especially Version 4.  If performance is important then you
need Casegen.

Also, if you need to make your own Windows calls - you can easily do that
with Casegen but I believe (correct me if I'm wrong Fujitsu) that you cannot
with Power Cobol.

Casegen is still on special offer until 7th February 1999 at 50 USD.  Please
email us for more details and testamonials.


Simon Cordingley
Casegen Systems Ltd

## Thread: MF Fileshare v2 Limits — 3 msgs

### MF Fileshare v2 Limits

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@ramtecjr.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<917374396.28289.0.nnrp-07.d4e45f36@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
I have been told that a fileshare server has a limit of 255 attached
clients.  I've looked (and looked) through the manuals and can't find any
reference to this except in the description of error 9/125 but I could
easily have missed it.

Does anybody know if this is a true limit or just a default which can be
changed?

We're working with mf cobol 4.0 and 4.1 on hp unix and aix.

We're not using fileshare for networking or roll forward recovery but just
for transaction commitment control with client and server on the same box.
We liked fileshare because, as it opens files exclusively, it allows file
sizes of over 1 gigabyte.

Does anyone know if commitment control works ok under the normal mf file
system just opening the files for shared access?

I guess we should have pushed our AS400 solution where the only worry about
file sizes and number of users is how much kit to buy.

tia
Rick
```

#### ↳ Re: MF Fileshare v2 Limits

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78ms1p$agq@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<917374396.28289.0.nnrp-07.d4e45f36@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
From  my sources at MF,

"From 4.0 onwards Fileshare allows up to 65535 simultaneous client
connections.

Transaction processing support is not provided outside of Fileshare."

Does this answer the question?

Note: (not from MF) - anything MF says, can  (and often is) modified when
you talk about the HP "port" of their product.  HP does funny things with
the Micro Focus product, so you may find what works under AIX (at the same
level) doesn't always work with HP.
```

<a id="t-211933c273"></a>
## Thread: Screen Save in MFCOBOL under UNIX — 1 msgs

### Re: Screen Save in MFCOBOL under UNIX

- **From:** "IriePeer" <Peter.Stoops.NoSpam@Cheops.be>
- **Date:** 1999-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be4910$44b57680$7b01a8c0@luc>`

```
I don't know under UNIX, but under DOS it's like this :

CALL X"B7" USING function,parameter,buffer.


Where :

function is a PIC 99 COMP-X which contains :
	0	to read characters from the screen
	1	to write characters to the screen
	2	to read attributes from the screen
	3	to write attributes to the screen
	4	to clear characters from the screen
	5	to clear attributes from the screen

parameter is a group item consisting of three data items :

	- a PIC 9(4) COMP-X field showing the length of the data to be read or
written

	- a PIC 9(4) COMP-X field giving the start position on the screen. Top
Left is 1, next line start at 81 etc.

	- a PIC 9(4) COMP-X field giving the start position in the buffer. The
starting buffer position is 1.

buffer is the COBOL data area. It's a PIX X(n) field and may be as small or
large as you need. An entire screen of text or attributes is 2000 bytes (80
* 25)

Maybe this also works under UNIX, you'll have to try it out.

Good luck,

Peter Stoops
```

<a id="t-719ec3d852"></a>
## Thread: RM/COBOL-85 : Problem again — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### RM/COBOL-85 : Problem again

- **From:** "popular Singnet" <popsinit@popular.com.sg>
- **Date:** 1999-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78jmlm$ma2$1@mawar.singnet.com.sg>`

```
I am currently RMCOBOL Version 5.13 program on Unix ATT System V.4.
When I activate one of the commonly used program, the system prompt with a
message;

RM/COBOL-85 : Operating System Initialization Error. OS Error Code: 13.

This program was executed on the data directory. However, when I run the
program under my $HOME directory, there was no message.
I cannot figure out what that code means.
Can someone who have any references to this code or had experienced the same
problem as I am, please assist.
Thank You ...

Colin Cheong
popsinit@popular.com.sg
```

#### ↳ Re: RM/COBOL-85 : Problem again

- **From:** Albert Ratzlaff <albert@conexion.com.py>
- **Date:** 1999-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36AD927B.D2B217CB@conexion.com.py>`
- **References:** `<78jmlm$ma2$1@mawar.singnet.com.sg>`

```
popular Singnet wrote:

> I am currently RMCOBOL Version 5.13 program on Unix ATT System V.4.
> When I activate one of the commonly used program, the system prompt with a
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> popsinit@popular.com.sg

13 means forbidden access (you can find these OS codes in /usr/include/errno.h
or /usr/include/sys/errno.h - been a long while since I used AT&T Unix). You
have a problem with file access permissions.

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff
```

<a id="t-270938ce99"></a>
## Thread: Is there a way? — 24 this month / 32 total

### Is there a way?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78nkm0$d0n@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Since the rather lengthy discussion on palindromes, I have been thinking
about a way to "squeeze out" all the non-character "bytes" from a text
field.  As I indicated in a separate note, I really do not like "looping
thru individual bytes" (as this always seems to me to be a "failure" on the
part of native COBOL - and is often hard to maintain).  I know that such
loops are almost ALWAYS more efficient at run-time, but I was wondering ...

 Can anyone come up with a "clear" way to get rid of all non-alphabet-
characters from an input field when creating an output (temp) field?
Assume that the input is 80 bytes long and may include spaces (any number at
the beginning, end, or middle) with any extraneous punctuation characters at
any point - but no "unprintable" characters.

It seems to me that there should be a way to "unstring" all the input and
then string the resulting "tokens" back together.  However, I haven't come
up with any thing that really seems like a worthwhile algorithm.

Anyone want to approach this totally hypothetical semi-challenge?  (All
Standard verbs and constructs are allowed - just not a method that does a
character by character loop)
```

- **From:** gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com (G Moore)

```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nSLr2.2440$VB.3698654@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<78nkm0$d0n@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <36b26d54.1182260@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
G Moore wrote:
>William M. Klein wrote:
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>call a turbo C program with the isprint() function from cobol.

That C program would still need to loop through the field character by
character.  And isprint() would accept punctuation characters. :-)
You would need 'isalpha()', or 'isalpha() || isdigit()' if letters and
numbers are okay.  Something like this:

#include <stdio.h>
main()
{
  char istr[50] = " Now is the time. ", ostr[50], *iptr, *optr;

  for (iptr = istr, optr = ostr; *iptr; iptr++)
     if (isalpha(*iptr) || isdigit(*iptr))
        *(optr++) = *iptr;
  *optr = '\0';
  printf(":%s:\n", istr);
  printf(":%s:\n", ostr);
}

: Now is the time. :
:Nowisthetime:
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way?

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78ohid$j8k$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<78nkm0$d0n@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <36b26d54.1182260@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <nSLr2.2440$VB.3698654@news4.mia>`

```

Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>G Moore wrote:
[...]
>>call a turbo C program with the isprint() function from cobol.
>
>That C program would still need to loop through the field character by
>character.  And isprint() would accept punctuation characters. :-)
[...]

Actually, a C program would not need to loop char by char. Use
'strtok()'.

This program gives the same results as my 'no-punc2', which
was posted elsewhere in this thread.

NOTE: I did not actually link the object into a COBOL program!

----------------
/* NoPunct.c - C version of no punctuation */

#define NOPUNCT_TESTING

#include <stdlib.h>
#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>

/*---------------------------------------------------------
   This routine is callable from COBOL. It assumes that
   neither the input or output string should be terminated
   by a nul character. Both istr and ostr must point to
   80 character records.
---------------------------------------------------------*/
void NoPunct ( char *istr, char *ostr )
{
 char *iptr;
 char *tokens = "~!@#$%^&*()_+`1234567890-={}|[]\\:"";'<>?,./";

 /* move spaces to output string */
 memset ( ostr, ' ', 80 );

 /* get memory for a copy of the input record */
 iptr = (char *) malloc (81);
 if ( iptr != NULL )
 {
  char *next_token;
  char *optr = ostr;

  /* create a nul terminated copy of input record */
  strncpy ( iptr, istr, 80 );
  iptr[80] = '\0';

  /* get the address of the first token */
  next_token = strtok ( iptr, tokens );
  while ( next_token != NULL )
  {
   /* copy the token into the output string */
   strncpy ( optr, next_token, strlen(next_token) );
   optr += strlen(next_token);

   /* get the address of the next token */
   next_token = strtok ( NULL, tokens );
  }

  /* free the memory */
  free ( iptr );
 }
 else
 {
  /* memory allocation failed */
  /* output record will contain spaces */
 }
}

#ifdef NOPUNCT_TESTING

int main ( void )
{
 char instring[81], outstring[81];
 char * string1 = "A man, a plan, a canal -- Panama.";

 memset ( instring, ' ', 80 );
 instring[80] = '\0';
 strncpy ( instring, string1, strlen(string1));
 NoPunct ( instring, outstring );
 outstring[80] = '\0';
 printf ( "%s\n", instring );
 printf ( "%s\n", outstring );
 return 0;
}

#endif
----------------

-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

those strtok() calls would be much less efficient than a simple loop
through character by character.

William M. Klein wrote in message <78nkm0$d0n@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>...
>Since the rather lengthy discussion on palindromes, I have been thinking
>about a way to "squeeze out" all the non-character "bytes" from a text
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

at
>the beginning, end, or middle) with any extraneous punctuation characters
at
>any point - but no "unprintable" characters.
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't think it's "clear" but here's my weak attempt...

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.  SQUEEZE.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  INPUT-STRING        PIC X(80) VALUE "The rain is spain falls
      -    "mainly in the   plain!".
       01  OUTPUT-STRING       PIC X(80) VALUE SPACE.
       01  INPUT-PTR           PIC S9(4) BINARY VALUE 1.
       01  INPUT-CNT           PIC S9(4) BINARY VALUE 0.
       01  OUTPUT-PTR          PIC S9(4) BINARY VALUE 1.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       MAIN SECTION.
           PERFORM UNTIL INPUT-PTR > FUNCTION LENGTH(INPUT-STRING)
               UNSTRING INPUT-STRING
                   DELIMITED   SPACE
                   INTO        OUTPUT-STRING(OUTPUT-PTR:)
                   COUNT       INPUT-CNT
                   POINTER     INPUT-PTR
               END-UNSTRING
               ADD INPUT-CNT TO OUTPUT-PTR
           END-PERFORM
           DISPLAY OUTPUT-STRING
           STOP RUN.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EsLr2.2431$VB.3684117@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<78nkm0$d0n@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <JHKr2.534$kA1.5643@news.rdc1.on.wave.home.com>`

```

all non-alphabetic characters.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way?

- **From:** "Karl Wagner" <kewagner@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
