- **References:** `<78nkm0$d0n@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <JHKr2.534$kA1.5643@news.rdc1.on.wave.home.com> <EsLr2.2431$VB.3684117@news4.mia>`

```

Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>That's well written Karl, but it only eliminates embedded spaces, not
>all non-alphabetic characters.

Fair enough. So toss in an 'INSPECT CONVERTING' and call it done. I guess I
missed that part of the spec in my haste to reply.

The following will work for ASCII. EBCDIC is left as an excercise for the
reader.

At this point things are getting more and more murky. Personally, I would
prefer a character-at-a-time loop.

In Rexx it would be something like
output_string =
space(translate(input_string,good_table,xrange(00'x,'ff'x)),0)

Take 2:

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.  SQUEEZE.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  FILLER.
         05  CAP-I1                   PIC S9(4) COMP VALUE +0.
         05  CAP-I2                   PIC S9(4) COMP VALUE +0.
         05  CAP-I3                   PIC S9(4) COMP VALUE +0.
       01  CAP-MSG                    PIC X(80) VALUE SPACE.
       01  CAP-ALPHA                  PIC X(80).
       01  FILLER REDEFINES CAP-ALPHA.
         05  CAP-ALPHA-X              PIC X OCCURS 80.
       77  CAP-OK                     PIC X.
       01  CAP-RETURN-CODE            PIC 9(3) VALUE 0.
       01  INPUT-STRING        PIC X(80) VALUE "The rain in spain falls
      -    "mainly in the   plain!".
       01  OUTPUT-STRING       PIC X(80) VALUE SPACE.
       01  INPUT-PTR           PIC S9(4) BINARY VALUE 1.
       01  INPUT-CNT           PIC S9(4) BINARY VALUE 0.
       01  OUTPUT-PTR          PIC S9(4) BINARY VALUE 1.

       01  WS-BAD-CHARS.
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE X"000102030405060708090A0B0C0
      -    "D0E0F".
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE X"101112131415161718191A1B1C1
      -    "D1E1F".
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE X"202122232425262728292A2B2C2
      -    "D2E2F".
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE X"303132333435363738393A3B3C3
      -    "D3E3F".
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE X"404142434445464748494A4B4C4
      -    "D4E4F".
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE X"505152535455565758595A5B5C5
      -    "D5E5F".
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE X"606162636465666768696A6B6C6
      -    "D6E6F".
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE X"707172737475767778797A7B7C7
      -    "D7E7F".
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE X"808182838485868788898A8B8C8
      -    "D8E8F".
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE X"909192939495969798999A9B9C9
      -    "D9E9F".
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE X"A0A1A2A3A4A5A6A7A8A9AAABACA
      -    "DAEAF".
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE X"B0B1B2B3B4B5B6B7B8B9BABBBCB
      -    "DBEBF".
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE X"C0C1C2C3C4C5C6C7C8C9CACBCCC
      -    "DCECF".
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE X"D0D1D2D3D4D5D6D7D8D9DADBDCD
      -    "DDEDF".
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE X"E0E1E2E3E4E5E6E7E8E9EAEBECE
      -    "DEEEF".
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE X"F0F1F2F3F4F5F6F7F8F9FAFBFCF
      -    "DFEFF".
       01  WS-GOOD-ASCII-CHARS.
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE SPACE.
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE SPACE.
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE SPACE.
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE SPACE.
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE X"204142434445464748494A4B4C4
      -    "D4E4F".
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE X"505152535455565758595A20202
      -    "02020".
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE X"206162636465666768696A6B6C6
      -    "D6E6F".
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE X"707172737475767778797A20202
      -    "02020".
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE SPACE.
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE SPACE.
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE SPACE.
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE SPACE.
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE SPACE.
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE SPACE.
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE SPACE.
         05  FILLER        PIC X(16) VALUE SPACE.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       MAIN SECTION.
           INSPECT INPUT-STRING CONVERTING WS-BAD-CHARS TO
            WS-GOOD-ASCII-CHARS.
           PERFORM UNTIL INPUT-PTR > FUNCTION LENGTH(INPUT-STRING)
               UNSTRING INPUT-STRING
                   DELIMITED   SPACE
                   INTO        OUTPUT-STRING(OUTPUT-PTR:)
                   COUNT       INPUT-CNT
                   POINTER     INPUT-PTR
               END-UNSTRING
               ADD INPUT-CNT TO OUTPUT-PTR
           END-PERFORM
           DISPLAY OUTPUT-STRING
           STOP RUN.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78sdih$gbj$1@server.cntfl.com>`

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-01-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36b20d9f.8408193@netnews>`
- **References:** `<78nkm0$d0n@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <JHKr2.534$kA1.5643@news.rdc1.on.wave.home.com> <EsLr2.2431$VB.3684117@news4.mia> <NMLr2.564$kA1.5899@news.rdc1.on.wave.home.com> <FTLr2.2441$VB.3699481@news4.mia> <36afa42b.47947108@netnews> <78sdih$gbj$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
'Twas Fri, 29 Jan 1999 08:35:04 -0500, when "Rick Smith"
<ricksmith@aiservices.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>Ah! The party-pooper sub-thread! It is much more efficient
>to NOT do a thing than to do that thing; but it is certainly less
>entertaining, challenging, or interesting!

That's right.  I like games to entertaining, challenging, and interesting.
I even like to decypher old code which is entertaining, challenging, and
interesting.  But I prefer to *write* code which is tediously obvious.

In fact, if you're going to be doing this more than once, I think the best
code is something like CALL SQUEEZE-IT USING REMOVE-THESE INPUT-STRING
OUTPUT-STRING which will be even less efficient because it involves
entry/exit/parameter passing overhead.  If efficiency is a concern,
rewrite SQUEEZE-IT in microcode.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way?

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-01-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be4d68$36e44660$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`
- **References:** `<78nkm0$d0n@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <JHKr2.534$kA1.5643@news.rdc1.on.wave.home.com> <36afdd44.775495466@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell <redsky@ibm.net> wrote in article
<36afdd44.775495466@news1.ibm.net>...
> What I would love to see is an efficient/fast space squisher.

I believe working forwards with two indices and moving character by
character skipping superfluous blanks will be the fastest algorithm, but
here is a working one going backwards (VS COBOL II syntax):

       Compress-text.
           perform varying I from length of TEXT-DATA by -1
               until I < 1
               or TEXT-DATA(I:1) not = space
           end-perform
           perform until I < 1
             perform varying J from I by -1
                 until J < 1
                 or TEXT-DATA(J:1) = space
             end-perform
             if J > 0
               perform varying I from J by -1
                   until I < 1
                   or TEXT-DATA(I:1) not = space
               end-perform
               if I > 0
                 if J > I + 1
                   move TEXT-DATA(J:) to TEXT-DATA(I + 1:)
                 end-if
               else
                 if STRIP-INITIAL-SPACES
                   move TEXT-DATA(J + 1:) to TEXT-DATA(1:)
                 end-if
               end-if
             else
               move zero to I
             end-if
           end-perform
           .

Was this any close to what you had in mind?

Gunnar.
```

#### ↳ Re: Is there a way?

- **From:** "Simon Cordingley" <simonc@casegen.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36af9891.0@mercury.nildram.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<78nkm0$d0n@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Since the introduction of reference modification in Ansi '85, I prefer to do
it a character at a time (better than those redefines).  Makes it easy to
read and maintenance easier.

Incidentally, using SPECIAL-NAMES to build your own list of acceptable
characters makes this simpler, i.e.

IF INPUT-STRING(INPUT-STRING-POINTER:1) IS MYTYPEOFSTRING
   MOVE INPUT-STRING(INPUT-STRING-POINTER:1) TO
OUTPUT-STRING(OUTPUT-STRING-POINTER:1)
END-IF

Obviously use an INLINE PERFORM for this...

BTW - Is there a function in the '89 Cobol spec to do it the "C" way?


Simon Cordingley
Casegen Systems Ltd

William M. Klein wrote in message <78nkm0$d0n@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>...
>Since the rather lengthy discussion on palindromes, I have been thinking
>about a way to "squeeze out" all the non-character "bytes" from a text
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Assume that the input is 80 bytes long and may include spaces (any number
at
>the beginning, end, or middle) with any extraneous punctuation characters
at
>any point - but no "unprintable" characters.
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
can't beat a one byte comparison in a perform loop of a string with
ref-mod. It usually all sits in the CPU cache on a PC.
```

#### ↳ Re: Is there a way?

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78o94d$6t2$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<78nkm0$d0n@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <78nkm0$d0n@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>...
>Since the rather lengthy discussion on palindromes, I have been thinking
[...]
>
I have supplied two subprograms. The first, "no-punc1," extracts the
"words" and places them in the output string  with the words separated
by a single space. The second, "no-punc2," is probably closer to what
you intended. It finds the non-alpha, non-space characters replacing
them with "nothing." It also leaves all spaces intact.

Comments are interspersed in the code.
---------------------
      $set ans85 flag"ans85" flagas"e"
       identification division.
       program-id. klein.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01 string1 pic x(80) value
             "A man, a plan, a canal -- Panama.".
       01 string2 pic x(80) value
             "  Able was I, ere I saw Elba.".
       01 string3 pic x(80) value
             "%^*(^(*a}{:>b|}{^%c@#$%^d>:{+e>;'-7258f*&()".
       01 string-out1 pic x(80).
       01 string-out2 pic x(80).
       procedure division.
       begin.
           display "start"
           display " "
           call "no-punc1" using string1 string-out1
           call "no-punc2" using string1 string-out2
           display string1
           display string-out1
           display string-out2
           display " "
           call "no-punc1" using string2 string-out1
           call "no-punc2" using string2 string-out2
           display string2
           display string-out1
           display string-out2
           display " "
           call "no-punc1" using string3 string-out1
           call "no-punc2" using string3 string-out2
           display string3
           display string-out1
           display string-out2
           display " "
           display "end"
           stop run.
       end program klein.

       identification division.
       program-id. no-punc1.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01 work-string pic x(80).

      * non-alpha-char is sensitive to the native character set
      * ASCII character set assumed
       01 non-alpha-char pic x(42) value
             "~!@#$%^&*()_+`1234567890-={}|[]\:"";'<>?,./".

      * non-alpha-space must have the same size as non-alpha-char
       01 non-alpha-space pic x(42) value space.

       01 token pic x(80).
       01 in-ptr pic s9(4) comp.
       01 out-ptr pic s9(4) comp.
       linkage section.
       01 input-string pic x(80).
       01 output-string pic x(80).
       procedure division using input-string output-string.
       begin.

      * replace with a simple move if upper-case is not needed
           move function upper-case (input-string)
             to work-string

      * replace punctuation with spaces
           inspect work-string
             converting non-alpha-char to non-alpha-space

           move space to output-string
           move 1 to in-ptr
           move 1 to out-ptr
           perform until in-ptr > 80

      * get a token
               unstring work-string delimited by all space
                 into token with pointer in-ptr

      * prevent leading space in output
               if token not = space

      * output the token followed by a single space
                   string token delimited by space
                     space delimited by size
                     into output-string with pointer out-ptr

               end-if
           end-perform
           exit program.
       end program no-punc1.

       identification division.
       program-id. no-punc2.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01 work-string pic x(80).

      * non-alpha-char is sensitive to the native character set
      * ASCII character set assumed
       01 non-alpha-char pic x(42) value
             "~!@#$%^&*()_+`1234567890-={}|[]\:"";'<>?,./".

      * non-alpha-lv must have the same size as non-alpha-char
       01 non-alpha-lv pic x(42) value low-values.

       01 token pic x(80).
       01 in-ptr pic s9(4) comp.
       01 out-ptr pic s9(4) comp.
       01 token-length pic s9(4) comp.
       linkage section.
       01 input-string pic x(80).
       01 output-string pic x(80).
       procedure division using input-string output-string.
       begin.

      * replace with a simple move if upper-case is not needed
           move function upper-case (input-string)
             to work-string

      * replace punctuation with low-values
           inspect work-string
             converting non-alpha-char to non-alpha-lv

           move space to output-string
           move 1 to in-ptr
           move 1 to out-ptr
           perform until in-ptr > 80

      * get a token
               unstring work-string delimited by low-values
                 into token count in token-length
                 with pointer in-ptr

               if token-length < 80
                   move low-values to token (token-length + 1 : 1)
               end-if

      * output the token
               string token delimited by low-values
                 into output-string with pointer out-ptr

           end-perform
           exit program.
       end program no-punc2.
---------------------

-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

#### ↳ Re: Is there a way?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-01-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36afdca4.775335081@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<78nkm0$d0n@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
On Wed, 27 Jan 1999 12:07:47 -0600, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:


> Can anyone come up with a "clear" way to get rid of all non-alphabet-
>characters from an input field when creating an output (temp) field?
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>character by character loop)

I have a process in place in a program at work that does this.  A byte
at a time using a table.  I would love to streamline it for speed, and
thought of string/unstring etc.... You can do an continous unstring
into using the pointer and counter features ... but my tests showed
the byte by byte array method to be faster.
```

#### ↳ Re: Is there a way?

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36B06F54.BE86F75D@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<78nkm0$d0n@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
To me it seems this the place for character by character code.  An 'INDEX BY
SUB1' phrase
can be added to the BUFF1 line if you prefer but SUB2 should be left as an
ordinary subscript.
Note SUB2 will contain a count of the number of characters when this perform is
over.

01  BUFFER-1.
     05 BUFF1 OCCURS 80  PIC X.
01 BUFFER-2.
     05 BUFF2 OCCURS 80 PIC X.
------------------------------------
MOVE ZERO TO SUB2.
PERFORM VARYING SUB1 FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL SUB1 > 80
  IF BUFF1(SUB1) ALPHABETIC AND BUFF1(SUB1) NOT = SPACE
    ADD 1 TO SUB2
    MOVE BUFF1(SUB1) TO BUFF2(SUB2)
  END-IF
END-PERFORM.

William M. Klein wrote:

> Since the rather lengthy discussion on palindromes, I have been thinking
> about a way to "squeeze out" all the non-character "bytes" from a text
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
offer at 50 USD, but will still take orders at this price until 7th February
```

```
On Thu, 28 Jan 1999 11:07:38 -0500, Clifford E Williams
<williams@southern.edu> wrote:

>Once more (with feeling) someone is *already* writing a freeware COBOL
>compiler.  If I had *any* of the required knowledge to help I would.

Not really. It's a COBOL to C translator with a hefty amount of
limitations. Ok, it's early but I think the premise is wrong.
Translating COBOL to C to compile is a big headache.
```

<a id="t-649402a23a"></a>
## Thread: [Long] Errr... you guys told me to post it when I'm done... — 1 msgs

### [Long] Errr... you guys told me to post it when I'm done...

- **From:** "kayin/ka khiong kwok" <kayin@mpx.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78lhe9$808$1@news1.mpx.com.au>`

```
Hi, you guys told me to post my code when I'm done. As one of the other guys
pointed out, and to my own inferno embarrassment, it wasn't the code. It was
the data. Lotus Notes left justified the index field (*** Turning bright red
***), so instead of 099, I got 990. Lord, I hate that! I knew it was staring
me in the face, and I could see it! :')

Anyway, the code is c/p'ed to at the bottom of this post. I appreciate any
suggestions and comments (good or bad) about it. I'm hoping it doesn't screw
up during posting. I'm going onto what is basically stage two of my little
pet project. Thanks for your time.

Cheers,

Ka.

====== Code start here ======

        IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
        PROGRAM-ID. PROCESS-EXERCISE-RECORDS.
      * AUTHOR. KA KHIONG KWOK.
      * DATA SOURCE. EXERCISE.NSF (LOTUS NOTES)
      * PURPOSE. EXPORT DATA FROM NOTES. PERFORM BATCH WORK ON DATA.

      * THINGS TO CONSIDER:
      *                         MULTI PAGE REPORTING.
      *                         PROVIDE OPTION FOR ENTERING FILE NAME.
      *                         FIX UP SOME OF THE SUCKY SECTION DUMMY.

      * ORDER OF PROCESSING.
      * 1) READ EXERCISE TYPE FILE TO DETERMINE WHETHER THERE
      * ARE NEW EXERCISES. DONE.
      * 2) PROCESS EXPORTED FILE1.
      * 3) PROCESS EXPORTED FILE2.

        ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
        INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
        FILE-CONTROL.
            SELECT ET-EXERCISE-TYPE ASSIGN TO "EXTYPES.DAT"
              ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
            SELECT EM-EXERCISE-MAIN ASSIGN TO "MAIN.DAT"
              ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
              ACCESS IS SEQUENTIAL
              RECORD KEY IS EM-EXERCISE-KEY.

        DATA DIVISION.
        FILE SECTION.
        FD  ET-EXERCISE-TYPE.
        01  ET-EXERCISE-TYPE-RECORD.
            02      ET-EXERCISE-KEY         PIC 9(04).
            02      FILLER                  PIC X(02).
            02      ET-EXERCISE-NAME        PIC X(20).
            02      ET-EXERCISE-COMMENTS    PIC 9(03).

        FD  EM-EXERCISE-MAIN.
        01  EM-EXERCISE-MAIN-RECORD.
            02      EM-EXERCISE-KEY         PIC 9(04).
            02      EM-EXERCISE-NAME        PIC X(20).
            02      EM-EXERCISE-TALLY       PIC 9(03).

        WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
        01  WS-USER-FILE-NAME               PIC X(20).
        01  WS-TEST-KEY                     PIC 9(03).
        01  WS-REDEFINED-KEY
            REDEFINES WS-TEST-KEY           PIC ZZ9.
        01  WS-TRUE                         PIC X(01) VALUE "T".
        01  WS-FALSE                        PIC X(01) VALUE "F".
        01  WS-END-OF-FILE-FLAG             PIC X(01) VALUE "F".
            88  WS-END-OF-FILE              VALUE "T".
        01  WS-WRITE-MAIN-RECORD            PIC X(60).
        01  WS-COUNT-DELETED-RECORDS        PIC 9(03).
        01  WS-COUNT-NUMBER-OF-RECORDS      PIC 9(03).


        PROCEDURE DIVISION.
        100-MAIN.
            MOVE ZEROES TO WS-COUNT-DELETED-RECORDS
            MOVE ZEROES TO WS-COUNT-NUMBER-OF-RECORDS
            MOVE ZEROES TO WS-TEST-KEY
            PERFORM 200-PROCESS-MAIN-FILE
            STOP RUN
            .


        200-PROCESS-MAIN-FILE.
            OPEN INPUT ET-EXERCISE-TYPE
            OPEN OUTPUT EM-EXERCISE-MAIN
            PERFORM 300-GET-EXERCISE-RECORD
              PERFORM UNTIL WS-END-OF-FILE
                IF ET-EXERCISE-TYPE-RECORD = SPACES
                  DISPLAY "SPACES - RECORD IGNORED"
                ELSE
                  ADD 1 TO WS-COUNT-NUMBER-OF-RECORDS
                  DISPLAY WS-COUNT-NUMBER-OF-RECORDS
                  PERFORM 300-WRITE-MAIN-RECORD
                END-IF
                PERFORM 300-GET-EXERCISE-RECORD
              END-PERFORM
            CLOSE ET-EXERCISE-TYPE
            CLOSE EM-EXERCISE-MAIN
            .


        300-GET-EXERCISE-RECORD.
            READ ET-EXERCISE-TYPE
              AT END MOVE WS-TRUE TO WS-END-OF-FILE-FLAG
            END-READ
            DISPLAY "=== Start ==="
            DISPLAY ET-EXERCISE-TYPE-RECORD
            .


        300-WRITE-MAIN-RECORD.
            MOVE ET-EXERCISE-KEY TO EM-EXERCISE-KEY
            DISPLAY "MAIN KEY: " EM-EXERCISE-KEY
            MOVE ET-EXERCISE-NAME TO EM-EXERCISE-NAME
            DISPLAY "MAIN NAME: " EM-EXERCISE-NAME
            MOVE ZEROES TO EM-EXERCISE-TALLY
            DISPLAY EM-EXERCISE-MAIN-RECORD
            WRITE EM-EXERCISE-MAIN-RECORD
              INVALID KEY PERFORM 400-DISPLAY-BAD-KEYS
            END-WRITE
            .


        400-DISPLAY-BAD-KEYS.
            DISPLAY "=== bad keys ==="
            DISPLAY "TYPE KEY: " ET-EXERCISE-KEY
            DISPLAY "OTHER KEY: " EM-EXERCISE-KEY
            .
====== Code finish here ======

====== Sample data start here ======

   1  Sit ups
   2  Push ups
   3  Squats
   4  Leg up              Needs to be careful
   5  Scissors leg lift
   6  Toe reach
   7  Cat stretch
   8  Hand grip
   9  Arm swing
  10  Neck roll
  11  Knee Lift
  12  Leg up twist
  13  Leg hold
  14  Cross toe reach
  15  Bicep curls         2 kg wrist weights

====== Sample data finish here ======
```

## Thread: Y2K COBOL JOBS - MUST HAVE 3 DAYS EXPERIENCE...Lamebrained Y2K Comments Of The Week... — 1 this month / 2 total

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k) · [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs)_

### Y2K COBOL JOBS - MUST HAVE 3 DAYS EXPERIENCE...Lamebrained Y2K Comments Of The Week...

- **From:** theodp@aol.com (Theo DP)
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990127014713.09408.00003479@ng-fb1.aol.com>`

```
...belong to Computerworld's Fran Quittel who, in her 1-25-1999 Career Advisor
column, advises an aspiring COBOL programmer to learn Java ("the winner over
time") instead, but goes on to suggest that if a COBOL career is truly what is
desired, a THREE MEETING course offered by UCLA (see below) should suffice!

--> Snipped From http://www.computerworld.com/home/print.nsf/all/9901258BCA

Dear Career Adviser: 

With 18 months of Web design (HTML) experience, I've recently become interested
in Cobol....what direction should I take? -- direction desired... 

Dear Direction: 

...Terry Werner at the University of California at Los Angeles' Extension
Computer and Information Systems Department says you might need two academic
quarters to get the skills you need, but UCLA does offer a three-meeting year
2000 crash course that starts Jan. 30. 


--> From
http://www.unex.ucla.edu/catalog/Catalog2.cfm?ID=8399&QUARTER=WI&searchtyp
e=Titles&keyword1=cobol

UCLA Extension Winter Programs 1999 

Y2K Cobol: Hands-On 
818.20 Management 2.40 CEU $525
(Includes course material.) 
This course examines the Cobol programming language from the perspective of the
Year 2000 (Y2K) computer problem. The goal of the course is to provide
sufficient knowledge to allow programmers to fix Y2K "bugs" in Cobol programs
to ensure Year 2000 compliance. Program managers also can benefit from this
course. Instruction emphasizes the parts of Cobol specific to Y2K; assignments
are Y2K- or date-related. Course material was developed around a score of Cobol
routines that perform Y2K-related tasks, arranged pedagogically, with lectures
on Cobol to explain the statements needed to write the programs. Topics include
Y2K remediation theory, pros and cons, when to use it, and the syntax of Cobol
statements. Students have the opportunity to test in-class solutions by
exchanging and running their programs. Prerequisite: Experience in computer
programming. Previous experience with Cobol is recommended. Enrollment prior to
the first class required. No refund after January 25.
```

<a id="t-fb17439d77"></a>
## Thread: Help needed — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: Help needed

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78mv1v$7ip@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Randall,
  Sorry, I am a little slow.  We have different definitions of  palindromes
(and I think yours DOES match the original example).  In my definition,
   "Able was I ere I saw Elba" is a palindrome but
  "A man a plan a canal panama"
is NOT a correct palindorme as the spaces don't match  (Sorry about that
Teddy!)  Having looked it up quickly, I think yours is the accepted
definition and therefore, I now understand the need to "get rid" of internal
spaces - even if there is no punctuation.  In which case, simply doing the
inspect to get rid of trailing spaces would NOT work.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help needed

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36AF22DA.DC02FF6A@home.com>`
- **References:** `<78mv1v$7ip@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> Randall,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> inspect to get rid of trailing spaces would NOT work.

What's important here is that people do have differences in what they
think the requirements are.  So the code should be written so that
switching between the two understandings is relatively painless.  (It
doesn't matter what the dictionary says - what matters is what the user
needs)!!
```

## Thread: MF COBOL and MFIOERR files — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF COBOL and MFIOERR files

- **From:** grjohn@bga.com (Glen Johnson)
- **Date:** 1999-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36af6fd8.0@feed1.realtime.net>`

```
The newer releases of Micro Focus COBOL have introduced
an error file in /usr/tmp called MFIOERR.pid.  Is there
a way to turn off the generation of these files?

We have a program that on SCO 4.0 10.06 and HP 4.1 11.02
fails but AIX 4.0 10.07 works.  If we replace the SCO
10.06 fherror.o file with the 10.02 version, the program
also works.  When it fails it fails by crashing.  It is as
if the rts is being stepped on.

Ring any bells for anyone?

					Glen Johnson
```

<a id="t-26e507d799"></a>
## Thread: DISPLAY xxx UPON CONSOLE. — 17 this month / 18 total

### DISPLAY xxx UPON CONSOLE.

- **From:** Peter Neidhart <neidhart@debis.de>
- **Date:** 1999-01-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36B03283.85E7820@debis.de>`

```
I want to write some messages to the system console (OS/390).
This is done by DISPLAY XXX UPON CONSOLE and works fine for single 
message lines. Now I need to write some multi line messages. As our
system
is very busy, this multi line messages are not contiguous
but interspersed with other messages. Is there
a way to have this multiple lines glued together.
Compiler is IBM VS COBOL II Rel. 4.0

regards
Peter
```

#### ↳ Re: DISPLAY xxx UPON CONSOLE.

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78q5gb$44o$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36B03283.85E7820@debis.de>`

```
Take a look at the screen section.  Essentially, the
data to be displayed and accepted is laid out record
style, just like working storage, but with from and to
statements.  You then accept and display the record
names, and all else is automatic.

The console is really very primitive methodology, originally
intended for things like getting tapes mounted by a
console operator.

Peter Neidhart wrote in message <36B03283.85E7820@debis.de>...
>I want to write some messages to the system console (OS/390).
>This is done by DISPLAY XXX UPON CONSOLE and works fine for single
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Peter
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DISPLAY xxx UPON CONSOLE.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78qco7$ppr@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36B03283.85E7820@debis.de> <78q5gb$44o$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald,
  Screen Section is NOT available under OS/390 (where this question was
aimed).

I am trying to find out if there are any IBM suggestions for doing this but
I did want to point out 2 things that might be a little helpful.

1)  The line/size limit is NOT 80 bytes for a display.  (I can't remember if
it is 132 or 255 - or something else).  If you can get it all that you want
in the allowed length, just displaying a bigger "field" may solve your
problem (with it flowing from line to line on the console).

2) As you indicated, the console gets LOTS of messages.  In most OS/390
environments that I know of "putting out" messages on the CONSOLE is no
longer considered a "nice" thing to do.  Such messages tend to scroll off
quickly.  Can you give us an idea of what type of multi-line message you are
thinking about and what you expect the console operator to do about it?
(are you doing an ACCEPT?)  If what you REALLY want to do is just get some
information on the SYSLOG, there may be better ways of doing this or
solutions to keeping your stuff together.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DISPLAY xxx UPON CONSOLE.

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78qjsd$ah3$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36B03283.85E7820@debis.de> <78q5gb$44o$1@news.igs.net> <78qco7$ppr@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Oh well ...
;<)

William M. Klein wrote in message <78qco7$ppr@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>...
>Donald,
>  Screen Section is NOT available under OS/390 (where this question was
>aimed).
<snip>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DISPLAY xxx UPON CONSOLE.

- **From:** Peter Neidhart <neidhart@debis.de>
- **Date:** 1999-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36B18F2C.7028B4C1@debis.de>`
- **References:** `<36B03283.85E7820@debis.de> <78q5gb$44o$1@news.igs.net> <78qco7$ppr@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> 1)  The line/size limit is NOT 80 bytes for a display.  (I can't remember if
> it is 132 or 255 - or something else).  If you can get it all that you want
> in the allowed length, just displaying a bigger "field" may solve your
> problem (with it flowing from line to line on the console).

I tried it, the length of a console message line is 100 bytes. I moved
the messages
to multiple 100 byte fields and displayed them with ONE display
instruction.
The are written neatly aligned to the console/log. 
I think I stay with this simple solution as I don't want to 
use any assembler to issua a multiline WTO.

> 
> 2) As you indicated, the console gets LOTS of messages.  In most OS/390
> environments that I know of "putting out" messages on the CONSOLE is no
> longer considered a "nice" thing to do.  Such messages tend to scroll off
> quickly.

That is no problem as the messages are also written to the syslog and
there
is the place to search. The syslog is kept much longer than job outputs.


> Can you give us an idea of what type of multi-line message you are
> thinking about and what you expect the console operator to do about it?

It's an information message about what happened. 
Usually they can't see the message. Most messages are not displayed,
only logged.

> (are you doing an ACCEPT?)  If what you REALLY want to do is just get some
> information on the SYSLOG, there may be better ways of doing this or
> solutions to keeping your stuff together.

Oh no, no ACCEPT. the operators would kill me :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DISPLAY xxx UPON CONSOLE.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78sgvn$gmo$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36B03283.85E7820@debis.de> <78q5gb$44o$1@news.igs.net> <78qco7$ppr@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <36B18F2C.7028B4C1@debis.de>`

```
What would be done with a command like:
Display[upon console] message-line-1, message-line-2, message-line-3.
?

Peter Neidhart wrote in message <36B18F2C.7028B4C1@debis.de>...
>William M. Klein wrote:
>>
>> 1)  The line/size limit is NOT 80 bytes for a display.  (I can't remember
if
>> it is 132 or 255 - or something else).  If you can get it all that you
want
>> in the allowed length, just displaying a bigger "field" may solve your
>> problem (with it flowing from line to line on the console).
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>> (are you doing an ACCEPT?)  If what you REALLY want to do is just get
some
>> information on the SYSLOG, there may be better ways of doing this or
>> solutions to keeping your stuff together.
>
>Oh no, no ACCEPT. the operators would kill me :-)
```

```
Peter Neidhart wrote in message <36B18F2C.7028B4C1@debis.de>...
>William M. Klein wrote:
>>
   <snippage>
>>
>> 2) As you indicated, the console gets LOTS of messages.  In most OS/390
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Given what you describe, I would suggest putting out a bunch of separate
(80-byte) DISPLAY statements - but start each one with some identifier (such
as the job name and a start-of-message timestamp).  In this way, they would
end up spread out in the SYSLOG, but you could scroll from part to part (or
even sort a section of the SYSLOG) to bring them together.

Again, I am a little concerned about using the SYSLOG as a repository for
informational (error) messages.  I have to think that there is a better
place to put them.  (Have you thought of an running "error" file that you
open MOD (JCL) or EXTEND (COBOL) and just add the messages from each run?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DISPLAY xxx UPON CONSOLE.

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz
- **Date:** 1999-01-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78tuho$r2j$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<36B03283.85E7820@debis.de> <78q5gb$44o$1@news.igs.net>`

```
In article <78q5gb$44o$1@news.igs.net>,
  "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote:

> Take a look at the screen section.  [.....]

> Peter Neidhart wrote in message <36B03283.85E7820@debis.de>...
> >I want to write some messages to the system console (OS/390).
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Compiler is IBM VS COBOL II Rel. 4.0

SCREEN SECTION is not part of Cobol85, but is an extension of many
PC and Unix compilers (but not all) and of X-Open.

So a 370 compiler is unlikely to have SCREEN SECTION even if it
is fully ANSI-85 and UPON CONSOLE is entirely relevant for outputting
to the system console.

What may be useful if a single DISPLAY UPON CONSOLE could include codes
to force new-lines on the console to give the desired effect.

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: DISPLAY xxx UPON CONSOLE.

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36b1132c.0@news2.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<36B03283.85E7820@debis.de>`

```
Ask your systems programmers about Netview automation.  It might be able to
help, but they'll have to be willing to do work (unless you're also familiar
with Netview).  Netview is capable of detecting messages in the syslog and
acting on them.

Can I ask why you want to write a message to the system console?

I believe there is a standard utility called WTO and WTOR (we have them, but
whether they are standard or not is another matter) that can be called from
JCL to put complete messages on the console, ones which don't go away until
erased or responded to.  I believe the same can be done from inside COBOL,
with a call to an assembler program.


I think Netview automation, though, is the way to go if your sysprogs are
willing to help.


Peter Neidhart wrote in message <36B03283.85E7820@debis.de>...
>I want to write some messages to the system console (OS/390).
>This is done by DISPLAY XXX UPON CONSOLE and works fine for single
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Peter
```

- **From:** Peter Neidhart <neidhart@debis.de>

```
Chris Osborne wrote:
>

> help, but they'll have to be willing to do work (unless you're also familiar
> with Netview).  Netview is capable of detecting messages in the syslog and
> acting on them.

I can't see how your answer matches my question.
I want to write messages, not detect.

> 
> Can I ask why you want to write a message to the system console?

If there is an error detected during program execution it has to be
logged.
It doesn't matter when it scrolls off the screen because the message is
also
recorded in the system log. And our system log is kept much longer 
than job output.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DISPLAY xxx UPON CONSOLE.

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36b37a56.0@news2.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<36B03283.85E7820@debis.de> <36b1132c.0@news2.uswest.net> <36B18210.966061DA@debis.de>`

```
Peter,

If Netview can detect your scrollable and disparate messages (which aren't
shown on consecutive lines of the syslog), then it can echo them back to the
system console as a fully contiguous, unscrollable, and white or red
highlighted lines.

Whenever Netview at our site detects an abend code/message in the syslog, it
is immediately redisplayed as an unscrollable  while-highlighted message
(accompanied by a loud beep).  The operator must manually delete the message
to deal with it.

If you don't want operators to actually see the message, then I'm unsure as
to why you need them all on consecutive lines.  If you ever need them after
the fact, just have each line prefixed or suffixed by a unique combination
of characters, and use one of the available utilities to strip your messages
out of the syslog.

As two years at a console as an operator showed me, if a message is
important enough to put in the syslog and further important enough to
cluster together, then it had better be geared to alert the operator
immediately.

Of course, sites elsewhere might not operate in this manner.  So I apologize
if these suggestions don't help.





Peter Neidhart wrote in message <36B18210.966061DA@debis.de>...
>Chris Osborne wrote:

to

familiar
```

## Thread: Problem with GOBACK in COBOL CICS — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Problem with GOBACK in COBOL CICS

- **From:** peterkr@ms.com
- **Date:** 1999-01-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78q2ar$dlo$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<78mlle$ehf$1@news.mclink.it>`

```


I've found a case where the execution of a GOBACK command in a CICS
COBOL program apparantly keeps the program (actually a COBOL sub-program)
active in CICS. Another call to the sub-program causes it to pick up
execution where it left off the last time instead of beginning execution
with a "fresh" version of the sub-program.

There is even a statement in the V2 compiler manual that says this
can happen, but I was wonfering if anyone else has encountered it.

Tia
Pjk

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem with GOBACK in COBOL CICS

- **From:** Philip Morten <pmorten@transarc.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36B0B40F.4DFAFE33@transarc.com>`
- **References:** `<78mlle$ehf$1@news.mclink.it> <78q2ar$dlo$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
peterkr@ms.com wrote:
> 
> I've found a case where the execution of a GOBACK command in a CICS
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pjk

What release of which CICS product? What Operating System ?  Whose COBOL ?

Philip Morten
TXSeries Development
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem with GOBACK in COBOL CICS

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78qd8p$mte@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<78mlle$ehf$1@news.mclink.it> <78q2ar$dlo$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Execution of a GOBACK (IBM extension) in a CICS environment OR ANYWHERE ELSE
from a subprogram *guarantees* that the next time the program is executed it
is re-entered in "last used state".   Given EXEC CICS HANDLE statements,
this may well mean that the execution will appear to "start in the middle".

Bottom-Line:
  I can't remember any case (well almost any) where you really want to reach
a GoBack (or EXIT PROGRAM - and certainly never Stop Run) in a CICS program.
Normally, you use some EXEC CICS statement to transfer control out of  CICS
program.  (You aren't by any chance doing a "real" conversational program
instead of pseudo-conversational - are you?)

You mention the "V2 compiler manual" but don't tell which compiler.  If this
is COBOL for this-and-that, then do look at the CBLPSHPOP (sp?) compiler
option which might be your problem.
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem with GOBACK in COBOL CICS

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<78mlle$ehf$1@news.mclink.it> <78q2ar$dlo$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Is that not the way it is *supposed* to work?  Unless one
uses the "INITIAL" option that is?
```

```
we used to initialize storage when invoking a program for the first time.
That may be your problem.

Alan

peterkr@ms.com wrote:

> I've found a case where the execution of a GOBACK command in a CICS
> COBOL program apparantly keeps the program (actually a COBOL sub-program)
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem with GOBACK in COBOL CICS

- **From:** WOB@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78r90b$gjk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<78mlle$ehf$1@news.mclink.it> <78q2ar$dlo$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Actually, this is the proper behavior of a Sub-Program in CICS that is Called,
either Statically or 'Dynamically' (with a PPT entry, so really not Dynamic).
If the Sub-Program was a CICS Program and that program issued a CICS RETURN as
opposed to a GOBACK, this would terminate the Run-Unit or Task, whichever the
case may be. Otherwise, non-CICS Sub-Program's must always issue a GOBACK.
Another thing to watch out for in Sub-Program's (CICS or non-CICS) is residual
or non-initialized storage. There is NO guarantee of initialized storage after
the first call and execution (just like Batch), even with a VALUE clause. So
all bets are off. This is why it's good practice to initialize Working-Storage
manually at the start of the Procedure Division in a Sub-Program. Note that
NONE of this applies to programs accessed via the LINK API. Although a CALL
'identifier' CANCEL will refresh your storage, the overhead involved is
substantial. But, having never used a CALL CANCEL in CICS, I'm not even sure
it's advisable.

To gain a re-entrancy factor, pass the Sub-Program's workarea as a parameter
from the caller. By doing so, you won't raise any residual storage problems.
The caller is normally COBOL and his storage is serially-reusable. It's also
good practice for the Sub-Program (Load Module) to be no more than 4-5 MVS
Pages (16384-20480) in length.

You might want to try posting your question to bit.listserv.cics-l for some
further opinions and/or explanations.

HTH....

WOB,
Atlanta

In article <78q2ar$dlo$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>,
  peterkr@ms.com wrote:
>
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
```

- **From:** daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch

```
  peterkr@ms.com wrote:

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

<snip the rest>

Peter,

I am quite sure that it depends whether you CALLed or EXEC CICS LINKed the
subprogram. In the manuals it written that you should code an EXEC CICS
RETURN instead of a GOBACK if the module has been LINKed, but I know that
GOBACK works, too. That may be the difference: LINK gets a new copy, CALL the
last state of the working storage.

Cheers

Daniel

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem with GOBACK in COBOL CICS

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-01-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36b96907.90670537@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<78mlle$ehf$1@news.mclink.it> <78q2ar$dlo$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
On Thu, 28 Jan 1999 16:13:24 GMT, peterkr@ms.com wrote:

>
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
A Short question:  Did you, by any means, mix COBOL and CICS calling environments?  That
is, if the main program is called A, and the sub program is called B, try any of

A:   EXEC CICS LINK PROGRAM('B') and
B:  GOBACK


or

A: MOVE 'B' TO PGM-NAME
    CALL pgm-name

B: EXEC CICS RETURN



If you did, this spells disaster..........

if EXEC CICS LINK, then EXEC CICS RETURN

if CALL, then GOBACK


with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

### Re: Student Question

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-01-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GF_r2.40$Mk.105952@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<36AFCB26.325377D2@yahoo.com> <rURr2.3657$oi.3907686@news3.mia> <36AFF9A8.B506BAAE@yahoo.com>`

```
NG Profile wrote:
>
>Thanks for the advice Judson.  That is why I would like to find maybe some
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>I left my "eureka."  Thanks again.

I wrote a brief introductory tutorial on arrays for use in QBasic, and have
posted it several times for people in QBasic newsgroups.  I'll take a stab
at modifying it for COBOL.  You also may want to obtain an excellent COBOL
tutorial "Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours" by Thane Hubbell.  Thane is a
great guy who frequents these COBOL newsgroups, and who worked hard on the
table handling section of the book.  For a more advanced text, you might try
"COBOL Unleashed" by Jon Wessler and several other authors, including Thane,
myself, and several other contributors to the COBOL newsgroups.

Data items are referenced by using their data name, such as STREET-ADDRESS,
CUSTOMER-NAME, etc.  You can think of a table as a mechanism for having a
number of data items, all with the same name.  This collection of data items,
all with the same name, is called a 'table'.  You can create a table in COBOL
using the OCCURS clause of a data declaration.

    03  COUNT    PIC  9(06)  OCCURS 20 TIMES.

This creates a table of 20 data items, all named COUNT.  In COBOL 85 the first
element in a table is always 1, and the last element is defined in the OCCURS.
Sometimes that maximum number is determined at runtime by the contents of
another numeric data item specified in the OCCURS.  For example:

    03  MAX      PIC  9(02)  VALUE 15.
    03  COUNT    PIC  9(06)  OCCURS 1 TO 20 TIMES DEPENDING ON MAX.

In this example, as the value of MAX is changed in the program, it will
be as if the OCCURS clause was simultaneously changed to the same value.

Because each data item has the same data name, each time you reference the
table, you must somehow indicate which of the several data items you are
referring to.  To do this we use what is called a 'subscript', as in
mathematical notation.  Since most programming languages use straight text,
and do not support the tiny fonts used in printed subscripts, the subscript
is typically enclosed in parenthesis () following the data name.

    COUNT(1)     Data name COUNT, #1
    COUNT(2)     Data name COUNT, #2
    COUNT(12)    Data name COUNT, #12

In some programming languages (e.g. C/C++), subscripts are enclosed in square
brackets [] instead of parenthesis (), but they do the same thing.

Tables and subscripting, along with loops, are some of the most powerful
features of programming languages.  They allow you to write one set of
instructions which can be applied over and over to different data.  This is
accomplished by using a numeric data item instead of a numeric literal as a
subscript into the table, like this:

    COUNT(SUB)   Data name COUNT, # contained in SUB

Obviously, SUB must be a numeric data item, and the value must be an integer
within the valid subscript range.  To add 5 to each data item COUNT in the
table, you could code a simple loop like this:

    PERFORM VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL SUB > 5
        ADD 1 TO COUNT(SUB).

The data item in the table may be any normal COBOL data item, even a group
item.  For example:

    03  FRIEND-TABLE        OCCURS 100 TIMES.
        05  FRIEND-NAME         PIC  X(20).
        05  FRIEND-STREET       PIC  X(20).
        05  FRIEND-CITY-ST      PIC  X(20).
        05  FRIEND-ZIP          PIC  9(05).

The OCCURS clause may not be used on a level 01, 66, 77 or 88 item, but only
on levels 02 through 49.  You may not REDEFINE an item containing an OCCURS
clause, but you may REDEFINE an item subordinate to an item containing an
OCCURS clause.

Tables with one subscript are very useful, but sometimes there are situations
when we need more.  For example, say you have divided your city into a 10x20
grid, and you want to store the population of each grid square into a table.
You could do this:

    03  CITY-GRID-X      OCCURS 10 TIMES.
        05  CITY-GRID-Y      OCCURS 20 TIMES.
            07  CITY-POP         PIC  9(06).

This creates a table with 200 data items named CITY-POP, but now when you
reference it, you must specify two subscripts, one for each dimension:

    CITY-POP(1, 1)              CITY-POP grid at X=1, Y=1
    CITY-POP(3, 5)              CITY-POP grid at X=3, Y=5
    CITY-POP(10, 20)            CITY-POP grid at X=10, Y=20
    CITY-POP(SUBX, SUBY)        CITY-POP grid at SUBX, SUBY

If you wanted to store the population of each grid square for ten cities
you could do this:

    03  CITY-CITY        OCCURS 10 TIMES.
        05  CITY-GRID-X      OCCURS 10 TIMES.
            07  CITY-GRID-Y      OCCURS 20 TIMES.
                09  CITY-POP         PIC  9(06).

This creates a table with 2000 data items named CITY-POP, but when you
reference it, you must specify three subscripts, one for the city and one
for each dimension.  To sum the population of all the cities, you could
code this:

    03  TOTAL-POP        PIC  9(08).
    03  CITY             PIC  9(02).
    03  SUBX             PIC  9(02).
    03  SUBY             PIC  9(02).

    MOVE ZERO TO TOTAL-POP.
    PERFORM VARYING CITY FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL CITY > 10
        AFTER VARYING SUBX FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL SUBX > 10
            AFTER VARYING SUBY FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL SUBY > 20
                ADD CITY-POP(CITY, SUBX, SUBY) TO TOTAL-POP.

As a matter of form, I prefer to indent my PIC and other clauses four more
spaces with each level of OCCURS.  This provides a clear visual cue when
you are scanning a record definition.

In addition to subscripting, COBOL provides a feature called 'indexing'.
Indexing works very similarly to subscripting, but there are differences.
First, an 'index' is defined as part of the table with which it is
associated, by using the INDEXED BY phrase of the OCCURS clause.  The city
table above could have been defined like this:

    03  TEMP             PIC  9(02).
    03  CITY-CITY        OCCURS 10 TIMES INDEXED BY CITY.
        05  CITY-GRID-X      OCCURS 10 TIMES INDEXED BY SUBX.
            07  CITY-GRID-Y      OCCURS 20 TIMES INDEXED BY SUBY.
                09  CITY-POP         PIC  9(06).

The above PERFORM would work on this table in the same way.  But if you want
to change the value of an index you normally must use the SET verb.  For
example:

    SET SUBX TO 4.
    SET SUBX UP BY 1.
    SET SUBX DOWN BY 2.
    SET TEMP TO SUBX.
    SET SUBY TO TEMP.
    SET SUBX TO SUBY.

Using indexes instead of subscripts can have advantages.  One is that you can
often write faster code using indexes, and the other is that you can use the
COBOL SEARCH verb on tables which have indexes declared.  But those are beyond
the scope of this brief introduction.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DISPLAY xxx UPON CONSOLE.

- **From:** WOB@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78r9n9$h58$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<78q581$uf1$1@lyon110.dtr.fr> <36B0AC8F.3FB923AF@mail.beyond-software.com>`

```
In article <36B0AC8F.3FB923AF@mail.beyond-software.com>,
  Wayne_Beavers@Beyond-Software.com wrote:
>
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> > As far as I know; you're just unlucky. The best way is to get your messages
redy for displaying,
> > and then to display it as quickly as possible. Or try to use a file ???
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The CICS WRITE OPERATOR Command, beginning with Version/Release 4.1.0, can
write up to a single 680 byte message to the console. You'd have to break the
message up into logical sub-messages and then write the entire area. From
BATCH, you could use the CICS EXCI facility to accomplish this using DPL to a
CICS program that issues the WRITE OPERATOR. Unfortunately, SYSLOG will show
the message as coming from the CICS Region, not from a BATCH JOB, unless you
build that information directly into the message body or maybe as a header.

HTH....

WOB,
Atlanta

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

- **References:** `<78q581$uf1$1@lyon110.dtr.fr> <36B0AC8F.3FB923AF@mail.beyond-software.com>`

```
From memory you can write out messages to the DDname that WTO reads to
display and then CALL WTO.  I do not know whether WTO actually was a
specially written front end on the site that I used it though.

Ken
```

```
While I am addressing this to Bill as the resident new standard
expert, it is really an open question.

Defining your own functions:  How do you do it under the
new standard, and has anybody implemented it yet?

If I understand the term correctly, a function can be used
as an operand ... for example I could define a function like
so:  IF (function-name) THEN
and the function would be executed and the return value
could be set to TRUE or FALSE.  Is that correct? I would
dearly love to be able to do that in Cobol.  Can I, under
any of the new constructs?
```

```
Thank you for the time you have taken to enter all this,

a

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

could

…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Functions

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78si7d$s2p@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<78q6tb$4ol$1@news.igs.net> <78rmnn$5bn@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com> <78sh8g$gvu$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Yes, Object/methods have a comparable (not same) feature called "inline
invocation".  Once again, I don't know of any vendor who supports it yet.
Look for syntax where there is a double colon (e.g. "::" ) in the middle of
a source line.  Again, this will return any type of "parameter" - so it will
depend on Boolean data types before you can use it in True/False tests.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Do I have a valid date?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-01-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<78qs5j$7g8@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<78adcv$6dd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36af8b01.607195@news1.frb.gov> <36AFD531.4330D680@home.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-OcdnBpFToU0c@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...
>On Thu, 28 Jan 1999 03:10:53, The Goobers <docdwarf@home.com> wrote:
>
  <much snippage>
>
>With LE and I *think* you need MLE: CEECBLDY
>

CEECBLDY does NOT require MLE.  It is available in all releases of LE (after
about 1.3 - I think).  Having said that, I can't swear that it is available
under VSE - but I think it is.
```

```
>> > On Fri, 22 Jan 1999 17:44:02 GMT, rtuset@dpi.state.nc.us wrote:
>> >
>> > >I'm writing a COBOL CICS program that accepts dates from the user in
>> > >mm-dd-yyyy format.  Is there a way to code a function or CALL
statement to
>> > >validate the input string as a valid date (i.e. 02-31-2000 or
14-22-1999
>> > >returns "bad date")?
>> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >     4.  If not, then the original date was invalid.


enough about modules and environments:

006700     02  YEAR-DIV                PIC S9(4)  COMP.

007400

008400                                                       +4, +6, +8.

008900                                                       +5, +7.

009100
009200     02  DATE-DIGITS.
009300         03  DATE-DIGIT          PIC X      OCCURS 8 TIMES.
009400

009900
010000     02  M-DATE-FORMAT           REDEFINES  DATE-DIGITS.
010100         03  M-DATE-MM           PIC 99.
010200         03  M-DATE-DD           PIC 99.
010300         03  M-DATE-YYYY         PIC 9999.
010400
010500     02  D-DATE-FORMAT           REDEFINES  DATE-DIGITS.
010600         03  D-DATE-DD           PIC 99.
010700         03  D-DATE-MM           PIC 99.
010800         03  D-DATE-YYYY         PIC 9999.
010900
011000     02  DATE-SCHEMAS.
011100         03  YMD-SCHEMA          PIC X(13)  VALUE "8765/43/21 1Y".
011200         03  MDY-SCHEMA          PIC X(13)  VALUE "43/21/8765 5M".
011300         03  DMY-SCHEMA          PIC X(13)  VALUE "21/43/8765 5D".
011400
011500         03  DATE-SCHEMA.
011600             04  DATE-SCHEMA-CHAR    PIC X  OCCURS 11 TIMES.
011700             04  DATE-SCHEMA-LAST    PIC 9.
011800             04  DATE-SCHEMA-FORMAT  PIC X.
011900
012000     02  INTERNAL-DATE.
012100         03  INT-YEAR            PIC 9999.
012200         03  INT-MONTH           PIC 99.
012300         03  INT-DAY             PIC 99.
012400     02  FILLER                  REDEFINES  INTERNAL-DATE.
012500         03  INT-DATE-CHAR       PIC X      OCCURS 8 TIMES.
012600
012700     02  EXTERNAL-MONTH.
012800         03  FILLER              PIC X(1).
012900         03  EXT-MONTH           PIC 9(2).
013000         03  FILLER              REDEFINES  EXT-MONTH.
013100             04  EXT-MONTH-CHAR  PIC X      OCCURS 2 TIMES.
013200
013300     02  EXTERNAL-YEAR           PIC X(4).
013400
013500 01  CHARACTER-VALUES.
013600     02  CURR-CHAR               PIC X.
013700         88  CHAR-IS-SPACE                  VALUE IS " ".
013800         88  CHAR-IS-DIGIT                  VALUE IS "0" THRU "9".
013900         88  CHAR-IS-LETTER                 VALUE IS "A" THRU "Z".
014000         88  CHAR-IS-SEPARATOR              VALUE IS "-"  "/" "."
014100                                                     "'".
014200     02  CURR-CHAR-VALUE         REDEFINES  CURR-CHAR
014300                                 PIC 9.
014400     02  ZERO-FORM               PIC X.
014500         88  NUMBER-IS-ZERO                 VALUE IS "0".
014600
014700 LINKAGE SECTION.
014800
014900 01  DATEPK-CONTROL.
015000     02  SESSION-TASK-ID         PIC X(4).
015100     02  DATEPK-OPERATION        PIC X.
015200         88  ENCODE-OPERATION               VALUE IS "E".
015300         88  DECODE-OPERATION               VALUE IS "D".
015400
015500     02  DATEPK-FEEDBACK         PIC X.
015600         88  NO-ERRORS                      VALUE IS SPACE.
015700         88  DATE-ERROR                     VALUE IS "E".
015800         88  OPERATION-ERROR                VALUE IS "O".
015900
016000     02  DATA-MODE               PIC X.
016100         88  YMD-FORMAT                     VALUES ARE "N" "Y".
016200         88  MDY-FORMAT                     VALUES ARE "S" "$".
016300         88  DMY-FORMAT                     VALUES ARE " " ".".
016400
016500     02  DATE-SEPARATOR          PIC X.
016600         88  MONTH-NAME-WANTED              VALUES ARE ":".
016700         88  NO-SEPARATOR-WANTED            VALUES ARE "=".
016800         88  VALID-SEPARATOR                VALUES ARE "/" "."
016900                                                       "-" ":"
017000                                                       "=".
017100 01  DATEPK-DATA.
017200     02  DATEPK-STRING-SIZE      PIC S9(3)  COMP.
017300
017400     02  DATEPK-INTERNAL-FORMAT.
017500         03  INT-CHAR            PIC X      OCCURS 80 TIMES.
017600
017700     02  DATEPK-EXTERNAL-FORMAT.
017800         03  EXT-CHAR            PIC X      OCCURS 80 TIMES.
017900     02  FILLER                  REDEFINES DATEPK-EXTERNAL-FORMAT.
018000         03  EXT-DAY             PIC 9(02).
018100         03  EXT-MONTH-NAME      PIC X(03).
018200         03  FILLER              PIC X(01).
018300         03  EXT-YEAR-VALUE      PIC X(74).
018400     02  FILLER                  REDEFINES DATEPK-EXTERNAL-FORMAT.
018500         03  FILLER              PIC 9(02).
018600         03  FILLER              PIC X(03).
018700         03  EXT-YEAR-SHIFTED    PIC X(05).
018800         03  FILLER              PIC X(70).
018900     02  FILLER                  REDEFINES DATEPK-EXTERNAL-FORMAT.
019000         03  FILLER              PIC 9(02).
019100         03  HYPHEN-1            PIC X(01).
019200         03  HYPHEN-MONTH        PIC X(03).
019300         03  HYPHEN-2            PIC X(01).
019400         03  HYPHEN-YEAR         PIC X(04).
019500         03  FILLER              PIC X(69).
019600
019700 PROCEDURE DIVISION
019800     USING DATEPK-CONTROL
019900           DATEPK-DATA.
020000
020100 BEGIN-PROGRAM.
020200     MOVE SPACE TO DATEPK-FEEDBACK
020300
020400     IF DECODE-OPERATION
020500         PERFORM DECODE-DATE
020600     ELSE
020700     IF ENCODE-OPERATION
020800         PERFORM ENCODE-DATE
020900     ELSE
021000         MOVE "O" TO DATEPK-FEEDBACK
021100     .
021200 RETURN-TO-CALLER.
021300     EXIT PROGRAM
021400     .
021500
021600**********************    DECODE DATE    *************************
021700
021800 DECODE-DATE.
021900     MOVE SPACES TO DATEPK-EXTERNAL-FORMAT
022000     PERFORM CHECK-FORMAT
022100     IF NO-ERRORS
022200         PERFORM MOVE-DATE-TO-EXTERNAL-FORMAT
022300     .
022400
022500 CHECK-FORMAT.
022600     IF NOT VALID-SEPARATOR
022700         MOVE "O" TO DATEPK-FEEDBACK
022800     ELSE
022900         MOVE DATEPK-STRING-SIZE TO DATE-LENGTH
023000         IF DATE-LENGTH < 8
023100         OR DATE-LENGTH > 80
023200             MOVE "O" TO DATEPK-FEEDBACK
023300         ELSE
023400             COMPUTE DATE-TOP = DATE-LENGTH + 1
023500             IF MONTH-NAME-WANTED
023600             OR DMY-FORMAT
023700                 MOVE DMY-SCHEMA TO DATE-SCHEMA
023800             ELSE
023900             IF MDY-FORMAT
024000                 MOVE MDY-SCHEMA TO DATE-SCHEMA
024100             ELSE
024200             IF YMD-FORMAT
024300                 MOVE YMD-SCHEMA TO DATE-SCHEMA
024400             ELSE
024500                 MOVE "O" TO DATEPK-FEEDBACK
024600     .
024700
024800 MOVE-DATE-TO-EXTERNAL-FORMAT.
024900     IF DATE-LENGTH < 10
025000         MOVE  6 TO DATE-CUTOFF
025100     ELSE
025200         MOVE  8 TO DATE-CUTOFF
025300     .
025400     MOVE ZERO TO ZERO-FORM
025500     MOVE 1 TO THIS-CHAR, SCHEMA-P
025600     PERFORM COPY-DATE-TO-EXTERNAL
025700       UNTIL THIS-CHAR > DATE-LENGTH
025800
025900     IF NUMBER-IS-ZERO
026000         MOVE SPACES TO DATEPK-EXTERNAL-FORMAT
026100     ELSE
026200     IF MONTH-NAME-WANTED
026300         PERFORM REPLACE-MONTH-BY-ITS-NAME
026400     .
026500
026600 COPY-DATE-TO-EXTERNAL.
026700     MOVE DATE-SCHEMA-CHAR (SCHEMA-P) TO CURR-CHAR
026800     IF CURR-CHAR IS NUMERIC
026900         MOVE CURR-CHAR-VALUE TO DEST-P
027000         IF DEST-P > DATE-CUTOFF
027100             SUBTRACT 1 FROM THIS-CHAR
027200         ELSE
027300             COMPUTE COPY-P = DATE-TOP - DEST-P
027400             MOVE INT-CHAR (COPY-P) TO EXT-CHAR (THIS-CHAR)
027500                                       CURR-CHAR
027600             IF CURR-CHAR = ZERO
027700                 PERFORM SUPPRESS-IF-LEADING
027800             ELSE
027900                 MOVE CURR-CHAR TO ZERO-FORM
028000     ELSE
028100     IF CURR-CHAR = "/"
028200         IF NO-SEPARATOR-WANTED
028300             SUBTRACT 1 FROM THIS-CHAR
028400         ELSE
028500             MOVE DATE-SEPARATOR TO EXT-CHAR (THIS-CHAR)
028600     ELSE
028700         SUBTRACT 1 FROM SCHEMA-P
028800         MOVE SPACE TO EXT-CHAR (THIS-CHAR)
028900     .
029000     ADD 1 TO SCHEMA-P
029100     ADD 1 TO THIS-CHAR
029200     .
029300
029400 SUPPRESS-IF-LEADING.
029500     IF THIS-CHAR = 1
029600         IF NOT (YMD-FORMAT OR NO-SEPARATOR-WANTED)
029700             MOVE SPACE TO EXT-CHAR (THIS-CHAR)
029800     .
029900
030000 REPLACE-MONTH-BY-ITS-NAME.
030100     MOVE EXT-MONTH-NAME TO EXTERNAL-MONTH
030200
030300     IF EXT-MONTH = 99
030400         MOVE 13 TO EXT-MONTH
030500     .
030600     IF EXT-MONTH < 01
030700     OR EXT-MONTH > 13
030800         MOVE "E" TO DATEPK-FEEDBACK
030900     ELSE
031000         MOVE NAME-OF-MONTH (EXT-MONTH) TO EXT-MONTH-NAME
031100         MOVE EXT-YEAR-VALUE      TO EXTERNAL-YEAR
031200         IF DATE-LENGTH < 11
031300             MOVE EXTERNAL-YEAR   TO EXT-YEAR-SHIFTED
031400         ELSE
031500             MOVE EXT-MONTH-NAME  TO EXTERNAL-MONTH
031600             MOVE "-"             TO HYPHEN-1, HYPHEN-2
031700             MOVE EXTERNAL-MONTH  TO HYPHEN-MONTH
031800             MOVE EXTERNAL-YEAR   TO HYPHEN-YEAR
031900     .
032000
032100**********************    ENCODE DATE    *************************
032200
032300 ENCODE-DATE.
032400     PERFORM CHECK-FORMAT
032500     IF NO-ERRORS
032600         PERFORM MOVE-DATE-TO-INTERNAL-FORMAT
032700     ELSE
032800         MOVE ZEROES TO DATEPK-INTERNAL-FORMAT
032900     .
033000
033100 MOVE-DATE-TO-INTERNAL-FORMAT.
033200     MOVE ZEROES TO DATE-DIGITS, NAME-CHAR-P
033300
033400     MOVE 0 TO NBR-OF-DIGITS, NBR-OF-SEPARATORS,
033500               NBR-OF-CONSECUTIVE-DIGITS
033600
033700     PERFORM GET-DATE-DIGIT
033800       VARYING THIS-CHAR FROM 1 BY 1
033900         UNTIL THIS-CHAR > DATE-LENGTH
034000
034100     PERFORM END-OF-NUMBER-ACTION
034200
034300     IF NBR-OF-DIGITS = 6
034400     AND DATE-LENGTH < 10
034500         PERFORM FILL-UP-WITH-CENTURY
034600     .
034700     IF NBR-OF-DIGITS = 0
034800         PERFORM EXTRACT-FORMATTED-DATE
034900     ELSE
035000     IF NBR-OF-DIGITS = 8 OR 4 OR 2
035100         PERFORM EXTRACT-FORMATTED-DATE
035200         PERFORM CHECK-THE-DATE
035300     ELSE
035400         MOVE "E" TO DATEPK-FEEDBACK
035500     .
035600     IF NO-ERRORS
035700         PERFORM ACCEPT-THE-DATE
035800     .
035900
036000 GET-DATE-DIGIT.
036100     MOVE EXT-CHAR (THIS-CHAR) TO CURR-CHAR
036200     IF CHAR-IS-DIGIT
036300         IF NBR-OF-DIGITS < 8
036400             ADD 1 TO NBR-OF-DIGITS
036500             ADD 1 TO NBR-OF-CONSECUTIVE-DIGITS
036600             MOVE CURR-CHAR TO DATE-DIGIT (NBR-OF-DIGITS)
036700         ELSE
036800             MOVE "E" TO DATEPK-FEEDBACK
036900     ELSE
037000     IF CHAR-IS-SEPARATOR
037100         ADD 1 TO NBR-OF-SEPARATORS
037200         PERFORM END-OF-NUMBER-ACTION
037300     ELSE
037400     IF CHAR-IS-LETTER
037500         PERFORM DETERMINE-THE-MONTH
037600     ELSE
037700     IF CHAR-IS-SPACE
037800         PERFORM END-OF-NUMBER-ACTION
037900     ELSE
038000         MOVE "E" TO DATEPK-FEEDBACK
038100     .
038200
038300 END-OF-NUMBER-ACTION.
038400     IF CONSECUTIVE-DIGITS-OK
038500     AND NBR-OF-SEPARATORS < 3
038600         PERFORM FILL-UP-TO-EVEN-DIGITS
038700     ELSE
038800         MOVE "E" TO DATEPK-FEEDBACK
038900     .
039000
039100 FILL-UP-TO-EVEN-DIGITS.
039200     MOVE ZERO TO NBR-OF-CONSECUTIVE-DIGITS
039300     IF ODD-NBR-OF-DIGITS
039400         MOVE NBR-OF-DIGITS TO THIS-D
039500         ADD 1 TO NBR-OF-DIGITS
039600         MOVE NBR-OF-DIGITS TO DEST-D
039700         PERFORM MOVE-DATE-DIGIT--THEN-ZERO
039800     .
039900
040000 MOVE-DATE-DIGIT--THEN-ZERO.
040100     MOVE DATE-DIGIT (THIS-D) TO DATE-DIGIT (DEST-D)
040200     MOVE        ZERO         TO DATE-DIGIT (THIS-D)
040300     .
040400
040500 FILL-UP-WITH-CENTURY.
040600     MOVE DATE-SCHEMA-LAST TO LAST-D
040700
040800     PERFORM MOVE-DATE-DIGITS-2-RIGHT
040900       VARYING THIS-D FROM NBR-OF-DIGITS BY -1
041000         UNTIL THIS-D < LAST-D
041100
041200     ADD 2 TO NBR-OF-DIGITS
041300     .
041400
041500 MOVE-DATE-DIGITS-2-RIGHT.
041600     COMPUTE DEST-D = THIS-D + 2
041700     PERFORM MOVE-DATE-DIGIT--THEN-ZERO
041800     .
041900
042000 EXTRACT-FORMATTED-DATE.
042100     IF NO-YEAR-ENTERED
042200         MOVE "E" TO DATEPK-FEEDBACK
042300     ELSE
042400     IF DATE-SCHEMA-FORMAT = "Y"
042500         MOVE Y-DATE-YYYY TO YEAR-VALUE
042600         MOVE Y-DATE-MM   TO MONTH-VALUE
042700         MOVE Y-DATE-DD   TO DAY-VALUE
042800     ELSE
042900     IF DATE-SCHEMA-FORMAT = "M"
043000         MOVE M-DATE-YYYY TO YEAR-VALUE
043100         MOVE M-DATE-MM   TO MONTH-VALUE
043200         MOVE M-DATE-DD   TO DAY-VALUE
043300     ELSE
043400     IF DATE-SCHEMA-FORMAT = "D"
043500         MOVE D-DATE-YYYY TO YEAR-VALUE
043600         MOVE D-DATE-MM   TO MONTH-VALUE
043700         MOVE D-DATE-DD   TO DAY-VALUE
043800     .
043900
044000 CHECK-THE-DATE.
044100     IF MONTH-VALUE = 99
044200         PERFORM CHECK-INFINITY-DATE
044300     ELSE
044400     IF MONTH-VALUE < 1 OR 12 < MONTH-VALUE
044500     OR (DAY-VALUE  < 1 OR DAYS (MONTH-VALUE) < DAY-VALUE)
044600         MOVE "E" TO DATEPK-FEEDBACK
044700     ELSE
044800         PERFORM ADJUST-YEAR
044900         IF MONTH-VALUE = 2 AND DAY-VALUE = 29
045000             PERFORM CHECK-LEAP-YEAR
045100     .
045200
045300 CHECK-INFINITY-DATE.
045400     IF DAY-VALUE NOT = 99
045500         MOVE "E" TO DATEPK-FEEDBACK
045600     ELSE
045700     IF YEAR-VALUE = 99 OR 9999
045800         MOVE 9999 TO YEAR-VALUE
045900     ELSE
046000         MOVE "E" TO DATEPK-FEEDBACK
046100     .
046200
046300 ADJUST-YEAR.
046400     IF DATE-LENGTH < 10 AND YEAR-VALUE > 100
046500         IF YEAR-VALUE < 1900 OR > 2099
046600             DIVIDE 100 INTO YEAR-VALUE
046700     .
046800     IF YEAR-VALUE < 50
046900         ADD 2000 TO YEAR-VALUE
047000     ELSE
047100     IF YEAR-VALUE < 100
047200         ADD 1900 TO YEAR-VALUE
047300     .
047400
047500 CHECK-LEAP-YEAR.
047600     DIVIDE 4 INTO YEAR-VALUE
047700         GIVING    YEAR-DIV
047800         REMAINDER YEAR-REM
047900
048000     IF YEAR-REM NOT = ZERO
048100         MOVE "E" TO DATEPK-FEEDBACK
048200     ELSE
048300         DIVIDE 100 INTO YEAR-VALUE
048400             GIVING      YEAR-DIV
048500             REMAINDER   YEAR-REM
048600
048700         IF YEAR-REM = ZERO
048800             DIVIDE 400 INTO YEAR-VALUE
048900                 GIVING      YEAR-DIV
049000                 REMAINDER   YEAR-REM
049100
049200             IF YEAR-REM NOT = ZERO
049300                 MOVE "E" TO DATEPK-FEEDBACK
049400     .
049500
049600 ACCEPT-THE-DATE.
049700     MOVE YEAR-VALUE  TO INT-YEAR
049800     MOVE MONTH-VALUE TO INT-MONTH
049900     MOVE DAY-VALUE   TO INT-DAY
050000
050100     MOVE DATE-TOP TO CHAR-P
050200     MOVE    8     TO COPY-P
050300
050400     PERFORM GET-INTERNAL-DATE
050500       UNTIL CHAR-P = 1
050600     .
050700
050800 GET-INTERNAL-DATE.
050900     SUBTRACT 1 FROM CHAR-P
051000     IF COPY-P > ZERO
051100         MOVE INT-DATE-CHAR (COPY-P) TO INT-CHAR (CHAR-P)
051200         SUBTRACT 1 FROM COPY-P
051300     ELSE
051400         MOVE ZERO TO INT-CHAR (CHAR-P)
051500     .
051600
051700 DETERMINE-THE-MONTH.
051800     IF NAME-CHAR-P = 3
051900         MOVE "E" TO DATEPK-FEEDBACK
052000     ELSE
052100         SUBTRACT 1 FROM THIS-CHAR
052200         PERFORM ACCEPT-MONTH-NAME-CHAR
052300           UNTIL NAME-CHAR-P = 3
052400
052500         IF NBR-OF-DIGITS < 6
052600             PERFORM FIND-MONTH-FROM-NAME
052700               VARYING MONTH-NBR FROM 1 BY 1
052800                 UNTIL NAME-OF-MONTH (MONTH-NBR) = THE-NAME
052900
053000             IF MONTH-NBR > 13
053100                 MOVE "E" TO DATEPK-FEEDBACK
053200             ELSE
053300                 PERFORM ADJUST-DAY-NBR
053400                 PERFORM ADJUST-MONTH-NBR
053500                 MOVE MONTH-NBR TO EXT-MONTH
053600                 PERFORM GET-MONTH-DIGIT
053700                   VARYING CHAR-P FROM 1 BY 1
053800                     UNTIL CHAR-P > 2
053900         ELSE
054000             MOVE "E" TO DATEPK-FEEDBACK
054100     .
054200
054300 ACCEPT-MONTH-NAME-CHAR.
054400     IF CHAR-IS-LETTER
054500         ADD 1 TO THIS-CHAR
054600         ADD 1 TO NAME-CHAR-P
054700         MOVE EXT-CHAR       (THIS-CHAR)
054800           TO THE-NAME-CHAR (NAME-CHAR-P)
054900     ELSE
055000         MOVE "E" TO DATEPK-FEEDBACK
055100     .
055200
055300 FIND-MONTH-FROM-NAME.
055400     EXIT
055500     .
055600
055700 ADJUST-DAY-NBR.
055800     IF NBR-OF-DIGITS = 1
055900         MOVE DATE-DIGIT (1) TO DATE-DIGIT (2)
056000         MOVE    ZERO        TO DATE-DIGIT (1)
056100         ADD 1 TO NBR-OF-DIGITS
056200         ADD 1 TO NBR-OF-CONSECUTIVE-DIGITS
056300     ELSE
056400     IF NBR-OF-DIGITS = 0
056500         MOVE MDY-SCHEMA TO DATE-SCHEMA
056600     .
056700
056800 ADJUST-MONTH-NBR.
056900     IF MONTH-NBR = 13
057000         MOVE 99 TO MONTH-NBR
057100     .
057200
057300 GET-MONTH-DIGIT.
057400     ADD 1 TO NBR-OF-DIGITS
057500     ADD 1 TO NBR-OF-CONSECUTIVE-DIGITS
057600     MOVE EXT-MONTH-CHAR (CHAR-P) TO DATE-DIGIT (NBR-OF-DIGITS)
057700     .
```

```
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

I haven't revisited any of the affected programs in quite a while,
but, if I recall, the routine is two COPY libraries--one for data and
one for procedure code--implemented via table searches and indexes.
If coded judiciously, table searching can be quite efficient on the
Unisys V-Series.

In newer stuff, most date validation is handled by interfacing
applications on client machines, which is outside the realm of
anything done in COBOL.
```
